title: The Birth of Rama
narrative_text:
The noble King Dasaratha, ruler of Ayodhya, was blessed with three queens: Kausalya, Kaikeyi, and Sumitra. Though he reigned with justice and might, a deep sorrow lingered in his heart, for he had no sons to inherit his glorious kingdom. To remedy this, the wise sage Vashishtha advised him to perform a grand sacrifice, the Putrakameshti Yajna, to propitiate the gods and beget progeny.

As the sacred rituals commenced, the heavens rejoiced. The Devas, troubled by the tyranny of the demon king Ravan, approached Lord Vishnu, pleading for his intervention. Vishnu, in his infinite compassion, promised to incarnate as four sons of Dasaratha to vanquish Ravan and restore dharma. He decreed that he would be born as four parts, distributed among the queens.

Upon the completion of the sacrifice, a divine being emerged from the sacrificial fire, bearing a golden bowl filled with celestial nectar, a sacred payasam. He presented it to King Dasaratha, instructing him to distribute it among his queens. Dasaratha, with immense joy, gave half of the payasam to Kausalya, a quarter to Kaikeyi, and the remaining quarter he divided equally between Sumitra.

In due course, the queens conceived. On the ninth day of the bright half of the month of Chaitra, under the auspicious constellation of Punarvasu, Queen Kausalya gave birth to the eldest son, Rama, an incarnation of Lord Vishnu himself, endowed with all divine attributes. He was born to destroy the wicked Ravan and protect the righteous.

Soon after, Queen Kaikeyi bore a son named Bharat, who was a quarter part of Vishnu's essence, known for his unwavering devotion and strength. Queen Sumitra, who had received two portions of the divine payasam, was blessed with twins: Lakshman and Shatrughna. Lakshman, fiercely devoted to Rama, embodied another part of Vishnu, while Shatrughna, equally loyal, represented the final portion.

The birth of the four princes filled Ayodhya with unparalleled joy and festivity. The city was adorned, and celebrations erupted with music, dance, and lavish feasts. King Dasaratha, overwhelmed with happiness, bestowed generous gifts upon Brahmins and the needy. The sage Vashishtha performed the naming ceremonies, bestowing upon them names that reflected their divine destinies.

The four brothers grew up together, exemplifying virtue, strength, and wisdom. Rama, the eldest, was the epitome of righteousness, beloved by all. Lakshman, inseparable from Rama, served him with unwavering loyalty and affection, always by his side. Bharat and Shatrughna, equally devoted to each other, also displayed immense love and respect for their elder brothers. Their presence brought immense delight to King Dasaratha, who saw in them the future protectors of his kingdom and the upholders of dharma.
moral: The story emphasizes the importance of righteousness, devotion, and the fulfillment of divine purpose. It highlights that divine intervention occurs to restore balance and vanquish evil, and that selfless service and fraternal love are virtues to be cherished.

performance_notes.opening_line: In the ancient kingdom of Ayodhya, ruled by the noble King Dasaratha, a deep longing for an heir led to a sacred ritual that would change the course of destiny.
pause_points: To remedy this, the wise sage Vashishtha advised him to perform a grand sacrifice, the Putrakameshti Yajna, to propitiate the gods and beget progeny.
Vishnu, in his infinite compassion, promised to incarnate as four sons of Dasaratha to vanquish Ravan and restore dharma.
On the ninth day of the bright half of the month of Chaitra, under the auspicious constellation of Punarvasu, Queen Kausalya gave birth to the eldest son, Rama, an incarnation of Lord Vishnu himself.
The birth of the four princes filled Ayodhya with unparalleled joy and festivity.

safety_notes: The story is culturally respectful and suitable for elderly listeners, focusing on divine birth and virtues. No elements are present that could be considered inappropriate or distressing.

title: Sita's Swayamvara: The Breaking of Shiva's Bow
narrative_text:
In the ancient and prosperous kingdom of Mithila, ruled by the wise and benevolent King Janaka, lived his beloved daughter, Sita. She was not born of mortal parents but was found by King Janaka in a furrow while plowing the land, a divine gift from Mother Earth herself. Sita grew up to be a maiden of unparalleled beauty, grace, and virtue, known for her gentle demeanor and inner strength.

King Janaka possessed a magnificent and ancient bow, the Pinaka, which once belonged to Lord Shiva. This bow was so immense and heavy that no ordinary man could even lift it, let alone string it. It was a sacred trust, passed down through generations of Mithila's kings. One day, in her childhood innocence, Sita effortlessly lifted and moved the mighty Pinaka while cleaning, a feat that astonished her father. Realizing that his daughter was no ordinary being and destined for a truly powerful and virtuous consort, King Janaka made a solemn vow. He declared that only the man who could lift, string, and break the divine bow of Shiva would be worthy of Sita's hand in marriage.

As Sita reached a marriageable age, King Janaka organized a grand Swayamvara, a ceremony where eligible princes and kings from across Aryavarta would gather to compete for her hand. The news of Sita's beauty and King Janaka's challenge spread far and wide, attracting many powerful and proud rulers. Among them was the mighty King Ravana of Lanka, who, despite not being formally invited, attempted to lift the bow, only to be humiliated by his utter failure to even budge it. Many other valiant kings tried their strength, but none could even lift the colossal bow, leaving them all disheartened and the assembly in despair.

At this time, the revered sage Vishwamitra arrived in Mithila, accompanied by two young princes from Ayodhya, Rama and his younger brother Lakshmana. They had come to Mithila after completing their spiritual journey with the sage. Witnessing the disheartened assembly and the unfulfilled challenge, Guru Vishwamitra, with a knowing smile, turned to Rama. He gently instructed his disciple, 'Rama, my son, behold the bow. It is time for you to fulfill the prophecy.'

With utmost humility and reverence, Prince Rama approached the divine bow. He first offered a silent prayer to Lord Shiva, seeking his blessings. Then, with a calm and serene countenance, he effortlessly lifted the massive Pinaka bow. A collective gasp swept through the assembly as Rama, with a single, graceful motion, strung the bow. The tension was immense, and with a sound like thunder, the mighty bow snapped into two pieces, echoing through the heavens and shaking the very foundations of the earth. The assembly erupted in joyous cheers, and flowers rained down from the sky.

King Janaka was overjoyed, his vow fulfilled. Princess Sita, her heart filled with love and admiration for the valiant and humble prince, stepped forward. With a radiant smile, she placed the ceremonial garland around Rama's neck, accepting him as her husband. Thus, the divine union of Rama and Sita was solemnized, marking the beginning of a legendary journey that would shape the destiny of the world. The Swayamvara of Sita became a testament to true strength, not just of physical prowess, but of virtue, humility, and divine grace.
moral: True strength lies not only in physical power but also in humility, virtue, and divine grace. A pure heart and righteous intent can achieve what mere might cannot.

title: The Abduction of Sita by Ravana
alt_titles: The Golden Deer and the Abduction of Sita
narrative_text: In the tranquil Dandaka forest, Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana lived a peaceful life in their hermitage. One day, the demon king Ravana, instigated by his sister Surpanakha's desire for revenge, plotted to abduct the beautiful Sita. He enlisted the help of the demon Maricha, who transformed himself into a magical golden deer with silver spots and gem-like horns. The deer wandered near their cottage, catching Sita's eye. Enchanted by its beauty, Sita pleaded with Rama to capture it for her. Despite Lakshmana's warning that the deer might be a demon in disguise, Rama, wanting to fulfill his wife's wish, set off in pursuit, leaving Lakshmana to guard Sita. After a long chase, Rama shot the deer with an arrow. As he lay dying, Maricha, in his true form, mimicked Rama's voice and cried out, 'Oh, Sita! Oh, Lakshmana!' Sita, hearing the cry, was filled with fear and urged Lakshmana to go to his brother's aid. Lakshmana tried to reassure her of Rama's invincibility, but Sita, distraught with worry, accused him of having ill intentions. Hurt by her harsh words, Lakshmana drew a protective line (the Lakshmana Rekha) around the cottage, instructing Sita not to cross it under any circumstances, and left to find Rama. Seizing the opportunity, Ravana, disguised as a holy ascetic, approached the hermitage, asking for alms. Sita, bound by her duty to a guest, was in a dilemma. Unwilling to refuse a holy man, she stepped across the protective line to offer him food. At that moment, Ravana revealed his true ten-headed form, seized the terrified Sita, and carried her away in his flying chariot. As they flew through the air, Sita cried out for help. The ancient vulture king, Jatayu, a friend of Rama's father, heard her cries and bravely fought Ravana to rescue her. But the old bird was no match for the powerful demon king, who cut off his wings with his sword, leaving him mortally wounded. As Ravana continued his journey to Lanka, Sita, in a final act of hope, dropped her ornaments, praying that Rama would find them and come to her rescue. Ravana imprisoned Sita in the Ashoka Grove in Lanka, surrounded by demonesses, and relentlessly tried to persuade her to become his queen, but Sita, ever-faithful to Rama, remained defiant.
moral: The story warns against the dangers of desire and deception, and the importance of heeding the advice of loved ones. It also highlights the virtues of unwavering faith, courage in the face of adversity, and the ultimate triumph of righteousness over evil.

performance_notes.opening_line: In the heart of the Dandaka forest, where Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana lived in their peaceful hermitage, a shadow of deceit was about to fall.
pause_points: Sita, hearing the cry, was filled with fear...

But the old bird was no match for the powerful demon king...

safety_notes: The story contains elements of abduction and violence which might be distressing for some listeners. The emotional distress of Sita is a central theme.

title: Hanuman's Leap to Lanka
narrative_text:
At last, a clue had surfaced regarding Sita Devi’s whereabouts! The Vanaras were delighted by the information relayed to them by Sampati.

Facing the Ocean

Now, the question was how to reach Lanka. As they gazed at the endless ocean, the Vanaras felt a wave of depression wash over them. Who knew how many deadly creatures lurked beneath the surface? The relentless surge of waves seemed to threaten dire consequences for anyone daring to cross!

The Vanaras in Dismay

“What should we do?” the Vanaras asked each other, their spirits dampened. Angada realized that the formidable nature of the task had cast a shadow over their enthusiasm. He encouraged them, “Instead of losing heart, we should see this as an excellent opportunity to test our valor and strength. Let us remain calm and see what we can achieve.”

A Call for Strength

They spent the night on the seashore, and in the morning, Angada and Hanuman gathered everyone together. Angada addressed the Vanaras, “I want to know which among us has the strength to cross the sea. The success of our mission depends on him. Lanka is situated a hundred yojanas away. If someone among us took upon himself the task of going there, all others could return to their homes and reunite with their families.”

The Challenge of the Distance

“Now, will each one of the heroes tell me the distance they can cover?” Angada waited for answers, but the group remained silent.

Seeing their hesitation, Angada urged them further, “I have no doubt about your bravery and sincerity. You are all well-known for these qualities. I repeat my question: what distance can you cover with your leap?”

The Heroes Step Forward

One by one, the heroes shared their capabilities: Gaja could cover ten yojanas, Gabaksha could go twenty, Gavaya could leap thirty, Sharava could reach forty, Gandhamadan could cover fifty, Maind sixty, Dwivata seventy, and Sushena could span eighty yojanas in a single leap.

Then it was Jambavan’s turn. “There was a time when I could accomplish much. But I am now old and weak. However, to serve Sri Rama, I believe I can still muster enough strength to cover ninety yojanas in a leap.”

The Resolve of Angada

At that, Angada said, “Well, I can cross a hundred yojanas, but I am uncertain if I can return.”

Jambavan responded, “We are no strangers to your might, O Angada. Why only a hundred? If you wish, you can cross a thousand yojanas and safely return too. But if you are willing to go, we cannot allow you to do so. You are destined to be our king, and your life is most precious. You must stay with us to lead and guide us. It is our duty to ensure you return to Kishkindha, safe and sound.”

Seeking Solutions

“Then what is the way out? If nobody is capable of crossing the sea, the only sensible course left would be to fast unto death! For I doubt Sugriva would pardon us if we report our failure,” Angada lamented. “Hence, O Jambavan, you being the most experienced among us, I look to you for proper counsel.”

“There is a solution to our problem,” Jambavan said. “One who can accomplish this task is among us. Let me present him to you.” He moved toward Hanuman, who sat quietly, seemingly lost in meditation. “O Hanuman! You are the greatest of the Vanaras. How can you afford to remain silent? Your arms possess the strength akin to Garuda’s wings, and in valor, you are equal to Sugriva and Lakshmana. There is none to compare with you in any respect! After all, you were the one who sprang up into the sky in an attempt to swallow the rising sun, mistaking it for a fruit, as soon as you were born! Who here can claim such a record?”

Hanuman’s Resolution

Hanuman felt inspired by these words. He stood up and stretched to his full stature. When the Vanaras saw him ready for the mission, they erupted in cheer and good wishes.

With a face aglow like a smokeless fire, Hanuman prostrated himself before the elder Vanaras, saying, “I will cross the sea with the ease of a breeze. I do not need to stop along the way. I could very well compete with the sun and reach the western horizon before him, returning to the east before he rises again!”

Confident and Determined

“My speed will make the sea swell beneath me! Clouds will part as I pass through them, and the hills will tremble. I have no doubt that I will return with Sita Devi. I see good omens. If necessary, I will uproot Lanka and bring it back with me.”

The Vanaras were thrilled by Hanuman’s self-assurance. Jambavan declared, “Son! You are our pride. You have lifted us from our despair. We will continuously pray for your success and safe return. Our blessings and goodwill accompany you. You are our only hope.”

The Launch from Mount Mahendra

“My pressure at the moment of takeoff might be overwhelming for the earth. I must climb Mount Mahendra and use it as my launching point!” said Hanuman. Without delay, he ascended the hill.

Standing atop Mount Mahendra, Hanuman tested its strength. The mountain trembled, flowers fell from trees, and stones rolled down. Creatures hiding in caves howled and shrieked in fear. The rishis and fairies dwelling there thought for a moment the mountain might explode as they marveled at the might of Hanuman.

The Final Words

Now ready to depart, Hanuman tensed his muscles, clenched his fists, took a deep breath, and spoke his parting words to the Vanaras: “I am leaving for Lanka. If I do not find Sita Devi there, I will proceed to heaven. Should she not be there, I will return to Lanka, capturing Ravana and bringing him back, if necessary, along with the whole city.”

The Leap into the Sky

Hanuman leapt into the sky, casting a shadow over the sea. The atmosphere uprooted several trees, which flew like feathers. His arms spread wide, resembling two gigantic five-headed snakes. His eyes shone like the sun and moon together, presenting an awe-inspiring sight. The air thundered as Hanuman gained speed, akin to a comet heralding the doom of Lanka!

The sea thought, “I should do whatever I can to assist Hanuman, for he is the emissary of Sri Rama. King Sagara, my great benefactor, hailed from the Ikshaku dynasty to which Sri Rama belongs. I must arrange for Hanuman to rest.”

A Moment of Rest

The sea then spoke to Mount Mainak: “You, who are deflecting the surge of demons from the netherworld, should raise your head above the water so Hanuman can rest on your golden peak for a moment.”

Mount Mainak obeyed, rising high above the water. Seeing this, Hanuman initially thought the mountain was trying to obstruct his path. He charged his chest against Mainak, causing it to sway aside in amazement at Hanuman’s strength. The spirit of the mountain took a divine form and addressed him, “I do not know how to praise your unparalleled strength. You have achieved the impossible.”

“However, I request you to take a moment to relax. The sea is indebted to Sagara and his sons, ancestors of Sri Rama, which is why it desires to serve you, as you venture as Sri Rama’s emissary,” Mainak continued.

“Moreover, you are the son of Vayu, the God of Wind. That endears you to me even more. Long ago, the mountains possessed wings, causing panic among the sages who sought shelter within them. Indra destroyed their wings with thunder. I too was nearly stripped of my wings when Vayu sheltered me within the sea. Hence, I am deeply grateful to Vayu and naturally feel an affection for you. Please, rest upon me for a short while.”
moral: The story of Hanuman's Leap to Lanka emphasizes the power of unwavering devotion, courage, and self-belief in overcoming seemingly insurmountable obstacles. It highlights the importance of recognizing one's inner strength and the support of others in achieving a noble goal.

title: The Story of Prahlada and Narasimha
narrative_text:
In ancient times, there lived a powerful asura king named Hiranyakashipu. He had gained a formidable boon from Lord Brahma, which made him virtually immortal: he could not be slain by anything born from a living womb, neither by a man nor an animal, neither during the day nor at night, neither indoors nor outdoors, neither on land nor in the air nor in water, and with no man-made weapon. This boon made him arrogant and cruel, and he began to demand that everyone worship him as the supreme deity, forbidding the worship of Vishnu, whom he despised.

Hiranyakashipu had a son named Prahlada, who was destined to be a great devotee of Lord Vishnu. While Prahlada was still in his mother Kayadhu's womb, she was abducted by Indra during Hiranyakashipu's penance. The divine sage Narada rescued Kayadhu and took her to his ashrama, where Prahlada, from within the womb, listened to Narada's chants of devotion towards Vishnu. As a result, Prahlada grew up with an unwavering faith and devotion to Lord Vishnu.

Hiranyakashipu was enraged by his son's devotion to his arch-enemy. He tried repeatedly to turn Prahlada away from Vishnu, but Prahlada remained steadfast. The young prince even converted other students of the asura clan to Vaishnavism by teaching them the Narayana mantra. This further infuriated Hiranyakashipu, who then began a series of cruel attempts to kill his own son.

First, Hiranyakashipu decided to poison Prahlada, but the poison had no effect. Next, he ordered his daitya soldiers to attack Prahlada with weapons, but Prahlada calmly informed them that their efforts were futile as Vishnu resided within them, protecting him. Hiranyakashipu then had Prahlada trampled by the Aṣṭadiggajas, the eight elephants who bear the weight of the earth, but their tusks broke upon contact with Prahlada, and they retreated. Prahlada was then placed in a room filled with venomous snakes, but the snakes formed a soft bed for him with their bodies.

In another attempt, Prahlada was thrown from a valley into a river, but he was saved by Bhumi, the companion of Vishnu and Lakshmi. Hiranyakashipu's sister, Holika, who had a boon that made her invulnerable to fire, was commanded to sit on a pyre with Prahlada on her lap. Prahlada prayed to Vishnu, and miraculously, Holika was burned to ashes while Prahlada remained unharmed. This event is celebrated as the Hindu festival of Holi.

Even after these failures, Hiranyakashipu did not give up. He ordered the daityas and danavas to collect all the mountains of the earth and construct a barrier over Prahlada in the ocean, intending to submerge him for a year. With bound hands and feet, Prahlada prayed to Vishnu, who granted him boons and returned the mountains to their places, saving Prahlada once more.

Finally, Hiranyakashipu, frustrated and enraged, confronted Prahlada, demanding to know where his Vishnu was. Prahlada calmly replied that Vishnu is omnipresent, existing everywhere, even in a pillar. In a fit of fury, Hiranyakashipu struck a pillar in his palace. From that very pillar, Lord Vishnu emerged in a terrifying and unique form – Narasimha, a being neither fully man nor fully animal, with the head of a lion and the body of a man.

Narasimha caught Hiranyakashipu and placed him on his thighs. The scene unfolded at the threshold of the palace, at dusk – a time that was neither day nor night, and a place that was neither indoors nor outdoors. With his sharp nails, which were neither man-made weapons nor natural animal claws, Narasimha disemboweled and killed Hiranyakashipu, thus nullifying all of Brahma's boons and fulfilling the conditions of his virtual immortality.

After the demise of his tyrannical father, Prahlada ascended the asura throne and ruled justly and virtuously. He continued his devotion to Vishnu and was known for his generosity and kindness. After a long and righteous life, Prahlada attained Vaikuntha, the abode of Vishnu and Lakshmi.
moral: The story of Prahlada and Narasimha teaches us the unwavering power of devotion (bhakti) and faith. It illustrates that true devotion can overcome any obstacle, and that divine protection is always available to those who sincerely believe. It also emphasizes the triumph of good over evil and the ultimate victory of dharma.

pause_points: ...he began to demand that everyone worship him as the supreme deity, forbidding the worship of Vishnu, whom he despised.
...As a result, Prahlada grew up with an unwavering faith and devotion to Lord Vishnu.
...This further infuriated Hiranyakashipu, who then began a series of cruel attempts to kill his own son.

...Narasimha disemboweled and killed Hiranyakashipu, thus nullifying all of Brahma's boons and fulfilling the conditions of his virtual immortality.
emphasis_words: neither fully man nor fully animal

safety_notes: The story contains descriptions of violence (Hiranyakashipu's attempts to kill Prahlada, Narasimha killing Hiranyakashipu). While essential to the narrative, these parts should be narrated with a calm and measured tone, focusing on the divine intervention and moral outcome rather than graphic details. The overall message of devotion and divine protection should be emphasized.

title: The Churning of the Ocean
narrative_text:
Once, the great sage Durvasa, known for his short temper, offered a celestial garland to Indra, the king of the gods. Indra, in his arrogance, placed the garland on the trunk of his elephant, Airavata, who promptly threw it to the ground. Enraged by this disrespect, Durvasa cursed Indra and all the devas to lose their strength, fortune, and immortality. Soon after, the asuras, led by their powerful king Bali, defeated the weakened devas and took control of the universe.

In their despair, the devas sought the help of Lord Vishnu, who advised them to churn the ocean of milk, Ksheer Sagar, to obtain the nectar of immortality, Amrit. This monumental task, however, required the co-operation of the asuras. The devas, swallowing their pride, approached the asuras and struck a deal to share the Amrit. And so, the great churning of the ocean began. Mount Mandara was used as the churning rod, and Vasuki, the serpent king, became the churning rope. The asuras, wanting to be on the safer side, chose to hold Vasuki's head, while the devas held his tail. As the churning began, Mount Mandara started to sink into the ocean. To prevent this, Lord Vishnu took the form of a giant tortoise, Kurma, and supported the mountain on his back. With Lord Brahma presiding from the top of the mountain, the churning commenced.

From the depths of the ocean emerged fourteen precious treasures, the ratnas. First came the dreadful poison, Halahala, which threatened to destroy the universe. The terrified devas and asuras pleaded with Lord Shiva for help. The compassionate Lord Shiva swallowed the poison, and his throat turned blue, earning him the name Neelkantha. Then emerged Kamadhenu, the wish-fulfilling cow; Uchhaishravas, the seven-headed horse; Airavata, the celestial elephant; the Parijat tree with its divine flowers; the goddess of wine, Varuni; the goddess of wealth, Lakshmi, who chose Lord Vishnu as her consort; Chandra, the moon, which Lord Shiva adorned on his head; and finally, Dhanvantari, the divine physician, carrying the pot of Amrit.

As soon as the Amrit appeared, the asuras snatched it and a fierce battle ensued. To save the devas, Lord Vishnu took the form of a beautiful celestial maiden, Mohini. The asuras, captivated by her beauty, forgot all about the Amrit and asked her to distribute it. Mohini, with a mischievous smile, agreed and made the devas and asuras sit in separate rows. She then began serving the Amrit to the devas first. One of the asuras, Rahu, disguised himself as a deva and sat among them. The sun and moon gods, Surya and Chandra, noticed this and alerted Mohini. Just as Rahu drank the Amrit, Mohini, in her true form as Vishnu, severed his head with the Sudarshana Chakra. Since the Amrit had already reached his throat, both his head and body became immortal, and they are known as Rahu and Ketu, who are said to cause solar and lunar eclipses. By the time the asuras realized they had been tricked, the devas had consumed all the Amrit and, with their powers restored, they defeated the asuras and regained control of the heavens.

The moral of the story is that good always triumphs over evil, and that co-operation and perseverance can lead to great rewards. It also teaches us that pride and arrogance lead to downfall, while humility and devotion are rewarded.
moral: Good always triumphs over evil. Co-operation and perseverance can lead to great rewards. Pride and arrogance lead to downfall, while humility and devotion are rewarded.

performance_notes.opening_line: Let me tell you the story of the great churning of the ocean, a tale of co-operation, conflict, and divine intervention.
pause_points: ...and so, the great churning of the ocean began.
...and his throat turned blue, earning him the name Neelkantha.
...and they are known as Rahu and Ketu, who are said to cause solar and lunar eclipses.

safety_notes: The story contains elements of conflict and deception, but it is a classic mythological tale with a clear moral.

title: The Birth of Krishna
narrative_text: In the ancient land of Bharat, the tyrant Kamsa ruled Mathura with an iron fist. A prophecy had sealed his fate: the eighth son of his sister, Devaki, would bring about his demise. Fearful, Kamsa imprisoned Devaki and her husband, Vasudeva, mercilessly slaying their first seven children. As the time for the eighth child's birth drew near, the very elements of nature seemed to hold their breath in anticipation. On a dark, stormy night, in the bleak confines of the prison, the divine child, Krishna, was born. At the moment of his birth, a celestial light filled the cell, and the chains that bound his parents fell away. The prison guards fell into a deep slumber, and a divine voice instructed Vasudeva to take the child to Gokula and exchange him with the newborn daughter of Nanda and Yashoda. With the infant Krishna in a basket, Vasudeva embarked on a perilous journey. He reached the banks of the raging Yamuna river, which, upon seeing the divine child, miraculously parted to allow him passage. In Gokula, Vasudeva found Nanda and Yashoda's household asleep. He gently placed Krishna by Yashoda's side and took her daughter, returning to the prison just as the magical slumber lifted. When Kamsa heard of the birth, he rushed to the prison to kill the child. But as he tried to slay the infant girl, she transformed into the goddess Yogamaya, who warned him that his destroyer was already safe and sound, growing up in Gokula. The birth of Krishna, the eighth avatar of Vishnu, marked the beginning of the end for Kamsa's tyranny and a new dawn of hope for the people.
moral: The story of Krishna's birth is a powerful reminder that even in the darkest of times, divine intervention can restore righteousness and that good will always triumph over evil. It underscores the importance of faith, devotion, and the unwavering belief in a higher power to guide and protect humanity.

performance_notes.opening_line: In the ancient city of Mathura, a prophecy foretold the end of the tyrannical King Kamsa at the hands of his own sister's eighth child.
pause_points: ...the eighth son of his sister, Devaki, would bring about his demise.
...a divine light filled the cell, and the chains that bound his parents fell away.
...miraculously parted to allow him passage.
...she transformed into the goddess Yogamaya, who warned him that his destroyer was already safe and sound, growing up in Gokula.

safety_notes: The story contains themes of imprisonment and the death of infants, which may be sensitive for some listeners. However, it is a classic story and the focus is on the divine birth and the triumph of good.

source_citations.title: Sri Krishna Janmashtami Story and Significance From Srimad Bhagavatam
source_citations.author: Prakriti Anand

title: Krishna and the Lifting of Govardhan Hill
narrative_text:
In the sacred Braj region, near Vrindavan, stood the Govardhan Hill, a life-sustaining ridge that provided for the pastoral community. The villagers, whose lives revolved around their cows and the bounty of nature, traditionally worshipped Indra, the Vedic god of rain and storms, through annual Indra-yajnas to ensure prosperity and abundant rainfall. 

Young Krishna, however, questioned this age-old practice. He explained to the villagers that true sustenance came not from blind rituals, but from nature itself—the cows, the land, and the principles of dharma. He proposed that their gratitude should be directed towards what truly sustained their lives: Govardhan Hill, the cows, and the local environment, rather than Indra. The villagers, swayed by Krishna's wisdom, agreed to worship Govardhan Hill instead of Indra. This was a revolutionary shift, moving from fear-based worship to devotion rooted in gratitude.

Feeling insulted and his authority challenged, Indra unleashed his wrath upon Vrindavan. A devastating storm descended upon the region, bringing heavy rain, thunder, flooding, and violent winds. Vrindavan was submerged, and the lives of the cows, children, and homes were in grave danger. In their despair, the villagers turned to Krishna for help.

With divine compassion, Krishna lifted the mighty Govardhan Hill with just one hand, holding it aloft like a child would hold a mushroom. He called upon all the villagers and their animals to take shelter beneath the colossal canopy of the hill, assuring them, "Do not fear. I am protecting you." For seven days and seven nights, Krishna held the mountain, providing a safe haven from Indra's relentless storm.

Realizing the futility of his pride and recognizing Krishna as the Supreme Being, Indra finally withdrew the storm and acknowledged his mistake. Krishna, in his infinite wisdom, did not punish Indra but instead restored balance. With the storm abated, Krishna gently placed Govardhan Hill back on the ground. The villagers, overwhelmed with relief and gratitude, celebrated with tears and offerings. From that day forward, Krishna became known as Govardhan-dhari, the lifter of the mountain.

This profound story teaches us that true strength lies not in power or ego, but in compassion and care. It highlights the sacredness of nature and the importance of humility. Krishna's act was not merely a display of strength, but a promise of divine protection for those who respect life and live in harmony with their surroundings. Govardhan Hill, therefore, is not just a geographical feature; it is a symbol of Krishna's unwavering care and protection.
moral: God protects those who respect life. Nature is sacred. Power without humility collapses. True strength lies in compassion and care, not in ego or brute force.

performance_notes.opening_line: In the sacred Braj region, near Vrindavan, stood the Govardhan Hill...
pause_points: ...agreed to worship Govardhan Hill instead of Indra.
...In their despair, the villagers turned to Krishna for help.
...For seven days and seven nights, Krishna held the mountain, providing a safe haven from Indra's relentless storm.
...From that day forward, Krishna became known as Govardhan-dhari, the lifter of the mountain.

title: The Story of Ganga's Descent
narrative_text:
The story begins with King Sagara of the Suryavamsa, who decided to perform the Ashvamedha Yagna. During the ritual, his sacrificial horse went missing. The king ordered his 60,000 sons to find the horse. These arrogant princes, in their quest, caused destruction and eventually arrived at Sage Kapila's hermitage. They found the sage in deep meditation with the horse tied nearby. Mistaking Kapila for the thief, they accused him and set fire beneath him. Disturbed, Sage Kapila opened his eyes, and with a powerful gaze, all 60,000 princes were instantly reduced to ashes.

King Sagara, worried about his missing sons, sent his grandson, Amsuman, to find them. Amsuman discovered the tragic fate of his uncles and learned from Sage Kapila that their souls could only find liberation if the sacred waters of Ganga, then residing in Swarga (heaven), flowed over their ashes. Amsuman dedicated his life to this arduous task but was unsuccessful.

His grandson, Bhagiratha, inherited this sacred duty. Bhagiratha performed intense penance, praying to Brahma to release Ganga from Brahmaloka. Brahma explained that Ganga's immense force would cause her to plunge directly into Patala (the nether regions) if released without proper control. He advised Bhagiratha to pray to Lord Shiva, who alone could withstand Ganga's descent.

Bhagiratha then undertook severe austerities to please Lord Shiva. Impressed by his devotion, Shiva agreed to hold Ganga in his matted locks (Jata) to break her fall. When Ganga descended, her mighty currents were trapped within Shiva's hair. Bhagiratha had to pray to Shiva again to release her gently. Shiva then released Ganga, allowing her to flow gracefully from his locks onto the earth.

As Ganga flowed, her powerful currents passed through the hermitage of Sage Jahnu. Angered by the disturbance, Sage Jahnu, in a fit of rage, swallowed the entire river. Distraught, Bhagiratha pleaded with Jahnu to release Ganga. Moved by Bhagiratha's earnest prayers, Jahnu released Ganga through his ear. From that day, Ganga also became known as Jahnavi, meaning 'daughter of Jahnu'.

Ganga then flowed over the ashes of King Sagara's 60,000 sons, purifying their souls and granting them liberation. This momentous event is celebrated as Ganga Dasara. Ganga is said to have flowed through three regions: Swarga (heaven), the astral region, and finally, Earth. She is known by various names, including Mandakini, Bhagirathi, and Jahnavi. The confluence of Bhagirathi and Alakananda at Devprayag marks the point where she is truly called Ganga, eventually joining the Yamuna and the unseen Sarasvati at Allahabad.
moral: The moral of the story emphasizes the power of persistent effort, devotion, and faith. Bhagiratha's unwavering determination across generations ultimately led to the purification and liberation of his ancestors. It also highlights the sanctity of Ganga and the belief that even a single drop of her water, or sincere devotion, can lead to spiritual liberation and freedom from the cycle of rebirth. The story also subtly conveys that true faith and sincerity can overcome any obstacle.

performance_notes.opening_line: In ancient times, a great king named Sagara of the Suryavamsa embarked on a sacred ritual...
pause_points: ...all 60,000 princes were instantly reduced to ashes.
...their souls could only find liberation if the sacred waters of Ganga...flowed over their ashes.
...Shiva agreed to hold Ganga in his matted locks...
...Sage Jahnu, in a fit of rage, swallowed the entire river.
...Ganga then flowed over the ashes of King Sagara's 60,000 sons, purifying their souls and granting them liberation.

safety_notes: The story contains a scene where princes are turned to ashes, which might be sensitive for some listeners. Emphasize the divine justice and the ultimate liberation.

title: The Story of Savitri and Satyavan
alt_titles: Savitri and the Lord of Death
narrative_text:
In Madra, there lived a noble king, Ashwapati, who was wise and just. He was childless and, desiring an heir, performed a great yajna to the Sun God, Savitr. Pleased with his devotion, the god granted him a boon: a daughter of radiant beauty and virtue would be born to him. In time, a daughter was born, and she was named Savitri, in honor of the god.

Savitri grew into a woman of such grace and intelligence that no man felt worthy to ask for her hand. Her father, seeing this, told her to travel and find a husband for herself. Savitri journeyed far and wide, and in a forest hermitage, she met Satyavan, the son of a blind, exiled king named Dyumatsena. Satyavan was handsome, kind, and virtuous, and Savitri’s heart was captured. She returned to her father’s kingdom and announced her choice.

At that moment, the great sage Narada was visiting the court. He praised Savitri’s choice but then revealed a terrible secret: Satyavan was destined to die in exactly one year. Ashwapati was horrified and begged his daughter to choose another husband, but Savitri was resolute. “I have chosen once,” she said, “and I will not choose again. I will marry Satyavan, and I will share his fate.”

And so, Savitri and Satyavan were married. Savitri left the palace and went to live with her husband and his family in the forest. She served them with love and devotion, never revealing the terrible secret she carried in her heart. As the day of Satyavan’s foretold death approached, Savitri grew more and more anxious. Three days before the fateful day, she began a strict fast and stood in prayer day and night.

On the morning of the last day, Satyavan prepared to go into the forest to chop wood. Savitri begged to accompany him, and he agreed. As he worked, Satyavan suddenly felt a sharp pain in his head and lay down, his head in Savitri’s lap. Soon, a dark figure appeared before them. It was Yama, the God of Death, who had come to take Satyavan’s soul.

Yama gently extracted the soul from Satyavan’s body and began to move away. But Savitri, her heart filled with love and determination, followed him. Yama tried to persuade her to turn back, but she refused. Impressed by her devotion, Yama offered her a boon, anything except the life of her husband. Savitri asked for her father-in-law’s sight and kingdom to be restored. Yama granted her wish and again told her to return, but she continued to follow him.

Again, Yama offered her a boon, and again Savitri chose wisely. She asked for a hundred sons for her father, and Yama granted it. Still, she followed. A third time, Yama offered a boon, and this time Savitri asked for a hundred sons for herself and Satyavan. Yama, in his haste, granted this wish as well. But then he realized his mistake: how could Savitri have sons with Satyavan if he were dead?

Impressed by her cleverness and unwavering devotion, Yama smiled. “You have won,” he said. “Your love is stronger than death.” He returned Satyavan’s soul to his body, and Satyavan awoke as if from a deep sleep. They returned to the hermitage to find that Dyumatsena had regained his sight and his kingdom. In time, Savitri and Satyavan had a hundred sons, and they lived a long and happy life together.
moral: Love, devotion, and unwavering determination can overcome even the greatest of obstacles, even death itself.

pause_points: In time, a daughter was born, and she was named Savitri, in honor of the god.
She returned to her father’s kingdom and announced her choice.
Ashwapati was horrified and begged his daughter to choose another husband, but Savitri was resolute.
As the day of Satyavan’s foretold death approached, Savitri grew more and more anxious.
Soon, a dark figure appeared before them. It was Yama, the God of Death, who had come to take Satyavan’s soul.
Yama granted her wish and again told her to return, but she continued to follow him.
But then he realized his mistake: how could Savitri have sons with Satyavan if he were dead?
He returned Satyavan’s soul to his body, and Satyavan awoke as if from a deep sleep.

title: The Monkey and the Crocodile
narrative_text:
On the shore of the sea was a great rose-apple tree that was never without fruit. In it lived a monkey named Red-Face.

Now one day a crocodile named Ugly-Mug crawled out of the ocean under the tree and burrowed in the soft sand. Then Red-Face said: "You are my guest, sir. Pray eat these rose-apples which I throw you. You will find them like nectar. You know the proverb:

A fool or scholar let him be,
Pleasant or hideous to see,
A guest, when offerings are given,
Is useful as a bridge to heaven.

Ask not his home or education,
His family or reputation,

But offer thanks and sacrifice:
For so prescribes the lawbook wise.

And again:

By honoring the guests who come
Wayworn from some far-distant home
To share the sacrifice, you go
The noblest way that mortals know.

And once again:

If guests unhonored leave your door,
And sadly sighing come no more,
Your fathers and the gods above
Turn from you and forget their love."

Thus he spoke and offered rose-apples. And the crocodile ate them and enjoyed a long and pleasant conversation with the monkey before returning to his home. So the monkey and the crocodile rested each day in the shade of the rose-apple tree. They spent the time in cheerful conversation on various subjects, and were happy.

Now the crocodile went home and gave his wife the rose-apples which he had not eaten. And one day she asked him: "My dear husband, where do you get such fruits? They are like nectar."

"My dear," he said, "I have an awfully good friend, a monkey named Red-Face. He gives me these fruits in the most courteous manner."

Then she said: "If anyone eats such nectar fruit every day, his heart must be turned to nectar. So, if you value your wife, give me his heart, and I will eat it. Then I shall never grow old or sick, but will be a delightful companion for you."

But he objected: "In the first place, my dear, he is our adopted brother. Secondly, he gives us fruit. I cannot kill him. Please do not insist. Besides, there is a proverb:

To give us birth, we need a mother;
For second birth we need another:
And friendship's brothers seem by far
More dear than natural brothers are."

But she said: "You have never refused me before. So I am sure it is a she-monkey. You love her and spend the whole day with her. That is why you will not give me what I want. And when you meet me at night, your sighs are hot as a flame of fire. And when you hold me and kiss me, you do not hug me tight. I know some other woman has stolen into your heart."

Then the crocodile was quite dejected, and said to his wife:

When I am at your feet
And at your service, sweet,
Why do you look at me
With peevish jealousy?

But her face swam in tears when she heard him, and she said:

"You love her, you deceiver;
Your wishes never leave her;

Her pretty shamming steals upon your heart.
My rivalry is vain, sir;
And so I pray abstain, sir,
From service that is only tricky art.

"Besides, if you do not love her, why not kill her when I ask you? And if it is really a he-monkey, why should you love him? Enough! Unless I eat his heart, I shall starve myself to death in your house."

Now when he saw how determined she was, he was distracted with anxiety, and said: "Ah, the proverb is right:

Remember that a single grab
Suffices for a fish or crab,
For fool or woman; and 'tis so
For sot, cement, or indigo.

"Oh, what shall I do? How can I kill him?" With these thoughts in mind, he visited the monkey.

Now the monkey had missed his friend, and when he saw him afflicted, he said: "My friend, why have you not been here this long time? Why don't you speak cheerfully, and repeat something witty?"

The crocodile replied: "My friend and brother, my wife scolded me today. She said: 'You ungrateful wretch! Do not show me your face. You are living daily at a friend's expense, and make him no return. You do not even show him the door of your house. You cannot possibly make amends for this. There is a saying:

The Brahman-murderer or thief,
Drunkard or liar, finds relief;
While for ingratitude alone
No expiation will atone.

'I regard this monkey as my brother-in-law. So bring him home, and we will make some return for his kindness. If you refuse, I will see you later in heaven.' Now I could not come to you until she had finished her scolding. And this long time passed while I was quarreling with her about you. So please come home with me. Your brother's wife has set up an awning. She has fixed her clothes and gems and rubies and all that, to pay you a fitting welcome. She has hung holiday garlands on the doorposts. And she is waiting impatiently."

"My friend and brother," said the monkey, "your lady is very kind. It is quite according to the proverb:

Six things are done by friends:
To take, and give again;
To listen, and to talk;
To dine, to entertain.

"But we monkeys live in trees, and your home is in the water. How can I go there? Rather bring your lady here, brother, that I may bow down and receive her blessing."

The crocodile said: "My friend, our home is on a lovely sand-bank under the water. So climb on my back and travel comfortably with nothing to fear."

When the monkey heard this, he was delighted and said: "If that is possible, my friend, then hasten. Why delay? Here I am on your back."

But as he sat there and saw the crocodile swimming in the bottomless ocean, the monkey was terribly frightened and said: "Go slow, brother. My whole body is drenched by the great waves."

And the crocodile thought when he heard this: "If he fell from my back, he could not move an inch, the water is so deep. He is in my power. So I will tell him my purpose, and then he can pray to his favorite god."

And he said: "Sir, I have deceived you and brought you to your death, because my wife bade me do it. So pray to your favorite god."

"Brother," said the monkey, "what harm have I done her or you? Why have you planned to kill me?"

"Well," replied the crocodile, "those nectar fruits tasted so sweet that she began to long to eat your heart. That is why I have done this."

Then the quick-witted monkey said: "If that is the case, sir, why didn't you tell me on shore? For then I might have brought with me another heart, very sweet indeed, which I keep in a hole in the rose-apple tree. As it is, I am forlorn in this heart, at being taken to her in vain, without my sweet heart."

When he heard this, the crocodile was delighted and said: "If you feel so, my friend, give me that other heart. And my cross wife will eat it and give up starving herself. Now I will take you back to the rose-apple tree."

So he turned back and swam toward the rose-apple tree, while the monkey murmured a hundred prayers to every kind of a god. And when at last he came to shore, he hopped and jumped farther and farther, climbed up the rose-apple tree, and thought: "Hurrah! My life is saved. Surely, the saying is a good one:

We dare not trust a rogue; nor must
We trust in those deserving trust:
For danger follows, and we fall
Destroyed and ruined, roots and all.

So today is my rebirthday."

The crocodile said: "My friend and brother, give me the heart, so that my wife may eat it and give up starving herself."

Then the monkey laughed, and scolded him, saying: "You fool! You traitor! How can anyone have two hearts? Go home, and never come back under the rose-apple tree. You know the proverb:

Whoever trusts a faithless friend
And twice in him believes,
Lays hold on death as certainly
As when a mule conceives."
moral: Never trust a faithless friend, for it can lead to betrayal and danger. Be wary of those who seek to harm you, even if they appear friendly.

pause_points: Thus he spoke and offered rose-apples.
Then she said: "If anyone eats such nectar fruit every day, his heart must be turned to nectar."
Now when he saw how determined she was, he was distracted with anxiety.
Then the quick-witted monkey said: "If that is the case, sir, why didn't you tell me on shore?"
Then the monkey laughed, and scolded him, saying: "You fool! You traitor!"

title: The Lion and the Mouse
alt_titles: Aesop's Fable: The Lion and the Mouse
narrative_text:
A Lion lay asleep in the forest, his great head resting on his paws. A timid little Mouse came upon him unexpectedly, and in in her fright and haste to get away, ran across the Lion's nose. Roused from his nap, the Lion laid his huge paw angrily on the tiny creature to kill her.

"Spare me!" begged the poor Mouse. "Please let me go and some day I will surely repay you."

The Lion was much amused to think that a Mouse could ever help him. But he was generous and finally let the Mouse go.

Some days later, while stalking his prey in the forest, the Lion was caught in the toils of a hunter's net. Unable to free himself, he filled the forest with his angry roaring. The Mouse knew the voice and quickly found the Lion struggling in the net. Running to one of the great ropes that bound him, she gnawed it until it parted, and soon the Lion was free.

"You laughed when I said I would repay you," said the Mouse. "Now you see that even a Mouse can help a Lion."

A kindness is never wasted.
moral: A kindness is never wasted. Even the smallest creature can help the largest.

The Lion was much amused to think that a Mouse could ever help him.
Unable to free himself, he filled the forest with his angry roaring.

title: The Four Friends
alt_titles: The Deer, the Crow, the Mouse, and the Tortoise
narrative_text:
In a serene forest, far from human settlements, lived a deer, a crow, a mouse, and a tortoise. These four creatures were the best of friends, sharing their days and discussing the happenings of the forest each evening. One day, the deer returned to their gathering place looking distressed. "Hunters are setting traps all over the forest! How can we protect ourselves?" the deer exclaimed. The clever crow quickly responded, "Our friend the mouse has a solution!" The crow recounted an incident where a flock of doves had been caught in a hunter's net, only to be freed by the mouse's sharp teeth. The doves, grateful for their freedom, flew away.

Later, while the three friends waited for the deer, who had gone in search of water, they grew worried when he didn't return. At dawn, the crow flew out to search for him and found the deer caught in a hunter's net. "Don't worry, my friend," the crow reassured the deer, then rushed back to inform the tortoise and the mouse. The tortoise, with a plan in mind, suggested, "Mouse, quickly go with the crow and chew through the net!" The crow carried the mouse to the deer's location. Without delay, the mouse gnawed through the net, freeing the deer.

Just as they celebrated, the anxious tortoise arrived. "Why did you come here?" the deer asked, "If the hunter returns, you'll never escape!" Indeed, the hunter returned, heard their voices, and saw the tortoise. The friends scattered, but the slow tortoise was caught and tied to a stick. "Oh no! Poor Tortoise is in trouble!" cried the deer. The deer then devised a plan. He lay down near a pond, pretending to be dead, while the crow pecked at him. When the hunter saw the 'dead' deer, he dropped the tortoise to grab the deer. As the hunter approached, the deer leaped up and ran into the pond, and the crow flew away. The mouse quickly chewed through the ropes, freeing the tortoise, who then slipped into the water. The hunter, bewildered and empty-handed, left the forest, believing it to be haunted. The four friends, reunited and safe, returned home, happy to be together once more.
moral: True friendship and clever teamwork can overcome any obstacle.

performance_notes.opening_line: In a serene forest, far from human settlements, lived a deer, a crow, a mouse, and a tortoise.
pause_points: How can we protect ourselves?
Don't worry, my friend
Why did you come here?
Oh no! Poor Tortoise is in trouble!

safety_notes: Ensure the hunter's actions are not depicted as overly violent or frightening for elderly listeners. Focus on the friends' ingenuity and cooperation.

title: The Ass in the Tiger's Skin
narrative_text:
In the city of Hastinapura, there lived a washerman named Vilaasa. He owned a donkey that, due to carrying excessive burdens, became weak and was on the verge of dying. To save his donkey, the washerman covered it with a tiger's skin and released it into a cornfield near the forest. 

When the farmers saw the donkey from afar, they mistook it for a tiger and quickly fled in fear. The donkey, disguised as a tiger, happily grazed on the corn in the field without any disturbance. 

One day, a corn guardian, whose body was protected by a grey blanket, stood in a lonely place with his bow and arrow ready. The well-fed donkey, seeing him from afar and mistaking him for a she-donkey, made a sound and ran towards him. 

Upon hearing the sound, the corn guardian realized it was a donkey and easily killed it. 

Hence, it is said: For a long time indeed, grazing in silence, the unintelligent sees prosperity. But covered by a tiger skin, the ass was killed from a fault of his speech.
moral: One should not pretend to be something they are not, and one's true nature will eventually be revealed, often with dire consequences. Silence can sometimes be golden, especially when deception is involved.

...fled in fear.
...without any disturbance.
...ran towards him.

title: The Old Tiger and the Greedy Traveler
alt_titles: The Old Tiger and the Traveller
narrative_text:
One day I was wandering in the southern forest and there I saw an old tiger who had taken the ceremonial bath and was holding a bunch of sacred grass in his paw. He was sitting on the edge of a pond and every once in a while he would call out: "Travellers! Travellers! Here is a golden bracelet which is yours for the taking."

Now one and another, after listening to the tiger, became frightened, and none would go near him. But by and by there came one greedy wayfarer who thought: "This is a lucky chance. Yet I suppose I ought not to take so great a risk. For I know:

The blessing of a blessing dies,
    If come upon in evil ways;
The very draught that deifies,
    If it be poisoned, slays.

On the other hand, the winning of a good thing always involves a certain risk. As the proverb says:

To him who never takes a chance,
Fortune her favor never grants;
But to the bold her favor gives,
And he is happy—if he lives.

Suppose I investigate."

So he said aloud: "Where is this bracelet of yours?"

The tiger held out his paw and showed the bracelet, but the traveller said: "How can I trust a bloodthirsty creature like you?"

"Listen, good traveller," answered the tiger, "long ago when I was young I was terribly wicked. I killed any number of cows and Brahmans and men, and in revenge I don’t know how many of my own sons were killed, and my wife too. To-day I am alone in the world. Then a certain pious man taught me to practise blessed charity, and I am now following his teachings. I take my ceremonial bath, I give gifts, I am old, my claws and teeth are fallen out, and I show mercy. Why should you not trust me? You know yourself what the eight cardinal virtues are:

Gifts to God's creatures, gifts to God,
    Study, and penance, self-restraint,
Absence of greed, and lack of fraud,
    And patience: these make up the saint.

Now the first four may practised be
For show, without sincerity;
But the last four are only found
In those whose inmost heart is sound.

Now I am so free from greed that, though I hold this bracelet of gold in my own paw, I am eager to give it to anyone who wants it. But there you have the saying ‘Tiger eats man,’ and it is hard to get around it. You know yourself:

In ancient ruts the world moves even now;
    And though sage words from harlot lips may flow,
    Men rather heed the Brahman's counsel, though
He may have murdered some unhappy cow.

Besides, I have studied the sacred law. Listen:

As heaven's water in a thirsty place,
    As food to them that faint,
Thrice blest the gift, O pride of Pandu's race,
    Which beggar takes from saint.

Their life to other creatures is as dear,
    As your life is to you;
The saint has pity for another's fear,
    And takes his point of view.

A man may read his duty clear
    In giving—and withholding too,
In woe and weal, in smile and tear:
    "Just take the other's point of view."

Furthermore:

Who sees in all that lives his own dear life,
    Who sees in others' wealth a clod, a weed,
As his own mother sees his neighbor's wife,
    He sees, he sees indeed.

Now you are a poor man. That is why I am particularly anxious to give you this. For the proverb says:

Unto the poor give alms, my son,
    Waste not your gold on men of wealth;
With medicine much can be done,
    But not for men in perfect health.

And again:

When place and time are rightly met,
    The gift in duty's name that's given
To one who cannot pay the debt,
    Will help the giver on to heaven.

Bathe in this pool therefore and then receive the golden bracelet."

So the traveller plucked up courage and went down into the pool to bathe; but he stuck fast in the mud and could not escape.

The tiger saw that he was stuck, and said: "Aha! You’re stuck fast in the mud. I’ll pull you out."

So he crept up very, very slowly and caught hold of the traveller, while the traveller was thinking:

Not by theology or Vedic lore
    Can scoundrels win the right to trust complete;
Look at the character; that counts for more:
    The milk of cows is naturally sweet.

The self-baptism of the elephant
    Is like the piety of lustful folk;
The hag beneath her load of gems must pant;
    And sin finds scripture a convenient cloak.

Plainly, I acted foolishly in putting my trust in this bloodthirsty creature. The proverb is right:

The things that can claw, and the things that can gore,
    Are very untrustworthy things;
And a man with a sword in his hand, furthermore,
    And rivers and women and kings.

And again:

The habits we acquire are little worth;
The nature that was ours before our birth
Will master us, while yet we live on earth.

And yet again:

The thousand-beamed moon that moves alone—
    Denying dark to sin—mid heaven's stars,
Fate's foreordained decree must still atone,
    And meet eclipse, that all her beauty mars.
What power of man can hope to alter now
The lines that fate once wrote upon the infant's brow?"

While he was turning these matters over in his mind, the tiger killed him and ate him.
moral: One must not act without thinking. Greed can be blinding and lead to dire consequences. Do not trust deceptive appearances.

performance_notes.opening_line: One day I was wandering in the southern forest and there I saw an old tiger who had taken the ceremonial bath and was holding a bunch of sacred grass in his paw.

Suppose I investigate.
How can I trust a bloodthirsty creature like you?
Why should you not trust me?
Bathe in this pool therefore and then receive the golden bracelet.
Aha! You’re stuck fast in the mud. I’ll pull you out.

source_citations.author: Arthur W. Ryder

title: Jataka Tales: The Monkey King
narrative_text:
Once in a royal orchard of a king there lived many monkeys. One day, when the chief priest was passing through the royal park after having taken his bath, a naughty monkey sitting on the arch of the gateway, pooed on his head. Baffled the priest looked all over; and when turned his eyes upward with the gaping mouth the monkey then voided in his mouth.

Infuriated, the priest swore vengeance on all the monkeys. And many monkeys in turn mimicked him in reply. But the king of the monkeys was alarmed. He took the swearings of the chaplain rather seriously. So, he called all his followers and counselled them to leave the royal park at once. Many monkeys followed him but for one haughty monkey and his five hundred followers.

By and by, one day, a female slave of the royal palace was drying some rice in the sun. A sheep then slipped into the place to eat the rice. Angry at the sheep, the slave girl hurled a burning stick on the sheep, which burnt its fur. It then ran to the elephants’ stall and the stall too, caught fire and inflicted burnt injuries on several royal elephants.

Now, looking for the most efficacious remedial measure the king summoned the chaplain and sought his advice. The chaplain, who was the sworn enemy of the monkeys tried to encash upon the opportunity for taking revenge against the monkeys. He, therefore, advised the king that the application of the monkey-fats would be the best remedial measure on any burnt-injury on an elephant. The king then ordered the royal archers to kill all the monkeys living in the park and bring their fats.
moral: Those who ignore the advice of the wise will perish.

title: The Golden Goose
narrative_text:
Once upon a time there was a Goose who had beautiful golden feathers. Not far away from this Goose lived a poor, a very poor woman, who had two daughters. The Goose saw that they had a hard time to get along and said he to himself:

“If I give them one after another of my golden feathers, the mother can sell them, and with the money they bring she and her daughters can then live in comfort.”

So away the Goose flew to the poor woman’s house. Seeing the Goose, the woman said: “Why do you come here? We have nothing to give you.”

“But I have something to give you,” said the Goose. “I will give my feathers, one by one, and you can sell them for enough so that you and your daughters can live in comfort.” So saying the Goose gave her one of his feathers, and then flew away. From time to time he came back, each time leaving another feather. The mother and her daughters sold the beautiful feathers for enough money to keep them in comfort. But one day the mother said to her daughters: “Let us not trust this Goose. Some day he may fly away and never come back. Then we should be poor again. Let us get all of his feathers the very next time he comes.”

The daughters said: “This will hurt the Goose. We will not do such a thing.”

But the mother was greedy. The next time the Golden Goose came she took hold of him with both hands, and pulled out every one of his feathers.

Now the Golden Goose has strange feathers. If his feathers are plucked out against his wish, they no longer remain golden but turn white and are of no more value than chicken-feathers. The new ones that come in are not golden, but plain white.

As time went on his feathers grew again, and then he flew away to his home and never came back again.
moral: Greed can lead to the loss of valuable blessings.

title: The Musical Donkey
narrative_text: There lived a washerman's donkey, whose name was Uddhata. During the day, the donkey would carry the washerman's bags, but during the night, he was set free to eat the green grass in a nearby field. However, instead of grazing in the nearby fields, he crept into nearby farms and ate vegetables of his choice. Before day-break it would come back to the washerman's house. One night, the donkey met a jackal while wandering in a nearby farm. They became good friends, and started meeting every night. The donkey, being fat, was able to break the fences of the farms. While he ate on the vegetable, the jackal would enter through the broken fence and ate the poultry on the farm. Before day-break, they would return to their respective home to meet again next night. This continued for many days. One night, the donkey said to the jackal, "Nephew, I feel like singing on nights like tonight, when the moon is full and beautiful. What _Raaga_ (note combination) shall I sing?" The jackal cautioned, "Uncle, we are here to steal. Thieves should keep as quiet as possible. I may add, your voice is not as pleasant as you think, and sounds like conch being blown! Your voice can be heard over a long distance. It will awaken the farmers who are sleeping, and you will have us caught." The jackal assured, "Please uncle, eat as much as you like, and forget about singing!" This annoyed the donkey and he said, "Dear nephew, it is because you are a wild animal that you don't appreciate music. I shall sing a melodious _Raaga_. Wait till you hear it!" Observing that the donkey was determined to sing; the jackal did not risk staying there anymore. He said, "Uncle, if you must sing, please wait till I go outside the fence and keep a watch on the farmers." He ran outside the fence, and hid himself. Then, the donkey started to bray at the top of his voice. When the farmers heard the donkey braying, they could see easily in the fullmoon-lit farm that the donkey was in their farm. The angry farmers chased the donkey with sticks, and beat him so hard that he fell on the ground. Then, they tied a wooden mortar around his neck and let him go. When the donkey was returning through the broken fence, the jackal laughed, "Musical uncle! That was a great _Raaga_! I see the farmers have rewarded you with this necklace!" The wise indeed say: There is always a proper place and time for doing anything.
moral: There is always a proper place and time for doing anything.

title: Birbal's Khichdi
narrative_text:
On a cold winter day, Akbar and Birbal took a walk along the lake. A thought came to Birbal that a man would do anything for money. He expressed his feelings to Akbar. Akbar then put his finger into the lake and immediately removed it because he shivered with cold. Akbar said, "I don’t think a man would spend an entire night in the cold water of this lake for money." Birbal replied, "I am sure I can find such a person." Akbar then challenged Birbal into finding such a person and said that he would reward the person with a thousand gold coins.

Birbal searched far and wide until he found a poor man who was desperate enough to accept the challenge. The poor man entered the lake and Akbar had guards posted near him to make sure that he really did as promised. The next morning the guards took the poor man to Akbar. Akbar asked the poor man if he had indeed spent the night in the lake. The poor man replied that he had. Akbar then asked the poor man how he managed to spend the night in the lake.

The poor man replied that there was a street lamp nearby and he kept his attention affixed on the lamp and away from the cold. Akbar then said that there would be no reward as the poor man had survived the night in the lake by the warmth of the street lamp. The poor man went to Birbal for help.

The next day, Birbal did not go to court. The king wondering where he was, sent a messenger to his home. The messenger came back saying that Birbal would come once his Khichri(Rice) was cooked. The king waited hours but Birbal did not come. Finally, the king decided to go to Birbal’s house and see what he was up to.

He found Birbal sitting on the floor near some burning twigs and a bowl filled with Khichri(Rice) hanging five feet above the fire. The king and his attendants couldn’t help but laugh.

Akbar then said to Birbal "How can the Khichri(Rice) be cooked if it so far away from the fire?"

Birbal answered, "The same way the poor man received heat from a street lamp that was more than a furlong away."

The King understood his mistake and gave the poor man his reward.
moral: A small ray of hope is enough to inspire the one who is ready to work hard to turn his dream into a reality.

Akbar then said that there would be no reward as the poor man had survived the night in the lake by the warmth of the street lamp.
The king waited hours but Birbal did not come.

title: Akbar-Birbal: The Crows in the Kingdom
narrative_text:
One day Emperor Akbar and Birbal were taking a walk in the palace gardens. It was a nice summer morning and there were plenty of crows happily playing around the pond. While watching the crows, a question came into Akbar’s head. He wondered how many crows were there in his kingdom.

Since Birbal was accompanying him, he asked Birbal this question. After a moment’s thought, Birbal replied, “There are ninety-five thousand four hundred and sixty-three crows in the Kingdom”.

Amazed by his quick response, Akbar tried to test him again, “What if there are more crows than you answered?” Without hesitating, Birbal replied, “If there are more crows than my answer, then some crows are visiting from other neighboring kingdoms”. “And what if there are less crows”, Akbar asked. “Then some crows from our kingdom have gone on holidays to other places”.
moral: There is always a way if you think with ease.

title: Akbar-Birbal: The Pot of Wit
narrative_text:
Once Emperor Akbar became very angry at his favorite minister Birbal. He asked Birbal to leave the kingdom and go away. Accepting the command of the Emperor, Birbal left the kingdom and started working in a farmer’s farm in an unknown village far away under a different identity.

As months passed, Akbar started to miss Birbal. He was struggling to solve many issues in the empire without Birbal’s advice. He regretted a decision, asking Birbal to leave the empire in anger. So Akbar sent his soldiers to find Birbal, but they failed to find him. No one knew where Birbal was. Akbar finally found a trick. He sent a message to the head of every village to send a pot full of the wit to the Emperor. If the pot full of wit can not be sent, fill the pot with diamonds and jewels.

This message also reached Birbal, who lived in one of the villages. The people of the village got together. All started talking about what to do now? The wit is not a thing, which can be filled in the pot. How will we arrange for diamonds and jewels to fill the pot and send to the Emperor? Birbal who was sitting among the villagers said, “Give me the pot, I will fill the wit in one month’s end”. Everyone trusted Birbal and agreed to give him a chance. They still didn’t know his identity.

Birbal took the pot with him and went back to the farm. He had planted watermelons on his farm. He selected a small watermelon and without cutting it from the plant, he put that in the pot. He started looking after it by providing water and fertilizer regularly. Within a few days, the watermelon grew into a pot so much that it was impossible to get it out of the pot.

Soon, the watermelon reached to the same size as the pot from inside. Birbal then cut the watermelon from the vine and separated it with the pot. Later, he sent a pot to Emperor Akbar with a message that “Please remove the wit without cutting it from the pot and without breaking the pot”.

Akbar watched the watermelon in the pot and realized that this can only be Birbal’s Work. Akbar himself came to the village, took Birbal back with him.
moral: Don’t hasten the decision. Think hard to find a solution for the strangest situations.

title: Akbar-Birbal: The List of Fools
narrative_text:
One day, an Arab merchant arrived at Emperor Akbar's court with a large collection of horses of various ages and breeds, offering them for sale. Akbar was greatly impressed by the quality of the horses. He purchased the ones he liked and then ordered the merchant to procure more of the finest horses from Arabia. The merchant agreed, requesting an advance of two lakh rupees. Akbar immediately instructed his court treasurer to pay the amount. The merchant then departed, promising to return once he had acquired the ordered horses.

Some time later, Akbar asked his wise minister, Birbal, to compile a list of all the fools in his kingdom. Birbal promptly replied, 'Your Majesty, I have already prepared such a list,' and presented a long scroll to the Emperor. To Akbar's astonishment, his own name was at the very top of the list.

Outraged, Akbar demanded, 'How dare you place your Emperor's name on a list of fools!'

Birbal calmly responded, 'Last week, Your Majesty gave two lakh rupees to the Arab merchant without any guarantee that he would deliver the horses you ordered and paid for. This act justifies placing your name at the top of the list of fools.'

Akbar then questioned, 'What if the merchant does indeed bring the horses I ordered?'

Birbal replied, 'Then, Your Majesty, I will simply replace your name with his.'

Akbar, realizing the error in his hasty decision to advance the money, remained silent.
moral: Hasty decisions, especially involving trust and money, can lead to foolish outcomes. It is wise to exercise caution and foresight.

title: Akbar-Birbal: The Farmer's Well
alt_titles: The Farmer Who Bought a Well
narrative_text:
A poor farmer once bought a well from a rich man so that he could irrigate his land using the water from the well. The farmer paid the price quoted by the rich man. The next day, when the farmer went to draw water from the well, the rich man stopped him and disallowed him from drawing water. He said that the farmer had bought only the well and not the water from him. So, he cannot draw any water from the well.

Not knowing what to do, the farmer went to the king’s court and told Akbar about his predicament. Akbar handed over the case to Birbal.

Akbar asked Birbal to help the poor farmer solve his problem.

Birbal visited the rich man who was causing problems for the farmer. The rich man reiterated what he told the farmer, to which Birbal said, "Since you have sold the well and not the water to the farmer, you will have to move all the water or pay rent to the farmer to keep the water in the well."

The rich man realized his ploy was not going to work and let the farmer use the well from the water.

Moral: Do not try to deceive people as you will end up paying the price.

title: Tenali Raman: The King's Dream
narrative_text:
Once Krishnadeva Raya had a wonderful dream. He dreamt of a beautiful palace made of glittering stones which floated in the air. The palace had everything that one could ever wish for. It could be lit up with a thousand lights or made to disappear into the darkness by merely wishing so. The palace glistened and glowed and whoever entered the palace felt themselves in heaven.

But suddenly the dream ended and the palace vanished. The king woke up with a start. The image of the palace was stuck in his mind. He could not forget the dream palace and one day, he summoned all his courtiers and told them about his dream.

Then the King then made a public announcement saying, "If someone can make such a palace for me, I will give a hundred thousand gold coins!"

The courtiers who had been singing praises of the King’s dream till then stopped mid-sentence. Their mouths fell open and each of them thought "Is the King mad? What on earth does he want us to build? Whoever heard of a palace floating in air! The King is indeed insane."

But no one had the courage to say so. Many of King’s well-wishers told him that such a palace could only exist in dreams.

But alas the King was in one of his fanciful moods and he refused to listen. He insisted that it could be built and he also threatened his courtiers with dire consequences if they did not produce someone who could make the dream come true.

The courtiers were aghast and worried. And when none of them could come up with a solution, they decided to approach Tenali Raman.

Raman listened to the story and assured them saying, "Give me a week’s time. I shall cure the King of his fanciful notions. Till then let no one know that you have approached me."

A few days later, a very old man tottered into the King’s court.

He cried, "My Lord, My Lord, I need justice. I need your justice."

King asked, "Old man, what is the problem? Tell me without fear and I shall see that justice is done."

"I have been looted, Your Majesty," wailed the old man, "All my savings are gone. I have nothing left."

"Who looted you?", thundered the King. "You name him and I will have him hanged immediately."

"Your Majesty! If you don’t take it as a personal affront, I will tell you. But you must assure that you won’t punish me," mumbled the old man.

"Yes, yes, I promise," replied the King.

"It’s you my Lord," said the old man.

"Me! How dare you. You insolent..." began the King, but then he remembered his promise and sat back.

"Your Majesty! Last night, I had a dream. It was quite terrifying. I dreamt that you would come with your entourage of ministers and commanders and looted my entire life’s savings, five thousand gold coins," said the old man.

"You are a fool indeed!" roared the King. "It was just a dream youhad. How can you be so stupid as to pretend that your dream is reality? Dreams are not true. Anyone and everyone knows that."

"But they are, Your Majesty! Dreams are very true. I am sure you know that. If your dream of a palace hanging in air can come true, why can’t my dream come true?"

The King was stunned by the old man’s words. Before he could fully comprehend and recover from what the old man was saying, the man tore off his beard and hair and stood before the King. It was none other than his favourite minister Tenali Raman!

Bowing low before the amazed King, Tenali Raman said, "Your Majesty! This was the only way of convincing you about the absurdity of your wish. Please forgive me if I have dishonoured you. I do hope you understand the impossibility."

"You are right Raman," agreed the King and burst into laughter. "You are really very shrewd! I am proud to have you in my court," saying this King presented Tenali Raman with a Diamond ring and one thousand gold coins.
moral: This story teaches us not to encourage foolish talk, but make the other person see the point.
