title: Tenali Raman And The Thieves
narrative_text: On one night, some thieves entered Tenali Raman's house and were hiding in the back yard of his house. They were only waiting for the right time, to get in and rob. Raman came to know that and so collected all the money. Then, he called his wife and said in a voice loud enough for the thieves to hear, "There are more incidence of robbery taking place in this city. So, we must keep all gold inside the well in our garden." Then he filled up a big box with some heavy unwanted things. They dropped the box to the well in the garden. The thieves, who were carefully observing all this, heard this. So, they decided not to enter inside the house, one of them got into the well, and found that the water was very deep. They realised that, the box can be easily taken out, only after taking out the water. So, the thieves spent the entire night to draw water from the well. Since, all the water drawn from the well was poured into the garden, all the plants in the garden got irrigated. The water level in the well never went down to the extent they expected. When the day was about to dawn, Raman said loudly, "The whole garden has been well irrigated and you may stop now, drawing further water." On hearing these words, the thieves ran away.
moral: Wisdom and quick thinking can outsmart any adversary.

title: Tenali Raman and the Horse That Ate Gram
alt_titles: The Horse That Ate Raj Guru's Beard
narrative_text: King Krishnadevraya, ruler of Vijayanagar, was visited by an Arab merchant who presented him with magnificent horses. Impressed, the King decided to purchase them all to expand his army. Recognizing the challenge of feeding and training such a large number of horses, he announced that each family in his kingdom would receive one horse to care for and train for three months, along with a monthly payment of three gold coins. Tenali Raman, the clever court jester, also received a horse and the gold coins. Instead of spending the coins on lavish feed, Tenali built a small stable with a single window in his backyard. He fed his horse only a small amount of hay through this window each day, making the horse accustomed to eating whatever little was offered. While other villagers spent more than the allocated three coins to keep their horses healthy, their own health deteriorated from the effort. After three months, the King summoned everyone to the palace with their horses. However, Tenali Raman arrived without his. Raj Guru, a rival courtier, seized this opportunity to discredit Tenali, questioning why he hadn't brought his horse. Tenali explained that his horse was too wild and dangerous to approach. Unconvinced, Raj Guru insisted on retrieving the horse himself, and the King agreed. As they approached Tenali's stable, Tenali warned Raj Guru about the horse's ferocity. Dismissing the warning, Raj Guru peered through the stable window. The starving horse, mistaking Raj Guru's beard for hay, immediately grabbed it. Raj Guru cried out in pain, attracting the villagers. They realized the only way to free him was to dismantle the stable. Even after the stable was broken, the horse refused to release its grip until Raj Guru's beard was cut off. The villagers then dragged the emaciated horse to the court. The King was dismayed by the horse's condition and questioned Tenali. To everyone's astonishment, Raj Guru interjected, claiming that despite its appearance, this skinny horse was the strongest of all. Tenali Raman then wisely stated, 'My lord, to prove your might, you need strong horses. Now you have the strongest horses but weak men. Strength does not lie only in your horsepower.' The King understood the profound lesson Tenali had imparted.
moral: True strength lies not just in physical power or resources, but also in the wisdom and well-being of those who manage them. Arrogance and a lack of foresight can lead to unexpected consequences.

title: Tenali Raman: The Most Valuable Thing
narrative_text:
After a glorious victory in battle, King Krishnadevaraya returned to his kingdom, Vijayanagara, to a grand welcome. To commemorate this significant triumph, the King decided to erect a magnificent victory pillar. He commissioned the most renowned sculptor in his kingdom, known for his exquisite craftsmanship, to create this masterpiece. The sculptor, a man of immense talent, worked tirelessly, and in due course, presented a pillar of unparalleled beauty, adorned with intricate carvings that depicted the King's valor and the grandeur of his empire.

The King was overjoyed and deeply impressed by the sculptor's work. He summoned the sculptor to his court and, in front of all his courtiers, praised his artistry. 'You have created a marvel, a true testament to our victory!' exclaimed the King. 'Ask for anything you desire, and it shall be yours. Gold, jewels, land – name your price!'

To everyone's surprise, the sculptor humbly declined all material riches. He bowed deeply and said, 'Your Majesty, I have no need for gold or jewels. I have a simple request. I wish for the most valuable thing in your kingdom.'

The King and his courtiers were perplexed. They began to ponder what could be more valuable than the vast treasures of the Vijayanagara empire. Some suggested the royal elephant, others the Kohinoor diamond, and some even proposed a portion of the kingdom itself. But the sculptor shook his head, indicating that none of these were what he sought.

Seeing the King's bewilderment, Tenali Raman, the witty and wise court jester, stepped forward. 'Your Majesty,' he said, 'I believe I know what the sculptor desires.' The King, eager for a solution, nodded for Tenali to proceed.

Tenali Raman turned to the sculptor and asked, 'Tell me, esteemed artist, what is it that you truly value above all else?'

The sculptor, with a twinkle in his eye, replied, 'My Lord Tenali, the most valuable thing in this kingdom, and indeed in life, is freedom. The freedom to create, to express, to live without fear, and to pursue one's passions. Without freedom, no amount of wealth or power holds true value.'

King Krishnadevaraya was deeply moved by the sculptor's profound words. He realized the truth in Tenali Raman's understanding and the sculptor's wisdom. He had been so focused on material wealth that he had overlooked the intangible yet invaluable treasure of freedom. The King granted the sculptor not only his freedom but also a lifetime pension, ensuring he could continue his art without any worldly concerns. From that day forward, the King also made efforts to ensure that all his subjects enjoyed greater freedom and justice.

Thus, Tenali Raman once again demonstrated his unparalleled wisdom, helping the King to understand that true value lies not in material possessions, but in the fundamental right to live freely and pursue one's aspirations.
moral: The most valuable thing in life is freedom, and true wealth lies beyond material possessions.

performance_notes.opening_line: After a glorious victory in battle, King Krishnadevaraya returned to his kingdom, Vijayanagara, to a grand welcome.
pause_points: 'Ask for anything you desire, and it shall be yours. Gold, jewels, land – name your price!'
'My Lord Tenali, the most valuable thing in this kingdom, and indeed in life, is freedom.'
The King was deeply moved by the sculptor's profound words.

safety_notes: This story is culturally respectful and suitable for elderly listeners, emphasizing wisdom and freedom without any objectionable content.

title: The Tenali Rama and the Three Dolls
narrative_text: There was a great king called Krishna Deva Raya. One of his great ministers was Tenali Rama. Though extremely wise, he believed in teaching everything through fun. So he was called Vikata kavi- humorous poet One day a wealth merchant came to the court of Krishna Deva Raya. He was from some other country. After saluting the king, he told, "Great king, People tell me that you have lot of wise ministers. If you permit me, I want to test them. " The king became curious and agreed. The merchant then gave the king three exactly similar looking dolls and told him, "Sir, these dolls look same but are different. Please ask your wise ministers to find out the difference. I would come back after 30 days. If none of you can find it, I will assume that you do not have any wise minister." The king agreed and merchant went back. Then the king called all his ministers except Tenali Rama and gave them each three days time to find out the difference between the dolls. None of them were able to find out. The king started getting worried. Then he called Tenali Rama. When Tenali Rama came, he gave the dolls told him, "Rama, I did not trouble earlier because I thought it must be very simple. But so far none of them have been able to find out the difference. I also tried and I was also not able to find out the difference. The pride of our country now depends on you." Tenali Rama accepted the dolls and spent three days with the dolls. He was also finding it very difficult. But at last he found out. Next day, he went to the court. The merchant also came that day. Then Tenali Rama told him, "These dolls are different. One is very good, another medium and another bad." The merchant, the king and all other ministers asked, "Which is which?" Then Tenali Rama showed them a tiny hole in the ears of the dolls. He took the first doll and put a very thin wire through this hole. The wire came out of the doll’s mouth. For the next doll, the wire came out of its other ear. For the third doll, the wire went to his heart and never came out. Tenali Rama told, "The first doll, where the wire came out of its mouth is bad. It represents people, to whom, if we tell a secret, they will tell it to others. The second doll, where the wire came out of the other ear, represents people who do not understand what you say. They are harmless and are medium. Tin case of the third, the secret goes to the heart and is preserved there." The merchant and the king were pleased by Tenali Raman’s finding and gave him lot of presents. Then Tenali Rama told them that his explanation was wrong .He told them, "There is another explanation. The fist doll represents people who hear and teach it to others. So they are good. The second doll represents people who hear and do not understand. So they are medium. The third type of people are those who learn things and do not teach it to any body and keep everything secret. They are bad people." Then the king asked, "Is there any other explanation?" Can you reply him?
moral: The story illustrates different ways people process and share information: some spread it, some ignore it, and some keep it to themselves. It encourages listeners to reflect on the value of discretion and the impact of their communication habits.

But at last he found out.
Which is which?
The secret goes to the heart and is preserved there.
Is there any other explanation?

title: Vikram-Betal: The Story of the Three Suitors
alt_titles: King Vikram and the Vetala: The Three Suitors
narrative_text: Once upon a time, there lived a Brahmin who had a beautiful daughter. Three suitors sought her hand in marriage: a scholar, an ascetic, and a wealthy merchant. The Brahmin, unable to decide, consulted his daughter. She, equally perplexed, asked for time to consider. Sadly, before she could make a choice, she died. The three suitors were heartbroken. The scholar, using his knowledge, preserved her bones in a jar. The ascetic, with his spiritual powers, cremated her body and preserved her ashes. The merchant, deeply saddened, built a magnificent temple in her memory and spent his days there. One day, the ascetic, through his yogic powers, brought the girl back to life. The three suitors were overjoyed. Now, the question arose: who among them should marry her? The scholar claimed her because he preserved her bones. The ascetic claimed her because he brought her back to life. The merchant claimed her because he spent his wealth and devotion on her memory. They approached King Vikram for a judgment. King Vikram, known for his wisdom, listened patiently to their claims. He pondered deeply and then delivered his verdict. He declared that the ascetic, who brought her back to life, was like her father. The scholar, who preserved her bones, was like her son. The merchant, who cherished her memory and built a temple, was her true husband. Thus, the merchant married the Brahmin's daughter. The moral of the story is that true love and devotion are more important than intellectual or spiritual claims.
moral: True love and devotion are more important than intellectual or spiritual claims.

title: Vikram-Betal: The Story of the Apsara and the Yogi
alt_titles: The Story of Vishwamitra and Menaka
narrative_text: The great sage Vishwamitra, through intense penance, had acquired immense power, so much so that it threatened the throne of Indra, the king of the gods. Fearing for his position, Indra devised a plan to disrupt Vishwamitra's austerities. He summoned Menaka, the most beautiful of the celestial nymphs, or Apsaras, and tasked her with seducing the sage. Menaka, aware of Vishwamitra's legendary temper, was hesitant but ultimately obeyed. With the help of the wind god Marut and the love god Manmatha, she appeared before Vishwamitra. The sage, captivated by her beauty, abandoned his penance and they fell in love. They lived together for many years, and Menaka gave birth to a daughter. However, Menaka's duty was to the heavens, and she left the child on the banks of the river Malini. The baby was protected by vultures until the sage Kanva found her and took her to his hermitage, naming her Shakuntala. Thus, the great sage's penance was broken, and a new life was brought into the world, all due to the celestial Apsara and the powerful Yogi.
moral: Even the most powerful can be swayed by love and desire, and destiny works in mysterious ways.

...naming her Shakuntala.

title: Vikram-Betal: The Story of the Prince and the Three Sensitive Wives
alt_titles: The Marvellous Delicacy of Three Queens
narrative_text:
In the Gaur country, in a city named Vardhman, there lived a king named Gunshekhar. His minister, Abhaichand, a Jain, influenced the king to adopt the Jain faith. Under this new decree, the worship of Shiva and Vishnu, the giving of cows and lands, rice balls, gambling, and spirit-drinking were all prohibited. No one in the city was allowed to perform these acts, and the minister, acting on the king's orders, proclaimed that anyone found doing so would be punished and banished.

One day, the Diwan addressed the Raja, emphasizing the Jain belief that taking a life leads to rebirth and suffering, and that true righteousness lies in cultivating religion. He spoke of how gods like Brahma, Vishnu, and Mahadeva are constantly incarnating due to love, wrath, pain, desire, and fascination. He extolled the virtues of the Cow, free from passion and enmity, as a supporter of mankind. He further explained that believing in gods is not good, and that protecting all life, from elephants to ants, is paramount. Those who consume the flesh of other creatures will suffer in Narak (hell) and be reborn maimed or imperfect. He also advised against consuming spirits and flesh.

Having heard his minister's views, the king fully embraced the Jain faith, and all his actions followed this creed. Brahmans, Jogis, Janganis, Sevras, and Sannyasis were no longer believed in.

One day, King Gunshekhar died, and his son Dharmadhwaj ascended the throne. He immediately had the minister Abhaichand seized, his head shaved except for seven locks, his face blackened, and paraded him through the city on an ass with drums beating, before banishing him from the kingdom. From then on, King Dharmadhwaj ruled without anxiety.

During the spring season, King Dharmadhwaj went for a stroll in the garden with his queens. In the garden, there was a large tank filled with blooming lotuses. The Raja, admiring the beauty, took off his clothes and went to bathe. After plucking a flower, he was about to give it to one of his queens when it slipped from his fingers, fell on her foot, and broke it. The Raja, alarmed, immediately came out of the tank and began to apply remedies to her foot.

As night fell, the moon shone brightly. The rays of the moon falling on the body of the second queen caused blisters to form. Suddenly, from a distance, the sound of a wooden pestle from a householder's dwelling reached them, and the third queen fainted away with a severe headache.

After narrating this much, the Baital asked King Vikram, "O my king! Of these three, which is the most delicate?" King Vikram replied, "She indeed is the most delicate who fainted in consequence of the headache." Hearing this, the Baital went and hung himself from the same tree. King Vikram, having gone there, took him down, fastened him in the bundle, placed him on his shoulder, and carried him away.
moral: The story highlights extreme sensitivity and poses a question about who is the most delicate, leaving the listener to ponder the different forms of fragility and perception.

performance_notes.opening_line: In the Gaur country, in a city named Vardhman, there lived a king named Gunshekhar.
pause_points: ...proclaimed that anyone found doing so would be punished and banished.
...From then on, King Dharmadhwaj ruled without anxiety.
...and began to apply remedies to her foot.
...and the third queen fainted away with a severe headache.

safety_notes: The story contains themes of religious conversion and punishment, which should be handled with sensitivity. The extreme delicacy of the queens might be perceived as humorous or exaggerated, depending on cultural context.

title: Vikram-Betal: The Story of the Four Learned Fools
alt_titles: The Vampire's Seventh Story: Showing the Exceeding Folly of Many Wise Fools
narrative_text: Of all the learned Brahmans in the learnedest university of Gaur (Bengal) none was so celebrated as Vishnu Swami. He could write verse as well as prose in dead languages, not very correctly, but still, better than all his fellows—which constituted him a distinguished writer. He had history, theosophy, and the four Vedas of Scriptures at his fingers' ends, he was skilled in the argute science of Nyasa or Disputation, his mind was a mine of Pauranic or cosmogonico-traditional lore, handed down from the ancient fathers to the modern fathers: and he had written bulky commentaries, exhausting all that tongue of man has to say, upon the obscure text of some old philosopher whose works upon ethics, poetry, and rhetoric were supposed by the sages of Gaur to contain the germs of everything knowable. His fame went over all the country; yea, from country to country. He was a sea of excellent qualities, the father and mother of Brahmans, cows, and women, and the horror of loose persons, cut-throats, courtiers, and courtesans. As a benefactor he was equal to Karna, most liberal of heroes. In regard to truth he was equal to the veracious king Yudhishtira. True, he was sometimes at a loss to spell a common word in his mother tongue, and whilst he knew to a fingerbreadth how many palms and paces the sun, the moon, and all the stars are distant from the earth, he would have been puzzled to tell you where the region called Yavana lies. Whilst he could enumerate, in strict chronological succession, every important event that happened five or six million years before he was born, he was profoundly ignorant of those that occurred in his own day. And once he asked a friend seriously, if a cat let loose in the jungle would not in time become a tiger. Yet did all the members of alma mater Kasi, Pandits as well as students, look with awe upon Vishnu Swami’s livid cheeks, and lack-lustre eyes, grimed hands and soiled cottons. Now it so happened that this wise and pious Brahmanic peer had four sons, whom he brought up in the strictest and most serious way. They were taught to repeat their prayers long before they understood a word of them, and when they reached the age of four they had read a variety of hymns and spiritual songs. Then they were set to learn by heart precepts that inculcate sacred duties, and arguments relating to theology, abstract and concrete. Their father, who was also their tutor, sedulously cultivated, as all the best works upon education advise, their implicit obedience, humble respect, warm attachment, and the virtues and sentiments generally. He praised them secretly and reprehended them openly, to exercise their humility. He derided their looks, and dressed them coarsely, to preserve them from vanity and conceit. Whenever they anticipated a “treat,” he punctually disappointed them, to teach them self-denial. Often when he had promised them a present, he would revoke, not break his word, in order that discipline might have a name and habitat in his household. And knowing by experience how much stronger than love is fear, he frequently threatened, browbeat, and overawed them with the rod and the tongue, with the terrors of this world, and with the horrors of the next, that they might be kept in the right way by dread of falling into the bottomless pits that bound it on both sides. At the age of six they were transferred to the Chatushpati or school. Every morning the teacher and his pupils assembled in the hut where the different classes were called up by turns. They laboured till noon, and were allowed only two hours, a moiety of the usual time, for bathing, eating, sleep, and worship, which took up half the period. At 3 P.M. they resumed their labours, repeating to the tutor what they had learned by heart, and listening to the meaning of it: this lasted till twilight. They then worshipped, ate and drank for an hour: after which came a return of study, repeating the day’s lessons, till 10 P.M. In their rare days of ease—for the learned priest, mindful of the words of the wise, did not wish to dull them by everlasting work—they were enjoined to disport themselves with the gravity and the decorum that befit young Samditats, not to engage in night frolics, not to use free jests or light expressions, not to draw pictures on the walls, not to eat honey, flesh, and sweet substances turned acid, not to talk to little girls at the well-side, on no account to wear sandals, carry an umbrella, or handle a die even for love, and by no means to steal their neighbours’ mangoes. As they advanced in years their attention during work time was unremittingly directed to the Vedas. Wordly studies were almost excluded, or to speak more correctly, whenever wordly studies were brought upon the carpet, they were so evil entreated, that they well nigh lost all form and feature. History became “The Annals of India on Brahminical Principles,” opposed to the Buddhistical; geography “The Lands of the Vedas,” none other being deemed worthy of notice; and law, “The Institutes of Manu,” then almost obsolete, despite their exceeding sanctity. But Jatu-harini had evidently changed these children before they were born; and Shani must have been in the ninth mansion when they came to light. Each youth as he attained the mature age of twelve was formally entered at the University of Kasi, where, without loss of time, the first became a gambler, the second a confirmed libertine, the third a thief, and the fourth a high Buddhist, or in other words an utter atheist. At length it so happened that the four young men, having frequently been surprised in flagrant delict, were summoned to the dread presence of the university Gurus, who addressed them as follows:— “There are four different characters in the world: he who perfectly obeys the commands; he who practices the commands, but follows evil; he who does neither good nor evil; and he who does nothing but evil. The third character, it is observed, is also an offender, for he neglects that which he ought toobserve. But ye all belong to the fourth category.” Then turning to the elder they said: “In works written upon the subject of government it is advised, ‘Cut off the gambler’s nose and ears, hold up his name to public contempt, and drive him out of the country, that he may thus become an example to others. For they who play must more often lose than win; and losing, they must either pay or not pay. In the latter case they forfeit caste, in the former they utterly reduce themselves. And though a gambler’s wife and children are in the house, do not consider them to be so, since it is not known when they will be lost. Thus he is left in a state of perfect not-twoness (solitude), and he will be reborn in hell.’ O young man! thou hast set a bad example to others, therefore shalt thou immediately exchange this university for a country life.” Then they spoke to the second offender thus:— “The wise shun woman, who can fascinate a man in the twinkling of an eye; but the foolish, conceiving an affection for her, forfeit in the pursuit of pleasure their truthfulness, reputation, and good disposition, their way of life and mode of thought, their vows and their religion. And to such the advice of their spiritual teachers comes amiss, whilst they make others as bad as themselves. For it is said, ‘He who has lost all sense of shame, fears not to disgrace another; ‘and there is the proverb, ‘A wild cat that devours its own young is not likely to let a rat escape;’ therefore must thou too, O young man! quit this seat of learning with all possible expedition.” The young man proceeded to justify himself by quotations from the Lila-shastra, his text-book, by citing such lines as— Fortune favours folly and force, and by advising the elderly professors to improve their skill in the peace and war of love. But they drove him out with execrations. To the third they said: “The shastras have thus laid down the whole duty of thieves. In the first place, he must make a breach in the wall of the house which he intends to rob. Then he must enter the premises, and, if the owner awake, he must frighten him by mentioning his name, or by threatening him with the weapon which he should hold in his hand. After that he must bind the master of the house, and carry off everything that has been treasured up. Thus, O youth, thou art a disgrace to thy family, and an encouragement to the evil-disposed. Go forth, therefore, and let us see thee no more.” Finally, the professors addressed the fourth, to this purpose: “The fool that says ‘There is no God,’ is a denier of the Veda, and the denier of the Veda is an infidel, and the infidel is a child of the devil. But thou art worse than this, for thou wouldest make men believe that they have no souls, and that good and evil are alike. Thou art a Nihilist, a nothing-ist, a no-man. Therefore be off with thee!” So the four foolish young men, the sons of the learned and pious Vishnu Swami, were expelled from the university of Kasi, and they went their ways.
moral: Knowledge without wisdom is folly.

title: The Story of the Boy who could not Stop Laughing
alt_titles: The Thief Who Laughed and Wept
narrative_text:
In the city of Ujjayani there lived a goldsmith, by name Hariswami, who had a son called Randhir. This youth, when he was five years old, was sent to school, where he learned to read and write, and to cast accounts. But he was a bad boy, and a great liar; and he was always playing mischievous tricks upon his schoolfellows. His father, seeing that he was of a perverse disposition, and that he would never learn to be honest, sent him to a distant city, where he hoped that he would mend his ways. But Randhir was incorrigible; he fell into bad company, and became a thief. He was so clever at his trade that he was never caught; and he soon became the chief of a gang of robbers.

One day, as he was prowling about the city, he saw a beautiful maiden, who was the daughter of a wealthy merchant. He fell in love with her at first sight, and resolved to make her his wife. He knew that her father would never consent to give her to a thief; so he determined to carry her off by force. He disguised himself as a Brahman, and went to the merchant’s house, where he was hospitably received. He told the merchant that he was a great astrologer, and that he could foretell the future. The merchant, who was a credulous man, asked him to cast his daughter’s horoscope. Randhir pretended to consult his books, and then declared that the maiden was destined to marry a thief. The merchant was greatly distressed at this, and asked what could be done to avert such a calamity. Randhir told him that the only way to prevent it was to marry her to a man who was so ugly that no one would ever suspect him of being a thief. The merchant, in his anxiety to save his daughter from such a fate, readily agreed to this; and Randhir, throwing off his disguise, declared himself to be the thief whom she was destined to marry.

The merchant was furious at being thus tricked, but he was obliged to keep his word. So he married his daughter to Randhir, who carried her off to his den in the jungle. The maiden was very unhappy at first, but Randhir treated her with such kindness that she soon grew to love him. He continued to ply his trade, and soon amassed a great fortune. He built a magnificent palace in the jungle, and lived there with his wife in great luxury. But he was not happy; for he knew that he was a thief, and that he had obtained his wife by fraud. He often thought of his old father, and wished that he could make amends for his past misdeeds.

One day, as he was sitting in his palace, he heard a voice calling his name. He went out and saw an old man standing at the gate. The old man was his father, who had come to seek him. Randhir was overjoyed to see his father, and begged his forgiveness. His father, who had long mourned him as dead, readily forgave him. Randhir then told his father the whole story of his life, and how he had become a thief. His father was greatly distressed at this, and urged him to give up his wicked ways. Randhir promised to do so, and from that day forward he lived an honest life. He distributed his wealth among the poor, and became a devout worshipper of Vishnu. He lived to a ripe old age, and was greatly respected by all who knew him.
moral: Honesty is the best policy. Even if one gains wealth and happiness through dishonest means, true peace and contentment can only be found through a life of virtue and integrity. The story also highlights the power of love and forgiveness to transform a person's life.

title: Akbar-Birbal: The Weight of the Elephant
alt_titles: The Clever Solution to Weighing an Elephant
narrative_text:
Emperor Akbar, known for his love of challenging his courtiers with perplexing questions, once posed a peculiar task: to determine the exact weight of his magnificent royal elephant. The courtiers, including the most learned scholars and experienced officials, were baffled. They deliberated for days, proposing various complex and expensive methods, none of which seemed feasible or accurate. There was no scale in the entire kingdom large enough to accommodate such a colossal creature, and their conventional thinking offered no solution.

Observing their struggle, Birbal, the Emperor's most trusted and witty advisor, stepped forward with a calm smile. He requested that the elephant be brought to the banks of a large river, along with a sturdy boat. The courtiers watched with curiosity as Birbal directed the royal elephant to slowly walk onto the boat. As the elephant's immense weight settled, the boat sank deeper into the water. Birbal, with keen observation, carefully marked the waterline on the side of the boat's hull.

Once the mark was made, Birbal instructed the mahout to lead the elephant off the boat. As the elephant disembarked, the boat rose back to its original position. Birbal then turned to the courtiers and explained his ingenious plan. He asked them to load the boat with stones, sandbags, and any other heavy objects available until the boat sank precisely to the waterline mark he had made earlier. The courtiers, though still somewhat bewildered, followed his instructions diligently.

After a considerable amount of effort, the boat finally settled at the exact same waterline. Birbal then declared, "The weight of all these stones and objects is precisely equal to the weight of the royal elephant!" The courtiers were astonished by the simplicity and brilliance of his solution. They had been so focused on finding an enormous scale that they had overlooked a much more practical and clever approach.

Emperor Akbar was immensely pleased with Birbal's wisdom and quick thinking. He praised Birbal for his ability to solve seemingly impossible problems with such elegance and practicality. Birbal's method demonstrated that true intelligence lies not just in knowledge, but in the ability to apply it creatively and think outside conventional boundaries.
moral: Problems can often be solved with intelligence, creativity, and unconventional thinking, rather than relying solely on traditional or brute-force methods.

...true intelligence lies not just in knowledge, but in the ability to apply it creatively and think outside conventional boundaries.

title: The Honest Woodcutter
narrative_text: There once lived a poor but honest woodcutter in a small village by a forest. Every day, he would diligently cut wood and sell it to merchants to support his family. One hot day, as he was cutting a tree by a deep river, his axe slipped from his sweaty hands and fell into the water. The woodcutter was distraught, as he had lost his only means of livelihood. He sat by the riverbank and prayed to the River Goddess for help. Moved by his sincere prayers, the River Goddess appeared before him. The woodcutter explained his predicament, and the Goddess, wishing to test his honesty, dived into the river. She reappeared with a gleaming silver axe and asked if it was his. The woodcutter, though tempted, truthfully denied it, stating that he could not cut trees with a silver axe. The Goddess dived again and returned with a golden axe, asking the same question. Again, the honest woodcutter denied it, explaining that it was not his simple tool. Finally, the Goddess dived a third time and brought forth his old, familiar iron axe. With a cry of joy, the woodcutter confirmed that it was indeed his axe. Impressed by his unwavering honesty and humility, the River Goddess rewarded him not only with his own axe but also with the silver and golden axes. The woodcutter returned home, overjoyed and grateful, to his family.
moral: Honesty is the best policy. A person's honesty and humility will always be rewarded in the end.

title: Tenali Raman and the Brinjal Curry
narrative_text: In the court of King Krishnadevaraya of Vizayanagra, Tenali Raman was a clever and spontaneous advisor. The King had a special garden with a rare variety of brinjal, which he guarded closely. The curry made from these brinjals was a favorite of the King, and no one was allowed to even see the plants without his permission. One day, the King hosted a dinner for his advisors, and the famous brinjal curry was served. Tenali Raman enjoyed it so much that he couldn't forget the taste. He went home and described the delicious curry to his wife. His wife, who also loved brinjals, pleaded with him to bring some from the King's garden. Tenali Raman knew the risks; the King would surely punish anyone who stole from his prized garden. But his wife's pleas were insistent, and Tenali Raman, left with no choice, snuck into the garden at night, jumped over the wall, and plucked a few brinjals. His wife cooked a delicious curry. She wanted to share it with their son, who was sleeping on the roof. Tenali Raman, fearing their son might reveal the secret, initially refused. But his wife insisted. So, Tenali Raman devised a clever plan. He went to the roof, poured a bucket of water on his sleeping son, and woke him up, exclaiming, 'It's raining! Let's go inside and have dinner.' Inside, he gave his son the brinjal curry. The next day, the King discovered that three brinjals were missing. He was furious and offered a reward to catch the thief. The chief advisor, Appaji, suspected Tenali Raman. The King summoned Tenali Raman, who denied knowing anything about the missing brinjals. Appaji suggested questioning Tenali Raman's son. When the boy was brought to the court, he was asked what he had for dinner the previous night. The boy replied, 'The brinjal curry was very tasty.' The advisor was triumphant, but Tenali Raman calmly said that his son had gone to bed early and was probably recounting a dream. The King then asked the boy to describe his evening. The boy said, 'I played, did my homework on the roof, and fell asleep. My father woke me up when it started raining. My clothes were all wet. We went inside, had dinner, and I went back to sleep.' The chief advisor was stunned, as there had been no rain the previous day. Convinced the boy had been dreaming, the King let Tenali Raman go. Later, Tenali Raman confessed the truth to the King, who, impressed by his cleverness, forgave him.
moral: While stealing is wrong, intelligence and wit can help one out of difficult situations. It is also important to be honest and confess one's mistakes.

...he couldn't forget the taste.
...and plucked a few brinjals.
...poured a bucket of water on his sleeping son...
...'The brinjal curry was very tasty.'
...impressed by his cleverness, forgave him.

title: Tenali Tales: Reward & Punishment
narrative_text:
Tenali Raman came to Hampi, the capital of Vijayanagara empire. At that time, Vijayanagara was ruled by Krishna Deva Raya.

Raman asked his wife to wait for him in a temple. "I’ll go to the palace and meet the king. He is a generous man. He may give me some gifts," said Raman.

When Raman reached the palace, he was stopped at the entrance. "You cannot enter the palace without permission," said the guard.

"I’ve come from a far-off place. Please allow me to enter the palace," said Raman. "I’m hoping that the generous king will give me gifts," he added.

"You may get gifts from the king. But what do I get?" asked the guard giving Raman a meaningful look.

"Whatever the king gives me, I’ll share it with you," Raman promised.

"Half of it. Whatever gifts the king will give you, you must give me half of it," said the guard. Raman agreed and walked into the palace.

In the palace, the king was holding a durbar in a hall. Raman was stopped at the entrance to the hall by another guard. Raman promised to give the second guard half of the gifts he would receive from the king.

When Raman walked in, the king was not in a good mood. Raman was very excited to be in the presence of the king; he rushed forward. The king was annoyed because no one could approach him without being called.

"Give him fifty lashes," ordered the king.

Raman folded his hands in salutation to the king and said, "I’m grateful to you for your generous gift. Please allow me to share the gifts with two of my friends."

The king wanted to know who his friends were.

"The guard at the entrance to this hall and the other one at the main entrance to the palace," said Raman.

The guards were summoned. The king was quite annoyed with his guards. "Now that I know you two are sharing the ‘gift’, I’ll increase it to one hundred lashes," said the king.

The guards were taken away to be given fifty lashes each.

"You wait here, until your friends return," said the king to Raman.

When the guards returned after receiving their shares of the ‘gift.,’ the king turned to Raman.

"You have tempted my honest guards with bribes. I give severe punishment to bribe givers," said the king sternly.

The king turned to the two guards. "Take him to the river side and cut off his head with a single stroke from your swords," the king ordered.

The guards were happy to have their revenge. They led Raman to the river front. However, Raman, with his quick wit, managed to escape the punishment by convincing the guards that the king's order was to cut off the head of the one who was being carried, not the one carrying. He then offered to carry one of the guards, who, eager for revenge, agreed. As soon as the guard was on Raman's shoulders, the other guard, following the king's literal order, cut off the head of the guard on Raman's shoulders. Raman then explained to the king that he had merely followed the king's order, and the king, impressed by his wit, pardoned him and rewarded him handsomely. The moral of the story is that greed can lead to severe consequences, and wit can save one from difficult situations.
moral: Greed leads to severe consequences, and wit can save one from difficult situations.

title: Vikram-Betal: The Story of the Exchanged Heads
alt_titles: The Two Brothers and the Goddess
narrative_text:
Once upon a time there lived a washerman named Dhavala. He once spotted a very beautiful lady at pond, who happened to be the daughter of another washerman. He instantly fell in love with her and pleaded with his parents to ask her parents for her hand in marriage. His parents did accordingly and the girl, Madanasundari, got married to him with her parents consent. She then went with Dhavala to live with him.

Once the brother of Madanasundari, came to Dhavala to invite his sister and brother-in-law to their place for festival season. Dhavala agreed and the three of them were returning to Madanasundari's home. On the way they happened to pass by the temple of Durga Devi, the all powerful goddess of power. Her brother wanted to pay a visit to the goddess, and went to the temple. But as soon as he came near the goddess, he wanted to make a massive sacrifice to the goddess. So saying he cut his head off for the goddess. Madanasundari was worried and she then sent her husband to see what the matter was. On seeing the state of his brother-in-law, the husband was also moved deeply and he decided to offer his own head too to the goddess and cut his own head with his scitmar. Madanasundari, after a long wait came and saw both her brother and husband lying on the ground and then decided to take her own life but asked the goddesss to get the same brother and husband in the next life. The goddess was pleased and stopped her from dying and asked her to join their heads and bodies and then she granted them life. In a hurry, Madanasundari, exchanged the heads of her brother and husband and was perplexed.

Betaal stops and asks King Vikram asks this question: Who among the two is the husband of Madanasundari? Vikram replies with a lot of thought, "The body that carries her husband's head is is her husband. The head is the most important part of the human body, and the rest of the body is identified by the head."

As soon as Vikram had finished his answer, Betaal disappeared back to the tree.
moral: The head is the most important part of the human body, and the rest of the body is identified by the head.

pause_points: ...Madanasundari, exchanged the heads of her brother and husband and was perplexed.
...Who among the two is the husband of Madanasundari?
...As soon as Vikram had finished his answer, Betaal disappeared back to the tree.

safety_notes: The story involves self-sacrifice by cutting off heads, which might be disturbing for some listeners. Emphasize the mythological context and the goddess's intervention.

title: The Sparrow and the Crow
narrative_text:
Once upon a time, a clever sparrow and a rather vain crow decided to cook a meal together. The sparrow gathered rice, and the crow brought lentils. The sparrow, being the cook, prepared a delicious khichri. When it was ready, the crow eagerly stepped forward to claim his share.

However, the sparrow, with a hint of disdain, remarked, "Whoever heard of anyone sitting down to eat looking as dirty as you? Your feathers are so black, and your head looks dusty. Please, for goodness sake, go and wash yourself in the pond first."

The crow, feeling a little offended but knowing the sparrow's stubborn nature, decided it was best to comply. He flew to the pond and, in a rather poetic manner, addressed it:

"Oh, Pond, dear Pond,
I am the Crow.
Could you spare some water,
So I can wash and glow?
My beak and feet I'll clean,
Then enjoy the khichri, so keen.
Though I can't quite see,
Why the sparrow finds fault with me,
For among all crows, I'm quite pristine!"

The pond, however, had a condition. "Certainly, I'll give you water," it replied, "but first, you must go to the deer and ask for a horn. With that, you can dig a small channel for the water to flow cleanly."

So, the crow flew to the deer and repeated his plea:

"Oh, Deer, kind Deer,
I am the Crow.
Could you lend me a horn, please,
So a clean channel can flow?
My beak and feet I'll clean,
Then enjoy the khichri, so keen.
Though I can't quite see,
Why the sparrow finds fault with me,
For among all crows, I'm quite pristine!"

The deer, too, had a condition. "Of course, I'll give you a horn," he said, "but first, you must bring me some milk from the cow. If I drink milk, I'll grow strong and won't mind the pain of losing a horn."

The crow then flew to the cow and, with a hopeful heart, requested:

"Oh, Cow, gentle Cow,
I am the Crow.
Could you give me some milk, now,
So the deer can grow?
Then he'll give his horn to me,
For a clean channel, you see.
My beak and feet I'll clean,
Then enjoy the khichri, so keen.
Though I can't quite see,
Why the sparrow finds fault with me,
For among all crows, I'm quite pristine!"

But the cow, in turn, had her own demand. "I'll gladly give you milk," she mooed, "but first, bring me some fresh grass. Who ever heard of a cow giving milk without eating grass?"

Undeterred, the crow flew to a patch of grass and sang:

"Oh, Grass, green Grass,
I am the Crow.
Could you spare some blades, alas,
For the cow to bestow?
Then she'll give milk to the deer,
Who'll lend his horn, my dear.
For a clean channel, you see,
My beak and feet I'll clean,
Then enjoy the khichri, so keen.
Though I can't quite see,
Why the sparrow finds fault with me,
For among all crows, I'm quite pristine!"

The grass, however, had a practical request. "I'll give you grass," it rustled, "but first, go to the blacksmith and ask him to make you a sickle. How else can you cut me?"

So, the crow went to the blacksmith and, with a sense of urgency, pleaded:

"Oh, Smith, strong Smith,
I am the Crow.
Could you forge me a sickle, forthwith,
So the grass I can mow?
Then the cow will give milk to the deer,
Who'll lend his horn, my dear.
For a clean channel, you see,
My beak and feet I'll clean,
Then enjoy the khichri, so keen.
Though I can't quite see,
Why the sparrow finds fault with me,
For among all crows, I'm quite pristine!"

"With pleasure," the blacksmith replied, "if you will light the fire and pump the bellows for me."

The crow, eager to finally get his khichri, began to light the fire and pump the bellows with all his might. But in his haste and distraction, he tumbled right into the very middle of the blazing fire and was burnt to a crisp!

And so, that was the unfortunate end of the crow. The clever sparrow, meanwhile, enjoyed all the delicious khichri by herself.
moral: Laziness and trickery often lead to one's own downfall, while diligence and honesty are rewarded.

pause_points: When it was ready, the crow eagerly stepped forward to claim his share.
Please, for goodness sake, go and wash yourself in the pond first.
The pond, however, had a condition.
The deer, too, had a condition.
But the cow, in turn, had her own demand.
The grass, however, had a practical request.
With pleasure," the blacksmith replied, "if you will light the fire and pump the bellows for me."

title: Folktale from Bengal: The Story of Tuntuni and the King
narrative_text:
THE tailor-bird had made her nest at a corner in the king’s garden. The coins in the king’s chest had been left to dry in the sun. At the close of the day, the king’s men forgot to take back one coin and it was left behind. Tuntuni caught sight of the glittering object and put it in her nest. She thought, “Hooey! How rich am I! I have the same treasure in my nest as the king has in his palace.”

From then onwards, she brooded over the same thought and said,

“I possess in my nest with pleasure,

The mighty king’s bright treasure.”

Hearing the twittering of the bird from his court the king said, “What is the bird saying? “His men with folded hands said, “Your Majesty! The bird is saying that it has the same treasure in her nest as you have in your palace.” The king burst into laughter and said, “Go and see what she has in her nest.” Coming back they reported, “Your Majesty! There is a rupee in her nest.” The king said, “That belongs to me, bring it back.” Immediately the king’s men fetched it from the bird’s nest. Poor little thing! She could do nothing. In deep sorrow she kept on saying,

“The king is a miserly person,

Takes away Tuni’s mite, O listen!”

The king heard this and smiled. He said, “That bird is surely a stiff-necked one. Go and return her the coin.” Tuni was delighted to get back the coin. She said,
“What a fright the king said!

Tuni’s money was sent back!”

The king asked, “What is she saying again?” His men replied, “She says, the king is frightened, and that is why he has returned the coin.” Hearing this the king became furious, he said, “What audacity! Seize her. I will fry her and eat her up.” No sooner had the king said this than his men rushed out, caught hold of her and brought her to the palace. The king put her in his mouth. But as soon as he did so, Tuntuni flew out through his nose and sat on a tree. The king was very angry. He ordered his men to catch her again. They did so. This time the king crushed her with his teeth and swallowed her. Tuntuni went inside his stomach and started creating trouble. The king could not sit, stand or lie down. He was in great pain. He asked his men to bring a sword and cut his stomach open. As soon as they did so, Tuntuni flew out and sat on a tree. The king’s stomach was cut open and he died. Tuntuni flew away happily.
moral: The story teaches that cleverness and wit can overcome brute force and arrogance. It also highlights the consequences of greed and pride.

performance_notes.opening_line: THE tailor-bird had made her nest at a corner in the king’s garden.
pause_points: Tuntuni caught sight of the glittering object and put it in her nest.
The king burst into laughter and said, “Go and see what she has in her nest.”
The king heard this and smiled. He said, “That bird is surely a stiff-necked one. Go and return her the coin.”
Hearing this the king became furious, he said, “What audacity! Seize her. I will fry her and eat her up.”
The king’s stomach was cut open and he died. Tuntuni flew away happily.

safety_notes: The story contains a depiction of violence where the king dies. This should be narrated carefully, emphasizing the king's own actions leading to his demise rather than graphic details.

title: The Legend of Dhola Maru
narrative_text:
The legend of Dhola Maru is a classic Rajasthani folk tale of love, separation, and reunion. In the ancient kingdom of Poogal, King Pingal arranged the marriage of his infant daughter, Maru, to Prince Dhola of Narwar. However, due to the untimely death of Dhola's father, King Nal, the connection between the two kingdoms was lost, and Dhola grew up unaware of his childhood bride. He later married Malvani. 

Maru, however, never forgot her childhood betrothal. As she grew into a beautiful young woman, she longed for her husband. She sent numerous messengers, bards, and singers across the desert, carrying her messages and songs to Dhola, reminding him of their childhood union. Initially, Dhola, under the influence of Malvani, ignored these messages. However, one persistent bard managed to convey Maru's plight and her unwavering love to Dhola. 

Moved by Maru's devotion and the bard's eloquent descriptions, Dhola decided to embark on a perilous journey across the Thar desert to reunite with Maru. His journey was fraught with dangers, including encounters with wild animals and attempts by Malvani's family to dissuade him. However, Dhola's determination led him to Poogal. 

Upon their reunion, Dhola and Maru's love blossomed. They decided to return to Narwar. On their journey back, they faced further challenges, including an ambush orchestrated by Malvani's jealous relatives. Through their courage and the timely intervention of a magical parrot (or sometimes a divine intervention), they overcame these obstacles. Finally, Dhola and Maru returned to Narwar, where Dhola eventually reconciled with Malvani, and all three lived in harmony, or in some versions, Dhola and Maru lived happily ever after, having overcome all adversities. The story emphasizes enduring love, perseverance, and the power of true affection to overcome all odds.
moral: The story emphasizes enduring love, perseverance, and the power of true affection to overcome all odds.

title: The Story of the Three Deaf Men
narrative_text:
In a remote village there lived a husband and wife. Both of them were quite deaf. They had made this household arrangement, namely, to cook cabbage with tamarind and soup without tamarind one day, and cabbage without tamarind and soup with tamarind on the other. Thus on every alternate day the same dishes were repeated. One day, when taking his meal, the husband found the tamarind cabbage so very tasty that he wanted to have it also next day, and gave instructions to that effect. The deaf wife did not understand the order. According to the established rule she cooked cabbage without tamarind next day. The husband, when he sat down to his meal, found his order disregarded and, being enraged thereat, threw the cabbage against the wall, and went out in a rage. The wife ate her fill, and prepared tamarind cabbage for her husband.

The husband went out, and sat down in a place where three roads crossed, to calm down his anger. At that time a shepherd happened to pass that way. He had lately lost a good cow and calf of his, and had been seeking them for some days. When he saw the deaf man sitting by the way, he took him for a soothsayer, and asked him to find out by his knowledge of Jôsyam where the cow was likely to be found. The herdsman, too, was very deaf; and the man, without hearing what he was saying, abused him, and wished to be left undisturbed. In abusing him the husband stretched out his hand, pointing to the shepherd’s face. This pointing the shepherd understood to indicate the direction where the lost cow and calf would be found. Thus thinking the poor shepherd went on in that direction, promising to present the soothsayer with the calf if he found it there with the cow. To his joy, and by mere chance, he found them. His delight knew no bounds. “That is a capital soothsayer. Surely I must present him with the calf.” So thought he to himself, and returned with them to the deaf man, and, pointing to the calf, requested him to accept it.

Just then, a third traveler came along, also deaf. He saw the shepherd trying to give the calf to the angry husband and misunderstood the situation. Thinking the husband was trying to steal the calf from the shepherd, he intervened. He loudly proclaimed that it was wrong to steal and that he would ensure justice. He believed the husband was trying to make off with the calf and that he, the traveler, was saving the day. The husband, still fuming from his earlier argument with his wife and now confronted by this new, equally deaf, and misunderstanding stranger, became even more agitated. He tried to explain, but his words were lost on the traveler. The traveler, convinced of his own interpretation, took the calf from the shepherd, believing he was returning it to its rightful owner, and walked off with it, leaving the husband and shepherd in their confused states.

The husband, on his return, sat down to his dinner, and his wife served him the tamarind cabbage. He happened to put his finger to the place where the cabbage without tamarind had previously been served on the leaf. On applying it to his mouth, he found it so very sweet that he demanded that dish again. The wife replied to him that she had already emptied the pan. “Then at least bring me the cabbage that is sticking to the saucepan,” said the husband; and the wife did accordingly.
moral: Miscommunication and stubbornness can lead to absurd and frustrating situations, often escalating simple misunderstandings into complex problems with unintended consequences. The story also highlights how people often interpret situations based on their own preconceived notions, rather than truly listening or understanding others.

pause_points: The deaf wife did not understand the order.
The herdsman, too, was very deaf; and the man, without hearing what he was saying, abused him, and wished to be left undisturbed.
Just then, a third traveler came along, also deaf.

The traveler, convinced of his own interpretation, took the calf from the shepherd, believing he was returning it to its rightful owner, and walked off with it, leaving the husband and shepherd in their confused states.

title: The Legend of Mahabali
narrative_text:
Once, there lived a benevolent and generous Asura king named Mahabali, the grandson of Prahlada. He ruled the land of Kerala with great wisdom and fairness, and his people prospered, living in peace and happiness. Mahabali was so powerful that he had conquered all three worlds – heaven, earth, and the netherworld. The Devas, fearing his growing power and dominion over the heavens, sought the help of Lord Vishnu.

Vishnu, however, refused to engage in violence against Mahabali, as the king was a devout follower and a righteous ruler. Instead, Vishnu decided to restore the cosmic balance by incarnating as Vamana, a dwarf Brahmin.

Mahabali, known for his immense generosity, was performing an Ashvamedha sacrifice, distributing gifts to all who came. Vamana approached the king and humbly requested three paces of land. Mahabali, amused by the dwarf Brahmin's small stature and seemingly modest request, readily agreed, despite the warnings from his guru, Shukra, who recognized Vamana's true divine identity.

As soon as Mahabali granted the boon, Vamana began to grow, transforming into a colossal form that encompassed the entire universe. With his first step, he covered all of heaven. With his second step, he covered the entire earth. Then, Vamana turned to Mahabali and asked where he should place his third step, as there was no more land left.

Mahabali, realizing the true nature of Vamana and accepting his fate, bowed his head and offered it as the place for the third step. Lord Vishnu, pleased by Mahabali's humility and devotion, placed his foot on the king's head, sending him to Patala, the netherworld.

However, Vishnu, touched by Mahabali's righteousness, granted him a boon: Mahabali would be allowed to return to his beloved kingdom and people once every year. This annual return of King Mahabali is celebrated with great joy and festivities in Kerala as Onam, a harvest festival that commemorates the golden age of his rule and his enduring spirit.

Moral: The story of Mahabali teaches us the virtues of humility, generosity, and devotion. Even in the face of immense power, true greatness lies in selflessness and adherence to dharma. It also highlights the cyclical nature of cosmic order and the importance of balance.
moral: The story of Mahabali teaches us the virtues of humility, generosity, and devotion. Even in the face of immense power, true greatness lies in selflessness and adherence to dharma. It also highlights the cyclical nature of cosmic order and the importance of balance.

...there was no more land left.
...sending him to Patala, the netherworld.

title: The Story of Tejimola
narrative_text:
Once upon a time, in Assam, there lived a kind merchant who had two wives. His elder wife had a beautiful daughter named Tejimola. Sadly, Tejimola's mother passed away when she was very young, and her stepmother raised her. Though her father adored her, the stepmother harbored deep resentment, only feigning care due to her husband's presence. As Tejimola grew to be ten or eleven, her father arranged for her to befriend a girl her age from a wealthy family in the village.

The merchant, whose business often took him away, prepared for a six or seven-month journey. Before leaving, he entrusted Tejimola's care to his wife, urging her to love and tend to the child. The stepmother saw this as an opportunity to rid herself of Tejimola, fearing that the girl's future dowry would diminish her own family's inheritance. She resolved to eliminate Tejimola entirely.

Soon, Tejimola's friend's wedding approached, and Tejimola longed to attend. The stepmother, seeing another chance, granted permission but with a cruel trick. She made a show of concern, discussing fine silk clothes for Tejimola to wear. She then secretly placed a mouse between the silk riha (breast-cloth) and mekhela (lower garment) and embers in the khonia (wrapper), packing them into a parcel. She instructed Tejimola to wear them only when she reached her friend's house, claiming they would get soiled otherwise.

As Tejimola neared her friend's home, she eagerly unpacked the parcel. To her horror, a mouse sprang out, and burnt ashes fell from the khonia. The mouse had shredded the silk garments, and the gold-embroidered wrapper was full of holes. Terrified and sobbing, Tejimola was consoled by an attendant who provided her with new clothes from her friend's house, allowing her to enjoy the wedding festivities.

Upon her return, the stepmother demanded to see the clothes. Quaking with fear, Tejimola presented the ruined garments. The stepmother, feigning rage, beat and abused her mercilessly. Still unsatisfied, she dragged Tejimola to the rice-pounding apparatus. She ordered Tejimola to supply paddy to the hole and began pounding with all her might. Suddenly, the stepmother pressed the instrument hard, crushing Tejimola's right hand. Tejimola shrieked in pain, but the relentless stepmother ordered her to use her left hand, which was also crushed. Then, her right foot, and then her left, met the same fate. Finally, her head was crushed, and poor Tejimola died.

The stepmother secretly buried Tejimola under the eaves of the rice-pounding shed. After some time, a beautiful pumpkin plant grew on that spot, laden with lovely pumpkins. When neighbors inquired about Tejimola, the stepmother lied, saying she hadn't returned from the wedding.

One day, a beggar woman asked the merchant's wife for a pumpkin. The stepmother, surprised as she had no pumpkins in her garden, had not ventured near the burial spot since the incident and thus hadn't seen the plant. She told the beggar woman she had no pumpkins. The beggar woman pointed out the beautiful plant creeping over the rice-pounding shed's roof. The merchant's wife, taken aback, told her to take as many as she liked. As the beggar woman reached for a pumpkin, the plant sang plaintively: "Stretch not thy hands nor pluck a pumpkin — thou strange beggar woman, my step-mother did crush me for the silk cloths and it is I, Tejimola." Frightened, the beggar woman fled. The merchant's wife understood, cut the plant, root and all, and threw it into a remote corner of her garden.

On that very spot, a shaddock tree grew, heavy with fruit. Some cowherd boys, unable to resist, asked the merchant's wife for some. She denied having such a tree, but the boys insisted they saw one in a remote corner. She told them to take what they found. As they reached for the fruit, the tree sang: "Oh, my brothers dear, cowherd boys of the village, neither stretch your hand nor pluck the fruits — return home — it is, I, Tejimola who am buried here crushed to death by my step-mother." The boys, thinking the tree haunted, fled and told the merchant's wife. Realizing it was Tejimola again, she uprooted the tree and threw it into the river.

The shaddock plant floated down the river and settled in a shallow pool, transforming into a lovely water-lily. Later, the merchant was returning home by boat. Seeing the beautiful lily, he wished to take it home for his dear daughter, Tejimola. He instructed his oarsmen to pluck it. As a boatman reached for it, the lily sang plaintively. The boatman, terrified, refused. Curious, the merchant reached for it himself. The lily then sang: "Father dear, neither stretch your hands nor pluck the lily. It is I, Tejimola who was crushed to death by my step-mother only for the silk cloths."

The father, aware of his wife's cruelty towards his daughter, suspected it was indeed Tejimola. To confirm, he took some chewed areca from his mouth and placed it on his left hand, and a sweet (laddoo) on his right. He said, "If you are truly my Tejimola, you will appear as a myna and chew the areca on my left hand; but if you are anyone else, you will take this sweet on my right hand." The lily immediately transformed into a myna bird, perched on his left hand, and chewed the areca. Convinced it was his Tejimola, the merchant placed the myna in a cage.

Upon reaching home, he asked his wife about Tejimola. She claimed Tejimola was at her uncle's place. However, the merchant's persistent questions revealed the truth. He then threw his gamocha (a traditional Assamese towel) over the myna and said, "If you are truly my Tejimola and love me, you will immediately regain your human form and wear this gamocha." Tejimola instantly returned to her human shape. The merchant then drove his cruel wife away from his home, and Tejimola lived happily ever after with her loving father.
moral: Goodness ultimately triumphs over cruelty and evil. Love and truth will always find a way to prevail, even in the face of immense suffering and injustice.

title: The Story of the Weaver and the Jackal
narrative_text:
Once, in a small village, lived a weaver who had fallen on hard times. His family was once wealthy, but now he lived in a humble hut. His only neighbor was a clever jackal who, taking pity on the weaver, decided to help him. The jackal promised the weaver a grand wedding with none other than the king's daughter.

The jackal, with a bundle of betel leaves, journeyed to the king's palace. He cleverly positioned himself by the royal bathing tank. When the princess and her maids came to bathe, they were astonished to see a jackal chewing betel leaves, a luxury few could afford. The jackal boasted of his king's immense wealth, a king who was, of course, the weaver.

Intrigued, the princess told her mother, the queen, about the extraordinary jackal. The queen, eager to marry her daughter to a powerful king, was easily convinced by the jackal's tales of the weaver-king's riches. The jackal, with his quick wit, arranged the marriage, convincing the king to have a small, private wedding to avoid the "burden" of hosting the weaver-king's massive (and non-existent) entourage.

On the wedding day, the jackal gathered a thousand jackals, a thousand crows, and a thousand paddy-birds to create a cacophony that sounded like a massive army, further convincing the king of the weaver's power. The weaver, dressed in borrowed clothes, married the princess. The princess, however, grew suspicious when her new husband muttered about looms and beams in his sleep. The ever-resourceful jackal explained that the "king" was simply concerned for his many weaver subjects.

The jackal knew they couldn't keep up the charade for long. He convinced the king to let the couple leave, and they returned to the weaver's humble hut. The princess was initially devastated, but she was also resourceful. She knew a secret to create gold from flour paste. With her newfound wealth, she built a magnificent palace and invited her father, the king, for a visit. The king was astounded by the opulence and the jackal proudly reminded him, 'Did I not tell you?' The weaver and the princess lived happily ever after, their good fortune a testament to the jackal's cleverness and the princess's resourcefulness.
moral: Wit and resourcefulness can overcome even the most daunting of circumstances. A clever friend is a treasure.

pause_points: ...a luxury few could afford.
...muttered about looms and beams in his sleep.
...The king was astounded...

safety_notes: This story is adapted from a Bengali folktale, not a Gujarati one, but fits the requested narrative. The original title is 'The Match-making Jackal' from 'Folk-tales of Bengal' by Lal Behari Dey.

title: The Seven Sisters and the Fire of Atonement (Gond Adaptation)
narrative_text:
Long ago, in the heart of Central India, seven sisters and their younger brother, Khetalo, journeyed together, riding upon a majestic elephant. They were a close-knit family, always looking out for one another. One day, as they arrived near a bustling village, Mhamai, the eldest and most responsible of the sisters, decided it was time to prepare a meal. She asked Khetalo to fetch fire from the nearby market so they could set up their cooking stove.

Young Khetalo, eager to help, set off towards the market. However, the vibrant sights and sounds of the village market quickly captivated him. He became engrossed in watching children play, completely forgetting his important task of fetching fire. As time passed, and Khetalo did not return, Mhamai grew concerned. She sent Lairai and Kelbai, two of her other sisters, to find him and understand the delay.

Upon reaching the market, Lairai and Kelbai found Khetalo still lost in play, oblivious to his forgotten errand. Lairai, known for her quick temper, became furious. In a fit of anger, she kicked Khetalo hard. Kelbai, though witnessing the harsh act, stood by silently, unable to intervene or stop her sister. The kick was so forceful that it left Khetalo with a permanent bent in his waist, a constant reminder of that painful moment.

As the initial anger subsided, Lairai was overcome with deep regret and remorse for her actions. She repented her harshness towards her brother and vowed to atone for her sin by walking through fire, a difficult and painful penance. Kelbai, too, felt a heavy burden of shame and guilt for her silent complicity. She had stood by and watched her brother suffer, and her inaction weighed heavily on her conscience. She also vowed to atone, promising to walk carrying fire upon her head.

After this incident, the seven sisters and their brother dispersed, settling in various villages across the region. Each year, to honor their bond and the vows made, the sisters and their brother gather. This annual meeting, known as a Jatra, is a time for them to reunite, exchange gifts, and remember the lessons learned. Lairai, in particular, is honored through a ceremonial procession where a kalash, a pot filled with water and jasmine buds, is carried by a priest. The ceremonies culminate with devotees walking through fire, symbolizing the sisters' enduring commitment to their vows of repentance and atonement. This tradition serves as a powerful reminder of the consequences of anger and inaction, and the profound importance of forgiveness and making amends within a family.
moral: The story teaches the importance of controlling one's anger, the pain caused by harsh actions, and the burden of silent complicity. It highlights the power of repentance, atonement, and the enduring strength of familial bonds, even after mistakes are made.

performance_notes.opening_line: Long ago, in the heart of Central India, a family of seven sisters and their younger brother journeyed together...
pause_points: ...completely forgetting his important task of fetching fire.
...a constant reminder of that painful moment.
...a powerful reminder of the consequences of anger and inaction, and the profound importance of forgiveness and making amends within a family.

safety_notes: The story contains a scene of physical discipline (kicking) and self-imposed penance involving fire. While presented in a traditional folklore context, care should be taken to emphasize the themes of repentance and the consequences of anger rather than glorifying the act of violence or self-harm. The fire-walking is a symbolic act of atonement within the narrative.

title: Kabir and the Weaver: The Path of True Devotion
alt_titles: The Weaver Saint and the Guru's Garland
narrative_text:
In the ancient city of Kashi, nestled by the sacred Ganges, lived a humble weaver named Kabir. Born into a Muslim family, Niru and Nima, he was destined for a life of simple toil at the loom. Yet, from a tender age, Kabir’s heart yearned for something beyond the threads and patterns of earthly existence. He possessed a keen intellect and a compassionate spirit, often giving away his family’s woven cloth to those in need, much to his parents’ initial dismay but eventual understanding. His spiritual hunger grew, leading him to question the rigid rituals and societal divisions that permeated his world.

Kabir observed the deep devotion of the Hindu sage Ramanand, a revered spiritual leader in Kashi. However, Ramanand, bound by the customs of his time, would not accept a low-caste Muslim as a disciple. Undeterred, Kabir devised a plan. He knew that Ramanand would visit the ghats of the Ganges every morning before dawn for his ritual bath. One chilly morning, Kabir lay down on the steps of the ghat, concealing himself in the dim light.

As Ramanand descended the steps, his foot accidentally touched Kabir’s head. Startled, the sage exclaimed, 'Ram, Ram!' Kabir quietly rose and went his way, his heart filled with joy. He now declared to all that he had been initiated by Ramanand, and that 'Ram, Ram' was his mantra. When orthodox Brahmins and some of Ramanand’s disciples heard this, they were greatly perturbed. They questioned Ramanand, asking how he could accept a Muslim weaver as a disciple when there were so many high-caste Hindus. Ramanand, initially refuting the claim, summoned Kabir.

To avoid looking directly at a low-caste individual, Ramanand spoke to Kabir from behind a curtain, asking why he made such a false claim. Kabir, with utmost respect, replied, 'Sir, you initiated me on the steps of the ghat. You touched my forehead with your foot and gave me the mantra 'Ram, Ram'.' Ramanand, a man of truth and nobility, could not deny the incident. Recognizing the profound spiritual insight in Kabir’s actions, he accepted Kabir as his disciple.

Their relationship, though unconventional, blossomed. Kabir, while always respectful, held his own unique views, often challenging traditional beliefs. One day, Ramanand was performing his elaborate mental worship (mansi puja) of Lord Ram. He would visualize the idol, bathe it, adorn it with finery, and offer delicacies. On this particular day, he forgot to place the garland of flowers around the deity’s neck before putting on the crown. He was perplexed, as in his mental image, the garland was not large enough to go over the crown, and removing the crown would be an act of disrespect.

From outside, Kabir, sensing his Guru’s predicament, called out, 'Gurudev, untie the knot of the garland and then tie it around the idol’s neck!' Ramanand was astonished. How could Kabir know his exact thoughts and dilemma? He realized that this humble weaver possessed a spiritual wisdom far beyond ordinary understanding. He instructed his disciples to remove the curtain, declaring, 'What can one hide from Kabir?' From that day, Ramanand’s own outlook began to change, influenced by the profound and simple truths embodied by his extraordinary disciple, Kabir, the weaver of God’s name.
moral: True devotion transcends caste, creed, and ritual. Inner purity and spiritual insight are more valuable than outward observances or societal status.

Ramanand, a man of truth and nobility, could not deny the incident.

safety_notes: The story addresses caste discrimination prevalent in historical India but frames it within Kabir's triumph over such prejudices, promoting inclusivity and spiritual equality. It is presented respectfully and warmly.

title: Mirabai's Unwavering Devotion to Krishna
narrative_text:
In the heart of Rajasthan, in the 16th century, a princess named Mirabai was born. From her earliest days, her heart was captivated by a small idol of Lord Krishna, whom she believed to be her true husband. This innocent childhood devotion blossomed into an unwavering, profound love that would define her entire life.

As was the custom, Mirabai was arranged to be married to Prince Bhoj Raj of Mewar. She entered her marital home with her beloved Krishna idol, her devotion burning brighter than ever. However, her new family, steeped in royal traditions and societal norms, did not understand her spiritual fervor. They expected her to worship the family deity, but Mirabai's heart belonged solely to Krishna. She spent her days singing devotional songs, known as bhajans, and dancing in ecstatic praise of her Lord, often in the company of saints and common folk, much to the dismay of her in-laws.

Her husband, Prince Bhoj Raj, though initially perplexed, grew to respect her devotion. Yet, his support was a lone beacon in a sea of opposition. After his untimely death in battle, Mirabai's challenges intensified. Her father-in-law, Rana Sanga, and later her brother-in-law, Vikram Singh, saw her spiritual practices as a disgrace to their royal lineage. They subjected her to relentless persecution, attempting to curb her devotion through various cruel means.

Legends tell of how her in-laws tried to poison her, sending her a cup of venom disguised as nectar. With unwavering faith, Mirabai drank it, offering it first to her Krishna idol, and miraculously, it transformed into sweet ambrosia, leaving her unharmed. Another time, they sent a basket containing a venomous snake, intending to kill her. But when Mirabai opened it, she found a beautiful garland of flowers or, in some accounts, a small idol of Krishna, protecting her once again.

Despite these trials, Mirabai's devotion only deepened. She defied all societal conventions, fearlessly proclaiming her love for Krishna. Her bhajans, filled with longing and surrender, resonated with countless souls, spreading the message of pure devotion far and wide. She eventually left the confines of the palace, embarking on pilgrimages to sacred places like Vrindavan and Dwarka, the land of her beloved Krishna.

It is said that in her final days, in Dwarka, Mirabai, overwhelmed by divine love, merged miraculously with the idol of Lord Krishna, becoming one with her eternal beloved. Her life became a testament to the power of unconditional love and unwavering faith, demonstrating that true devotion transcends all worldly barriers and fears.

Mirabai's story continues to inspire millions, reminding us that with pure love and unwavering faith, one can overcome any obstacle and attain divine union. Her life teaches us the profound truth that devotion, when sincere and absolute, is the highest path to spiritual liberation and everlasting joy.
moral: Unwavering faith and pure devotion can overcome all worldly obstacles and lead to spiritual liberation and divine union.
