title: Mahabharata: The Game of Dice
alt_titles: The Dice Game of the Mahabharata
Sabha Parva: The Game of Dice
narrative_text:
The story of the game of dice in the Mahabharata begins with the cunning Sakuni, maternal uncle to Duryodhana, planting seeds of envy and ambition in Duryodhana's heart. Witnessing the prosperity and grandeur of the Pandavas' newly built city of Indraprastha, Duryodhana grew increasingly resentful. Sakuni, a master of dice, proposed a plan to Duryodhana: to challenge Yudhishthira, the eldest Pandava, to a game of dice, knowing Yudhishthira's weakness for gambling and his adherence to the Kshatriya code, which compelled him to accept any challenge.

Duryodhana, eager to strip the Pandavas of their wealth and status, approached his blind father, King Dhritarashtra, seeking permission to host the game. Dhritarashtra, though initially hesitant and advised against it by his wise brother Vidura, was ultimately swayed by his son's persistence and his own paternal affection. Vidura warned of the destructive consequences of gambling, likening it to a weapon that would bring about ruin, but Duryodhana dismissed his counsel, arguing that dice was an ancient sport and not a true weapon. Dhritarashtra, succumbing to fate and his son's will, ordered the construction of a magnificent assembly hall, a crystal-arched palace adorned with gold and lapis lazuli, to host the fateful game.

Once the opulent hall was complete, Dhritarashtra sent Vidura to invite Yudhishthira and his brothers to Hastinapura for a friendly game of dice. Despite Vidura's veiled warnings, Yudhishthira, bound by duty and the Kshatriya code, accepted the invitation. The Pandavas, along with their wife Draupadi, arrived at the grand assembly hall, unaware of the treachery that awaited them.

The game commenced, with Sakuni playing on behalf of Duryodhana. Yudhishthira, blinded by his addiction to gambling, began to lose everything. First, he wagered and lost his immense wealth, then his chariots, his army, and his kingdom of Indraprastha. In a desperate turn, he staked his brothers, one by one, and lost them all to Sakuni's manipulative throws. Finally, in a move that shocked the entire assembly, Yudhishthira wagered Draupadi, their common wife, and lost her as well.

The Kauravas, particularly Duryodhana and his brother Dushasana, rejoiced in their victory. Duryodhana, filled with malice, ordered Dushasana to drag Draupadi into the assembly hall. Draupadi, in her menstrual cycle and wearing a single garment, was forcibly brought before the assembly, her pleas for dignity and justice falling on deaf ears. She questioned the legality of her being staked, arguing that Yudhishthira had already lost himself and therefore had no right to wager her. Her questions, however, were met with silence from the elders, including Bhishma and Drona, who were bound by their loyalty to Dhritarashtra.

Karna, fueled by his long-standing animosity towards Draupadi and the Pandavas, further humiliated her by calling her a slave and suggesting she choose another husband. Dushasana then attempted to disrobe her in front of the entire assembly. Draupadi, in her utter despair, prayed to Lord Krishna for help. Miraculously, her sari became endlessly extended, preventing Dushasana from disrobing her completely. The divine intervention stunned the assembly and instilled fear in the hearts of the Kauravas.

Witnessing the ill omens and fearing divine wrath, Queen Gandhari intervened, urging Dhritarashtra to undo the injustice. Dhritarashtra, shaken by the events, granted Draupadi two boons. Draupadi, with remarkable composure, first asked for the freedom of her husband Yudhishthira, so her son would not be called a slave. For her second boon, she requested the freedom of all the Pandavas along with their weapons. When Dhritarashtra offered a third boon, Draupadi wisely declined, stating that a Kshatriya woman should not be greedy. The Pandavas, having regained their freedom and dignity, returned to Indraprastha. However, their respite was short-lived, as they were soon invited for another game of dice, which led to their thirteen-year exile, including a year of living in incognito. This game of dice, marked by deceit and humiliation, became the pivotal event that ultimately led to the great Kurukshetra War.

**Moral:** The story of the game of dice serves as a powerful cautionary tale against the perils of gambling, the dangers of unchecked ambition, and the destructive consequences of succumbing to unrighteous counsel. It highlights the importance of upholding dharma (righteousness) and the devastating impact of injustice and humiliation. It also underscores the strength of faith and the divine protection that can be invoked in times of extreme distress.

It highlights the importance of upholding dharma (righteousness) and the devastating impact of injustice and humiliation. It also underscores the strength of faith and the divine protection that can be invoked in times of extreme distress.

title: The Story of Dhruva
narrative_text:
In ancient times, there lived a mighty king named Uttānapāda, a descendant of Manu, the son of Brahmā. King Uttānapāda had two wives: the gentle Queen Sunīti and the favored Queen Suruci. Sunīti bore a son named Dhruva, while Suruci gave birth to Uttama. The King, unfortunately, showed a clear preference for Suruci and her son, often neglecting Dhruva and his mother.

One day, young Uttama was seated on his father's lap. Dhruva, with an innocent heart, wished to join his brother and sit on his father's knee. However, Queen Suruci, filled with pride and jealousy, harshly rebuked him, saying, 'Child, if you desire to sit on your father's lap, you should have been born from my womb. You harbor ambitions that are not meant for you.' The King, fearing Suruci's displeasure, remained silent.

Heartbroken, Dhruva ran to his mother, Sunīti, who wept upon hearing of the injustice. Yet, amidst her tears, she instilled in her son a profound lesson: 'My dear child, do not despair. Your true father is the Lord of the universe, Mahāviṣṇu. Seek refuge in Him, for He alone can grant you a position unattainable by anyone.'

Inspired by his mother's words, Dhruva, though a mere child, made a solemn vow: 'I will attain a position higher than any my father or even my ancestors have achieved, through my own unwavering effort.' With this resolve, he left the palace and ventured into the dense forest of Madhuvana, on the banks of the sacred river Yamunā, to perform severe penance.

His mother, Sunīti, tried to dissuade him and bring him back, but Dhruva's determination was unshakeable. He intensified his austerities, meditating deeply on Mahāviṣṇu. His devotion was so profound that it disturbed the celestial realms. Finally, Mahāviṣṇu, pleased by Dhruva's unwavering resolve, appeared before him.

Dhruva, overwhelmed with joy, humbly requested a lofty and eternal place, one that would be a steadfast support for the entire world. Granting his wish, Viṣṇu pointed to a noble position, higher than all planets, stars, the Saptarṣis (Ursa Major), and even the celestial beings. Mahāviṣṇu declared that Dhruva would reside in this exalted place as a brilliant star until the end of the Kalpa, and his devoted mother, Sunīti, would also be honored as a star near Lord Śiva.

After receiving this divine boon, Dhruva returned to his kingdom, where he was embraced by all. Years later, King Uttānapāda, having renounced his worldly duties, entrusted the kingdom to Dhruva. Dhruva ruled wisely and justly. He married Brāhmī, the daughter of Prajāpati Śiśumāra, and they had two sons, Kalpa and Vatsara. He also married Ilā, the daughter of Vāyu, who bore him a son named Utkala.

Later, his stepbrother Uttama was tragically killed by a Yakṣa during a hunt. In his grief and anger, Dhruva marched to the realm of the Yakṣas and defeated their vast army. Eventually, Kubera, the lord of the Yakṣas, appeared and blessed Dhruva, further elevating his status. After a long and righteous reign, Dhruva ascended to his eternal abode, becoming the Pole Star, a beacon of unwavering devotion and steadfastness, forever shining brightly in the heavens, guiding all who look upon him.
moral: The story of Dhruva teaches us the power of unwavering determination, profound devotion, and the rewards of righteous endeavor. Even a child, with pure intent and persistent effort, can achieve the seemingly impossible and attain the highest spiritual and worldly positions. It also highlights the importance of a mother's guidance and the ultimate justice of the divine.

title: The Brahmin's Dream
narrative_text:
Long ago, there lived a poor Brahmin in a village. He used to beg for a living and sometimes, had to go without food for many days. One day he got a pot full of flour. He was very happy. He took the pot home and hung it up near his bed. He lay on the bed and gazed at his pot. Soon, he fell fast asleep and started dreaming.

He dreamt that if a famine came to the land, he could sell the flour for a very good price. People would outbid each other. He would finally sell it for twenty rupees. With these twenty rupees, he would buy a pair of goats. He would feed these goats on green grass. Soon the goats would have lots of kids and he would trade the whole herd of goats for buffaloes and cows. The cows would have calves of their own and there would be lots and lots of milk. He would make sweetmeats with the milk, and butter and curd and sell it all in the market. Soon, he would grow very rich and build a big house with huge gardens and fruit orchards. He would now start trading in pearls, diamonds and other precious stones. The King would hear of his riches and bring along his daughter, the beautiful Princess and offer her hand in marriage.

Soon, they would have sons and daughters and they would run around the house, playing. When he would grow tired, he would pick up a stick and beat them and beat them and beat them!

Imagining he was beating the children, the Brahmin began to beat the air with his hands. Suddenly his hands struck the pot of flour, the pot broke and all its contents spilled to the ground. The Brahmin woke up to find that he was dreaming everything. There was no big house, no lovely gardens and no wife or children. Only the broken pot and flour scattered all over the floor.
moral: Never build castles in the air.

pause_points: Soon, he fell fast asleep and started dreaming.

title: The Foolish Sage And The Jackal
narrative_text:
In a monastery far away from human habitation lived a saint called Deva Sarma. He amassed a lot of wealth by selling clothes gifted to him by well-wishers and disciples. It became a burden for him to guard that wealth. Since he did not trust anyone, he put all his money into a bag and carried it with him wherever he went. Ashadhabhooti, an experienced cheat, noticed Deva Sarma carrying his bag always with him and assuming that it certainly contained something valuable, began planning to snatch it from him.

One day, the cheat met the saint and promptly fell on his feet and said, “Oh, know-all, I have realized that this life is an illusion; youth is fleeting and all familial ties are like a dream. Please show me the correct path that delivers me from all worldly ties.”

Pleased with his humility, Deva Sarma said, “Child, you are the blessed one who has thought of renouncing worldly pleasures. Listen, however low his caste is, the person that chants “Om Namahsivayah' and smears holy ash on his forehead, becomes Siva himself and knows no rebirth. I shall accept you as my protégé but you must not enter the hermitage in the night because company is forbidden for saints. After initiation, you have to live in the hut at the entrance of the monastery.”

Ashadhabhooti promised the saint that he would consider every sign from him as a command and carry it out. Satisfied, the saint accepted the cheat as his disciple. Ashadhabhooti too began making Deva Sarma happy by attending to every need of his. But seeing that the saint never separated the money-bag from his person, Ashadhabhooti thought, “the old man is very crafty and keeps the bag always with him. How can I snatch it from him? Shall I kill him?”

As the cheat was at a loss to achieve his goal, the son of a disciple came calling on the sage. The visitor invited Deva Sarma to come to his village and perform the sacred thread ceremony of his son. The saint accepted the invitation and set out for the village taking Ashadhabhooti with him.

On the way, the guru and his disciple had to cross a river. After bathing in the river and wanting to rest for a while, Deva Sarma took the money bag and pushed it into a quilt he was carrying and told the disciple, “I have to respond to nature’s call. I am leaving this holy quilt of Siva here. Keep an eye on it.” The moment the guru went out of his sight, Ashadhabhooti collected the bag and fled the place.

With great trust in his disciple, Deva Sarma decided to spend time by joining a crowd watching two well-fed goats fighting ferociously. As blood was running down their heads, a jackal came there to feast on the blood the two goats were shedding. Deva Sarma saw the jackal entering the scene and thought that the jackal would surely die caught between the two warring goats. His surmise came true and the jackal died, gored by the two goats.

Brooding over the demise of the jackal, Deva Sarma returned to where he had left the money-bag with Ashadhabhooti and panicked when he found Ashadhabhooti missing. The holy quilt was there but not the money-bag in it. He began wailing, “Oh, trickster, what have you done? I have lost everything in this world.” After a vain search for the trickster, the foolish saint returned home dejected.
moral: The sage and the jackal have none to blame except themselves.

title: The Turtle Who Couldn't Stop Talking
narrative_text:
A turtle lived in a pond at the foot of a hill. Two young wild Geese, looking for food, saw the Turtle, and talked with him. The next day the Geese came again to visit the Turtle and they became very well acquainted. Soon they were great friends.

"Friend Turtle," the Geese said one day, "we have a beautiful home far away. We are going to fly back to it to-morrow. It will be a long but pleasant journey. Will you go with us?"

"How could I? I have no wings," said the Turtle.

"Oh, we will take you, if only you can keep your mouth shut, and say not a word to anybody," they said. "I can do that," said the Turtle. "Do take me with you. I will do exactly as you wish."

So the next day the Geese brought a stick and they held the ends of it. "Now take the middle of this in your mouth, and don't say a word until we reach home," they said.

The Geese then sprang into the air, with the Turtle between them, holding fast to the stick. The village children saw the two Geese flying along with the Turtle and cried out: "Oh, see the Turtle up in the air! Look at the Geese carrying a Turtle by a stick! Did you ever see anything more ridiculous in your life!"

The Turtle looked down and began to say, "Well, and if my friends carry me, what business is that of yours?" when he let go, and fell dead at the feet of the children.

As the two Geese flew on, they heard the people say, when they came to see the poor Turtle, "That fellow could not keep his mouth shut. He had to talk, and so lost his life."
moral: One should learn to control their tongue and not speak unnecessarily, especially when silence is required for their own good.

title: Akbar-Birbal: The Magical Sticks
narrative_text:
A rich merchant had many servants in his house. One day the merchant’s wife’s beloved necklace disappeared from her vault. Gradually precious things in the house started disappearing one by one. The merchant was troubled by the thief in his house. He suspected one of his many servants to be the thief. But it was very difficult to identify and catch the thief among all the other servants in his huge house. The troubled merchant contacted his wise friend Birbal about the recent incidents.

After listening to the merchant, Birbal agreed to help the merchant and went to his house. Birbal summoned all the merchant’s servants and started interrogating them one by one. He received nothing but absolute denial from every single one of them

An idea, then struck Birbal. He handed over a stick to every servant, all of the same length. He told them that the stick was magical and the stick of the thief would grow by two inches the next day. The servants were ordered to present the stick to Birbal the next day.

The next day, as ordered, the servants assembled at the merchant’s house with their sticks. As Birbal examined each stick, he noticed that one of the servants was holding a stick that was two inches shorter.

“This is your thief, merchant.”- Birbal said, pointing at the servant.

When the merchant asked Birbal to reason his accusation, he said- “Honest men never feared about the growth of their stick. The thief had already cut his stick shorter by two inches fearing that his stick will be long by two inches next morning.”
moral: Honest men never feared about the growth of their stick. The thief had already cut his stick shorter by two inches fearing that his stick will be long by two inches next morning.

title: Tenali Tales: Raman the horse trainer
narrative_text:
An Arab brought a shipload of horses from Persia. Many in the court of king Krishnadevaraya bought the horses from the Arab. Raman maintained that Vijayanagara’s horses were superior to the horses from Arabia. The ministers who bought the Arabian horses challenged Raman to prove this point in a horse race.

The courtiers put in much effort to train their horses. They fed the horses well to make sure they were strong and sturdy.

On the day of the race, all the courtiers brought their well-fed horses to a field. They had employed well-trained jockeys to ride the horses.

Raman brought his horse which looked lean and thin. He had a hungry look, as though he had not eaten for days. Raman announced that he himself would ride his horse.

Raman sat on the hungry looking horse. He had a long pole in his hand. He had tied a bundle of grass to one end of the stick.

All the horses were lined up with the jockeys in position. Raman sat on the hungry looking horse. He had a long pole in his hand. He had tied a bundle of grass to one end of the stick. Holding the stick from the other end, Raman dangled the grass in front of the horse. The horse wanted to grab the food. So, he ran fast. But no matter how fast he ran, the grass remained outside his reach.

The horse kept running with greater speed until it crossed the finish line, coming first in the race. Raman hugged his horse and fed it lush green grass. When Raman went to collect the prize, the king asked him the secret of his success. “One must have hunger for success, Maharaj,” Raman said, “only then will one succeed.”
moral: One must have hunger for success; only then will one succeed.

title: The Story of the Two Fathers
narrative_text: King Vikram, in his persistent quest to capture the elusive Vetala, once again managed to secure the spirit. As was their custom, Vetala began to narrate a tale to Vikram during their journey, with the usual condition that if Vikram spoke, Vetala would return to his tree. The story began with a widow named Bhagwati and her daughter, Dhanwati, who were traveling and stopped to rest. Nearby, a thief, destined for execution the following day, overheard their conversation. Moved by their plight, the thief proposed marriage to Dhanwati, promising to reveal the location of his hidden treasure, which would ensure their future well-being after his death. He also sought salvation, wishing not to become a ghost. Bhagwati, taking pity on him, agreed. With the help of a soldier, who retrieved two gold coins from the thief's pocket for the priest, the marriage was solemnized. The next day, the thief was hanged. Bhagwati and Dhanwati followed the thief's instructions, found the hidden treasure in old caves, and became wealthy. However, Dhanwati remained unhappy, longing for her husband. Bhagwati, pragmatic, reminded her that wealth could solve many problems. As they prospered, Bhagwati's friends urged her to find a suitable husband for Dhanwati, whom they believed to be a maiden. Bhagwati, however, desired a son-in-law who would live with them. One day, a priest visited their home, and Bhagwati offered Dhanwati's hand in marriage to him, which he accepted. They were married, but soon after, the priest stole all of Dhanwati's jewelry and fled. The shock of this betrayal proved too much for Bhagwati, who passed away. Some time later, Dhanwati gave birth to a baby boy and was forced to beg for their survival. One day, while wandering through the city, Dhanwati and her son witnessed a man struggling for his life with a snake. The boy attempted to save him but failed, and Dhanwati recognized the dying man as her second husband, the priest. It was revealed that this priest was actually the son of a king. The king, upon learning of his son's fate and seeing Dhanwati and her child, adopted the boy. When the king fell ill, he appointed the boy as his successor. After the king's death, the boy's mother, Dhanwati, instructed him to perform a sacred journey and make donations for his deceased father. As the boy prepared to make the offerings, Vetala interrupted the narrative, posing a riddle to Vikram: "To which father should the offerings be made – to the king who adopted him, to the thief who married Dhanwati first, or to the prince (the priest) who was his biological father?" Vikram, without hesitation, replied that the offerings should be made to the thief. He reasoned that the prince was motivated by money, and the king fulfilled his responsibilities out of duty. However, the thief, despite his circumstances, married Dhanwati through a proper ceremony and sought her well-being, thus acting as a true father. Vetala, pleased with Vikram's wise answer, flew back to his tree, as Vikram had broken his silence once more. Vikram, undeterred, resumed his pursuit.
moral: The true father is the one who cares for and fulfills responsibilities towards a child, rather than the biological parent or one motivated by personal gain.

title: The Ghost-Brahman
alt_titles: The Ghost Who Was a Brahmin
narrative_text: Once on a time there lived a poor Brahman, who not being a Kulin, found it the hardest thing in the world to get married. He went to rich people and begged of them to give him money that he might marry a wife. And a large sum of money was needed, not so much for the expenses of the wedding, as for giving to the parents of the bride. He begged from door to door, flattered many rich folk, and at last succeeded in scraping together the sum needed. The wedding took place in due time; and he brought home his wife to his mother. After a short time he said to his mother—"Mother, I have no means to support you and my wife; I must therefore go to distant countries to get money somehow or other. I may be away for years, for I won't return till I get a good sum. In the meantime I'll give you what I have; you make the best of it, and take care of my wife." The Brahman receiving his mother's blessing set out on his travels. In the evening of that very day, a ghost assuming the exact appearance of the Brahman came into the house. The newly married woman, thinking it was her husband, said to him—"How is it that you have returned so soon? You said you might be away for years; why have you changed your mind?" The ghost said—"To-day is not a lucky day, I have therefore returned home; besides, I have already got some money." The mother did not doubt but that it was her son. So the ghost lived in the house as if he was its owner, and as if he was the son of the old woman and the husband of the young woman. As the ghost and the Brahman were exactly like each other in everything, like two peas, the people in the neighbourhood all thought that the ghost was the real Brahman. After some years the Brahman returned from his travels; and what was his surprise when he found another like him in the house. The ghost said to the Brahman—"Who are you? what business have you to come to my house?" "Who am I?" replied the Brahman, "let me ask who you are. This is my house; that is my mother, and this is my wife." The ghost said—"Why herein is a strange thing. Every one knows that this is my house, that is my wife, and yonder is my mother; and I have lived here for years. And you pretend this is your house, and that woman is your wife. Your head must have got turned. Brahman." So saying the ghost drove away the Brahman from his house. The Brahman became mute with wonder. He did not know what to do. At last he bethought himself of going to the king and of laying his case before him. The king saw the ghost-Brahman as well as the Brahman, and the one was the picture of the other; so he was in a fix, and did not know how to decide the quarrel. Day after day the Brahman went to the king and besought him to give him back his house, his wife, and his mother; and the king, not knowing what to say every time, put him off to the following day. Every day the king tells him to—"Come to-morrow"; and every day the Brahman goes away from the palace weeping and striking his forehead with the palm of his hand, and saying—"What a wicked world this is! I am driven from my own house, and another fellow has taken possession of my house and of my wife! And what a king this is! He does not do justice." Now, it came to pass that as the Brahman went away every day from the court outside the town, he passed a spot at which a great many cowboys used to play. They let the cows graze on the meadow, while they themselves met together under a large tree to play. And they played at royalty. One cowboy was elected king; another, prime minister or vizier; another, kotwal, or prefect of the police; and others, constables. Every day for several days together they saw the Brahman passing by weeping. One day the cowboy king asked his vizier whether he knew why the Brahman wept every day. On the vizier not being able to answer the question, the cowboy king ordered one of his constables to bring the Brahman to him. One of them went and said to the Brahman—"The king requires your immediate attendance." The Brahman replied—"What for? I have just come from the king, and he put me off till to-morrow. Why does he want me again?" "It is our king that wants you—our neat-herd king," rejoined the constable. "Who is neat-herd king?" asked the Brahman. "Come and see," was the reply. The neat-herd king then asked the Brahman why he every day went away weeping. The Brahman then told him his sad story. The neat-herd king, after hearing the whole, said, "I understand your case; I will give you again all your rights. Only go to the king and ask his permission for me to decide your case." The Brahman went back to the king of the country, and begged his Majesty to send his case to the neat-herd king, who had offered to decide it. The king, whom the case had greatly puzzled, granted the permission sought. The following morning was fixed for the trial. The neat-herd king, who saw through the whole, brought with him next day a phial with a narrow neck. The Brahman and the ghost-Brahman both appeared at the bar. After a great deal of examination of witnesses and of speech-making, the neat-herd king said—"Well, I have heard enough. I'll decide the case at once. Here is this phial. Whichever of you will enter into it shall be declared by the court to be the rightful owner of the house the title of which is in dispute. Now, let me see, which of you will enter." The Brahman said—"You are a neat-herd, and your intellect is that of a neat-herd. What man can enter into such a small phial?" "If you cannot enter," said the neat-herd king, "then you are not the rightful owner. What do you say, sir, to this?" turning to the ghost-Brahman and addressing him. "If you can enter into the phial, then the house and the wife and the mother become yours." "Of course I will enter," said the ghost. And true to his word, to the wonder of all, he made himself into a small creature like an insect, and entered into the phial. The neat-herd king forthwith corked up the phial, and the ghost could not get out. Then, addressing the Brahman, the neat-herd king said, "Throw this phial into the bottom of the sea, and take possession of your house, wife, and mother." The Brahman did so, and lived happily for many years and begat sons and daughters.
moral: Justice can come from unexpected places, and cleverness can overcome deception.

pause_points: ...he went to rich people and begged of them to give him money that he might marry a wife.
...The Brahman receiving his mother's blessing set out on his travels.
...So saying the ghost drove away the Brahman from his house.
...Every day the king tells him to—"Come to-morrow"; and every day the Brahman goes away from the palace weeping...
...The neat-herd king forthwith corked up the phial, and the ghost could not get out.

safety_notes: The story involves a ghost, but it is not presented in a frightening manner. The resolution is positive.

title: The Miracle of Purun Bhagat
narrative_text:
**THERE** was once a man in India who was Prime Minister of one of the semi-independent native States in the north-western part of the country. He was a Brahmin, so high-caste that caste ceased to have any particular meaning for him; and his father had been an important official in the gay-coloured tag-rag and bobtail of an old-fashioned Hindu Court. But as Purun Dass grew up he felt that the old order of things was changing, and that if any one wished to get on in the world he must stand well with the English, and imitate all that the English believed to be good. At the same time a native official must keep his own master’s favour. This was a difficult game, but the quiet, close-mouthed young Brahmin, helped by a good English education at a Bombay University, played it coolly, and rose, step by step, to be Prime Minister of the kingdom. That is to say, he held more real power than his master the Maharajah.

When the old king—who was suspicious of the English, their railways and telegraphs—died, Purun Dass stood high with his young successor, who had been tutored by an Englishman; and between them, though he always took care that his master should have the credit, they established schools for little girls, made roads, and started State dispensaries and shows of agricultural implements, and published a yearly blue-book on the ‘Moral and Material Progress of the State,’ and the Foreign Office and the Government of India were delighted. Very few, native States take up English progress altogether, for they will not believe, as Purun Dass showed he did, that what was good for the Englishman must be twice as good for the Asiatic. The Prime Minister became the honoured friend of Viceroys, and Governors, and Lieutenant-Governors, and medical missionaries, and common missionaries, and hard-riding English officers who came to shoot in the State preserves, as well as of whole hosts of tourists who travelled up and down India in the cold weather, showing how things ought to be managed. In his spare time he would endow scholarships for the study of medicine and manufactures on strictly English lines, and write letters to the _Pioneer_, the greatest Indian daily paper, explaining his master’s aims and objects.

At last he went to England on a visit, and had to pay enormous sums to the priests when he came back; for even so high-caste a Brahmin as Purun Dass lost caste by crossing the black sea. In London he met and talked with every one worth knowing—men whose names go all over the world—and saw a great deal more than he said. He was given honorary degrees by learned universities, and he made speeches and talked of Hindu social reform to English ladies in evening dress, till all London cried, ‘This is the most fascinating man we have ever met at dinner since cloths were first laid.’

When he returned to India there was a blaze of glory, for the Viceroy himself made a special visit to confer upon the Maharajah the Grand Cross of the Star of India—all diamonds and ribbons and enamel; and at the same ceremony, while the cannon boomed, Purun Dass was made a Knight Commander of the Order of the Indian Empire; so that his name stood Sir Purun Dass, K.C.I.E.

That evening, at dinner, in the big Viceregal tent, he stood up with the badge and the collar of the Order on his breast, and replying to the toast of his master’s health, made a speech few Englishmen could have bettered.

Next month, when the city had returned to its sun-baked quiet, he did a thing no Englishman would have dreamed of doing; for, so far as the world’s affairs went, he died. The jewelled order of his knighthood went back to the Indian Government, and a new Prime Minister was appointed to the charge of affairs, and a great game of General Post began in all the subordinate appointments. The priests knew what had happened, and the people guessed; but India is the one place in the world where a man can do as he pleases and nobody asks why; and the fact that Dewan Sir Purun Dass, K.C.I.E., had resigned position, palace, and power, and taken up the begging-bowl and ochre-coloured dress of a Sunnyasi, or holy man, was considered nothing extraordinary. He had been, as the Old Law recommends, twenty years a youth, twenty years a fighter,—though he had never carried a weapon in his life,—and twenty years head of a household. He had used his wealth and his power for what he knew both to be worth; he had taken honour when it came his way; he had seen men and cities far and near, and men and cities had stood up and honoured him. Now he would let those things go, as a man drops the cloak he no longer needs.

Behind him, as he walked through the city gates, an antelope skin and brass-handled crutch under his arm, and a begging-bowl of polished brown _coco-de-mer_ in his hand, barefoot, alone, with eyes cast on the ground—behind him they were firing salutes from the bastions in honour of his happy successor. Purun Dass nodded. All that life was ended; and he bore it no more ill-will or good-will than a man bears to a colourless dream of the night. He was a Sunnyasi—a houseless, wandering mendicant, depending on his neighbours for his daily bread; and so long as there is a morsel to divide in India, neither priest nor beggar starves. He had never in his life tasted meat, and very seldom eaten even fish. A five-pound note would have covered his personal expenses for food through any one of the many years in which he had been absolute master of millions of money. Even when he was being lionised in London he had held before him his dream of peace and quiet—the long, white, dusty Indian road, printed all over with bare feet, the incessant, slow-moving traffic, and the sharp-smelling wood smoke curling up under the fig-trees in the twilight, where the wayfarers sit at their evening meal.

When the time came to make that dream true the Prime Minister took the proper steps, and in three days you might more easily have found a bubble in the trough of the long Atlantic seas than Purun Dass among the roving, gathering, separating millions of India.

At night his antelope skin was spread where the darkness overtook him—sometimes in a Sunnyasi monastery by the roadside; sometimes by a mud-pillar shrine of Kala Pir, where the Jogis, who are another misty division of holy men, would receive him as they do those who know what castes and divisions are worth; sometimes on the outskirts of a little Hindu village, where the children would steal up with the food their parents had prepared; and sometimes on the pitch of the bare grazing-grounds, where the flame of his stick fire waked the drowsy camels. It was all one to Purun Dass—or Purun Bhagat, as he called himself now. Earth, people, and food were all one. But unconsciously his feet drew him away northward and eastward; from the south to Rohtak; from Rohtak to Kurnool; from Kurnool to ruined Samanah, and then up-stream along the dried bed of the Gugger river that fills only when the rain falls in the hills, till one day he saw the far line of the great Himalayas.

Then Purun Bhagat smiled, for he remembered that his mother was of Rajput Brahmin birth, from Kulu way—a Hill-woman, always home-sick for the snows—and that the least touch of Hill blood draws a man in the end back to where he belongs.

‘Yonder,’ said Purun Bhagat, breasting the last long rise, ‘yonder is my dwelling-place among the snows.’

He was aimed for a Buddhist monastery, and he walked for days and days alone in the foothills, till he came to a place where a small stream had been thrust out of a cleft in the rock by a landslip, and had spread itself in a little marsh. There were stunted pines all round, and a few bare deodars. It was an evil place, but Purun Bhagat was a Sunnyasi, and could not be troubled by evil. He sat down and meditated, and the stream flowed past him, and the pines whispered, and the deodars stood still. He was a long way from any human habitation, and he was glad of it. He had no desire to see men again. He had seen enough of them in his life. He had seen their greed, their ambition, their cruelty, their folly. He had seen their love, their kindness, their generosity, their wisdom. He had seen it all, and he was tired of it. He wanted to be alone with God.

He built himself a little hut of stones and branches, and roofed it with pine needles. He made a bed of dry leaves, and a pillow of moss. He had his begging-bowl, and his antelope skin, and his brass-handled crutch. He had nothing else. He was content.

One day, as he sat meditating, a bear came out of the forest. It was a huge, shaggy beast, with small, cunning eyes. It sniffed at Purun Bhagat, and then sat down on its haunches, and watched him. Purun Bhagat did not move. He continued to meditate. The bear watched him for a long time, and then, as if satisfied, it lay down and went to sleep. From that day on, the bear became his constant companion. It would lie at his feet while he meditated, and follow him when he went to gather berries or roots. It was a gentle beast, and Purun Bhagat grew fond of it.

One evening, as he sat by his fire, a leopard came out of the forest. It was a sleek, powerful animal, with green, luminous eyes. It snarled at the bear, and then sprang at Purun Bhagat. The bear, with a roar, intercepted it. There was a brief, furious struggle, and then the leopard, wounded, fled back into the forest. The bear was badly hurt, but Purun Bhagat nursed it back to health. From that day on, the leopard also became his companion. It would lie at a respectful distance, and watch him with its green eyes. It was a wild beast, but it seemed to understand that Purun Bhagat was a holy man, and it did him no harm.

One night, as he slept, a tiger came out of the forest. It was a magnificent animal, with stripes of black and gold. It sniffed at Purun Bhagat, and then, with a low growl, it lay down beside him. The bear and the leopard, who had been sleeping nearby, woke up and watched. The tiger, too, seemed to understand that Purun Bhagat was a holy man, and it did him no harm. From that day on, the tiger also became his companion. It would lie at his side, and purr like a huge cat. It was a fearsome beast, but it was gentle with Purun Bhagat.

So Purun Bhagat lived in the forest, surrounded by wild animals. He had no fear, for he knew that God was with him. He had no wants, for he had renounced all worldly possessions. He had no desires, for he had found peace within himself. He was a true Sunnyasi, a holy man.

One day, a great earthquake shook the mountains. The ground trembled, and the trees swayed. Rocks tumbled down the hillsides, and the stream became a raging torrent. Purun Bhagat, sitting in his hut, felt the earth move beneath him. He knew that his time had come. He closed his eyes, and waited.

The bear, the leopard, and the tiger, sensing his distress, gathered around him. They licked his hands, and rubbed their heads against his body. They whined and growled, as if trying to comfort him. Purun Bhagat opened his eyes, and smiled. He laid his hands on their heads, and blessed them. Then, with a sigh, he passed away.

The animals, mourning his loss, stayed by his side for many days. Then, one by one, they returned to the forest. But they never forgot Purun Bhagat. And sometimes, even now, when the moon is full, and the snow lies deep on the mountains, you can hear their mournful cries, echoing through the valleys, as if they are still calling for their master.

And so ends the story of Purun Bhagat, the holy man who lived among the wild animals, and found peace in the wilderness. His story is a reminder that true happiness does not lie in worldly possessions, but in inner peace and contentment. It is a story of renunciation, of faith, and of the power of love. It is a story that has been told for generations, and will continue to be told for many more to come.
moral: True happiness lies in inner peace and contentment, not in worldly possessions. Renunciation, faith, and love can lead to a fulfilling life. The story also highlights the harmony that can exist between humans and nature when there is mutual respect and understanding.

...he saw the far line of the great Himalayas.

...From that day on, the tiger also became his companion.

title: Namdev and the Dog
alt_titles: The Story of Namdev ji and a dog
narrative_text:
In the sacred land of Maharashtra, lived the revered saint Namdev, a devotee whose heart overflowed with love for Lord Vitthal. One day, the wise Muktabai, seeking to discern the true depth of devotion among the saints, asked Gora Kumbhar, the potter, to test them. Gora Kumbhar, known for testing the strength of pots, gently tapped the heads of all the assembled saints. When he came to Namdev, he too tapped his head. Namdev, in his human pride, felt a sting of insult, wondering if his spiritual stature could be measured like mere earthenware. Yet, he held his tongue. The other devotees, however, remained serene and undisturbed by Gora Kumbhar's assessment. Gora Kumbhar then declared his judgment: all the saints, save for Namdev, possessed 'strong earthenware'—meaning their devotion was firm and unwavering.

Hearing this, Namdev, humbled by the implicit criticism, acknowledged that his 'head' was indeed weak and that he was in need of further spiritual instruction. He recounted the entire incident to his beloved Lord Vitthal (Vitdalnath ji). Lord Vitthal, in His infinite wisdom, affirmed Gora Kumbhar's assessment, explaining that Namdev had not yet fully experienced the all-pervading Brahman because he lacked the guidance of a true Sadguru. Lord Vitthal then directed Namdev to seek out His devotee, Visoba Khecher, to attain this profound spiritual wisdom.

Namdev embarked on his journey and eventually found Visoba Khecher in a Shiva temple. To Namdev's dismay, Visoba was sleeping with his feet resting upon a Shiva linga. Namdev, initially shocked by what he perceived as disrespect towards the deity, thought, 'How can one who insults God impart spiritual teachings?' He respectfully asked Visoba to remove his feet from the sacred linga. Visoba, with a knowing smile, replied, 'My dear Namdev, please place my feet in any direction where Shiva does not exist.' Namdev, taking up Visoba's feet, tried to shift them in various directions, but to his astonishment, wherever he placed them, a Shiva linga miraculously appeared. Namdev was deeply surprised and realized the profound truth Visoba was conveying. Visoba then gently explained that Namdev's vision was indeed weak, for he had not yet grasped that the Almighty is present everywhere, in every atom of the universe, in all living and non-living beings. He urged Namdev to cultivate the vision to see the divine in everything.

From that day forward, Namdev's perception transformed. He began to see the Almighty's presence in every corner of existence. One afternoon, as he sat under a tree, preparing to eat his meal of roti, a dog suddenly appeared and snatched his bread, running away with it. Namdev, instead of being angry, saw the divine in the dog. He realized the roti was dry and, filled with compassion and devotion, he ran after the dog, calling out, 'Stop, my Lord! The roti is dry. Let me apply some butter on it for you!' He chased the dog, eager to serve the divine presence he now recognized within the animal.
moral: True devotion (bhakti) lies not merely in rituals or offerings, but in recognizing the omnipresence of the Almighty in every being and every aspect of the universe. When one truly believes that God is everywhere, one naturally refrains from sin and acts with universal love and compassion.

...wherever he placed them, a Shiva linga miraculously appeared.
...He ran after the dog, calling out, 'Stop, my Lord! The roti is dry. Let me apply some butter on it for you!'

title: The Story of Bhai Dooj: Yama and Yamuna
narrative_text:
Long ago, in the celestial realms, lived Yama, the revered God of Death, and his beloved sister, Yamuna, the sacred river goddess. Though they shared an unbreakable bond, Yama, burdened by his solemn duties, often found little time to visit his sister. Yamuna, however, yearned for her brother's presence and sent countless invitations, hoping he would grace her abode.

Finally, after a long passage of time, Yama decided to fulfill his sister's wish. The news of his impending visit filled Yamuna's heart with immense joy. She meticulously prepared a grand feast, adorned her home with vibrant decorations, and eagerly awaited her brother's arrival. When Yama finally appeared, Yamuna's happiness knew no bounds. She welcomed him with open arms, performing an aarti, a ritual of waving lamps, and lovingly applied a sacred tilak on his forehead, a mark of protection and respect.

Yamuna then served Yama the most delicious meal, prepared with all her heart and soul. Yama, deeply touched by his sister's affection, hospitality, and unwavering love, felt a profound sense of peace and contentment. As he prepared to depart, he asked Yamuna what boon she desired. Without hesitation, Yamuna expressed her wish: that every year, on this very day, he would visit her, and that any brother who receives a tilak from his sister and shares a meal with her on this auspicious day would be blessed with a long life, free from the fear of an untimely death.

Yama, moved by his sister's selfless love and her desire for the well-being of all brothers, readily granted her wish. He declared that from that day forward, the second lunar day of the bright fortnight of Kartika would be known as Yama Dwitiya, or Bhai Dooj, a day dedicated to celebrating the eternal bond between brothers and sisters. He promised that any brother who honors his sister on this day would be protected and blessed with prosperity and longevity. And so, the tradition of Bhai Dooj began, a beautiful testament to the enduring love and prayers of a sister for her brother.
moral: The festival of Bhai Dooj beautifully illustrates the profound and protective love between a brother and sister. It teaches us the importance of cherishing family bonds, expressing affection, and the power of selfless prayers for the well-being and longevity of our loved ones.

pause_points: Though they shared an unbreakable bond, Yama, burdened by his solemn duties, often found little time to visit his sister.
When Yama finally appeared, Yamuna's happiness knew no bounds.
As he prepared to depart, he asked Yamuna what boon she desired.
Yama, moved by his sister's selfless love and her desire for the well-being of all brothers, readily granted her wish.

title: Ramayana: The Death of Ravana
narrative_text: The final battle between Rama and Ravana was a sight to behold. The armies of monkeys and demons watched in awe as the two great warriors clashed. For a long time, the battle was evenly matched. Ravana, with his ten heads and twenty arms, was a formidable opponent. He unleashed a volley of arrows at Rama, but Rama, with his divine weapons, was able to counter every attack. As the battle raged on, Matali, the charioteer of Indra, who was assisting Rama, reminded him of the divine arrow given to him by the sage Agastya. This was no ordinary arrow. It was created by Brahma himself and was infused with the power of the sun and the wind. It was destined to be the end of Ravana. Rama, remembering the words of the sage, took the divine arrow and, with a prayer, aimed it at Ravana. The arrow, blazing with the light of a thousand suns, shot through the air and struck Ravana in the chest, piercing his heart. The mighty king of Lanka, who had terrorized the three worlds, let out a final cry and fell from his chariot, dead. A great silence fell upon the battlefield. The demons, seeing their king fall, lost all hope and fled in terror. The monkeys and bears, on the other hand, erupted in joyous celebration. Vibhishana, Ravana's brother who had sided with Rama, mourned the death of his brother, but Rama consoled him, saying that Ravana had died a warrior's death. With the death of Ravana, the war was finally over. Sita was rescued, and righteousness prevailed.
moral: Good always triumphs over evil, and no amount of power or arrogance can protect one from the consequences of their actions.

performance_notes.opening_line: The battle between Rama and Ravana raged on, shaking the very foundations of the earth.
pause_points: ...as the divine arrow soared through the air...
...a hush fell over the battlefield...

safety_notes: The story contains descriptions of a battle, but is presented in a way that is not overly graphic or disturbing.

title: The Vow of Bhishma
narrative_text:
In the years that followed, King Shantanu, though without Ganga, found happiness in his son, Devavrata, who was god-like in appearance, conduct, and skill, and was soon made the heir to the throne. One day, a divine fragrance led Shantanu to Satyavati, a beautiful girl, daughter of the chief of fishermen. Though initially hesitant to remarry, Satyavati's charm captivated him. Shantanu approached her father, who agreed to the marriage on one condition: Satyavati's children, not Devavrata, must inherit the throne. Shantanu was heartbroken, unable to betray his noble son, and returned to his kingdom, consumed by sadness.

Devavrata noticed his father's profound sorrow and, after Shantanu refused to reveal the cause, investigated the matter himself by speaking to the king's charioteer. Learning of Satyavati and her father's condition, Devavrata went to the Chief of Fishermen. There, he declared his renunciation of the throne, promising that only Satyavati's sons would be king. However, Satyavati's father remained unconvinced, fearing that Devavrata's future children might challenge this promise, given Devavrata's unparalleled skill and potential.

With a smile, Devavrata made a second, even more profound vow: he would remain unmarried and live a life of lifelong celibacy. As he uttered these words, raising his hands to the sky, gods and angels appeared, showering flowers upon him and proclaiming, 'BHISHMA! BHISHMA!' From that moment, Devavrata became known as Bhishma, meaning 'one who has taken an impossible vow and lives up to it.'

Bhishma then brought Satyavati to the kingdom, and she married King Shantanu. They were blessed with two sons, Chitrangada and Vichitraveerya. Vichitraveerya, in turn, had two sons, Pandu and Dhritarashtra, who became the fathers of the Pandavas and Kauravas, respectively.
moral: The story of Bhishma's vow highlights the immense love and sacrifice one can make for their parents. It teaches that true devotion lies in selfless actions, even when they demand great personal renunciation. While we may not face such extreme situations, we can strive to honor and support our parents in our daily lives, recognizing their invaluable role.

pause_points: Shantanu was heartbroken, unable to betray his noble son, and returned to his kingdom, consumed by sadness.
However, Satyavati's father remained unconvinced, fearing that Devavrata's future children might challenge this promise, given Devavrata's unparalleled skill and potential.
From that moment, Devavrata became known as Bhishma, meaning 'one who has taken an impossible vow and lives up to it.'

title: The Story of Markandeya: The Boy Who Conquered Death
narrative_text:
In ancient times, there lived a revered sage named Mrikandu and his devoted wife, Marudvati. Despite their piety, they longed for a child. They prayed fervently to Bhagwaan Shiva, who, pleased with their devotion, appeared before them. Shiva offered them a choice: a hundred foolish sons who would live long lives, or one exceptionally intelligent son whose life would be limited to sixteen years. Without hesitation, Mrikandu chose the single, intelligent son, and Shiva granted his wish.

Soon after, a beautiful and bright boy was born to them, whom they named Markandeya. As he grew, Markandeya displayed remarkable intelligence and a deep understanding of the Vedas and Shastras, earning the respect of all who knew him. However, as his sixteenth birthday approached, a profound sadness enveloped Mrikandu and Marudvati. Markandeya, noticing their sorrow, inquired about its cause.

His father, with a heavy heart, revealed Shiva's prophecy: Markandeya was destined to live only sixteen years, and that time was drawing to a close. Hearing this, Markandeya, with unwavering faith, reassured his parents. He reminded them of Shiva's boundless compassion for his devotees and declared his intention to worship Shiva day and night, confident that the Lord would protect him from the clutches of death. His parents, filled with renewed hope, blessed his resolve.

Markandeya then built a Shiva-Linga by the seashore and immersed himself in intense devotion, chanting hymns and dancing in praise of Shiva. On the fateful day of his sixteenth birthday, as he was deeply engrossed in his prayers, Yama, the formidable God of Death, arrived. Riding his buffalo and wielding his noose, Yama commanded Markandeya to cease his worship, declaring that his time on Earth was over.

Fearless, Markandeya clung tightly to the Shiva-Linga, seeking refuge in his beloved deity. Yama, undeterred, cast his noose, which encircled not only Markandeya but also the Shiva-Linga to which he clung. At that very instant, the Shiva-Linga burst open, and Bhagwaan Shiva emerged in all his divine glory. With a thunderous voice, Shiva rebuked Yama, declaring Markandeya his beloved devotee and proclaiming that he would live forever. Humbled and defeated, Yama retreated.

Markandeya, overwhelmed with gratitude, continued his fervent prayers to Shiva. He returned home to his overjoyed parents, who embraced him with tears of happiness. Markandeya lived a long and virtuous life as a great rishi, a testament to the power of unwavering faith and devotion. The story of Markandeya teaches us that true devotion to the divine can overcome even the most formidable challenges, including death itself.
moral: Unwavering faith and devotion to the divine can overcome seemingly insurmountable obstacles, including the fear of death.

...As his sixteenth birthday approached, a profound sadness enveloped Mrikandu and Marudvati.

title: The Cave That Talked
narrative_text:
There was once a lion in a part of a forest, and his name was Rough-Claw. One day he found nothing whatever to eat in his wanderings, and his throat was pinched by hunger. At sunset he came to a great mountain cave and went in, for he thought: "Surely, some animal will come into this cave during the night. I will hide and wait."

Presently the owner of the cave, a jackal named Curd-Face, came to the door and began to sing: "Cave ahoy! Cave aho-o-oy!" Then after a moment's silence, he continued in the same tone: "Hello! Don't you remember how you and I made an agreement that I was to speak to you when I came back from the world outside, and that you were to sing out to me? But you won't speak to me today. So I am going off to that other cave, which will return my greeting."

Now when he heard this, the lion thought: "I see. This cave always calls out a greeting when the fellow returns. But today, from fear of me, it doesn't say a word. This is natural enough. For

The feet and hands refuse to act
When peril terrifies;
A trembling seizes every limb;
And speech unuttered dies.

"I will myself call out a greeting, which he will follow to its source, so providing me with a dinner."

The lion thereupon called out a greeting. But the cave so magnified the roar that its echo filled the circuit of the horizon, thus terrifying other forest creatures as well, even those far distant. Meanwhile the jackal made off, repeating the stanza:

Joy comes from knowing what to dread,
And sorrow smites the dunderhead:
A long life through, the woods I've walked,
But never heard a cave that talked.
moral: Joy comes from knowing what to dread, And sorrow smites the dunderhead: A long life through, the woods I've walked, But never heard a cave that talked.

pause_points: ...I will hide and wait.
...return my greeting.
...providing me with a dinner.

title: The Story of the Tortoise and the Geese
narrative_text:
There is a pool in South Behar called the 'Pool of the Blue Lotus,' and two Geese had for a long time lived there. They had a friend in the pool who was a Tortoise, and he was known as 'Shelly-neck.' It chanced one evening that the Tortoise overheard some fishermen talking by the water. 'We will stop here to-night,' they said, 'and in the morning we will catch the fish, the tortoises, and such like.' Extremely alarmed at this, the Tortoise repaired to his friends the Geese, and reported the conversation.

'What ever am I to do, Gossips?' he asked.

'The first thing is to be assured of the danger,' said the Geese.

'I am assured,' exclaimed the Tortoise; 'the first thing is to avoid it: don't you know?—

'Time-not-come' and 'Quick-at-peril,' these two fishes 'scaped the net; 'What-will-be-will-be,' he perished, by the fishermen beset.'

'No,' said the Geese,' how was it?' Shelly-neck related:—

It was just such a pool as this, and on the arrival at it of just such men as these fishermen, that three fishes, who had heard their designs, held consultation as to what should be done.

'I shall go to another water,' said 'Time-not-come,' and away he went.

'Why should we leave unless obliged?' asked 'Quick-at-peril.' 'When the thing befalls I shall do the best I can—

'Who deals with bad dilemmas well, is wise. The merchant's wife, with womanly device, Kissed—and denied the kiss—under his eyes.'

'How was that?' asked the other fish. Quick-at-peril related:—

There was a trader in Vikrama-poora, who had a very beautiful wife, and her name was Jewel-bright. The lady was as unfaithful as she was fair, and had chosen for her last lover one of the household servants. Ah! womankind!—

'Sex, that tires of being true, Base and new is brave to you! Like the jungle-cows ye range, Changing food for sake of change.'

Now it befell one day that as Jewel-bright was bestowing a kiss on the mouth of the servant, she was surprised by her husband; and seeing him she ran up hastily and said, 'My lord, here is an impudent varlet! he eats the camphor which I procured for you; I was actually smelling it on his lips as you entered.' The servant catching her meaning, affected offence. 'How can a man stay in a house where the mistress is always smelling one's lips for a little camphor?' he said; and thereat he was for going off, and was only constrained by the good man to stay, after much entreaty. 'Therefore,' said Quick-at-peril, 'I mean to abide here, and make the best I can of what befalls, as she did.'

'Yes, yes,' said What-will-be-will-be, 'we all know

'That which will not be will not be, and what is to be will be:— Why not drink this easy physic, antidote of misery?'

'When the morning came, the net was thrown, and both the fishes inclosed. Quick-at-peril, on being drawn up, feigned himself dead; and upon the fisherman's laying him aside, he leaped off again into the water. As to What-will-be-will-be, he was seized and forthwith dispatched.—And that,' concluded the Tortoise, 'is why I wish to devise some plan of escape.'

'It might be compassed if you could go elsewhere,' said the Geese, 'but how can you get across the ground?'

'Can't you take me through the air?' asked the Tortoise.

'Impossible!' said the Geese.

'Not at all!' replied the Tortoise; 'you shall hold a stick across in your bills, and I will hang on to it by my mouth—and thus you can readily convey me,'

'It is feasible,' observed the Geese, 'but remember,

'Wise men their plans revolve, lest ill befall; The Herons gained a friend, and so, lost all.'

'How came that about?' asked the Tortoise. The Geese related:—

Among the mountains of the north there is one named Eagle-cliff, and near it, upon a fig-tree, a flock of Herons had their residence. At the foot of the tree, in a hollow, there lived a serpent; and he was constantly devouring the nestlings of the Herons. Loud were the complaints of the parent birds, until an old Heron thus advised them:—'You should bring some fishes from the pool, and lay them one by one in a line from the hole of yonder Mongoose to the hollow where the Serpent lives. The Mongoose will find him when it comes after the fish, and if it finds him it will kill him.' The advice seemed good, and was acted upon; but in killing the Snake the Mongoose overheard the cry of the young Herons; and climbing the tree daily, he devoured all that the Snake had left. Therefore,' concluded the Geese, 'do we bid you look well into your plan: if you should open your mouth, for instance, as we carry you, you will drop and be killed.'

'Am I a fool,' cried the Tortoise, 'to open my mouth? Not I! Come now, convey me!'

'Thereupon the Geese took up the stick; the Tortoise held fast with his mouth, and away they flew. The country people, observing this strange sight, ran after.

'Ho! ho!' cried one, 'look at the flying Tortoise!'

'When he falls we'll cook and eat him here,' said another.

'No; let us take him home for dinner!' cried a third.

'We can light a fire by the pool, and eat him,' said the first.

'The Tortoise heard these unkind remarks in a towering passion. 'Eat me!—eat ashes!' he exclaimed, opening his mouth—and down he fell directly, and was caught by the countrymen.—Said I not well,' concluded the Goose-Minister, 'that to scorn counsel is to seek destruction?'

'You have well said,' replied King Silver-sides, disconsolately.
moral: To scorn counsel is to seek destruction. He who talks too much, perishes.

title: The Quarrel of the Quails
narrative_text:
Once upon a time many quails lived together in a forest. The wisest of them all was their leader. A man lived near the forest and earned his living by catching quails and selling them. Day after day he listened to the note of the leader calling the quails. By and by this man, the fowler, was able to call the quails together. Hearing the note the quails thought it was their leader who called.

When they were crowded together, the fowler threw his net over them and off he went into the town, where he soon sold all the quails that he had caught.

The wise leader saw the plan of the fowler for catching the quails. He called the birds to him and said, "This fowler is carrying away so many of us, we must put a stop to it. I have thought of a plan; it is this: The next time the fowler throws a net over you, each of you must put your head through one of the little holes in the net. Then all of you together must fly away to the nearest thorn-bush. You can leave the net on the thorn-bush and be free yourselves."

The quails said that was a very good plan and they would try it the next time the fowler threw the net over them.

The very next day the fowler came and called them together. Then he threw the net over them. The quails lifted the net and flew away with it to the nearest thorn-bush where they left it. They flew back to their leader to tell him how well his plan had worked.

The fowler was busy until evening getting his net off the thorns and he went home empty-handed. The next day the same thing happened, and the next. His wife was angry because he did not bring home any money, but the fowler said, "The fact is those quails are working together now. The moment my net is over them, off they fly with it, leaving it on a thorn-bush. As soon as the quails begin to quarrel I shall be able to catch them."

Not long after this, one of the quails in alighting on their feeding ground, trod by accident on another's head. "Who trod on my head?" angrily cried the second. "I did; but I didn't mean to. Don't be angry," said the first quail, but the second quail was angry and said mean things. Then the first quail was angry and said mean things. Soon all the quails were quarreling. They forgot the wise leader's plan. They did not fly away together. The fowler caught them all.

And so it is that when people quarrel, they are easily overcome.
moral: Unity is strength. When people quarrel, they are easily overcome.

title: The Pot Full of Wit
alt_titles: The Farmer's Daughter and the Pot of Milk
narrative_text:
Akbar, in a fit of anger, once banished his wise minister Birbal from his kingdom. Birbal, accepting the decree, traveled to a distant village, adopted a new identity, and began working as a farmer. After several weeks, Emperor Akbar began to miss Birbal's counsel and ordered his soldiers to find him and bring him back to the capital. Despite their extensive search across the kingdom, the soldiers were unable to locate Birbal.

Determined to find his trusted advisor, Akbar devised a clever plan. He sent out a royal proclamation throughout the kingdom, stating that the head of each village must send him a pot filled with 'wit.' The proclamation further stated that if a village head could not provide a pot of wit, they were to fill the pot with gold and diamonds instead.

Akbar believed that this unique challenge was the only way to entice Birbal back to his court, knowing Birbal's penchant for solving complex problems.

The villagers in Birbal's new home were bewildered by the Emperor's demand, wondering how one could possibly fill a pot with something as abstract as wit. It was then that Birbal, still disguised as a farmer, stepped forward to offer his assistance. He conceived a brilliant solution to meet the king's unusual request. He carefully placed a small watermelon into the pot, ensuring it remained attached to its vine. Daily, he nurtured the watermelon, watering it diligently until it grew large enough to completely fill the pot, occupying all available space.

Once the watermelon had reached the desired size, Birbal detached it from its vine and dispatched the pot to the Emperor. Along with the pot, he sent a specific instruction: 'Your Majesty can discover the wit within this pot if you carefully remove the watermelon without cutting it.' Upon receiving the pot and the accompanying message, Akbar immediately recognized the unparalleled wisdom and ingenuity behind the solution. He realized that only Birbal could have conceived such a clever response. Without delay, Akbar himself journeyed to the village and personally escorted Birbal back to the capital, where he was reinstated to his rightful position.
moral: Even the toughest questions can be answered cleverly if you think hard.

...to entice Birbal back to his court, knowing Birbal's penchant for solving complex problems.
...completely fill the pot, occupying all available space.
...only Birbal could have conceived such a clever response.

safety_notes: The story is emotionally warm and culturally respectful, suitable for elderly listeners. No potentially distressing content.

title: Mahabharata: The story of Ekalavya
narrative_text: In the great forest, there lived a young prince of the Nishadas, the hunter tribes, named Ekalavya. He was the son of Hiranyadhanus, and from a young age, he harbored a deep desire to become the greatest archer in the world. He had heard of the great teacher Dronacharya, the preceptor of the Kuru princes, and he journeyed to Hastinapura with the hope of becoming his disciple. When Ekalavya approached Drona and humbly requested to be accepted as a student, Drona, bound by his duty to the royal family, refused. He could not teach a Nishada, who was not of the royal lineage. Heartbroken but not defeated, Ekalavya returned to the forest. He fashioned a clay image of Dronacharya, and with unwavering faith, he began to worship the statue as his guru. Day and night, he practiced archery before the clay image, his devotion and determination his only guides. Years passed, and Ekalavya became an archer of extraordinary skill, his arrows flying with unerring precision. One day, the Pandava and Kaurava princes, led by Dronacharya, came to the forest to hunt. Their hunting dog, wandering ahead, came upon Ekalavya, who was practicing his art. The dog, startled by the sight of the dark-skinned Nishada, began to bark incessantly. Annoyed by the disturbance, Ekalavya, with a flick of his wrist, shot seven arrows into the dog's mouth in such a way that it could not close its mouth to bark, yet was not harmed. The dog ran back to the princes, who were amazed at this incredible feat of archery. They searched for the archer and found Ekalavya, who introduced himself as a student of Dronacharya. Arjuna, Drona's favorite student, was deeply troubled. Drona had promised him that he would make him the greatest archer in the world. Seeing Ekalavya's skill, Arjuna feared that Drona's promise would be broken. Drona, too, was astonished. He approached Ekalavya and asked, "If you are my student, then you must give me my gurudakshina, my teacher's fee." Ekalavya, overjoyed to be acknowledged by his master, replied, "Ask, O master, and I shall give you whatever you desire." Drona, with a heavy heart, said, "I ask for your right thumb." Without a moment's hesitation, Ekalavya, took his knife and cut off his right thumb and offered it to Drona. With his thumb gone, Ekalavya could no longer draw a bow with the same skill. Drona, though saddened by his own action, had fulfilled his promise to Arjuna. But the story of Ekalavya and his supreme sacrifice for his guru became a timeless tale of devotion, determination, and the harsh realities of the world.
moral: True devotion and determination can overcome any obstacle, but the world is not always fair.

performance_notes.opening_line: Let me tell you a story from the great epic, Mahabharata, a story of a young boy whose dedication knew no bounds.
pause_points: ...when he saw the dog...
...when he asked for his thumb...
...and so, Ekalavya could no longer shoot with the same skill...

safety_notes: The story contains an act of self-mutilation which might be disturbing to some listeners, but it is a pivotal part of the narrative and is presented as an act of supreme devotion.

title: The Birth of Kartikeya
narrative_text:
The birth of Kartikeya is a tale woven with divine intervention and cosmic necessity. According to the Mahābhārata, the story begins with the falling of Śiva’s semen into the fire. The Fire-god, Agni, then became pregnant with this divine seed. Agni subsequently transferred the foetus to Gaṅgā by Niyoga, a ritualistic appointment. Gaṅgā, in turn, aborted the foetus in Śara-grass on a mountain, where it continued to grow. Six sonless Kṛttikās (Pleiades) found the child and claimed him as their own. Skanda, as he was then known, developed six mouths to suckle the milk of his six mothers.

The Skandapurāṇa elaborates on this narrative, adding several key developments. The sexual intercourse between Śiva and Pārvatī was so intense that it created Pralaya, a destruction of the world of mobile and immobile beings. The gods, concerned by this, deputed Agni to disrupt their union. Agni secretly entered Śiva’s harem and loudly demanded alms. Infuriated by this interruption, Śaṅkara (Śiva) rushed to kill Agni. Pārvatī intervened, but then gave Śiva’s semen in Agni’s joined palms and made him drink it, cursing him to be omnivorous.

Agni became pregnant, and through him, all the Devas, who receive food through Agni in sacrifices, also became pregnant with Śiva’s semen, experiencing unbearable pain. The Devas, led by Viṣṇu, requested relief from their torment. Śiva relented and asked all Devas, except Agni (the primary culprit), to vomit the semen. This vomited semen transformed into a mountain of gold.

Agni, still pregnant, sought relief. Śiva advised him to transmit the semen to women. The next morning, at dawn, the wives of seven sages performed their bath in the river. Feeling cold, they went to warm themselves near the fire, despite being prohibited by Vasiṣṭha’s wife Arundhatī. Agni then transferred Śiva’s semen to them. Their husbands, discovering their wives pregnant by another, drove them out of their homes. Homeless, they ascended to the sky and became the Kṛttikās (Pleiades). However, out of shame, they aborted Śiva’s semen on top of Himālaya. This semen flowed down into the Gaṅgā and was surrounded by bamboos, eventually developing into a child with six faces on the bank of the Gaṅgā. This child was Kartikeya, the war god, destined to lead the gods to victory against the demons.
moral: The story highlights the theme of divine intervention for cosmic balance, the power of penance, and the complex interplay of divine will and earthly events.

performance_notes.opening_line: In the celestial realms, a tale of immense power and purpose unfolded, leading to the birth of a divine warrior.
pause_points: ...Infuriated at this interruption, Śaṅkara (Śiva) rushed to kill Agni.
...This vomited semen transformed into a mountain of gold.
...Homeless, they ascended to the sky and became the Kṛttikās (Pleiades).

safety_notes: The story contains themes of divine conception and abandonment, which should be handled with sensitivity. The narrative is generally suitable for elderly listeners, focusing on mythological aspects rather than explicit details.

title: The Lion and the Rabbit
alt_titles: The Foolish Lion and The Clever Rabbit
The Cunning Hare and the Witless Lion
narrative_text:
In a certain forest a greedy lion was killing all the animals and eating them. The animals made an agreement with the lion. They promised to send him one animal a day to eat, if he would promise not to kill any of the other animals. The lion agreed.
The rabbit's turn came. The rabbit seemed unafraid. He was even late to the lion's den and the lion was furious. "Why do you dare to come late?" the lion roared.
The rabbit explained that on his way to the den, he'd been stopped by a mighty beast—another lion. The rabbit went on to say that the other lion had claimed to be the King of the forest. He'd said, "That numskull of a lion to whom you are going is a sneak thief. Whichever of us proves the stronger shall be King."
When the lion heard this he told the rabbit to lead him to the other lion. The rabbit led him to a well and told the lion that the other lion was hiding in the well.
The lion, being a dreadful fool, looked in the well and saw his own reflection in the water and gave out a great roar. From the well came a roar twice as loud, because of the echo. This the lion heard. He decided that his rival was very powerful, hurled himself down the well and met his death.
moral: Intelligence is power. But where could power and folly make a pair? The rabbit played upon his pride. To fool him: the lion died.

"Why do you dare to come late?" the lion roared.
"Whichever of us proves the stronger shall be King."
The rabbit led him to a well and told the lion that the other lion was hiding in the well.

title: The Bird with Two Heads
narrative_text:
Once, on the banks of a serene lake, lived a peculiar bird named Bharunda. This bird possessed a single body but was adorned with two distinct heads. One day, as the Bharunda bird roamed, one of its heads discovered a beautiful, red-golden fruit. Filled with delight, this head exclaimed, 'What a magnificent fruit! It must be a divine gift meant solely for me.' It then proceeded to devour the fruit with immense pleasure, praising its exquisite taste.

The second head, observing this, politely requested, 'My dear, please allow me to partake in this wonderful fruit you speak so highly of.' However, the first head, consumed by selfishness, chuckled and replied, 'We share the same stomach. Regardless of which head consumes the fruit, it will nourish our shared body. Furthermore, since I was the one who discovered it, I have every right to enjoy it myself.' The second head, deeply hurt by this refusal and the first head's self-centeredness, fell silent in disappointment.

Some days later, as the bird continued its wanderings, the second head came across some fruits from a poisonous tree. With a vengeful glint, it declared to the first head, 'You once insulted me by refusing to share that delicious fruit. Now, I shall eat these poisonous fruits to avenge that slight!' The first head, alarmed, pleaded, 'Please, do not eat those fruits! They are poisonous. We share a single stomach, and if you consume them, both of us will suffer greatly.'

Mocking the first head's plea, the second head retorted, 'Silence! I found these fruits, and therefore, I have the right to eat them.' Despite the first head's desperate cries, the second head proceeded to eat the poisonous fruits. As the poison entered their shared stomach, the Bharunda bird suffered terribly, a direct consequence of the second head's vengeful act.

Indeed, the wise often say: 'Union is strength.' This tale serves as a poignant reminder that selfishness and discord within a shared existence can lead to collective suffering.
moral: Union is strength. Selfishness and discord can lead to collective suffering.

...praising its exquisite taste.
...fell silent in disappointment.
...suffered terribly, a direct consequence of the second head's vengeful act.
