title: The Elephant and the Dog
narrative_text:
Once upon a time a Dog used to go into the stable where the king’s Elephant lived. At first the Dog went there to get the food that was left after the Elephant had finished eating. Day after day the Dog went to the stable, waiting around for bits to eat. But by and by the Elephant and the Dog came to be great friends. Then the Elephant began to share his food with the Dog, and they ate together. When the Elephant slept, his friend the Dog slept beside him. When the Elephant felt like playing, he would catch the Dog in his trunk and swing him to and fro. Neither the Dog nor the Elephant was quite happy unless the other was nearby. One day a farmer saw the Dog and said to the Elephant-keeper: “I will buy that Dog. He looks good-tempered, and I see that he is smart. How much do you want for the Dog?”

The Elephant-keeper did not care for the Dog, and he did want some money just then. So he asked a fair price, and the farmer paid it and took the Dog away to the country.

The king’s Elephant missed the Dog and did not care to eat when his friend was not there to share the food. When the time came for the Elephant to bathe, he would not bathe. The next day again the Elephant would not eat, and he would not bathe. The third day, when the Elephant would neither eat nor bathe, the king was told about it. The king sent for his chief servant, saying, “Go to the stable and find out why the Elephant is acting in this way.”

The chief servant went to the stable and looked the Elephant all over. Then he said to the Elephant-keeper: “There seems to be nothing the matter with this Elephant’s body, but why does he look so sad? Has he lost a playmate?”

“Yes,” said the keeper, “there was a Dog who ate and slept and played with the Elephant. The Dog went away three days ago.”

“Do you know where the Dog is now?” asked the chief servant. “No, I do not,” said the keeper. Then the chief servant went back to the king and said, “The Elephant is not sick, but he is lonely without his friend, the Dog.” “Where is the Dog?” asked the king. “A farmer took him away, so the Elephant-keeper says,” said the chief servant. “No one knows where the farmer lives.” “Very well,” said the king. “I will send word all over the country, asking the man who bought this Dog to turn him loose. I will give him back as much as he paid for the Dog.” When the farmer who had bought the Dog heard this, he turned him loose. The Dog ran back as fast as ever he could go to the Elephant’s stable. The Elephant was so glad to see the Dog that he picked him up with his trunk and put him on his head. Then he put him down again. When the Elephant-keeper brought food, the Elephant watched the Dog as he ate, and then took his own food.

All the rest of their lives the Elephant and the Dog lived together.
moral: True friendship transcends differences and brings joy and comfort. The absence of a dear friend can cause deep sadness, highlighting the profound impact of companionship.

title: Akbar-Birbal: The Story of the Three Questions
narrative_text:
In the grand court of Emperor Akbar, there lived a wise and witty minister named Birbal, who was greatly admired by the King. This admiration, however, stirred deep jealousy in the heart of a particular courtier who secretly coveted Birbal's esteemed position as chief minister. One day, as Akbar lauded Birbal's intelligence, the envious courtier, unable to contain his resentment, challenged Birbal's wisdom. He declared that if Birbal could answer three profound questions, he would concede Birbal's superior intellect. Always eager to witness Birbal's sharp wit, Emperor Akbar readily accepted the challenge.

The three questions posed by the courtier were:

1. "How many stars are there in the sky?"
2. "Where is the exact center of the Earth?"
3. "How many men and how many women are there in the world?"

King Akbar immediately presented these questions to Birbal, stating that failure to answer them would result in his resignation as chief minister.

Birbal, unfazed, calmly addressed the first question. He ordered a hairy sheep to be brought before the court and declared, "There are precisely as many stars in the sky as there are hairs on this sheep's body. My dear courtier, you are welcome to count them yourself if you doubt my answer."

For the second question, Birbal drew a few lines on the court floor and firmly planted an iron rod into the ground at their intersection. "This very spot," he announced with conviction, "is the center of the Earth. Should the courtier have any reservations, he is free to measure it for himself."

Finally, addressing the third and most intriguing question, Birbal offered a thoughtful response. "To accurately count the number of men and women in the world would be a challenging task," he began, "as there are certain individuals, much like our esteemed courtier friend here, who cannot be easily categorized as either. Therefore, only if all such ambiguous specimens were removed, could one hope to achieve an exact count."

The court erupted in laughter, and the jealous courtier, left speechless by Birbal's ingenious and humorous replies, had no choice but to accept his defeat. King Akbar, once again, was immensely pleased with Birbal's unparalleled wisdom and wit.
moral: There is Always a Way to solve problems, no matter how challenging they seem.

title: The Story of Tenali Raman and the Cat That Drank Milk
alt_titles: Tenali Raman and the Clever Cat
narrative_text: Long long ago there was a man called Tenali Raman. He was minister to a great king called Krishna Deva Raya. He was supposed to be very clever. Once in that city there was a lot of problems due to rats. Rats were everywhere. They were eating all sorts of books and papers. They also used to make holes in wooden shelves and also eat rice. Sometimes they even nibbled the ears of little clever girls. Everybody knows that Cats Kill rats. But there were so many rats in the city but very few cats. So the king asked every house in the city to grow cats. But cats need milk to drink. Most of the houses did not have cows. So the king gave cows to every house and asked them to grow them too. All people were happy and used to give cats a lot of milk to drink. Tenali Raman was a lazy man and liked to drink milk. So the first day he boiled the milk and poured it in a pan. He gave the piping hot milk to the cat. The cat, which was hungry, came and sipped the milk from the pan. Since the milk was hot it burnt its mouth. The cat ran away. The next day and the next, Tenali Raman did the same thing. After a few days he stopped giving milk to the cat and started drinking it himself. Tenali Raman’s cat was lean and hungry and all other cats were stout and happy. One day the king wanted to see all the cats given by him and give a prize to the fat cat. He was very angry with Tenali Raman for not growing his cat properly. Then Tenali Raman told the king that he was not lucky since his cat did not drink any milk. The king wanted to put Tenali Raman in Jail for telling a lie and wanted him to prove what he said. Then Tenali Raman brought the pan of milk and his cat. The cat on seeing the milk ran away. It thought that it was being given again hot milk. The king pitied Tenali Raman and let him away. The clever Tenali Raman lived happily forever drinking lots and lots of milk.
moral: Wit and cleverness can help you out of difficult situations.

title: The Wife Who Rescued Her Husband
narrative_text:
In a small Kashmiri village, a wealthy merchant and his wife often argued about their son, Buddhu, whom they considered foolish. The merchant believed Buddhu was entirely hopeless, citing an incident where he fed chickens egg sandwiches. His wife, however, defended their son, suggesting they marry him off so he could inherit their wealth. The merchant, skeptical, agreed to a final test: Buddhu was given three copper coins. With the first, he could buy anything; the second, he was to throw into the river; and with the third, he had to buy items for eating, drinking, chewing, sowing, and feeding cows.

Buddhu easily spent the first coin on a hearty breakfast. When it came to the second coin, he hesitated at the river, worried about how he would fulfill the third task without it. A clever ironsmith's daughter, Chatur, observed his dilemma. Upon hearing his predicament, she advised him not to throw away the second coin, assuring him his businessman father secretly hoped he wouldn't. For the third coin, she suggested he buy a watermelon, explaining how it met all the father's requirements: juice to drink, pulp to eat, rind to gnaw, seeds to sow, and the shell to feed cows. Buddhu, impressed by her brilliance, followed her advice.

He returned home, presenting the watermelon as his own idea. His mother was overjoyed, but his father, still suspicious, pressed him until Buddhu confessed Chatur's help. Despite this, his mother insisted that Buddhu had completed the task, and it was time for him to marry. She suggested Chatur, recognizing her intelligence and support as ideal qualities for a wife. Buddhu's parents arranged the marriage, and Chatur's parents, seeing the advantageous match, agreed.

However, Buddhu's foolishness resurfaced after marriage. Influenced by resentful friends, he tried to assert dominance over Chatur. On their wedding night, he demanded she wash all the dishes from the hundred-guest wedding. Chatur, having anticipated his friends' bad advice, cleverly invoked a superstition: terrible things would happen if she did housework in the first week of marriage. Buddhu, surprisingly, believed her. She also knew about a tradition where new brides visited their parents after the first week, further delaying his attempts to 'crush her spirit.'

Later, Buddhu's father, fulfilling a promise, gave him a ship, money, and fidget spinners to trade. Buddhu, despite his foolishness, initially found success due to the high demand for fidget spinners. However, his poor haggling skills led him to sell them at throwaway prices. After selling out, he decided to gamble his profits, and eventually his ship, at a local gambling establishment. He lost everything to Kapati, a poker expert who cheated with the help of her well-trained cat. Buddhu was then imprisoned for failing to pay his debts.

In jail, Buddhu wrote two letters, one to his wife and one to his father, but accidentally switched the envelopes. His father received a letter filled with made-up tales of wealth and palaces, while Chatur received the truth: Buddhu's plea for help, detailing his miserable jail conditions. Chatur, realizing she had to intervene, disguised herself and traveled to the neighboring country. She bribed officials to learn about Kapati's cheating methods.

During a game with Kapati, Chatur released a mouse, distracting Kapati's cat and leveling the playing field. Kapati, without her cat's help, proved to be a terrible player and lost everything to Chatur, including Buddhu's debts and ship. Chatur then freed Buddhu and other prisoners, offering them a chance to sail back home on the ship she had won. Buddhu, not recognizing his disguised wife, eagerly agreed when she offered him the captaincy, hoping to impress his wife with his 'earnings.'

Chatur returned home separately, waiting to welcome Buddhu. Despite her initial intention to forgive and forget, Buddhu's continued domineering attitude and bragging about his 'success' infuriated her. When he began ordering her to do menial chores in front of his parents, Chatur, with all the evidence she had carefully collected (his prisoner clothes, gambling card, Kapati's affidavit, and debt records), exposed his foolishness and her role in his rescue. Buddhu and his parents realized the extent of his folly.
moral: Do not try to dominate or disrespect those who have helped you, especially if they are more capable. True wisdom lies in recognizing and valuing the intelligence of others, rather than succumbing to foolish pride or external influences.

performance_notes.opening_line: In a small Kashmiri village, there lived a merchant and his wife, who often found themselves in disagreement over their son, Buddhu.
pause_points: ...Buddhu easily spent the first coin on a hearty breakfast.
...Buddhu, impressed by her brilliance, followed her advice.
...However, Buddhu's foolishness resurfaced after marriage.
...Buddhu was then imprisoned for failing to pay his debts.
...Kapati, without her cat's help, proved to be a terrible player and lost everything to Chatur...
...Buddhu and his parents realized the extent of his folly.

safety_notes: The story contains themes of marital disagreement, gambling, and imprisonment, but these are resolved positively through the wife's cleverness. The 'foolish' nature of the husband is a central theme, but it is presented in a way that highlights the wife's intelligence and resourcefulness. Suitable for elderly listeners, as it emphasizes wisdom and the consequences of foolish pride.

title: The Tale Of The Tiger And The Crab
narrative_text:
Once upon a time, a tiger and a crab met in the forest. The crab, with a beaming face, asked the tiger where he was going. The tiger replied he was moving dens and asked the crab the same. The crab explained he lived nearby and was alone in the world. The tiger, feeling a similar loneliness, befriended the crab, and they settled to live near each other. They lived in peace, with the tiger hunting deer and sharing the meat fairly with the crab. Other animals kept their distance, fearing the tiger.

One day, the crab, tired of meat, suggested they become cultivators and grow vegetables. The tiger agreed. The crab proposed that the tiger fetch rice plants from the village while he ploughed a nearby field. The crab began ploughing, assisted by the tiger, who brought manikimadhuri rice seedlings and planted them. As the rice bloomed, the crab suggested growing vegetables too, asking the tiger to bring various vegetable seedlings, including brinjal plants, from the village. Just as the tiger left, the crab called out, reminding him not to forget chili plants, which go well with acid curry.

When the paddy was ripe, the tiger asked the crab if he wanted the top or bottom end. The clever crab chose the top end, securing the actual paddy, while the tiger was left with the stubble. Later, when plucking aubergines, the tiger asked if the crab wanted the seed or the plants. The crab chose the seed, thus getting the brinjals, leaving the plants for the tiger. After gathering their produce, the tiger invited the crab to dinner. The tiger cooked the stubble and brinjal plants as best he could, but the crab, unable to eat such fare, merely pretended to eat and left. Despite this, the crab continued to enjoy full meat breakfasts and dinners with the tiger, never returning the invitation.

The tiger, feeling slighted, eventually asked the crab why he had never been invited to dinner. The crab, feigning humility, explained he had been thinking of it but lacked space in his small dwelling to properly honor the tiger. He then invited the tiger for 'pot luck' the next morning, which the tiger gladly accepted.

The next morning, the crab boiled his manikimadhuri rice and cooked a delicious brinjal curry. When the tiger arrived, the crab asked him to sit outside the entrance, citing his cramped space. As the crab served, the tiger, with his large appetite, finished the small portions in two mouthfuls. The crab, fearing the tiger might eat him if unsatisfied, devised a plan. He told the tiger he was tired of going up and down with dishes and suggested the tiger put his tail into the hole, and he would tie the dishes to it. The unsuspecting tiger complied. As soon as his tail was in the hole, the crab squeezed it so hard that the tiger could not extricate himself. The more the tiger struggled, the tighter the crab squeezed. The tiger roared in pain.

Fortunately, a farmer (the only son of a widow) was hoeing nearby. The tiger, seeing the farmer about to flee in fear, entreated him for help, promising not to eat him and to bring him a deer every day if he saved him. The farmer, emboldened by the assurance, chopped off the tiger's tail. The tiger thanked him and whispered a warning: he would bring a deer daily, but if the farmer ever told anyone, the tiger would carry him off, no matter where he was.

The tiger kept his word, supplying the farmer with a deer every day. After some time, the farmer fell ill. His mother consulted a fortune-telling woman, who advised a feast for friends and relatives would aid his recovery. The farmer, informing his mother, ordered the tiger to bring two deer for the feast, which the tiger did.

On the day of the feast, as guests were seated, they found themselves short one banana leaf. A guest went to the garden to fetch one and discovered a huge pile of bones, as high as a small hill. Without cutting a leaf, he returned and told the other guests that their host seemed to be eating forbidden food, urging them to see for themselves. The guests, upon seeing the bones, refused to eat until the farmer explained. The farmer assured them the animals were not forbidden by the Shastras but hesitated to reveal more, fearing the tiger's threat. The guests insisted, promising to protect him with spears and lances. Seeing no other option, the farmer knelt and began to narrate his tale, surrounded by the armed guests. As soon as he finished, the tiger sprang from nowhere and carried him off, confounding the guests, who searched in vain.

The tiger carried the boy to a dense forest. As luck would have it, the boy felt hungry, and his stomach rumbled. The tiger asked about the rumbling. The quick-witted farmer replied that when he was a child, his mother had given him 120 crabs to eat, and three score had come out long ago, with the remaining three score about to emerge. At the mere mention of 'crabs,' the tiger shrank in terror, remembering his painful encounter. He thought if one crab could do so much, what would three score do? He immediately left the farmer in the forest and fled for his life. Thus, the farmer saved himself from the tiger's clutches.
moral: Cleverness and wit can overcome brute strength. It is also important to be cautious of those who are stronger than you, and to use your intelligence to outsmart them.

pause_points: They lived in peace, with the tiger hunting deer and sharing the meat fairly with the crab.
The crab, fearing the tiger might eat him if unsatisfied, devised a plan.
The tiger roared in pain.
As soon as he finished, the tiger sprang from nowhere and carried him off, confounding the guests, who searched in vain.
At the mere mention of 'crabs,' the tiger shrank in terror, remembering his painful encounter.

safety_notes: The story contains themes of deception and a minor act of violence (chopping off the tiger's tail), but these are presented in a folkloric context and are not graphic. The tiger's fear of crabs could be seen as humorous. Overall, suitable for elderly listeners.

title: The Devotion of Surdas: A Blind Poet's Vision of Krishna
narrative_text:
In the sacred land of Braj, where Lord Krishna spent his childhood, lived a saintly poet named Surdas. Though born blind, his inner vision was sharper than any ordinary sight, for it was solely focused on the divine form of Krishna. His life was a testament to unwavering devotion, and his melodious bhajans, sung in the sweet Braj Bhasha, painted vivid pictures of Krishna's playful exploits and divine beauty.

One day, as Surdas wandered lost in his devotion, he accidentally fell into a deep well. Alone and helpless, he cried out to his beloved Lord Krishna with all his heart. His prayers, pure and fervent, reached the heavens, and a miracle unfolded. Lord Krishna, accompanied by Radha, appeared before him and pulled him out of the well. In his blind state, Surdas could not see them, but as his hand reached out, he grasped Radha's anklets. Radha, with a gentle smile, asked him to release her anklets. Surdas, recognizing the divine presence through touch and the celestial aura, humbly replied that being blind, he could not confirm her identity. Moved by his profound devotion, Krishna blessed him with temporary sight. For a fleeting moment, Surdas beheld the glorious forms of both Krishna and Radha, a vision that transcended all earthly beauty.

After this divine darshan, Krishna, pleased with his devotee, offered Surdas a boon. To everyone's surprise, Surdas, with tears of joy streaming from his eyes, expressed his wish to remain blind. He declared that having seen the most beautiful forms of Krishna and Radha, he no longer desired to see anything else with those eyes. His heart was content, and his inner vision was now eternally imprinted with the divine image.

Surdas's unique ability to perceive the divine was further demonstrated on another occasion. Vitthalnathji, the son of Surdas's guru Vallabhacharya, wished to test the depth of Surdas's spiritual insight. He instructed his own son, Giridharlalji, to adorn the murti (idol) of Krishna with nothing but a garland of pearls. When Surdas was brought before the deity, he burst into laughter. With his inner eye, he 'saw' the murti and immediately composed a bhajan, singing, 'See the Lord (Hari) is stark naked, He looks beautiful in every limb, Clothed in pearls, His nakedness adds to the beauty in every limb, Surdas joins the maidens of Vraj in the laughter.' This incident left everyone astonished and convinced of Surdas's extraordinary ability to perceive his beloved Lord, even without physical sight.

Through his life and his countless devotional songs, Surdas taught that true devotion transcends physical limitations and worldly desires. His unwavering love for Krishna granted him a divine vision that saw beyond the superficial, revealing the true essence of the Lord's beauty and grace. His story continues to inspire countless devotees, reminding them that a pure heart and steadfast faith can lead to the most profound spiritual experiences.
moral: True devotion and unwavering faith can grant one a divine inner vision that transcends physical limitations and worldly desires, leading to profound spiritual experiences and contentment.

title: The Prince, the Serpent, and the Clever Wife
narrative_text:
Long ago, there lived a benevolent King Hima, who had a beloved son. However, a grim prophecy loomed over the young prince's life: astrologers foretold that he would meet his end on the fourth day of his marriage, bitten by a serpent. As the fateful day approached, the prince's newlywed wife, a woman of remarkable courage and wisdom, resolved to defy destiny. She refused to let her husband sleep that night. Instead, she meticulously arranged a towering pile of gold coins and precious ornaments at the entrance of their chamber. Beside this shimmering heap, she lit numerous oil lamps, their flames dancing to create a dazzling, blinding glow. Throughout the night, she kept her husband awake by regaling him with enchanting stories and singing sweet songs. 

As predicted, Yamraj, the Lord of Death, arrived at their doorstep, disguised as a venomous serpent. But as he slithered closer, the intense brilliance emanating from the lamps and the gold coins overwhelmed his vision. Temporarily blinded, he could not enter the chamber directly. He ascended the pile of gold, waiting for an opportune moment. Yet, as he waited, he became captivated by the princess's captivating tales and melodious songs. Time slipped away unnoticed. When the auspicious moment for the prince's predicted demise had passed, Yamraj, unable to fulfill his grim task, silently departed. Thus, the young prince's life was spared, all thanks to his wife's ingenuity and devotion. This miraculous event is why Dhanteras is celebrated, with people buying gold and precious metals, and offering prayers to Lord Kuber and Goddess Lakshmi, symbolizing protection from untimely death and inviting prosperity.
moral: With cleverness, devotion, and courage, one can overcome even the gravest of fates. It also teaches the importance of vigilance and the protective power of light and wealth.

...he became captivated by the princess's captivating tales and melodious songs.
...Thus, the young prince's life was spared, all thanks to his wife's ingenuity and devotion.

safety_notes: None. The story is culturally respectful and suitable for elderly listeners.

title: Mahabharata: The Story of Karna's Birth and Abandonment
narrative_text:
In ancient times, Princess Pritha, later known as Kunti, served the revered sage Durvasa with unwavering devotion. Pleased by her dedicated service, the sage bestowed upon her a divine boon: a powerful mantra that would enable her to invoke any god and bear a child by them, all while retaining her maidenhood. Driven by youthful curiosity and a desire to test the efficacy of this extraordinary gift, Kunti, still unmarried, decided to invoke Surya, the radiant Sun God. 

As Kunti chanted the sacred mantra, Surya appeared before her in all his celestial glory. Overwhelmed by his dazzling presence, Kunti, realizing the gravity of her impulsive act, pleaded with him, explaining that she had merely wished to test the mantra's power and sought his forgiveness. However, Surya, bound by the divine law that an invocation could not be in vain, gently but firmly insisted that his presence must bear fruit. Thus, Kunti, despite her unmarried status, conceived a son by the Sun God.

With a heavy heart and consumed by fear of societal condemnation and the potential wrath of her family, Kunti secretly gave birth to a magnificent son. The infant was born with natural armor (Kavacha) and brilliant earrings (Kundala), divine attributes that marked his extraordinary origin. In a desperate attempt to conceal her secret and protect her honor, Kunti, with profound sorrow, placed the newborn in a wicker basket and set him adrift upon the sacred waters of the river Ganga.

The basket, carrying the fated child, floated downstream until it was discovered by Adhiratha, a kind-hearted charioteer in the service of King Dhritarashtra, and his devoted wife, Radha. Childless themselves, Adhiratha and Radha were overjoyed by this miraculous find. They lovingly adopted the infant, naming him Vasusena, though he would later become renowned as Karna. They raised him as their own, showering him with affection and care, completely unaware of his true divine parentage and the royal lineage he carried.

Karna grew into a formidable warrior, possessing unparalleled skill in archery and all martial arts. Despite his innate talents and noble character, he faced constant discrimination and scorn due to his perceived low birth as a Suta (charioteer's son). His life became a poignant saga of unacknowledged greatness, marked by profound challenges, sacrifices, and an unwavering determination to prove his worth in a world that often judged him unfairly. The secret of his birth and his abandonment cast a long shadow over his destiny, shaping him into one of the most complex and tragic figures in the Mahabharata.
moral: The story of Karna serves as a powerful reminder of the far-reaching consequences of choices made out of fear and societal pressure. It underscores the enduring power of destiny and the profound importance of recognizing an individual's true worth based on their character, actions, and virtues, rather than their birth or circumstances. Furthermore, it highlights themes of loyalty, sacrifice, and the tragic repercussions of unacknowledged lineage and identity.

title: The Churning of the Milky Ocean
narrative_text:
The Churning of the Milky Ocean (Samudra Manthan) is a pivotal tale in Hindu mythology, illustrating the eternal struggle between the Devas (gods) and the Asuras (demons). Once, the Devas, weakened by a curse from the irascible sage Durvasas, sought to regain their strength and immortality. They approached Lord Vishnu, who advised them to churn the cosmic ocean of milk to obtain Amrita, the elixir of immortality. However, this monumental task required the cooperation of the Asuras.

With a temporary truce established, the Devas and Asuras began the arduous churning. Mount Mandara, a towering peak, was uprooted to serve as the churning rod, and Lord Vishnu, in his avatar as the giant tortoise Kurma, steadied the mountain on his back at the bottom of the ocean. The great serpent Vasuki, king of the Nagas, offered himself as the churning rope, wrapping around Mount Mandara. The Asuras eagerly grasped Vasuki's head, while the Devas held his tail.

As the churning commenced, the friction caused Vasuki to spew forth a deadly poison, Halahala, which threatened to engulf the universe. Terrified, both Devas and Asuras appealed to Lord Shiva. In an act of immense compassion, Shiva consumed the poison, holding it in his throat, which turned blue, earning him the epithet 'Nilakantha' (the blue-throated one). Parvati, his consort, gently pressed his throat to prevent the poison from descending further into his body.

The churning continued for a millennium, and gradually, wondrous treasures began to emerge from the ocean's depths. These included Chandra (the moon), Parijata (a fragrant tree), Airavata (Indra's four-tusked elephant), Kamadhenu (the wish-fulfilling cow), Madira (goddess of wine), Kalpavriksha (the wish-granting tree), the Apsaras (celestial dancers), Uccaihshravas (the celestial horse), Lakshmi (goddess of wealth and Vishnu's consort), Panchajanya (Vishnu's conch), Vishnu's mace and magic bow, various gems, and finally, Dhanvantari, the divine physician, who emerged holding the pot of Amrita.

Upon the appearance of the Amrita, the fragile truce shattered, and a fierce battle erupted between the Devas and Asuras for its possession. To prevent the Amritas from falling into the wrong hands, Lord Vishnu assumed the enchanting form of Mohini, a celestial enchantress. Mohini captivated the Asuras, persuading them to let her distribute the Amrita, promising fairness. However, she cunningly served the Amrita only to the Devas, while the Asuras received only ordinary water.

One Asura, Rahu, disguised himself as a Deva and managed to partake of the Amrita. The Sun and Moon gods, Surya and Chandra, recognized him and alerted Vishnu. Before the Amrita could fully pass his throat, Vishnu severed Rahu's head with his discus, Sudarshana Chakra. As Rahu had consumed a drop of Amrita, his head became immortal, forming the celestial body Rahu, while his body became Ketu. They forever chase the Sun and Moon, causing eclipses in their vengeance.

With the Amrita consumed, the Devas regained their immortality and strength, restoring cosmic balance. The Asuras, deprived of the elixir, were defeated. The story of Samudra Manthan symbolizes the arduous spiritual journey, where one must overcome obstacles and temptations to achieve divine knowledge and immortality. The poison represents the difficulties and suffering encountered, while the treasures signify the material and spiritual gains along the path. Lord Shiva's act of consuming the poison highlights the importance of self-sacrifice and compassion for the greater good. The ultimate moral is that true immortality and lasting peace are achieved through righteous actions, cooperation, and the triumph of good over evil, even when faced with immense challenges and temptations.
moral: True immortality and lasting peace are achieved through righteous actions, cooperation, and the triumph of good over evil, even when faced with immense challenges and temptations. Self-sacrifice and compassion are vital for the greater good.

title: The Musical Donkey
alt_titles: Panchatantra: The story of the musical donkey
narrative_text: There lived a washerman's donkey, whose name was Uddhata. During the day, the donkey would carry the washerman's bags, but during the night, he was set free to eat the green grass in a nearby field. However, instead of grazing in the nearby fields, he crept into nearby farms and ate vegetables of his choice. Before day-break it would come back to the washerman's house. One night, the donkey met a jackal while wandering in a nearby farm. They became good friends, and started meeting every night. The donkey, being fat, was able to break the fences of the farms. While he ate on the vegetable, the jackal would enter through the broken fence and ate the poultry on the farm. Before day-break, they would return to their respective home to meet again next night. This continued for many days. One night, the donkey said to the jackal, "Nephew, I feel like singing on nights like tonight, when the moon is full and beautiful. What Raaga (note combination) shall I sing?" The jackal cautioned, "Uncle, we are here to steal. Thieves should keep as quiet as possible. I may add, your voice is not as pleasant as you think, and sounds like conch being blown! Your voice can be heard over a long distance. It will awaken the farmers who are sleeping, and you will have us caught." The jackal assured, "Please uncle, eat as much as you like, and forget about singing!" This annoyed the donkey and he said, "Dear nephew, it is because you are a wild animal that you don't appreciate music. I shall sing a melodious Raaga. Wait till you hear it!" Observing that the donkey was determined to sing; the jackal did not risk staying there anymore. He said, "Uncle, if you must sing, please wait till I go outside the fence and keep a watch on the farmers." He ran outside the fence, and hid himself. Then, the donkey started to bray at the top of his voice. When the farmers heard the donkey braying, they could see easily in the fullmoon-lit farm that the donkey was in their farm. The angry farmers chased the donkey with sticks, and beat him so hard that he fell on the ground. Then, they tied a wooden mortar around his neck and let him go. When the donkey was returning through the broken fence, the jackal laughed, "Musical uncle! That was a great Raaga! I see the farmers have rewarded you with this necklace!"
moral: There is always a proper place and time for doing anything.

title: The Blue Jackal
narrative_text:
Once there was a jackal in a forest by the name of Chandaraka. One day, Chandaraka, driven by hunger, went to a nearby village in search of food. He was extremely hungry. Unfortunately, he met a group of dogs and they started chasing him. The jackal got frightened and fled in panic. He entered a washerman's house in a hurry. While trying to hide himself, the jackal slipped and fell into a tub full of blue colour, which the washerman had kept ready to dye the clothes.

Soon the bark of the dogs ceased and jackal came out of the tub. There was a big mirror fixed on the wall of the house. Jackal saw himself in the mirror and was surprised to see his blue coloured body. He came out of the house and quickly ran back to the forest. When Chandaraka reached the forest, every animal failed to recognize this new creature. In fact, they got frightened and ran in all directions.

Taking advantage of the situation, the jackal planned to keep the situation in his favour. He asked, "Why are you running like this?"

"There is no need to panic. I am a unique creation of God. He told me that the animals in this jungle had no ruler and he had nominated me as your king. He had named me, Kakudruma and told me to rule this forest. Therefore, all of you can live safely under the cover of my protection."

The innocent animals believed the shrewd jackal and accepted him as the king. The jackal appointed the lion as his minster, the tiger as his chamberlain and the wolf as the gatekeeper. After allotting positions to the animals, the new king Kakudruma banished all the jackals from the forest.

One day, as the jackal king was holding court, a pack of jackals passed by, howling loudly. On hearing their cries, Kakudruma's true nature came out. He forgot that he was the king and started howling loudly in response.

The animals were shocked to see their king howling like a jackal. They immediately realized that they had been fooled by the blue jackal. The lion, tiger, and wolf were very angry. They pounced on the jackal and killed him.
moral: One should never forget one's true identity.

...ready to dye the clothes.
...ran in all directions.
...cover of my protection.
...from the forest.
...loudly in response.
...and killed him.

safety_notes: The story involves deception and violence (the jackal being killed). While common in fables, it should be presented with a calm and reflective tone for elderly listeners.

title: The Birth Story about the Golden Deer
narrative_text:
In the past when Brahmadatta was reigning in Benares, the Bodhisatta came to life as a young stag, and grew up a beautiful and graceful creature, of the colour of gold. His fore and hind feet were covered, as it were, with a preparation of lac. His horns were like a silver wreath, his eyes resembled round jewels, and his mouth was like a ball of crimson wool. The doe that was his mate was also a handsome creature, and they lived happily and harmoniously together. Eight myriads of dappled deer followed in the train of the Bodhisatta. While they were thus living there, a certain hunter set a snare in the deer drives.

So one day the Bodhisatta, while leading his herd, entangled his foot in the snare, and thinking to break the noose he tugged at it and cut the skin of his foot. Again he tugged it, and hurt the flesh, and a third time and injured the tendon. And the noose penetrated to the very bone. Not being able to break the snare, the stag was so alarmed with the fear of death that he uttered the cry of capture. On hearing it the herd of deer fled in a panic. But the doe, as she fled, looking amongst the deer, missed the Bodhisatta, and thought: “This panic must certainly have something to do with my lord,” and flying in haste to him, with many tears and lamentations she said: “My lord, you are very strong. Why can you not get the better of the snare? Put forth your strength and break it.” And thus stirring him up to make an effort, she uttered the first verse:

“O golden foot, no effort spare
To loose thyself from thongéd snare.
How could I joy, bereft of you,
To range amidst the woodland free?”

The Bodhisatta, on hearing this, responded in a second verse:

“I spare no effort, but in vain,
My liberty I cannot gain.
The more I struggle to get loose,
The sharper bites the thongéd noose.”

Then the doe said: “My lord, fear not. By my own power will I entreat the hunter, and by giving up my own life I will gain yours in exchange.” And thus comforting the Great Being, she continued to embrace the blood-stained Bodhisatta. But the hunter approached, with sword and spear in hand, like to the destroying flame at the beginning of a cycle. On seeing him, the doe said: “My lord, the hunter is coming. By my own power I will rescue you. Be not afraid.” And thus comforting the stag, she went to meet the hunter, and standing at a respectful distance, she saluted him and said: “My lord, my husband is of the colour of gold, and endued with all the virtues, the king of eight myriads of deer.” And thus singing the praises of the Bodhisatta, she begged for her own death, if only the king of the herd might remain intact, and she repeated the third verse:

“Let on the earth a leafy bed,
Hunter, where we may fall, be spread:
And drawing from its sheath your sword,
Slay me and afterwards my lord.”

The hunter, on hearing this, was struck with amazement and said: “Even human beings give not up their lives for their king; much less the beasts. What can this mean? This creature speaks with a sweet voice in the language of men. This day will I grant life to her and to her mate.” And greatly charmed with her, the hunter uttered the fourth verse:

“A beast that speaks with voice of men,
Ne’er came before within my ken.
Rest you in peace, my gentle deer,
And cease, O golden foot, to fear.”

The doe seeing the Bodhisatta set at his ease, was highly delighted and returning thanks to the hunter, repeated the fifth verse:

“As I today rejoice to see
This mighty beast at liberty,
So, hunter, that did loose the trap,
Rejoice with all your kith and kin.”

And the Bodhisatta thought: “This hunter has granted life to me and this doe, and to eight myriads of deer. He has been my refuge, and I ought to be a refuge to him.” And in his character of one supremely virtuous he thought: “One ought to make a proper return to one’s benefactor,” and he gave the hunter a magic jewel which he had found in their feeding ground and said: “Friend, henceforth take not the life of any creature, but with this jewel set up a household and maintain a wife and children, and give alms and do other good works.” And thus admonishing him, the stag disappeared in the forest.

At that time Channa was the hunter, this female novice was the doe, and I myself was the royal stag.
moral: The story teaches the importance of compassion, self-sacrifice, and gratitude. It highlights how acts of kindness and selflessness can inspire others and lead to positive change, even in those who initially act out of self-interest.

title: Akbar Birbal: Red Hot Test
alt_titles: Birbal and the Hot Iron Rod
narrative_text:
One day, a rich man wanted to get an innocent man named Hasan, punished for some grudge that he held against him. He accused him of stealing his wife’s valuable necklace, and reported this theft in the Emperor’s court.

Emperor Akbar asked the man, “Why do you think that Hasan has stolen the necklace from your place?” The rich man replied, “Your Majesty, I have myself seen him stealing the necklace!” Hasan said, “No Huzoor, I am innocent! I do not know anything about the necklace.”

The rich man said, “Huzoor, if he is innocent, ask him to prove his innocence by holding a hot iron rod in his bare hands. If he is able to do so, then I will believe that he has not stolen my necklace, and he is speaking the truth.”

The rich man also convinced the Emperor that if Hasan was telling the truth then the holy deities above would not let any harm come to him and it was the most ideal way to test the innocence of the accused person. The Emperor ordered Hasan to appear in his court the next day and do as directed by the rich man.

Poor Hasan was at his wit’s end. He went to Raja Birbal for help. Birbal could understand that the man was telling the truth and suggested a clever idea to him.

When he returned the next day, he said, “I am ready for the test. But the same thing should be applied to the rich man as well. If he is speaking the truth, then the red hot iron should not burn his hands also. So, first let him hold the hot iron rod in his hands, and then I will hold that iron in my bare hands.”

Now, the rich man was speechless! He requested the Emperor Akbar to let him go and look for the necklace again in his house; maybe his wife had misplaced it somewhere in the house itself.

Emperor Akbar ordered the rich man to give the necklace to Hasan as a punishment and praised Raja Birbal for his clever idea.
moral: Truth always prevails, and cleverness can expose deceit.

...and suggested a clever idea to him.
...and then I will hold that iron in my bare hands.

safety_notes: The story involves a false accusation and a test with a hot iron rod, which should be handled with care to avoid misinterpretation by young or sensitive listeners. Emphasize the symbolic nature of the test and Birbal's wisdom in resolving the conflict without actual harm.

title: Tenali Raman and the Sadhu
narrative_text: These were the days when the King Krishnadava Raja ruled Vijay Nagar, a self proclaimed Sage came to the town. He mesmerized all the citizens of the town with his magical triumphs. Every morning and evening people would gather at the temple with specially prepared delicacies as offerings to the Sadhu. The news also reached the royal court. When Tenali Raman heard of this, he smelt a rat. He went to the temple and sat near the Sadhu. The Sadhu began reciting shlokas. The Sadhu went on repeating the same shloka over and over again. Tenali Raman realized that he was a fraud. Tenali Rama intended to teach the fraud sadhu a lesson and also did not want to hurt the sentiments of his people. Thoughtfully, he leaned forward and plucked a strand of hair from the Sadhu’s beard. “I have the key to Heaven!” he shouted triumphantly. The villagers looked startled. The villagers looked startled. “This Sadhu is so great that if I keep the hair from his beard with me, I will be blessed forever!” said Tenali Raman. Immediately there was a scramble as everybody rushed to get hold of a hair from the Sadhu’s beard. The frightened Sadhu ran for his life and was never heard of again.
moral: Do not be easily deceived by appearances. Use your wisdom and critical thinking to discern the truth, and do not blindly follow those who claim to possess special powers or knowledge.

title: The Prince and the Redeemed Rakshasi
narrative_text:
In the ancient land of Aryavarta, where valor and virtue were held in high esteem, reigned a young and noble ruler, Prince Veer. His kingdom, though prosperous, was afflicted by a mysterious blight that withered crops and brought despair to his people. Determined to find a cure, Prince Veer embarked on a perilous journey into the enchanted forests that bordered his realm.

Deep within the shadowed woods, he stumbled upon a serene clearing where a maiden of unparalleled beauty sat by a shimmering pond. Her name was Maya, and her eyes held a captivating allure. Unbeknownst to the prince, Maya was a powerful rakshasi, cursed by a wise sage, Vimal, to remain in human form until she experienced true, selfless love and performed an act of pure compassion. Her demonic nature, though suppressed, occasionally flickered in moments of anger or fear, manifesting as a fleeting shadow or an unnatural chill in the air. Yet, she yearned for release from her curse, a longing that softened her heart.

Prince Veer, enchanted by Maya's grace and apparent kindness, fell deeply in love. He saw past her occasional enigmatic demeanor, attributing it to her mysterious origins. He proposed marriage, and Maya, seeing a glimmer of hope for her redemption, accepted. They returned to his kingdom, where she was welcomed as Queen. Under Prince Veer's just and benevolent rule, and with Maya's subtle, often unseen, influence (sometimes using her latent powers for good, disguised as fortunate coincidences), the kingdom slowly began to heal. The blight receded, and prosperity returned, though Maya still struggled with the vestiges of her true self, battling the urges of her former life.

One scorching summer, a devastating drought gripped the land, threatening to undo all their progress. The rivers dried, the fields cracked, and the people faced starvation. Panic spread through the kingdom. Maya, witnessing the suffering, felt a profound ache in her heart—a sensation she had never known as a rakshasi. She saw the despair in Prince Veer's eyes and the silent suffering of her adopted people. Her inner conflict raged: her demonic instincts urged her to seize power amidst the chaos, to revel in destruction. But the love she had found with Veer, and the genuine affection she had developed for the kingdom, pulled her towards a different path.

One night, as the moon hung heavy and full, Maya sought out the oldest and wisest sage in the kingdom, who happened to be Sage Vimal, the very one who had cursed her. She confessed her true identity and her desperate desire to save the kingdom, even if it meant revealing her nature. Sage Vimal, seeing the genuine remorse and love in her heart, revealed that the curse could only be broken by a selfless act of sacrifice, using her inherent power not for destruction, but for creation and sustenance. He instructed her to channel her energy into the parched earth, but warned that the act would drain her, potentially ending her existence as she knew it.

Without hesitation, Maya returned to the dried-up riverbed. As Prince Veer and his people watched in awe and fear, a radiant light enveloped her. She closed her eyes, focusing all her will and power, not on herself, but on the dying land. A tremor ran through the earth, and from the deepest crevices, water began to surge forth, revitalizing the river. The parched earth drank deeply, and the air filled with the scent of life. The drought was broken. As the last drop of water flowed, Maya's radiant form shimmered and then solidified, her eyes now filled with a pure, gentle light, completely free of any demonic trace. The curse was lifted, and she was truly human, her heart overflowing with love and peace.

From that day forward, Queen Maya ruled alongside Prince Veer, not as a rakshasi, but as a compassionate and beloved queen, her past a testament to the transformative power of love and selfless sacrifice. The kingdom flourished, and the story of their love became a legend, whispered from generation to generation, reminding all that true beauty lies not in outward appearance, but in the purity of one's heart and actions.
moral: True love and selfless sacrifice have the power to transform even the darkest of natures, leading to redemption and lasting peace.

performance_notes.opening_line: In the ancient land of Aryavarta, where valor and virtue were held in high esteem, reigned a young and noble ruler, Prince Veer.
pause_points: ...Unbeknownst to the prince, Maya was a powerful rakshasi...
...Her inner conflict raged...
...A tremor ran through the earth, and from the deepest crevices, water began to surge forth...

safety_notes: The story contains elements of a 'rakshasi' (demoness) and a curse, but these are resolved positively through love and sacrifice, making it suitable for elderly listeners. No graphic or frightening descriptions.

title: The Story of Shakuntala
narrative_text:
Once, the strong-armed King Dushyanta, with his mighty host, entered a thick wood for hunting. After slaying many wild creatures, he pursued a deer into another part of the forest. There, he beheld a beautiful hermitage on the bank of the sacred river Malini, the abode of the blessed sage Kanva. Desiring to meet the holy sage, the king laid aside his royal insignia and entered alone. Finding the hermitage deserted, he cried aloud, "Who is here?" until the forest seemed to echo his call. A maiden, lovely as Shri, emerged from the hermitage, clad in hermit garb. She greeted him warmly, asking, "What may be done for you?" The king, captivated by her sweet voice, inquired about the holy sage Kanva. Shakuntala replied that her father had gone to gather fruits and would return soon, inviting the king to wait.

Though the king did not see the sage, he was struck by the maiden's radiant beauty, her hard vows, and self-restraint. He asked her, "Who are you? Whose are you, lovely maiden? Why did you come to the forest? Whence are you, sweet girl, so lovely and so good? Your beauty stole my heart at the first glance. I wish to know you better. Answer me, sweet maid." The maiden laughed sweetly and replied, "O Dushyanta, I am known as blessed Kanva's daughter, and he is austere, steadfast, wise, and of a lofty soul." Dushyanta expressed his perplexity, knowing Kanva to be chaste and holy, and asked how she could be his daughter. Shakuntala then explained her true parentage: she was the child of the sage Vishwamitra and the celestial nymph Menaka, abandoned at birth, cared for by birds (shakuntas), and found and reared by Kanva, who named her Shakuntala.

Upon hearing this, Dushyanta declared, "You are clearly a king's daughter, sweet maiden, as you say. Become my lovely wife. Tell me, what shall I do for you? Let all my kingdom be yours today. Become my wife, sweet maid." Shakuntala, in turn, asked for a secret promise: "The son that is born to me must be your heir. If you promise, Dushyanta, I will marry you." Without hesitation, the king agreed, saying, "So be it," and added, "I will bring you too to my city, sweet-smiling girl." The king then took the faultlessly graceful maiden by the hand and dwelt with her. Bidding her be of good courage, he departed, promising to send a complete army to bring his sweet-smiling bride to his palace. As he returned to his capital, he wondered thoughtfully about Kanva's reaction.

No sooner had Dushyanta left than Kanva returned to the hermitage. Shakuntala, ashamed, did not come to meet her father. However, the blessed and austere Kanva, possessing divine discernment, perceived all that had transpired with celestial vision. He was pleased and said, "What you have done today, dear, forgetting me and meeting a man, this does not break the law. A man who loves may marry secretly the woman who loves him without a ceremony; and Dushyanta is virtuous and noble, the best of men. Since you have found a loving husband, Shakuntala, a noble son shall be born to you, mighty in the world."

In due course, Shakuntala gave birth to a boy of immense prowess. His hands were marked with the wheel, and he quickly grew into a glorious child. As a six-year-old in Kanva's hermitage, he rode on the backs of lions, tigers, and boars, taming them and playing with them. Those in the hermitage named him "All-tamer," for he tamed everything. When the sage Kanva witnessed the boy's extraordinary deeds, he told Shakuntala, "It is time for him to be anointed crown prince." Seeing the boy's strength, Kanva instructed his pupils, "Quickly bring my Shakuntala and her son from my house to her husband's palace. A long abiding with their relatives is not proper for married women. It destroys their reputation, and their character, and their virtue; so take her without delay." The pupils agreed and set out with Shakuntala and her son for Gajasahvaya.

When Shakuntala arrived and was recognized, she was invited to enter. She said to the king, "This is your son, O King. You must anoint him crown prince, just as you promised before, when we met." The king, though he remembered her, feigned ignorance and replied, "I do not remember. To whom do you belong, you wicked hermit-woman? I do not remember a union with you for virtue, love, and wealth. Either go or stay, or do whatever you wish." At these words, the sweet hermit-girl half fainted from shame and grief, standing stiff as a pillar. Her eyes darkened with indignation, her lips quivered, and she seemed to consume the king with her gaze. Concealing her feelings and nerved by anger, she held in check the magic power her ascetic life had given her. After a moment of meditation, overcome by grief and anger, she passionately addressed her husband: "O shameless king, although you know, why do you say, 'I do not know,' like any other ordinary man?"

Dushyanta retorted, "I do not know the son born of you, Shakuntala. Women are liars. Who will believe what you say? Are you not ashamed to say these incredible things, especially in my presence? You wicked hermit-woman, go!" Shakuntala, undeterred, declared, "O King, sacred is holy God, and sacred is a holy promise. Do not break your promise, O King. Let your love be sacred. If you cling to a lie, and will not believe, alas! I must go away; there is no union with a man like you. For even without you, Dushyanta, my son shall rule this foursquare earth adorned with kingly mountains." As she turned to leave, a bodiless voice from heaven spoke to Dushyanta: "Care for your son, Dushyanta. Do not despise Shakuntala. You are the boy's father. Shakuntala tells the truth."

Upon hearing the divine utterance, the king joyfully addressed his chaplain and ministers, "Hear the words of this heavenly messenger. If I had received my son simply because of her words, he would be suspected by the world, he would not be pure." Then the king gladly and joyfully received his son, kissing his head and embracing him lovingly. He also honored Shakuntala, as justice required. The king soothed her, explaining, "This union which I had with you was hidden from the world. Therefore I hesitated, O Queen, in order to save your reputation. And as for the cruel words you said to me in an excess of passion, these I pardon you, my beautiful, great-eyed darling, because you love me." King Dushyanta then named Shakuntala's son Bharata and had him anointed crown prince.
moral: The story emphasizes the sanctity of promises, the power of truth, and the ultimate triumph of justice, often through divine intervention. It also highlights the importance of honoring one's commitments and the consequences of deceit.

Shakuntala then explained her true parentage: she was the child of the sage Vishwamitra and the celestial nymph Menaka, abandoned at birth, cared for by birds (shakuntas), and found and reared by Kanva, who named her Shakuntala.

title: The Story of the River Saraswati
narrative_text:
In ancient times, a terrible and all-consuming fire known as Vadavagni threatened to engulf the entire world. This destructive fire was born from a fierce battle between the Bhargavas, a group of Brahmanas, and the Hehayas, a group of Kshatriyas. The Devas, fearing for the safety of creation, sought the counsel of Lord Shiva.

Lord Shiva, in his infinite wisdom, advised the Devas to approach Goddess Saraswati, the embodiment of knowledge, arts, and purity. He suggested that she alone possessed the power to contain the Vadavagni by transforming herself into a river and carrying the destructive fire to the depths of the ocean.

Saraswati, ever benevolent, agreed to this monumental task, but with a condition: Lord Brahma, her father and the creator of the universe, must instruct her to do so. Brahma, understanding the gravity of the situation and the necessity of Saraswati's intervention, readily gave his consent and blessings.

Thus, with Brahma's divine command, Goddess Saraswati descended from Brahmaloka. Her journey led her to the ashram of the revered sage Uttanka. There, she encountered Lord Shiva once more, who entrusted her with the Vadavagni, contained within a sacred pot. Shiva revealed that the fire had originated from the divine Plaksha tree.

Embracing her destiny, Saraswati merged with the sacred Plaksha tree, transforming herself into a magnificent and life-giving river. With the Vadavagni held safely within her waters, she embarked on her journey, flowing across lands, nurturing life, and ultimately carrying the destructive fire to its rightful place in the ocean, where it would be neutralized and rendered harmless. And so, the divine Saraswati River, born from a celestial purpose, continued to flow, a symbol of purity, knowledge, and the eternal cycle of creation and preservation.
moral: The story of the River Saraswati teaches us the importance of divine intervention and the power of selfless action to overcome destructive forces. It highlights how knowledge and purity, embodied by Goddess Saraswati, can bring balance and preservation to the world.

title: The Loyal Mongoose
narrative_text: Once there was a farmer who lived with his wife and his infant son in a village. The farmer and his wife loved their son very much. One day, while returning from the fields, the farmer found a little mongoose lying near the road. It looked pale and hurt. The farmer picked it up and brought it to his home. He told his wife that the little mongoose would be a pet for their son. His wife did not like the idea of having a mongoose near their son but accepted reluctantly. The farmer tendered to the wounds of the mongoose and gave it food and water. The mongoose recovered very quickly. The farmer's son and the mongoose started growing together. One day the farmer's wife had to go to the market. She put her son to sleep and told her husband to look after him. She told him not to let the mongoose go near their son. She somehow felt that it is not safe to let the animal go near their son. The husband assured her that he would look after the baby. Soon after his wife had left for the market, the farmer was called by the local moneylender. The moneylender wanted his money to be returned that he had lent to the farmer, some time back. The farmer had a good harvest that season and had the money ready with him. The baby was sleeping and thus the farmer could not take the child with him. He placed the child in his cradle and left him with the mongoose. He believed that the mongoose was an intelligent animal and could take care of their son. After some time, the farmer's wife came back with a basketful of vegetables. She found the mongoose waiting for her outside the house. She looked at the mongoose and realized that something was not right. The face and paws of the mongoose were covered with blood. She was dumbstruck. "You dirty creature, you have killed my son" she cried and struck the mongoose on the head with her basket full of vegetables. The mongoose died on the spot. The farmer's wife rushed inside the house and found her son sleeping peacefully in the cradle. Near the cradle, there was a dead snake. She realized that the mongoose had killed the snake and saved her son. She cried bitterly for her mistake. She had killed her loyal pet without thinking. The moral of the story is: Think before you act.
moral: Think before you act.

safety_notes: The story contains a scene of violence (mongoose killing a snake, wife killing the mongoose) and themes of rash judgment and regret. Ensure appropriate framing for elderly listeners.

title: The Crow and the Serpent
narrative_text:
In a verdant tree, a pair of crows made their home, diligently raising their young. However, their joy was repeatedly marred by a cunning black serpent that resided in the hollow of the tree's trunk. This serpent, with cruel regularity, devoured their hatchlings, leaving the crow couple heartbroken and despairing. 

One day, as the female crow found herself with new eggs, her heart was heavy with dread. She turned to her mate, her voice trembling, "My dear, we must abandon this tree. We will never be able to raise our offspring here, not with that vile black serpent lurking. It is said that 'A bad wife, a false friend, servants who give pert answers, and living in a house infested by serpents, is death, as it were, inevitable.'"

The male crow, however, was resolute. "My dear," he replied, "you shall have no further cause for alarm. I have tolerated his offenses many times, but this time, he shall be stopped." 

"But how, my husband?" she questioned, "How can you contend with such a powerful foe?"

With a knowing glint in his eye, the male crow answered, "Never fear. 'He who hath sense hath strength. Where hath he strength who wanteth judgment? See how a lion, when intoxicated with anger, was overcome by a rabbit.'"

Intrigued, the female crow asked, "How was that?" And so, the male crow recounted the tale of the clever rabbit and the mighty lion, illustrating how wit can triumph over brute force. (The story of the rabbit and the lion is omitted here for brevity, as per the task's focus on the crow and serpent story.)

Returning to their own predicament, the male crow revealed his plan. "Every day," he explained, "the king's son comes to bathe in the nearby river. I intend to take a golden chain he wears, when he removes it, and drop it into the hole where the serpent lives. When the king's attendants, searching for the lost chain, investigate the tree's hollow, they will discover the black serpent and swiftly destroy it."

True to his word, some time later, as the king's son bathed in the river, the crow seized the opportunity. He snatched the glittering golden chain and, with precision, dropped it into the serpent's lair. As anticipated, the king's men, diligently searching for the valuable ornament, followed its trail to the tree. Upon finding the black serpent within the hollow, they immediately killed it, thus freeing the crows from their tormentor.

Therefore, it is truly said: That may be effected by stratagem, which could not be effected by strength. A female crow, by means of a gold chain, caused the death of a black serpent.
moral: That may be effected by stratagem, which could not be effected by strength.

...illustrating how wit can triumph over brute force.
...thus freeing the crows from their tormentor.

title: The Crab and the Crane
narrative_text: In the Long Ago there was a summer when very little rain fell. All the Animals suffered for want of water, but the Fishes suffered most of all. In one pond full of Fishes, the water was very low indeed. A Crane sat on the bank watching the Fishes. "What are you doing?" asked a little Fish. "I am thinking about you Fishes there in the pond. It is so nearly dry," answered the Crane. "Yes," the Crane went on, "I was wishing I might do something for you. I know of a pond in the deep woods where there is plenty of water." "I declare," said the little Fish, "you are the first Crane that ever offered to help a Fish." "That may be," said the Crane, "but the water is so low in your pond. I could easily carry you one by one on my back to that other pond where there is plenty of water and food and cool shade." "I don't believe there is any such pond," said the little Fish. "What you wish to do is to eat us, one by one." "If you don't believe me," said the Crane, "send with me one of the Fishes whom you can believe. I 'll show him the pond and bring him back to tell you all about it." A big Fish heard the Crane and said, "I will go with you to see the pond—I may as well be eaten by the Crane as to die here." So the Crane put the big Fish on his back and started for the deep woods. Soon the Crane showed the big Fish the pool of water. "See how cool and shady it is here," he said, "and how much larger the pond is, and how full it is!" "Yes!" said the big Fish, "take me back to the little pond and I 'll tell the other Fishes all about it." So back they went. The Fishes all wanted to go when they heard the big Fish talk about the fine pond which he had seen. Then the Crane picked up another Fish and carried it away. Not to the pool, but into the woods where the other Fishes could not see them. Then the Crane put the Fish down and ate it. The Crane went back for another Fish. He carried it to the same place in the woods and ate it, too. This he did until he had eaten all the Fishes in the pond. The next day the Crane went to the pond to see if he had left a Fish. There was not one left, but there was a Crab on the sand. "Little Crab," said the Crane, "would you let me take you to the fine pond in the deep woods where I took the Fishes?" "But how could you carry me?" asked the Crab. "Oh, easily," answered the Crane. "I 'll take you on my back as I did the Fishes." "No, I thank you," said the Crab, "I can't go that way. I am afraid you might drop me. If I could take hold of your neck with my claws, I would go. You know we Crabs have a tight grip." The Crane knew about the tight grip of the Crabs, and he did not like to have the Crab hold on with his claws. But he was hungry, so he said: "Very well, hold tight." And off went the Crane with the Crab. When they reached the place where the Crane had eaten the Fishes, the Crane said: "I think you can walk the rest of the way. Let go of my neck." "I see no pond," said the Crab. "All I can see is a pile of Fish bones. Is that all that is left of the Fishes?" "Yes," said the Crane, "and if you will let go of my neck, your shell will be all that will be left of you." And the Crane put his head down near the ground so that the Crab could get off easily. But the Crab pinched the Crane's neck so that his head fell off. "Not my shell, but your bones are left to dry with the bones of the Fishes," said the Crab.
moral: Deception and greed ultimately lead to one's downfall. Those who use cunning to harm others may find themselves outsmarted by their own victims.

title: The Blind Saint
narrative_text: There lived a saint in an ashram in the kingdom of Emperor Akbar. He was believed to prophecy the future correctly. Once he had a visitor who had come to treat their niece. The child's parents were killed in front of the girl's eyes. Once she saw the saint, she started to scream loudly saying that that saint was the culprit. Angered by the girl's words, the saint demanded the couple to get away with their child. The whole day the girl cried which made the couple to realize that the girl was not lying. Therefore, they decided to seek the help of Birbal. Birbal consoled them and asked them to wait at the Emperor's assembly. Birbal had invited the saint to Akbar's court too. Then in front of all the ministers he drew a sword and neared the saint to kill him. The saint in bewilderment immediately drew another sword and began to fight. Thus by this act of the saint it was proved that he wasn’t blind. Therefore, Akbar demanded to hang the culprit and rewarded the girl for her bravery for telling the truth even at the critical situation.
moral: Truth always prevails, and one should not be afraid to speak it, even in difficult situations. Deception will eventually be exposed.

title: Tenali Rama & the Invisible Fabric
narrative_text: A gorgeous woman entered the royal court of Sri Krishna Deva Rayalu when he was busy with the courtiers. With all her pleasing manners, attire and conversation, she attracted the gathering that day. After a little dialogue with the King and his prime staff, she took out the most delicate and flimsiest sari from a small box that would hardly be sufficient to place a pair of ear-tops.  Exhibiting the saree to the royal court attendants, she addressed the king, “King of Kings! A group of divine weavers are working for me. They can weave similar delicate, thinnest and beautiful saris. They are capable of weaving celestial fabric, which cannot be seen with the naked eye.  “If we have your majesty’s blessings and support in addition to appropriate allocation of funds, they can do wonders and exhibit before the honourable King.”  Satisfied with the exhibit, Rayalu believed her words. Allotting required funds for weaving the celestial fabric, Rayalu asked her to get the job done with thorough research and of course within the year.  The gorgeous woman and her weavers enjoyed the comforts and treatment extended by the King to the royal court guests for one year. Even after the lapse of one year, there was no news from the woman about the research nor the weavers exhibited any products before the King.  Rayalu ordered some of his employees to inspect the weavers’ guesthouse and enquire about the weaving of celestial fabric that was fit to be worn by the Gods. The royal employees at once left for the guesthouse.  The woman welcomed the inspectors and took them to the location where the weavers were working seriously not even noticing the presence of guests in the room. The inspectors were aghast when they entered the room. There were no looms, no thread spools, not even a string of thread in the room. All the weavers sitting there were pretending to draw threads and weave. Every act was as if they were weaving something, with empty hands. The inspectors could not realise what was happening there. They asked the woman about the invisible fabric. She threw a lovely smile at them and told in a low husky voice. “Gentlemen, the invisible or celestial fabric is visible to only those who are pure along with purity of their parents. Of course, I believe that you can see the clarity of design and beauty of the craftsmanship of our weavers.”  The inspectors were worried of being branded as sinners, if they spoke truth about what they saw. They did not dare to probe against the guests. Moreover, they appreciated the delicacy, accuracy, and what not about the work being done at the guesthouse. They stood by the roles they played at the guesthouse, in front of the King Rayalu too.  The feedback of the employees augmented the enthusiasm of Rayalu. He was eagerly waiting for the finished products to be exhibited before him and the royal court members. Some days later, Rayalu ordered his men to bring the weavers to his court along with the finished products.  An unusually big gathering assembled in the royal court to witness the celestial fabric exhibition. As part of the introductory speech, the weavers addressed the gathering. They pointed out that the fabric was made from various divine materials. Hence, they would be visible only to those who are clean by soul along with purity of their parents. The whole gathering went silent for a moment, as no one could see any fabric in the hands of the weavers. Still, they maintained, out of fear of humiliation, that they were able to view the exhibit, started applauding the beauty, and praised the weavers.  Just then, King Rayalu accompanied by Ramakrishna, entered the court to witness the exhibition. Weavers repeated their standard phrases. Rayalu stared at the hands of the weavers for a moment and whispered to Ramakrishna, “I regret to say, I cannot see any material in their hands. Is that my own sin or my parents’?” “Nothing comes to be visible in thin air, My Lord!” replied Ramakrishna.  Rayalu grew suspicious, “What do you mean?” he asked. Ramakrishna in a low tone audible only to the King said, “King of Kings! These weavers are cheating our eyes and brains with their deceptive speech.”  Rayalu urged Ramakrishna to make public, the cheating of the weavers. Ramakrishna turned towards the gorgeous woman and in an astoundingly inquisitive tone told her, “What a beauty! How rich are these textiles, Lady. The King of Kings Rayalu is anxious to view your stepped up decorum, if you wear those celestial material.”  The lady understood that Ramakrishna deciphered the secret of invisible weaving. She was confused for a moment. She could neither disregard the King’s order nor stand nude in the crowded court in the guise of wearing something, which actually was nothing. She thought that the only way out would be to fall on the King’s feet pleading mercy. Immediately she did so begging for pardon. This was how Ramakrishna was once again instrumental in protecting his King.  Since then, nudity is being referred as wearing divine clothes.  Tenali Raman once again proved his with and intelligence with this episode.
moral: The story highlights the importance of truth and exposes deception. It teaches that one should not be afraid to speak the truth, even when it is unpopular, and that wit and intelligence can be used to uncover fraud.

title: Vikram-Betal: The story of the man who could enter another's body
alt_titles: The Baitâl Pachchisi: Tale of the Yogi who could enter another's body
narrative_text: In the kingdom of King Chandrabhan, there lived a yogi who possessed the power to enter another's body. The king, intrigued by this, sent a courtesan to bring the yogi to his court. The courtesan, with her wit and charm, managed to beget a child from the yogi and brought the child, on the yogi's shoulder, to the king. The yogi, realizing he had been tricked, killed his child in a fit of rage. This act led to the birth of three children in the same city at the same time with linked destinies: the sons of a king, a potter, and an oil merchant. The potter's son killed the oil merchant's son. The king's son was Vikramaditya, the potter's son was the yogi, and the oil merchant's son was the body inhabited by the Vetala. The yogi, wanting to attain immortality, devised a plan to sacrifice Vikram. He gifted Vikram fruits with jewels inside to gain his trust and then asked him to bring a corpse from a tree in a cremation ground. The corpse was possessed by a Vetala, who told Vikram stories to pass the time. The Vetala, in the end, revealed the yogi's evil plan to Vikram. The yogi intended to make Vikram bow before an idol and then behead him. The Vetala advised Vikram to ask the yogi to demonstrate how to bow and then to seize the opportunity to kill the yogi. Vikram followed the Vetala's advice, killed the yogi, and was blessed with great powers.
moral: Be wary of those who offer gifts with hidden motives. True wisdom lies in discerning the intentions of others.

The Vetala, in the end, revealed the yogi's evil plan to Vikram.
Vikram followed the Vetala's advice, killed the yogi, and was blessed with great powers.

safety_notes: The story contains themes of death and sacrifice which might be disturbing for some listeners.

title: Mena Gurjari: The Brave Shepherdess of Gujarat
narrative_text:
In the heart of medieval Gujarat, amidst the pastoral lands known as Gurjar Desh, lived Mena Gurjari, a woman of extraordinary bravery and unwavering spirit. Born to Merubha, Mena grew up with a strong sense of justice, unafraid to voice her convictions against any wrongdoing. She found love and companionship with Chandio, the chieftain of a shepherd warrior tribe residing in Gadh Gokul. Their life was simple, marked by Mena's daily routines of fetching water and selling curd in the bustling market, all while holding deep loyalty to her husband and profound devotion to Goddess Kali.

One day, driven by a natural curiosity, Mena ventured to the royal camp, carrying her pots of curd and milk, dressed in her finest attire. There, her striking beauty caught the eye of the arrogant Sultan of Delhi. Enamored, he attempted to lure her with promises of immense wealth, power, and luxuries, boasting of his grand status. However, Mena, with sharp wit and unyielding resolve, defiantly rejected his advances. She mocked his pretensions, emphasizing her unwavering commitment to her honor, her family, and the cherished values of her community, choosing them over any imperial temptation.

Her bold refusal, however, escalated into a grave conflict. Despite her resistance, the Sultan, fueled by his arrogance, abducted Mena, holding her captive within his opulent camp on the village outskirts. Undeterred by her predicament, Mena's resilience shone through. She prayed fervently to Goddess Kali for strength and ingeniously managed to dispatch a secret message to her husband, Chandio, and his loyal warrior, Hirio. Upon receiving the urgent plea, Chandio, alongside Hirio, rallied a formidable army of nine lakh Gurjars, uniting them in a fierce battle against the Sultan's forces. This epic confrontation was fought not just for Mena's freedom, but in defense of justice and honor, highlighting the collective strength and bravery of the Gurjar community against oppression.

Following the triumphant battle and her rescue, Mena returned to her village, only to face a different kind of adversity. Her mother-in-law, swayed by societal suspicions regarding her chastity after her abduction, unjustly rejected her. Heartbroken but still affirming her purity, Mena embarked on a solitary journey to Pawagadh. There, in a profound act of spiritual merging, she became one with the local deity, Goddess Mahakali, forever affirming her unblemished honor and transforming her personal tragedy into a symbol of divine vindication and communal reverence.

Mena Gurjari's story is a testament to the enduring power of individual agency within collective solidarity, showcasing how a woman's intellectual and moral strength can challenge patriarchal norms and systemic injustices. Her legacy continues to inspire narratives of gender empowerment and community justice in Gujarati folklore.
moral: The story of Mena Gurjari teaches us that true strength lies in unwavering honor, courage, and the defiance of oppression, even in the face of overwhelming power. It underscores the importance of communal solidarity in upholding justice and reminds us that purity of spirit and character ultimately triumphs over societal judgment.
