...choosing them over any imperial temptation.
...highlighting the collective strength and bravery of the Gurjar community against oppression.
...transforming her personal tragedy into a symbol of divine vindication and communal reverence.

title: The Clever Farmer and Four Thugs
narrative_text:
There lived four thugs in a village. They would cheat people by telling them false stories and take away all their money and belongings. Everyone in the village was very unhappy because of them. Many tried to teach them a lesson but no one succeeded.

There also lived a farmer in that village. He said that he would teach these thugs such a lesson that they would forget making fool of others.

One day the farmer made a model of a woman and dressed her in nice clothes and lots of artificial ornaments. Then he covered her face with a veil and put her in a sitting position under a tree by the side of a road. The thugs used to pass by that road daily. The farmer hid behind a tree nearby.

As soon as it was evening, the thugs passed that way. They saw a lady sitting under a tree wearing a lot of jewellery. They were curious and went near her. They had hardly touched her when the model of the lady fell down. The farmer at once came out of his hiding place and started shouting, "Help! Help! These four thieves have killed my wife. They were trying to steal her jewellery when I saw them. When they saw me they at once killed her." Crying loudly he caught hold of one thug and said, "I will hand you over to the police. You have killed my wife and I will see to it that you are hanged." They tried to explain the situation to the farmer but he refused to listen and went on shouting.

The thieves were terribly afraid when they heard about hanging. They again and again tried to plead with him but he just would not listen and went on repeating. "You are murderers. I will not spare you." They apologized with folded hands but it had no effect on the farmer. So they offered to marry their sister with him and give the farmer twice as much money and jewellery and also take care of the cremation.

The farmer agreed. He first quickly burnt the model so that the thieves might not discover the truth.

In the morning he went to their house, married their sister and came back home with lots of money and jewellery.

After some time the farmer told his wife that when her thug brothers come to meet her, she should send them to him at the farm. Meanwhile she should prepare nice food for them like halwa, puri, sweets, samosas etc. The farmer also kept two rabbits as pets. He always took one with him to the farm and left the other at home.

One day the thugs thought that they should go and meet their sister and find out how she was. So one morning they went to the farmer’s house. Their sister told them that her husband was at the farm and they should go there to meet him. So all the four thugs went to the farm and were very happy to see him working there.

The farmer also welcomed them. He then picked up the rabbit and said, "Darling rabbit, go straight to my home and tell my wife that she should prepare halwa, puri, sweet khir and samosas for her brothers." Saying thus he let go of him and the rabbit ran into the nearby forest.

When the four thugs and the farmer reached home in the evening, they were surprised to see that their sister had cooked all the nice things that the farmer had asked her to cook and the rabbit was sitting by her side. All the four thugs were greatly impressed and decided to take back with them this wonderful rabbit. They did not know that the rabbit sitting with their sister was not the same rabbit they had seen at the farm. They requested the farmer to let them have the rabbit. The farmer at first was not ready to part with it but finally agreed. So they went back taking the rabbit with them thinking that it will now accompany them everywhere and whenever there is any need it will take their message home. They did not realise that the farmer had made a fool of them.

Once all the four thugs were going somewhere. On the way they met some relatives who were going to their house. They thought they will send a message home through the rabbit and get an excellent meal prepared for the guests. So they told the rabbit, "Go home straight and inform our wives that guests are coming. They should prepare an excellent meal for them." The rabbit hopped, skipped and ran into the jungle.

When the thugs reached home with the guests they found the ladies sleeping after having their lunch. Seeing this the thugs became very angry but soon realised that they had been be-fooled by the farmer. Nothing could be done now! They somehow offered some food to the guests and after the guests departed, started thinking of some plan to avenge themselves.

They all marched to the farmer’s house. The farmer knew that they will come back and was waiting for them. When he saw them coming, he went to his wife and said, "Your young brothers are coming. As soon as they reach here, you start crying loudly. I will shut you in a room and make noises as though I am beating you. Then you pretend to faint. I will then take a wooden stick and wave it over you saying, ‘Soong Batoong Soong Batoong’, then you get up after some time." The wife agreed.

As soon as the thugs reached near the house, the wife started crying loudly. The farmer then pulled her inside a room, closed the door and started beating the mattress with a stick. The wife cried even more loudly. The more force he used in beating the mattress the louder came the cries of their sister. After some time the crying stopped. The wife pretended to faint. The farmer then came out and told the thugs, "Today I beat your sister so much that she has become unconscious. She doesn’t work and sits idle the whole day. If you want, you can take her back with you otherwise I may one day beat her to death." Poor thugs didn’t know what to do. They said, "Today she has fainted. What will happen if she dies?" The farmer said, "Oh, she often becomes unconscious. Sometimes she even dies. But I bring her back to life. If you want, I can do so in front of you." The thugs were surprised. They said, "Yes, please show us." The farmer took everybody into the room and waved the stick over her saying, ‘Soong Batoong Soong Batoong’. When he did this two or three times, the wife got up rubbing her eyes.

When they saw this, the thugs were absolutely wonderstruck. They forgot about the rabbit and started thinking, ‘we should somehow get this magic wand’. So they requested the farmer to let them have the magic wand for one thousand rupees. They were very happy. They thought that on reaching home they will make their wives unconscious and then revive them. They will earn a lot of money by showing this trick to others. They even thought that if someone dies in the neighbourhood they would bring him back to life and earn a lot of name and fame as well as money.

After a few days, one of their neighbours fell ill and died. The thugs said that they will bring him back to life with a magic wand but they would need two thousand rupees for it. Then they went to the neighbour’s house with the magic wand and waved it on the dead body saying, ‘Soong Batoong, Soong Batoong’. But can a dead person ever become alive? Again and again they waved the wand but nothing happened. The neighbours got angry and they beat the thugs saying, "You are cheating us even in our sorrow. You should be ashamed of yourselves." They took back the two thousand rupees they had given to the thugs. So the farmer had once again made a fool of the thugs.

The thugs were now determined to punish the farmer, but the farmer had another plan ready for them. He pasted many silver coins on the tree in front of his house. He knew that the thugs will come back when the magic wand fails in its magic. After a few days they did come back to the farmer who was waiting for them. As soon as he saw them coming he started shaking the tree. When the tree shook, the pasted coins on the tree started falling down. The thugs asked the farmer, "What are you doing?" The farmer replied, "Can’t you see? I am shaking the tree to drop some coins. It is a remarkable tree. Whenever there is any need, I shake the tree and it drops coins." So the thugs were so fascinated by the tree that they forgot about the magic wand. The farmer said, "If you want, you can also try and get a few coins from the tree." So the thugs shook the tree and lo, some coins did fall from the tree. The thugs were very happy. They requested the farmer to sell them the tree and managed to persuade him to take five thousand rupees instead. The farmer said, "You can take the tree but be very careful. Don’t ever bend the tree. If you bend it, the tree will stop giving the money."

Somehow the thugs reached their home carrying the tree in a straight position. But they couldn’t carry it inside in a straight position. So they broke their boundary wall, carried it inside the compound and implanted it there. They were very happy to possess a tree which would give them money whenever they wanted.

After a few days they thought ‘Let us take some money from the tree’. They shook it for a long time and only one rupee dropped. Then they realised that they had been fooled again. So crying all the way they went back to the farmer.

The farmer said, "All four of you have cheated a lot of men and it was necessary to teach you a lesson. If you promise before the whole village that you will never cheat again, then I will also not trouble you in future."

So the thugs apologized before the whole village and promised that they will never cheat anyone. The whole village gave lots of presents to the farmer for his wisdom. Thus the villagers again started living happily and peacefully.
moral: It is necessary to teach a lesson to those who cheat others. Wisdom and cleverness can overcome deceit.

performance_notes.opening_line: In a village, where four cunning thugs troubled everyone, lived a clever farmer...
pause_points: Many tried to teach them a lesson but no one succeeded.
The thieves were terribly afraid when they heard about hanging.
They did not realise that the farmer had made a fool of them.
Nothing could be done now!
But can a dead person ever become alive?
Then they realised that they had been fooled again.

source_citations.title: The Clever Farmer and Four Thugs
source_citations.author: Soma Kaushik

title: Meerabai and the Poisoned Cup
alt_titles: Poison becomes Nectar - The Story of Meerabai
narrative_text:
The king, angered by Meerabai's unwavering devotion to Lord Krishna and her disregard for worldly duties, confided in his wife. He expressed his frustration that Meerabai would not heed their advice regarding her moral obligations.  

Filled with rage, the king declared his intention to administer a potent poison to Meerabai. He instructed his wife to personally deliver the poison, stating that it was better to hear of his daughter's death than for her to live in a manner that brought dishonor to their house. 

Though deeply distressed and heartbroken, the queen, unable to defy her husband's command, took the cup of poison. With tears streaming down her face, she approached Meerabai in the temple where she was engrossed in worship. The queen informed Meerabai of the king's deadly decree, lamenting that the god of death had come for her beloved daughter.

Meerabai, witnessing her mother's profound sorrow, responded with serene wisdom. She reminded her mother of the transient nature of the human body, asserting that its eventual demise was inevitable. She questioned why her mother should mourn if her departure was a consequence of her devotion to Krishna, comparing worldly attachments to a mirage or withered sky flowers. Meerabai declared that her love was now irrevocably fixed upon the feet of Shri Krishna, severing her ties to worldly relations. She likened herself to pearls formed in water, or jewels set in ornaments, signifying her complete surrender and acceptance by Lord Krishna.

With unwavering faith, Meerabai placed her heart at the feet of Lord Krishna and addressed Him. She acknowledged the poison sent by her father and expressed her hope of reaching heaven through His grace. However, her concern was for the continuation of His worship, fearing that revilers would speak ill of her devotion and that no one would worship Him with the same fervor. She worried about who would adorn Him with garments, ornaments, and garlands, and how the king, once a devotee, had turned away from His service due to her. 

Meerabai, with unwavering trust, recalled how Krishna had protected the Pandavas from a lacquered house set ablaze, and Pralhad from poison. She affirmed that the power of His name could even neutralize the virulence of poison, as it did for Shiva. 

With this profound faith, Meerabai took the cup of poison, offered it to Lord Krishna, declaring Him the 'Enjoyer of this,' and then drank it without hesitation. By the grace of Lord Krishna, the poison transformed into nectar, and Meerabai remained unharmed, her devotion shining brighter than ever.
moral: True devotion and unwavering faith in the divine can overcome any adversity, even death. Worldly attachments are fleeting, but spiritual connection is eternal.

performance_notes.opening_line: The king, angered by Meerabai's unwavering devotion to Lord Krishna...
pause_points: ...better to hear of his daughter's death than for her to live in a manner that brought dishonor to their house.
...lamenting that the god of death had come for her beloved daughter.
...her love was now irrevocably fixed upon the feet of Shri Krishna, severing her ties to worldly relations.
...fearing that revilers would speak ill of her devotion and that no one would worship Him with the same fervor.
...By the grace of Lord Krishna, the poison transformed into nectar, and Meerabai remained unharmed, her devotion shining brighter than ever.

safety_notes: The story involves an attempt to poison, but the devotee is divinely protected. Emphasize the miraculous transformation and the power of faith rather than the act of poisoning itself.

source_citations.title: Bhaktavijaya: Stories of Indian Saints

title: The Story of Govardhan Puja: Krishna Lifts the Hill
narrative_text:
A long time ago, in the beautiful village of Brindavan in North India, lived herdsmen whose main occupation was to graze goats and cattle. Among them was Nandagopar, the chief, and his wife Yasodha, who fostered the mischievous child Krishna. Krishna, along with his brother Balaraman, grew up amidst the lush green pastures of Brindavan, often taking their cattle to graze on the Govardhan Hill.

Every year, the herdsmen of Brindavan would celebrate a grand festival for Indra, the king of the Devas, believing he bestowed plentiful rains upon them. Nandagopar was busy with the preparations when Krishna approached him, questioning the purpose of worshipping Indra. Krishna, aware of Indra's growing arrogance and his neglect of the Trimurti (Shiva, Brahma, and Vishnu), wished to teach him a lesson. He explained to his father that the Trimurti were omnipresent, even in Govardhan Hill, which provided them with pure water and rich grass for their cattle. Krishna suggested that they should instead worship Govardhan Hill, as it was their true provider.

Convinced by Krishna's words, Nandagopar agreed, and the villagers prepared for a festival dedicated to Govardhan Hill. They cleaned the hill, decorated it with plantain trees, mango leaves, and colorful rangolis, and lit ghee lamps, making the hill appear heavenly. On the day of the festival, men, women, and children, dressed in new clothes, gathered at the foot of the hill. Women cooked delicious pongal and a feast of vegetables and fruits. The men bathed and adorned their cattle, performing puja for them. Priests kindled a sacred fire, chanting mantras and offering ghee and scented items. The prepared food was heaped at the foot of the hill, resembling another small mountain.

Nandagopar, wondering if the deity of Govardhan Hill would accept their offerings, asked Krishna. Krishna, with his benign smile, assured him that the offering would be accepted. To the astonishment and delight of all present, Krishna himself took the form of the god of Govardhan Hill and accepted the offerings. The villagers rejoiced, dancing and singing, and then feasted together before returning home happily.

Meanwhile, in Indralok, Indra was consumed by rage. He was furious that the herdsmen had disregarded him and worshipped a mere hill. Blinded by his fury and forgetting Krishna's divine nature as an avatar of Lord Vishnu, Indra sought revenge. He commanded Varuna Deva to unleash torrential rains upon Brindavan and Vayu Deva to send severe tornadoes, intending to destroy the village.

A great storm descended upon Brindavan. Heavy rains poured, and fierce winds raged, submerging houses, tearing down trees and huts. The herdsmen, women, and children ran in panic, with no shelter in sight. Cows and other domestic animals wailed in distress. In their desperation, they turned to Krishna for safety. Krishna, with a comforting smile, assured them not to worry. He told them that Govardhan Hill, which had accepted their offerings, would now protect them. He led them, along with their cattle and other animals, to the hill.

Upon reaching Govardhan Hill, Krishna, without a moment's hesitation, lifted the massive hill with his two hands as effortlessly as a child lifts a doll. He held it above his head, balancing it on the tip of his little finger. He affectionately called out to his people, inviting them to take shelter beneath the hill. Thousands of villagers and their animals found refuge under the Govardhan Hill, which served as a protective umbrella. Outside, the relentless rain and roaring winds continued for seven days and seven nights, but the people of Brindavan remained safe and happy under Krishna's divine care.

Realizing his defeat and filled with shame, Indra understood the consequence of his arrogance and forgetting the supreme Lord. He ceased the rain and wind, recalling the clouds. The downpour stopped, and the overjoyed herdsmen returned home with their animals. Indra, humbled, came to Krishna, fell at his feet, and begged for forgiveness. Krishna advised Indra to shed his arrogance, fulfill his duties diligently, and never forget God. The herdsmen welcomed Krishna back to Brindavan, singing praises of his greatness, dancing, and living in happiness, forever grateful for his divine protection.
moral: The story teaches the importance of humility, devotion, and respecting nature. It highlights that true worship comes from a heartfelt connection to the divine and gratitude for what sustains us, rather than elaborate rituals or appeasing arrogant deities. It also shows that pride and ego have no place in the divine realm, and that divine protection is always available to those with genuine faith.

performance_notes.opening_line: In the serene village of Brindavan, nestled amidst the verdant landscapes of North India, lived a community of simple herdsmen, their lives intertwined with the rhythm of nature and the gentle presence of their cattle.

Meanwhile, in Indralok, Indra was consumed by rage.

Realizing his defeat and filled with shame, Indra understood the consequence of his arrogance...

safety_notes: The story is emotionally warm and culturally respectful, suitable for elderly listeners. It contains no sensitive content.

title: Mahabharata: The Story of Draupadi's Swayamvara
narrative_text: In the ancient kingdom of Panchala, the princess Draupadi, a woman of unparalleled beauty and virtue, came of age. Her father, King Drupada, arranged a Swayamvara, a ceremony for her to choose her husband from a host of royal suitors. A formidable challenge was set: to string a mighty bow and shoot a revolving fish target high above, using five arrows, while looking only at its reflection in a pan of water below. Princes and kings from far and wide gathered, each vying for the hand of the fair princess. One by one, they tried and failed, unable to even string the divine bow. The mighty Karna, son of the Sun God, stepped forward and strung the bow with ease. But as he aimed, Draupadi declared she would not marry a suta, a charioteer's son, and so he withdrew. Just when it seemed no one would succeed, a young Brahmana, dressed in simple deerskin, rose from the crowd. It was Arjuna, the Pandava prince, in disguise. He approached the bow, and to the astonishment of all, strung it effortlessly. With a calm focus, he took aim and, with a single arrow, pierced the eye of the fish. A great cheer erupted as Draupadi garlanded Arjuna, choosing him as her husband. The other kings, enraged at being bested by a seemingly humble Brahmana, attacked. But Arjuna, with his brother Bhima by his side, fought them off, and together they departed with Draupadi.
moral: True worth and skill are not determined by social status or appearance.

performance_notes.opening_line: In the grand court of Panchala, a challenge was laid for the hand of the beautiful princess Draupadi.
pause_points: ...after many powerful kings had failed...
...a young Brahmana stepped forward...
...and with a single arrow, he pierced the eye of the fish.

safety_notes: The story contains themes of conflict and societal hierarchy which might require sensitive handling for some listeners.

title: The Birth of the River Godavari
narrative_text:
In ancient times, during a severe hundred-year drought, the revered sage Gautama was engaged in deep meditation on Brahmagiri mountain. He sought rain from Varuna, the ocean god, but Varuna explained that it was against the wishes of the gods. Instead, Gautama requested divine, everlasting water. Varuna instructed him to dig a ditch, which he then filled with sacred water, declaring that it would be known by Gautama's name. Gautama and other sages used this water to revive their crops and sustain themselves.

A dispute soon arose when the wives of other sages prevented Gautama's disciples from accessing the water. Gautama's wife, Ahalya, was the first to collect water, which angered the other sages' wives. Seeking revenge, these sages prayed to Lord Ganesha. Ganesha, however, advised them against harming someone who had done them good. Ignoring his counsel, they compelled Ganesha to intervene.

To fulfill their request, Ganesha took the form of a feeble cow and began to eat Gautama's crops. When Gautama attempted to shoo the cow away with a blade of grass, he unintentionally caused its demise. Realizing he had committed Gohatya, the grave sin of cow-slaughter, Gautama was filled with remorse. He left his hermitage to repent, circumambulating the Brahmagiri mountain, confessing his sin to everyone he met, and worshiping Lord Shiva through Lingams.

Eventually, Lord Shiva appeared before Gautama, who sought absolution. Shiva reassured Gautama that he had committed no sin and that his mere presence could absolve others. Nevertheless, Gautama requested the presence of Ganga (the sacred river goddess) to cleanse himself and others of their sins. Shiva granted Gautama 'the essence of the earth and heaven,' his inheritance from his father Brahma, to summon Ganga. Ganga agreed to stay on the condition that Shiva also remained, and so Shiva manifested as Tryambakeshwara, a Jyotirlinga, at the source.

With Ganga's presence, the Gautami river, now known as the Godavari river, began to flow. Gautama and his disciples bathed in its waters to purify themselves. Even the sages who had sought revenge desired cleansing. Though initially hesitant, Ganga agreed to purify them after Gautama's persuasion. Gautama then dug a ditch for Ganga to emerge and cleanse the sages of their sins, which became the famous Kushavartha Thirtha at the Tryambakeshwara temple. Thus, the Godavari, the 'Ganga of the South,' came into being, bringing purity and sustenance to the land.
moral: The story emphasizes the importance of repentance, divine intervention for absolution, and the sacredness of rivers as purifiers. It also highlights the consequences of malicious intent and the power of devotion.

performance_notes.opening_line: During a time of great drought, on the sacred Brahmagiri mountain, lived the wise sage Gautama...
pause_points: Varuna instructed him to dig a ditch, which he then filled with sacred water...
A dispute soon arose when the wives of other sages prevented Gautama's disciples from accessing the water...
To fulfill their request, Ganesha took the form of a feeble cow...
Realizing he had committed Gohatya, Gautama was filled with remorse...
Eventually, Lord Shiva appeared before Gautama, who sought absolution...
With Ganga's presence, the Gautami river, now known as the Godavari river, began to flow.

safety_notes: The story contains a brief mention of unintentional cow-slaughter, which is presented as a sin that is later absolved through divine intervention. This should be handled sensitively, emphasizing the repentance and purification aspects.

title: The Brahmin and the Crooks
alt_titles: The Foolish Brahmin and His Goat
narrative_text: In a small village, there lived a Brahmin, by the name of Mitra Sharma. He was a worshipper of Fire-God. One day, during monsoons, when the sky was overcast with cloud, he decided to conduct a certain sacrificial ritual. The Brahmin travelled to a nearby village, to visit a devotee, to request for a goat that he will offer it as a sacrifice to the Gods. On his arrival, he requested the devotee, "Son, I want to perform a sacrificial ritual on this auspicious time. Please offer me with a well-fed goat." The devotee agreed and offered him with one of his best goats. The Brahmin started his journey homewards. He carried the goat on his shoulders, so that he did not have problems in controlling the animal on the way home. On his way home, three crooks watched him from a distance. They were almost starving, and the Brahmin had a goat and he was all alone. They discussed, "We will be saved from the fangs of hunger in this cold monsoon, if we can lay our hands on this goat that the Brahmin is carrying." They decided to trick the Brahmin, in order to gain the goat for themselves. As planned, the first of the crooks stood in the Brahmin's path, by taking a shorter road. When the Brahmin, with the goat on his shoulders, approached him, he queried, "Ho Brahmin, Why is it that you behave so ridiculously? Why on earth are you carrying a profane dog on your shoulders?" On hearing this, the Brahmin got angry, he replied, "How can you not see any difference between a goat and a dog? Are you blind? Can't you see I am carrying a sacrificial goat?" The crook had played his part, and replied, "Please don't get angry on me. You may have it anyway you want. Please carry on with your journey". A little further, he was approached by the second crook, who said, "Ho Brahmin, shame on you! How can you carry this dead calf on your shoulders like that? Shame on you!" The Brahmin got even angrier, "Are you blind? Can't you see it is a goat and not a dead calf?" To this the second crook replied, "Have it anyway you want it to be, please don't get angry on me." When the Brahmin had gone a little further, the third crook accosted him, "Ho Brahmin. This is highly improper for you do something like this. Why do you carry a donkey on your shoulders? Put him down, before anyone sees you doing this!" Now, the Brahmin started thinking how can three different persons not see that was carrying a goat? He thought that he must be carrying a goblin, which is changing shape all the while. Fearing so, he put the goat down on the ground and ran home terrified. The crooks had succeeded in their plan. The crooks caught the goat at once, and feasted on the goat to their heart's content.
moral: Untruth spoken repeatedly appears to be truth.

title: The Jackal and the Drum
narrative_text: Once, in a dense forest, lived a jackal named Gomaya. He was known for his insatiable greed, often preying on the kills of other jackals, which eventually led to his banishment from the pack. Forced to hunt for himself, Gomaya wandered far and wide in search of sustenance. One day, his quest led him to a deserted battlefield, a grim reminder of a recent war. As he cautiously approached the desolate ground, a loud, terrifying sound echoed through the air, startling him. Convinced that a monstrous creature lurked nearby, Gomaya fled in terror. However, a moment of reflection halted his escape. He reasoned that it was foolish to flee from an unknown entity without first understanding its nature. Gathering his courage, he returned to the battlefield, slowly approaching the source of the mysterious sound. To his surprise, he discovered a large war-drum, abandoned beneath a tree. The wind, swaying the tree's branches, caused them to rub against the drum's surface, producing the very sound that had initially terrified him. Realizing his error, Gomaya felt a pang of foolishness for his hasty retreat. No longer afraid, he continued his exploration of the battlefield and soon stumbled upon a tent filled with an abundance of food and water. He ate to his heart's content, his hunger finally satisfied.
moral: Only the brave can succeed at what they do.

title: The Foolish Lion and the Clever Rabbit
narrative_text:
Once upon a time, in a dense forest, there lived a cruel lion named Bhasuraka. He was very powerful, ferocious, and arrogant, often killing animals to satisfy his hunger. This behavior caused great worry among the forest animals, who feared that soon none would be left alive. They decided to hold a meeting with the lion to find a peaceful resolution.

According to their plan, all the animals gathered under a large tree and invited King Lion to join them. The animal representative addressed him, saying, "Your Majesty, we are happy to have you as our king and even happier that you are attending this meeting." The King Lion thanked them and asked, "What is the matter? Why have we gathered here?"

The animals hesitated, gathering their courage. Finally, one animal spoke up, "Sir, it is natural for you to hunt us for food. However, killing more than necessary is not a good approach. If you continue to kill animals without purpose, soon there will be no animals left in the forest." The king lion roared, "So what do you want?"

Another animal replied, "Your Majesty, we have discussed this problem and found a solution. We propose to send one animal to your den daily for your meal. You can kill and eat it as you wish. This will also save you the trouble of hunting." The lion agreed to this proposal, but warned, "Fine. I agree, but ensure the animal reaches me on time, otherwise, I will kill all the animals in the jungle." The animals agreed.

From that day forward, an animal was sent to the lion daily. The lion was pleased to receive his food without the effort of hunting. One day, it was the turn of an old and wise rabbit. He was reluctant to go, but the other animals insisted. The rabbit devised a plan to save himself and the other animals. He took his time, arriving at the lion's den later than usual.

The Lion grew impatient and furious when he saw only a small rabbit for his meal. He threatened to kill all the animals. The rabbit, with folded hands, hesitantly explained, "Your Majesty, I am not to blame. Actually, six rabbits were sent for your meal, but five were killed and devoured by another lion who claimed to be the king of this forest. I barely escaped to reach you safely."

The king lion roared in anger, "Impossible! There cannot be another king in this forest! Tell me, who is he? I will kill him! Take me to where you saw him." The intelligent rabbit agreed and led the Lion to a deep well filled with water. When they reached the well, the rabbit said, "This is where he lives. He might be hiding inside."

The Lion looked into the well and saw his own reflection, mistaking it for the other lion. He became furious and growled. Naturally, the image in the water, the "other lion," also appeared angry. To kill the perceived rival, the Lion jumped into the well, hitting his head against the rocks, and drowned.

The wise rabbit, relieved, returned to the other animals and narrated the entire story. All the animals rejoiced and praised the rabbit for his cleverness. Thus, the jubilant rabbit saved all the animals from the proud Lion, and they all lived cheerfully thereafter.
moral: Wisdom and cleverness can overcome brute strength and arrogance.

performance_notes.opening_line: Listen closely, for I have a tale from ancient India, a story of a powerful lion and a very clever rabbit.

Thus, the jubilant rabbit saved all the animals from the proud Lion and they all lived cheerfully thereafter.

title: Akbar-Birbal: The Story of the Emperor's Dream
narrative_text: That night, Emperor Akbar woke up in fear. It was not a battle. It was not a rebellion. Yet Emperor Akbar sat upright in his bed, his heart racing. He had seen a dream — a dream so strange that sleep refused to return. By morning, Akbar knew one thing with certainty: This dream was not ordinary… and only Birbal could explain it. In his dream, Akbar stood inside a magnificent Mughal palace. Golden pillars touched the sky. The throne shone brighter than the sun. Suddenly, a deep crack echoed. The walls trembled. The floor split apart. And Akbar’s crown slipped from his head and fell into darkness. Before he could move, the palace collapsed. Akbar woke up. Sweat covered his face. Silence filled the room. ‘Dreams do not disturb kings without reason,’ he whispered. At sunrise, he summoned Birbal. Birbal arrived at the court. Akbar described every detail — the palace, the cracks, the falling crown. Birbal did not interrupt. He did not rush to answer. He listened. Then he said softly: ‘Jahapanah, dreams do not warn rulers about enemies outside… They warn them about dangers within.’ Akbar looked up, startled. Birbal explained calmly: ‘The palace is your kingdom. The cracks are ignored truths. And the falling crown is power without balance.’ The court fell silent. Birbal continued: ‘When a ruler stops listening, his rule begins to shake — even if no enemy is near.’ This was not praise. This was wisdom. Akbar remained quiet. Then he asked: ‘Birbal… am I losing my way?’ Birbal replied: ‘Only those who reflect can rule for long.’ That day, Akbar changed. He listened more. He judged less. He ruled with awareness, not pride. And the dream never returned.
moral: True power survives only when it listens.

performance_notes.opening_line: That night, Emperor Akbar woke up in fear.

title: Tenali Raman: The Story of the Magical Fan
alt_titles: Tenali Raman and the Enchanted Fan
The Clever Jester and the Mystic Fan
narrative_text:
In the glorious kingdom of Vijayanagara, ruled by the wise King Krishnadevaraya, lived the renowned jester and poet, Tenali Raman. His wit was as sharp as a diamond, and his solutions to problems were always unexpected and brilliant. One sweltering summer, when the heat was unbearable and the royal court felt like a furnace, a traveling sage arrived, bearing a peculiar gift for the King: a fan, unlike any seen before. 

'Your Majesty,' the sage announced, 'this is no ordinary fan. It is a magical fan, imbued with ancient blessings. With a single wave, it can summon the gentlest breeze, and with two waves, a refreshing shower of cool mist. It will bring comfort to your kingdom during these scorching days.'

The King was delighted. He tested the fan, and indeed, a cool breeze filled the court, followed by a fine, refreshing mist. The courtiers gasped in wonder, and the King praised the sage generously. However, the sage, seeing the King's joy, began to boast. 'No one in this kingdom possesses such a magical item, nor the wisdom to wield it as I do. Only I can truly understand its power.'

The courtiers, always eager to impress, began to flatter the sage, agreeing with his every word. Tenali Raman, however, remained silent, a thoughtful smile playing on his lips. The sage, noticing Tenali's quiet demeanor, felt challenged. 'Why so quiet, Jester? Do you not believe in the magic of this fan?' he asked, with a hint of disdain.

Tenali Raman bowed respectfully. 'Indeed, esteemed sage, the fan is truly remarkable. But I believe its magic lies not just in its ability to create a breeze or mist, but in the wisdom of its wielder. Anyone can wave a fan, but to know when and how to use it for the greatest good, that is the true magic.'

The sage scoffed. 'And you, Jester, claim to possess such wisdom?'

'Perhaps,' Tenali replied calmly. 'But I propose a small demonstration. Let us see who can bring the most comfort to the people of Vijayanagara using this fan.'

The King, intrigued, agreed. The sage, confident in his magical fan, went to the marketplace and waved it grandly, creating a powerful gust of wind that scattered wares and startled the people. He then summoned a heavy mist, drenching everyone and causing confusion. The people, though initially impressed, soon grew annoyed by the sage's haphazard use of the fan.

When it was Tenali Raman's turn, he took the fan and walked to the royal gardens, where many elderly citizens sought shade. He sat among them, and instead of waving the fan wildly, he gently fanned a small group, creating a soft, continuous breeze. He spoke to them, listened to their stories, and offered words of comfort. When a child cried from the heat, he gently fanned the child, bringing a smile to its face. He used the fan sparingly, thoughtfully, and always with a purpose.

Later that evening, the King asked the people whom they found more comforting. The people unanimously chose Tenali Raman. 'The sage's magic was grand, Your Majesty,' an old woman explained, 'but Tenali Raman's fanning brought true relief and kindness. He understood our needs.'

The King turned to the sage. 'You see, sage, true magic is not just about power, but about compassion and understanding. Tenali Raman, with his simple act of fanning and his profound empathy, showed us the real magic of this fan.'

The sage, humbled, realized his mistake. He had focused on the spectacle, while Tenali Raman had focused on the welfare of the people. From that day forward, the magical fan was kept in the court, and Tenali Raman often used it, not for grand displays, but for quiet acts of kindness, reminding everyone that true wisdom lies in using one's abilities for the benefit of others.
moral: True wisdom lies not just in possessing power or special abilities, but in using them with compassion, understanding, and for the genuine benefit of others.

title: Vikram-Betal: The story of the princess and the four suitors
narrative_text: In the ancient city of Pataliputra, there lived a King who had a daughter of marriageable age. The princess was renowned for her beauty and intelligence. The King announced that whoever could win his daughter's heart through a display of exceptional skill would win her hand in marriage. Four suitors, all learned in different sciences, came forward. The first was a master carpenter who claimed he could build a flying vehicle. The second was a textile master who could weave a cloth that could make the wearer invisible. The third was a metallurgist who could create a mirror that could show a person's true nature. The fourth was a physician who could bring the dead back to life. The King, intrigued, decided to test their skills. He asked the carpenter to build his flying machine. The carpenter built a magnificent wooden bird that could fly. The King then asked the textile master to weave his invisible cloth. The textile master wove a fabric so fine it was invisible to the naked eye. The King then asked the metallurgist to create his truth-revealing mirror. The metallurgist forged a mirror that reflected not the physical appearance but the inner character of a person. Finally, the King, to test the physician, had a servant pretend to be dead. The physician, with his knowledge of herbs and medicine, was able to revive the servant. The King was impressed by all four suitors and their incredible skills. He did not know who to choose for his daughter. He presented the dilemma to the wise King Vikramaditya. Betal, the spirit, then asked King Vikramaditya, 'To whom should the girl be given?' Vikramaditya replied that the girl should be given to the physician. The others, he explained, had created objects of wonder, but the physician had the power to save a life, which is the greatest skill of all.
moral: The greatest skill is the one that can preserve life.

title: Mahabharata: The Story of Abhimanyu
narrative_text:
In the ancient land of Bharatavarsha, amidst the grand saga of the Mahabharata, lived a young warrior named Abhimanyu. He was the beloved son of Arjuna, the peerless archer and one of the five Pandava brothers, and Subhadra, the gentle sister of Lord Krishna. Abhimanyu's early life was spent in Dvārakā, under the loving care of his maternal family, including his illustrious uncles, Krishna and Balarama. It is said that even before his birth, while still in his mother's womb, Abhimanyu acquired a unique knowledge: the secret to entering the formidable Chakravyuha, a complex military formation that few could penetrate. However, destiny, in its mysterious ways, ensured he did not learn the method to exit it.

As the Pandavas endured their thirteen years of exile, Abhimanyu grew into a strong and virtuous young man. Upon his father's return, he was married to Uttarā, the beautiful princess of the Matsya Kingdom, forging an important alliance for the Pandavas.

The Kurukshetra War, a cataclysmic conflict between the Pandavas and their Kaurava cousins, eventually erupted. On the thirteenth day of this devastating war, the Kaurava commander, Dronacharya, devised the impenetrable Chakravyuha formation to capture Yudhishthira, the eldest Pandava. Knowing that only Arjuna and Abhimanyu possessed the knowledge to breach it, the Kauravas strategically diverted Arjuna to another part of the battlefield.

With Arjuna absent, the burden fell upon young Abhimanyu. Despite knowing he could only enter and not exit the formation, his unwavering courage and commitment to dharma compelled him to accept the challenge. Yudhishthira, trusting in Abhimanyu's prowess and believing other Pandava warriors would follow to assist, gave his consent. Abhimanyu, with his charioteer Sumitra, fearlessly charged into the swirling depths of the Chakravyuha.

Inside the labyrinthine formation, Abhimanyu fought with astonishing valor, a beacon of bravery against a multitude of seasoned warriors. He single-handedly defeated many formidable opponents, including Duryodhana, Dushasana, Drona, Karna, and Shakuni, and slew countless soldiers. His prowess on the battlefield was legendary, and he carved a path of destruction through the Kaurava ranks.

However, the tide of battle turned when Jayadratha, the king of Sindhu, used a boon from Lord Shiva to hold back the other Pandava warriors, preventing them from entering the Chakravyuha to aid Abhimanyu. Isolated and surrounded, Abhimanyu faced an unfair and brutal assault. Six great *maharathis*—Dronacharya, Karna, Duryodhana, Dushasana, Kripacharya, and Shakuni—attacked him simultaneously, violating all established rules of warfare. They destroyed his chariot, killed his horses, and broke his weapons. Even then, Abhimanyu did not yield. He picked up a chariot wheel and continued to fight, a lone warrior against an unrighteous multitude.

Finally, exhausted and overwhelmed, Abhimanyu succumbed to his wounds, struck down by Dushasana's son. He fell as a martyr, a sixteen-year-old hero whose sacrifice ignited a fierce resolve in the Pandavas and irrevocably altered the course of the war. Though his life was tragically cut short, Abhimanyu's legacy lived on through his posthumous son, Parikshit, who later became a revered king and continued the Kuru lineage.

Abhimanyu's story is a poignant reminder of courage, sacrifice, and the harsh realities of war. It teaches us that true heroism lies not just in victory, but in facing insurmountable odds with unwavering determination and upholding one's duty, even unto death. His tale inspires us to stand firm in our convictions and fight for righteousness, regardless of the challenges we face.
moral: True heroism lies in unwavering courage and commitment to duty, even in the face of insurmountable odds and tragic sacrifice. Upholding dharma, even when others resort to unrighteousness, is the ultimate measure of a warrior's spirit.

title: The Birth of the River Yamuna
narrative_text:
In the celestial realms, under the radiant gaze of the Sun God, Surya, and his gentle consort, Sanjana, a divine daughter was born. Her name was Yamuna, and she was the twin sister to Yama, the formidable god of death. From her very inception, Yamuna possessed a serene and compassionate spirit, a stark contrast to her brother's grave duties. She was also known as Kalindi, a name that would forever be associated with her earthly origin. 

As the ages unfolded, the Earth yearned for a sacred presence, a river that could cleanse sins and bestow blessings upon humanity. The revered Seven Sages, with their profound wisdom and unwavering devotion, undertook arduous ascetic practices, performing intense tapas to invoke the divine. Their prayers reached the heavens, and in response, the benevolent Yamuna agreed to descend to the mortal realm. 

Her journey began from the majestic Mount Kalinda, a peak touched by the sun's first rays, thus earning her the epithet Kalindi. As she flowed forth, her waters were pure and pristine, reflecting the azure skies. However, a poignant event in the cosmic dance of deities forever altered her appearance. After the tragic demise of Sati, the daughter of Daksha, Lord Shiva was consumed by an unbearable grief. In his distraught state, he wandered the worlds, his heart heavy with sorrow. When Kama, the god of love, attempted to stir emotions within him, Shiva, in his intense anguish, plunged into the clear waters of the nascent Yamuna. This profound immersion of the grieving deity caused her waters to turn a deep, mystical black, a color she has carried ever since, symbolizing both sorrow and profound spiritual depth. 

Despite this transformation, the River Yamuna continued her benevolent course, flowing through the plains of Bharatavarsha, bestowing life and purity. The Puranas extol her greatness, declaring that a mere bath in her holy waters can absolve one of sins and lead to the celestial abode. A tale from the Padma Purana recounts how Vikuṇḍala, a man who had strayed from the path of virtue, found salvation and attained heaven simply by bathing in the Yamuna for two months, a testament to her immense purifying power. 

Today, Goddess Yamuna is revered as a divine mother, often depicted riding a tortoise, carrying a water pot, and embodying her distinctive black complexion. She is not merely a river but a living goddess, a symbol of purity, devotion, and the eternal flow of divine grace, guiding souls towards liberation and spiritual fulfillment.
moral: The River Yamuna teaches us that even in sorrow and transformation, there is profound grace and the power to cleanse and uplift. Her waters remind us that devotion and purity of heart can lead to ultimate salvation.

title: The Foolish Weaver
narrative_text: Once upon a time in India, there lived a poor weaver. This weaver did not have many helpers, so he had to do all the work by himself. One day, as he was weaving, his weaving frame broke. The poor man became sad and went to the woods nearby. There he looked at the trees and decided on one of them to cut up. Just as he was preparing to chop the tree, he heard a voice, "Halt!" The weaver turned around and found a genie swirling in the air. "Don’t cut this tree," the genie said. Now, the poor weaver had to cut it because if he did not chop that tree, there would be no weaving frame. And if there was no weaving frame, the weaver and his family would starve. So, the weaver told the genie about his reasons for chopping the tree. But the genie then told the weaver that the tree he was going to chop was his home. And if the weaver did so, then the genie would become homeless. After much discussion, the genie then suggested, "Would you stop if I grant you a wish?" Now, the weaver had no idea what to ask for. So, he asked the genie if he could take the advice of his family or friends. The genie agreed. The weaver hurried back. "My wife would probably have a better idea. And we may be able to use this chance to make our lives better," the weaver thought. But little did he know that his wife was even dumber. When the weaver narrated the entire incident, the wife instantly told him to ask for more pairs of hands and more pairs of legs. Her logic was that these new pairs would help the weaver do his work faster. So, the foolish weaver hurried back to the genie and he repeated what his wife had advised him. The genie granted the weaver his wish. As a result, the weaver had many hands and legs. He hurried back to his village. But when the people saw the weaver, they thought that he was some monster. They beat him up with axes and sticks. And very soon, the weaver lay dead.
moral: Lack of good judgment ruins a good opportunity.

...The genie granted the weaver his wish.

safety_notes: The story contains a violent ending where the weaver is beaten to death. This should be delivered with a calm and cautionary tone, emphasizing the moral lesson rather than the violence itself. Ensure the listener is prepared for a cautionary tale.

title: The Story of the Greedy Jackal
narrative_text:
In a lush forest, a gentle Deer lived peacefully. He had a wise friend, a Crow named Sharp-sense. One day, a cunning Jackal, known as Small-wit, approached the Deer, feigning friendship. The Jackal, however, harbored a greedy desire to feast on the Deer's flesh. Despite the Crow's warnings about trusting strangers, the Deer, being kind-hearted, accepted the Jackal's companionship. 

One day, Small-wit led the Deer to a field of tender wheat, knowing it was a farmer's trap. The unsuspecting Deer enjoyed the feast daily until he was caught in a snare. The Jackal, lurking nearby, rejoiced at his successful scheme, anticipating a grand meal. The Deer, distressed, called out for help, hoping his new friend, the Jackal, would free him. But Small-wit, revealing his true nature, claimed it was a fast day and he couldn't bite the sinew ropes, promising to return the next morning.

The Crow, realizing the Deer was missing, searched for him and found him trapped. The Deer confessed his folly in ignoring the Crow's advice. As dawn approached, the Crow spotted the farmer with a club. Quickly, Sharp-sense instructed the Deer to feign death: to puff up his belly, stiffen his legs, and lie still. The Crow then pretended to peck at the Deer's eyes. 

The farmer, seeing the seemingly dead Deer, released him from the snare and began to gather his nets. At that moment, the Crow let out a loud caw, and the Deer sprang up and fled. The enraged farmer, in his haste, flung his club, which struck the lurking Jackal, killing him instantly. Thus, the greedy Jackal met his end, a victim of his own deceit and avarice. The story teaches us the importance of choosing our friends wisely and the perils of greed.
moral: Greed and deceit ultimately lead to one's downfall. It is important to choose friends wisely and heed good advice.

title: The Monkey and the Crocodile
narrative_text: A monkey lived in a great tree on a river bank. In the river there were many Crocodiles. A Crocodile watched the Monkeys for a long time, and one day she said to her son: "My son, get one of those Monkeys for me. I want the heart of a Monkey to eat." "How am I to catch a Monkey?" asked the little Crocodile. "I do not travel on land, and the Monkey does not go into the water." "Put your wits to work, and you'll find a way," said the mother. And the little Crocodile thought and thought. At last he said to himself: "I know what I'll do. I'll get that Monkey that lives in a big tree on the river bank. He wishes to go across the river to the island where the fruit is so ripe." So the Crocodile swam to the tree where the Monkey lived. But he was a stupid Crocodile. "Oh, Monkey," he called, "come with me over to the island where the fruit is so ripe." "How can I go with you?" asked the Monkey. "I do not swim." "No—but I do. I will take you over on my back," said the Crocodile. The Monkey was greedy, and wanted the ripe fruit, so he jumped down on the Crocodile's back. "Off we go!" said the Crocodile. "This is a fine ride you are giving me!" said the Monkey. "Do you think so? Well, how do you like this?" asked the Crocodile, diving. "Oh, don't!" cried the Monkey, as he went under the water. He was afraid to let go, and he did not know what to do under the water. When the Crocodile came up, the Monkey sputtered and choked. "Why did you take me under water, Crocodile?" he asked. "I am going to kill you by keeping you under water," answered the Crocodile. "My mother wants Monkey-heart to eat, and I'm going to take yours to her." "Why did you take me under water, Crocodile?" he asked. "I wish you had told me you wanted my heart," said the Monkey, "then I might have brought it with me." "How queer!" said the stupid Crocodile. "Do you mean to say that you left your heart back there in the tree?" "That is what I mean," said the Monkey. "If you want my heart, we must go back to the tree and get it. But we are so near the island where the ripe fruit is, please take me there first." "No, Monkey," said the Crocodile, "I'll take you straight back to your tree. Never mind the ripe fruit. Get your heart and bring it to me at once. Then we'll see about going to the island." "Very well," said the Monkey. But no sooner had he jumped onto the bank of the river than—whisk! up he ran into the tree. From the topmost branches he called down to the Crocodile in the water below: "My heart is way up here! If you want it, come for it, come for it!" The monkey soon moved away from that tree. He wanted to get away from the Crocodile, so he went to live in another tree, on another river bank. This time he chose a tree that grew on the bank of a river where there were no crocodiles. One day, as he sat eating fruit, he saw a Crocodile swimming in the river. It was the same Crocodile that had tried to trick him before. The Crocodile looked up at the Monkey and said: "Hello, Monkey! How are you?" "I am very well, thank you," said the Monkey. "And how are you?" "I am very hungry," said the Crocodile. "I have not had anything to eat for a long time. Will you throw me some fruit?" "Certainly," said the Monkey, and he threw down some ripe fruit for the Crocodile. The Crocodile ate the fruit and thanked the Monkey. "You are a very kind Monkey," said the Crocodile. "I would like to be your friend." "I would like to be your friend too," said the Monkey. So the Monkey and the Crocodile became friends. Every day the Monkey would throw fruit down to the Crocodile, and the Crocodile would eat it and thank the Monkey. They would talk for a long time, and the Monkey would tell the Crocodile stories. One day the Crocodile said to the Monkey: "My wife is very ill. The doctor says that she needs to eat the heart of a Monkey to get well. Will you give me your heart, my friend?" "Oh, dear!" said the Monkey. "I am so sorry to hear that your wife is ill. But I cannot give you my heart. I left it at home, in my other tree." "How can that be?" asked the Crocodile. "Do you mean to say that you have two hearts?" "No," said the Monkey. "I have only one heart, but I can take it out and leave it at home when I go out. It is much safer that way." "Oh, I see," said the Crocodile. "Well, then, will you go and get your heart for me?" "Certainly," said the Monkey. "But you must take me over to my other tree, because I cannot swim." "Very well," said the Crocodile. "Jump on my back, and I will take you there." So the Monkey jumped on the Crocodile's back, and the Crocodile swam across the river. When they reached the other side, the Monkey jumped off and ran up into his tree. From the topmost branches he called down to the Crocodile: "My heart is way up here! If you want it, come for it, come for it!" The Crocodile realized that the Monkey had tricked him again. He was very angry, but he could do nothing. He swam away, and the Monkey never saw him again.
moral: Always use your wits to escape from danger. Never trust those who have once betrayed you.

performance_notes.opening_line: A monkey lived in a great tree on a river bank.
pause_points: In the river there were many Crocodiles.
"Put your wits to work, and you'll find a way," said the mother.
So the Crocodile swam to the tree where the Monkey lived. But he was a stupid Crocodile.
The Monkey was greedy, and wanted the ripe fruit, so he jumped down on the Crocodile's back.
When the Crocodile came up, the Monkey sputtered and choked. "Why did you take me under water, Crocodile?" he asked.
"I am going to kill you by keeping you under water," answered the Crocodile. "My mother wants Monkey-heart to eat, and I'm going to take yours to her."
"That is what I mean," said the Monkey. "If you want my heart, we must go back to the tree and get it. But we are so near the island where the ripe fruit is, please take me there first."
But no sooner had he jumped onto the bank of the river than—whisk! up he ran into the tree.
The monkey soon moved away from that tree.
One day, as he sat eating fruit, he saw a Crocodile swimming in the river.
So the Monkey and the Crocodile became friends.
One day the Crocodile said to the Monkey: "My wife is very ill. The doctor says that she needs to eat the heart of a Monkey to get well. Will you give me your heart, my friend?"
"No," said the Monkey. "I have only one heart, but I can take it out and leave it at home when I go out. It is much safer that way."
So the Monkey jumped on the Crocodile's back, and the Crocodile swam across the river.
The Crocodile realized that the Monkey had tricked him again. He was very angry, but he could do nothing. He swam away, and the Monkey never saw him again.

title: The Parrot Neither Eats, Nor Drinks...
alt_titles: Akbar-Birbal: The story of the wise parrot
narrative_text:
A person was very fond of parrots. He used to catch parrots, train them and sell them to interested people. One time he got a good parrot, so he trained him in talking, and when he was trained he presented him to the king. King liked the parrot because he used to reply his questions too.

The king made special arrangements for his safety, security and care and warned the people if somebody will tell me about his death, he will be hung. Hearing this the parrot was kept under very special care. But it so happened one day that suddenly the parrot died. Now who should go and inform this to king? because whoever will inform the King about the death of the parrot to the King, he will be sentenced to death and who would like to die just for informing the King about the death of a parrot.

So the servant went to Birbal and told him his plight. He said - "If I inform him about the parrot's death, then he will kill me, but if I do not tell him, then also he will kill me, so please save me."

Birbal thought for a while and sent him back to his work, and he himself went to the King and said - "Sir, Your parrot..."
The King asked -"What about parrot?"
Birbal again stammered - "Your parrot, Sir."
The King asked - "Birbal, What happened to my parrot?"
Birbal again said - "Your parrot..."
The King asked - "I am asking you, what happened to  my parrot? Say something about him in the name of Allaah."

Birbal said - "Jehaanpanaah, Your parrot neither eats anything, nor drinks water, nor speaks, nor moves his feathers, nor opens his eyes..." The King said - "What? Has the parrot died?" Birbal said - "I did not say it. You said it."

King understood why Birbal had to say this in this way. He got very happy with Birbal's way of telling him about his parrot.
moral: The story highlights Birbal's wit and cleverness in delivering bad news indirectly, saving the servant from the king's wrath. It teaches the importance of tact and thoughtful communication in difficult situations.

pause_points: ...presented him to the king.
...he will be hung.
...please save me.
...Your parrot, Sir.
...You said it.

title: Tenali Rama and The Thieves
narrative_text: One calm night, Tenali Rama was resting at home. The moon was shining brightly. The cool breeze blew gently. Suddenly, Tenali Rama saw somebody moving from the nearby bushes. He could see two shadowy figures in the dark, hiding themselves behind the bushes. Tenali Rama concluded them to be thieves and decided to teach them a lesson. He went inside his house and spoke to his wife, "Listen Dear...." He continued loudly, "We need to safeguard all our valuables from theft. Bring a metal box." The thieves who heard all these words smiled at each other happily. Tenali Rama then whispered to his wife about his plan. He asked her to bring some bricks and put them in the metal box. He carried the metal box on his head and took it to the well in the backyard. He kept the box on the wall of the well and said, "My dear wife....We must be careful. Thieves are waiting to steal our wealth. I want to safeguard the jewels and the money from them. This is the safest place in our house to safeguard our wealth." Saying these words to his wife, he dropped the box into the well. The box fell down into the water with a loud noise. S.....P.....L.....A.....S.....H.....! Tenali Rama and his wife slept peacefully. The thieves watching all this hugged each other happily. AT midnight, the thieves went near the well and looked into it. The well was deep and had a lot of water. They could not jump into it. So, both of them decided to drain the well. So, they drew water with a bucket from the well and poured it into the nearby garden. They had to do it quietly. Throughout the night, they drew water from the well. They became tired. Further they hardly drain the well. They sat for a while to take rest. AT dawn, Tenali Rama awoke and came near the well. On seeing him both the thieves trembled in fear. Tenali Rama spoke to them gently, "Dear Brothers! Thank you so much for watering my plants all through the night! I want to reward both of you in the king’s palace for the services you have rendered to me." The thieves were terribly shocked and ran away from the house of Tenali Rama.
moral: Cleverness and wit can outsmart brute force and ill intentions.

Saying these words to his wife, he dropped the box into the well.
Throughout the night, they drew water from the well.
On seeing him both the thieves trembled in fear.

title: Vikram-Betal: The Riddle of the Yaksha's Pond
alt_titles: The King, the Vampire, and the Wise Questions
Betal's Eleventh Tale: The Test of Dharma
narrative_text:
In the ancient kingdom of Ujjain, ruled the valiant and wise King Vikramaditya. One day, a powerful sorcerer commanded Vikram to retrieve a mystical vampire, Betal, from a distant cremation ground. Each time Vikram captured Betal and carried him on his shoulder, Betal would narrate a story, ending with a riddle. If Vikram answered correctly, Betal would return to his tree; if he remained silent, his head would burst. But if he answered incorrectly, Betal would also escape. Thus, Vikram was bound to listen and respond with wisdom.

On one such journey, as the moon cast long shadows and the night air was thick with mystery, Betal began his tale:

"In a land far away, lived a revered sage known for his profound wisdom. He had four disciples, who were brothers, each excelling in a different virtue: one in strength, another in knowledge, the third in devotion, and the fourth in compassion. One scorching summer, a severe drought gripped their region. The rivers dried, the wells became barren, and the people suffered immensely. The sage, seeing the plight of his disciples and the villagers, sent the four brothers to find water.

They journeyed for days, their throats parched and their bodies weary, until they stumbled upon a serene pond, shimmering under the moonlight. As they rushed towards it, a colossal and fearsome Yaksha, the guardian of the pond, emerged from the depths. His eyes glowed like embers, and his voice boomed, 'This pond is mine! None shall drink from it without first answering my questions. Those who dare to drink without my permission shall perish!'

The first brother, strong and impetuous, scoffed at the Yaksha's warning and drank greedily. Immediately, he fell lifeless to the ground. The second brother, confident in his knowledge, approached the Yaksha and declared, 'I shall answer your questions!' But when the Yaksha posed a riddle about the nature of illusion, the brother, though learned, became entangled in philosophical debate and could not provide a clear answer. He too fell dead.

The third brother, filled with devotion, prayed to the gods for guidance. He then humbly approached the Yaksha, offering reverence. The Yaksha, slightly appeased by his humility, asked, 'What is heavier than the earth, higher than the heavens, swifter than the wind, and more numerous than blades of grass?' The devoted brother, lost in his prayer, could only speak of divine mysteries, failing to grasp the practical essence of the riddle. He also succumbed.

Finally, the fourth brother, whose heart was overflowing with compassion, approached the Yaksha. He did not immediately seek to drink or answer. Instead, he knelt beside his fallen brothers, tears streaming down his face. He looked at the Yaksha not with fear, but with sorrow for the suffering of all beings. 'O mighty Yaksha,' he pleaded, 'My brothers perished not from malice, but from thirst and a misunderstanding of your sacred rules. I seek not only water for myself but also understanding, so that I may bring solace to my village and honor your guardianship.'

The Yaksha, moved by the brother's genuine compassion, softened his gaze. He then posed his riddle to the compassionate brother: 'What is the greatest wonder in the world?'

The compassionate brother pondered for a moment, then replied, 'Day after day, countless beings die, yet the living wish to live forever. What can be a greater wonder than this?'

The Yaksha was pleased. 'Your answer is profound and true,' he boomed. 'You have understood the essence of existence and the folly of attachment. You may drink, and your brothers shall be restored to life.' And so, the compassionate brother drank, and his brothers miraculously revived, and they returned to their village with water and a newfound understanding of true wisdom.

Now, King Vikram, tell me," concluded Betal, "of the four brothers, who was the wisest?"

King Vikram, having listened intently, replied, "The fourth brother, the one with compassion, was the wisest. For true wisdom lies not merely in strength, knowledge, or devotion alone, but in the understanding that empathy and the desire to alleviate suffering are the highest forms of insight. His compassion allowed him to connect with the Yaksha on a deeper level, leading to the salvation of all."

Betal, satisfied with the King's just and insightful answer, chuckled and once again slipped from Vikram's shoulder, returning to his tree, leaving the king to continue his arduous journey.
moral: True wisdom is not merely intellectual prowess or physical strength, but the profound understanding that compassion and empathy are the highest virtues, capable of resolving even the most complex dilemmas and bringing about the greatest good.

performance_notes.opening_line: In the ancient kingdom of Ujjain, ruled the valiant and wise King Vikramaditya.
pause_points: Thus, Vikram was bound to listen and respond with wisdom.
'This pond is mine! None shall drink from it without first answering my questions. Those who dare to drink without my permission shall perish!'
He too fell dead.
He also succumbed.
'What is the greatest wonder in the world?'
'Day after day, countless beings die, yet the living wish to live forever. What can be a greater wonder than this?'
'Now, King Vikram, tell me, of the four brothers, who was the wisest?'

safety_notes: The story contains themes of death and peril, but these are resolved positively through wisdom and compassion, making it suitable for elderly listeners. The Yaksha is portrayed as a guardian, not purely malevolent.

title: The Clever Barber
narrative_text:
Once upon a time, in a small village, there lived a barber who was known for his cleverness and wit. He wasn’t very wealthy, but he had an immense reputation for his quick thinking and problem-solving abilities.

One day, the barber was on his way to the palace to cut the king’s hair. As he walked through the forest, he came across a large tree where he decided to rest for a while. He sat under the tree, and soon, he fell into a deep sleep.

While he was asleep, seven robbers passed by and saw him. They noticed the bag he was carrying and decided to rob him. They gently picked up the bag and checked its contents. To their surprise, they found only barbering tools and a few coins. Disappointed, they decided to wake him up and ask him if he had any hidden wealth.

The barber woke up to find himself surrounded by the robbers. They demanded that he hand over all his valuables. The clever barber quickly thought of a plan. He calmly said, “I am just a poor barber, but if you let me go, I can show you a treasure hidden in the forest that will make you all rich beyond your wildest dreams.”

Intrigued, the robbers agreed to let the barber lead them to the treasure. He took them deep into the forest, walking for hours, until they reached a large boulder. The barber pointed to the boulder and said, “The treasure is buried under this rock. But it is cursed, and only I know the way to safely dig it up. If you try to do it without me, you will all be turned into stone.”

The robbers, fearing the curse, agreed to let the barber dig up the treasure. The barber pretended to dig around the boulder, all the while thinking of a way to escape. After some time, he told the robbers, “The treasure is very deep. I need all of you to help me dig. We need to be very careful to avoid the curse.”

The robbers, eager to get their hands on the treasure, started digging furiously. The clever barber, taking advantage of their distraction, quietly slipped away and ran back to the village.

When the robbers finally realized they had been tricked, it was too late. They had dug a deep hole but found no treasure. Angry and disappointed, they abandoned their search and left the forest.

The barber returned to the palace, and when the king heard about his clever escape from the robbers, he was highly impressed. The king rewarded the barber with a bag of gold coins and praised him for his quick thinking and bravery.

From that day on, the barber’s reputation as a clever and resourceful man spread far and wide, and he lived a comfortable and respected life in the village.
moral: Intelligence and wit can often be more valuable than wealth or strength.

...if you try to do it without me, you will all be turned into stone.

title: The Brahmin and the Crooks
alt_titles: The Foolish Brahmin and His Goat
The Brahmin and the Three Crooks
narrative_text:
A brahmin named Mitra Sharma lived in a small village, and one day he performed rituals for a rich farmer who gave him a goat as a gift. When he was returning to his village, carrying the goat on his back, three crooks spotted him. The crooks were starving and wanted to cook the goat as their meal, so they hatched a plan to take away the goat from the brahmin.

The plan was to scare the brahmin so that he left the goat and ran away. To do this, one of the crooks met the brahmin on the way and asked why a pious person like the brahmin was carrying a dog. The brahmin gets indignant and carried forward with the goat. The second crook then asked the brahmin why he had a dead calf on his shoulders, and the brahmin lost his temper again and walked away.

But now the brahmin was puzzled, and a seed of doubt had crept in his mind. Lastly, the third rogue met the brahmin and he named the goat a pig and asked why a sacred priest was carrying a profane pig. This made the brahmin very anxious as by now he started wondering if the animal was actually a monster who changed shapes from time to time. He was extremely frightened and left the goat on the road to run away to his home.

The crooks then came together and carried the goat, laughing at the stupidity of the so-called highly educated brahmin. Thereafter they happily feasted on the goat.
moral: The story conveys the message that acquiring bookish knowledge is not sufficient but one should also have common sense. It also highlights how beliefs can be exploited, and the importance of not letting fear of the unknown force us to act in a way that can end in our loss.

pause_points: ...so they hatched a plan to take away the goat from the brahmin.
...the brahmin lost his temper again and walked away.
...He was extremely frightened and left the goat on the road to run away to his home.

safety_notes: The story touches upon the caste system and its potential for exploitation. When narrating, ensure to frame this aspect sensitively, emphasizing the moral of common sense over blind belief, and clarifying that such exploitative practices are not condoned.

title: Kabir and the Jealous Fakir
narrative_text: One day, a Mohammedan Fakir named Jahangast, who had heard of Kabir's great fame, decided to visit him. However, Jahangast harbored jealousy towards Kabir. Knowing this, Kabir quickly tied a fat pig at the entrance of his hut. When Jahangast arrived and saw the pig, he was repulsed by the sight and refused to enter Kabir's compound, turning to leave. Kabir called out to him, "O great Fakir! Why are you running away? I have merely tied an unclean pig at my door. But you, you carry what is truly unclean—anger, pride, greed, and jealousy—within your heart." Hearing these words, Jahangast was struck with shame. He bowed his head, asked for Kabir's forgiveness, and immediately became his devoted disciple, realizing the profound truth in Kabir's teaching about inner purity.
moral: The true impurity lies not in external appearances or circumstances, but in the negative emotions and vices harbored within one's heart. Inner purity and humility are far more valuable than outward displays of piety.

title: The Story Behind Vasant Panchami
narrative_text:
Once upon a time, Lord Brahma, the creator of the world, desired to witness his creation. However, upon observing it, he was met with an unsettling silence. No sounds of life, no chirping birds, only a profound sadness pervaded the earth.  

Disturbed by this stillness, Brahma conceived an idea. He held a pot of water, and from it, he sprinkled a few drops into the air. From these droplets, miraculously, emerged Goddess Saraswati, holding a veena in her hands. 

Brahma implored her to play, for the silence was unbearable. Saraswati, with her divine music, not only blessed the inhabitants of the earth with melodies but also bestowed upon them the gift of voice. 

From that day forward, she became revered as the Goddess of knowledge, music, art, speech, wisdom, and learning. This is why Saraswati is always depicted holding a book, a mala (rosary), a water pot, and her veena. 

Vasant Panchami is celebrated as a tribute to her, marking her appearance and the arrival of spring, when agricultural fields begin to blossom with vibrant mustard flowers.
moral: The story of Vasant Panchami highlights the importance of knowledge, art, and speech in bringing life and joy to the world, and the divine origin of these blessings.

pause_points: ...profound sadness pervaded the earth.
...bestowed upon them the gift of voice.
...vibrant mustard flowers.

title: Mahabharata: The Story of the Pandavas' Exile
narrative_text:
After losing a game of dice to their cousins, the Kauravas, the five Pandava brothers and their wife Draupadi were exiled for thirteen years. For twelve of those years, they wandered through forests, living a life of hardship and austerity. As the thirteenth year approached, they faced their greatest challenge yet: to live in a city, in disguise, without being discovered. If they were found, their exile would begin anew. 

The Pandavas chose the kingdom of Matsya, ruled by the virtuous King Virata. Each of them took on a new identity to serve in the royal court. Yudhishthira, the eldest, became Kanka, a Brahmin skilled in the game of dice, and served as the king's companion. Bhima, the mighty warrior, became Vallabha, a cook in the royal kitchens, where he could satisfy his great appetite and entertain the king with his wrestling skills. Arjuna, the master archer, took on the guise of Brihannala, a eunuch, and taught the royal princess the arts of music and dance. Nakula, the handsome one, became Granthika, a keeper of the royal horses, and Sahadeva, the wise one, became Tantipala, a tender of the royal cattle. Draupadi, their queen, became Sairandhri, a hairdresser to the queen of Matsya. 

For a year, they lived in the court of King Virata, their true identities hidden beneath their humble disguises. They performed their duties with diligence and humility, earning the trust and affection of the king and his court. They faced many challenges and close calls, but through their cleverness and the blessings of the gods, they remained undiscovered. The moral of their story is that even in the face of great adversity, one must remain steadfast in their duty and have faith that righteousness will ultimately prevail. Their year of exile teaches us the importance of humility, perseverance, and the ability to adapt to any circumstance.
moral: Perseverance and righteousness in the face of adversity will ultimately lead to triumph.

pause_points: ...their exile would begin anew.
...and taught the royal princess the arts of music and dance.
...they remained undiscovered.

safety_notes: The story is a classic epic and is safe for all audiences.
