title: The Story of Sage Durvasa
narrative_text:
In the ancient lands of Bharatavarsha, among the revered sages, lived Durvasa, a muni known for his profound spiritual powers and, equally, for his quick temper. His very being was said to be an "aṃśa," a part or aspect, of Lord Shiva's fiery energy. 

One prominent account of his birth tells of a time when a quarrel arose between Lord Brahma and Lord Shiva. Witnessing Shiva's intense rage, even Goddess Parvati became frightened, exclaiming, 'Durvāsam bhavati me!' (It has become impossible for me to live happily with you). Realizing his anger caused distress, Shiva decided to transfer this trait to another. 

This incident coincided with the tale of Śīlavatī, a woman renowned for her unwavering chastity. When the muni Aṇumāṇḍavya cursed her leper husband, Ugraśravas, to die, Śīlavatī, in her devotion, countered with a curse that prevented the sun from rising. Chaos ensued until the Trimūrtis (Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva) and the Devas intervened through Anasuyā, the virtuous wife of Atrimaharṣi. Pleased by Anasuyā's piety, the Trimūrtis offered her a boon. She wished for them to be born as her partial incarnations. Thus, Brahma was born as Candra, Vishnu as Dattātreya, and Shiva deposited his fiery anger into Anasuyā, leading to the birth of Durvasa. His name, 'Durvasas,' meaning 'one difficult to live with,' perfectly reflected the circumstances of his birth, stemming from Shiva's anger that had made life difficult for Parvati. 

Durvasa's life was marked by several significant encounters. Once, while Kuntī, the mother of the Pandavas, resided in the palace of Kuntibhoja, Durvasa visited. Pleased by her devoted service, he bestowed upon her five sacred mantras. These powerful incantations later enabled Kunti to invoke various deities and bear children, including the mighty Karṇa and the Pandavas. 

Another notable incident involved Lord Krishna and his consort Rukmiṇī in Dvārakā. Durvasa, in his unpredictable nature, would sometimes eat sparingly and at other times demand much, often creating challenging situations. Krishna and Rukmiṇī served him with utmost patience and respect. On one occasion, Durvasa asked Krishna to prepare a special pudding (pāyasa) and then instructed him to smear his entire body with it. Krishna complied, smearing the pudding everywhere except his feet. After this, Durvasa made Krishna and Rukmiṇī pull his chariot through the forest, treating them as horses. Despite the harsh treatment, neither Krishna nor Rukmiṇī showed any resentment. Impressed by their unwavering devotion and humility, Durvasa blessed Krishna, declaring that no weapon would harm the parts of his body covered in the pāyasa. It is said that Krishna's eventual demise was caused by an arrow striking his foot, the only part not covered by the blessed pudding. 

Durvasa also played a role in the salvation of Sage Mudgala. When Durvasa visited Mudgala, who lived a life of austerity, he repeatedly tested Mudgala's righteousness by demanding food and smearing himself with it, leaving Mudgala to starve. For six days, Mudgala endured this without complaint. Deeply moved by Mudgala's unwavering virtue, Durvasa blessed him, and Mudgala ascended bodily to heaven. 

Though often portrayed as irascible, Sage Durvasa's actions, though severe, were always intended to test the virtues of others, ultimately leading them to spiritual growth or divine blessings. His stories serve as profound lessons on patience, humility, and the consequences of one's actions, both good and ill.
moral: The stories of Sage Durvasa teach us the importance of patience, humility, and unwavering devotion in the face of adversity. They also highlight that even seemingly harsh actions from spiritual masters can be tests leading to greater blessings and spiritual growth. One's reaction to provocation reveals true character and determines destiny.

performance_notes.opening_line: In the ancient lands of Bharatavarsha, among the revered sages, lived Durvasa, a muni known for his profound spiritual powers and, equally, for his quick temper.
pause_points: His very being was said to be an "aṃśa," a part or aspect, of Lord Shiva's fiery energy.
His name, 'Durvasas,' meaning 'one difficult to live with,' perfectly reflected the circumstances of his birth.
These powerful incantations later enabled Kunti to invoke various deities and bear children.
It is said that Krishna's eventual demise was caused by an arrow striking his foot, the only part not covered by the blessed pudding.
Deeply moved by Mudgala's unwavering virtue, Durvasa blessed him, and Mudgala ascended bodily to heaven.

safety_notes: The narrative includes descriptions of anger and curses, which are integral to Durvasa's character. These elements are presented within the context of ancient Puranic stories and are intended for moral instruction. Care has been taken to frame these aspects respectfully and focus on the lessons learned rather than sensationalizing the anger itself. The story of Ugraśravas's leprosy and visit to brothels is mentioned briefly as context for Śīlavatī's curse, but not elaborated upon.

title: The Tale of Two Snakes
narrative_text: There was a king called Devashakti who was very frustrated on his son, who was very lean and weak. He grew leaner and weaker every day. Well-known physicians from far off places could not cure him, because there was a snake inside his stomach. They tried all sorts of treatments, but in vain. The young prince was also frustrated to see his father sad for him, and he was fed-up with his life. One night, he crept outside the palace and went to another kingdom. He started living in a temple, and ate from whatever alms he collected from kind people. This new country was ruled by a king, who had two young daughters. They were raised with the best of learning. Every morning they would bow at their father's feet to seek his blessings. One of the daughters said, "O Father, all joys of the world is bestowed upon us, with your blessings", and the other daughter would say, "O King, one only gets the fruits that are destined for one's actions!". The comments of the second daughter made the king very angry and one day, he summoned his ministers, "Let her enjoy the fruits that are destined for her actions! Take her and marry her off to anybody you meet outside the palace." The ministers did so, and were unable to find anybody when they saw the young prince who was living in the temple. The two were married. The princess was a religious girl, and considered her husband as her God. She was very happy and content with the marriage. They decided to travel to a different part of the country, as it would be improper for them to make the temple as their home. On the way, the prince became tired and wanted to rest under the shade of a tree. He was getting weaker every day, and could not walk long distances. The princess decided to buy some food from a nearby market. When she returned, she saw her husband fast asleep and a snake emerging from an anthill nearby. As she was about to caution, she saw another snake emerging from her husband's mouth. She hid herself, to keep a watch. The snake from the anthill said to the other snake, "Why do you torture this handsome prince? You risk your own life, too. If the prince drinks soup of cumin seeds and mustard, you would die for sure!" The snake from the prince's mouth said, "Why do you guard two pots of gold which you do not have any need of? You risk your life, too. If someone pours hot water and oil on the anthill, you would die for sure!" After a heated argument, they went inside their respective places, but the princess had already known their secrets. She acted accordingly and fed her husband with a soup of cumin seeds and mustard. Within hours, the young prince began to recover and regained much strength. After that, they poured hot water and oil on the anthill, and dug out the two pots of gold that the snake was guarding. Now, that the young prince had started recovering, and they had two pot full of gold, they lived happily ever after.
moral: When your enemies quarrel, you are the winner.

title: Hitopadesha: The Story of the Wise Minister
narrative_text:
In the ancient kingdom of Pataliputra, reigned King Sudarshan, a just and benevolent ruler. However, his sons, Prince Vivek and Prince Vinay, were more inclined towards frivolous pursuits than matters of statecraft. Concerned for the future of his kingdom, King Sudarshan sought the counsel of his wise and trusted minister, Sumati. 

Minister Sumati, known for his profound wisdom and diplomatic skills, listened patiently to the King's worries. He understood that direct admonishment would only alienate the young princes. Instead, he proposed a subtle approach. 'Your Majesty,' he began, 'I shall impart wisdom to your sons through stories and fables, for lessons learned through engaging narratives are far more potent than dry lectures.'

The King, trusting Sumati implicitly, agreed. Thus, every evening, Minister Sumati would gather the princes and narrate tales from the Hitopadesha, weaving in moral lessons about governance, friendship, prudence, and the consequences of folly. He spoke of animals who, through their actions, demonstrated the virtues of cooperation and the pitfalls of arrogance. He recounted stories of kings who prospered by heeding wise counsel and those who fell due to their stubbornness.

One evening, Sumati narrated the tale of a foolish lion who, despite his strength, was outwitted by a clever rabbit, leading to his downfall. The princes, captivated by the narrative, began to discuss the underlying message. Prince Vivek remarked, 'Indeed, strength alone is not enough; intelligence and foresight are paramount.' Prince Vinay added, 'And a king must always be open to the advice of his wise counselors, for even the mightiest can err.'

Over time, the princes, through these engaging stories, gradually absorbed the principles of good governance and ethical conduct. They began to show keen interest in state affairs, seeking Sumati's guidance and applying the wisdom gleaned from his tales. The kingdom rejoiced as the young princes transformed into sagacious and responsible heirs, all thanks to the subtle yet profound influence of the wise minister, Sumati.

Thus, the kingdom of Pataliputra continued to flourish under the guidance of its wise minister, who, through his ingenious method of storytelling, ensured a prosperous future for generations to come.
moral: True wisdom lies not only in possessing knowledge but also in the art of imparting it effectively, especially when guiding those who are reluctant to learn. Patient and indirect methods can often achieve what direct confrontation cannot.

title: Jataka: The Story of the Foolish Carpenter
narrative_text:
Once upon a time, when Brahmadatta was king of Benares, there stood near Benares a great town of carpenters, containing a thousand families. The carpenters from this town used to profess that they would make a bed, or a chair, or a house, and after receiving a large advance from men's hands, they proved able to make nothing whatever. The people used to upbraid every carpenter they met with, and interfered with them. So those debtors were so much harassed that they could live there no longer. "Let us go into some foreign land," said they, "and find some place or other to dwell in;" so to the forest they went. They cut down trees, they built a mighty ship, and launched her in the river, and took her away from that town, and at a distance of some three-quarters of a league they laid her up. Then in the middle of the night they returned to the town to fetch their families, whom they conveyed on board ship, and then proceeded in due course to the ocean. There they sailed at the wind's will, until they reached an island that lay in the midst of the sea. Now in that island grew wild all manner of plants and wild fruit-trees, rice, sugar-cane, banana, mango, rose-apple, jack, cocoanut, and what not. There was another man who had been shipwrecked and had taken possession of that island before them, and lived therein, eating the rice and enjoying the sugar-cane and all the rest, by which he had grown stout and sturdy; naked he went, and his hair and beard were grown long. The carpenters thought, "If yonder island is haunted of demons, we shall all perish; so we will explore it." Then seven brave men and strong, arming them with the five kinds of weapons, disembarked and explored that island.

At that moment the castaway had just broken his fast, and drunk of the juice of the sugar-cane, and in high contentment was lying on his back in a lovely spot, cool in the shade on some sand which glistered like silver plate; and he was thinking, "No such happiness as this have they who dwell in India, that plough and sow; better to me is this island than India!" He sang for joy, and was at the height of bliss.

The scouts who were exploring the isle caught the sound of his singing, and said, "It seems the voice of man that we hear; let us make acquaintance with him." Following the sound, they came upon the man, but his aspect horrified them. "'Tis a goblin!" they cried, and put arrow to bow. When the man saw them, he was in fear of being wounded, so he called out—"I am no goblin, sirs, but a man: spare my life!" — "What!" said they, "do men go all naked and defenceless like you?" and asked him again and again, only to receive the same answer, that he was a man. At last they approached him, and all began to talk pleasantly together, and the new-comers asked how he came thither. The other told them the truth of it. "As a reward for your good deeds you have come hither," said he, "this is a first-rate island. No need here to work with your hands for a living; of rice and sugar-cane, and all the rest, there is no end here, and all growing wild; you may live here without anxiety." "Is there nothing else," they asked, "to hinder our living here?" "No fear is there but this: the isle is haunted by demons, and the demons would be incensed to see the excretions of your bodies; so when you would relieve yourselves, dig a hole in the sand and hide it there. That is the only danger; there is no other; only always be careful on this point."

Then they took up their abode in the place.

But among these thousand families there were two master workmen, one at the head of each five hundred of them; and one of these was foolish and greedy of the best food, the other wise and not bent on getting the best of everything.

In course of time as they continued to dwell there, all grew stout and sturdy. Then they thought, "We have not been merry men this long time: we will make some toddy from the juice of the sugar-cane." So they caused the strong drink to be made, and being drunken, sang, danced, sported, then in thoughtlessness relieved themselves here, there, and everywhere without hiding it, so that they made the island foul and disgusting. The deities were incensed because these men made their playing-place all foul. "Shall we bring the sea over it," they deliberated, "and cleanse the island? — This is the dark fortnight: now our gathering is broken up. Well, on the fifteenth day from now, at the first of the full moon, at the time of the moon's rising, we will bring up the sea and make an end of them all." Thus they fixed the day. At this a righteous deity who was one of them thought, "I would not that these should perish before my eyes." So in his compassion, at the time when the men were sitting at their doors in pleasant converse, after their evening meal, he made the whole island one blaze of light, and adorned in all splendour stayed poised in the air towards the north, and spoke to them thus: "O ye carpenters! the deities are wroth with you. Dwell no longer in this place, for in half a month from this time, the deities will bring up the sea, and destroy you one and all. Therefore flee from this place."

With this advice, he returned to his own place. He gone, another comrade of his, a cruel god, thought, "Perhaps they will follow his advice and escape; I will prevent their going, and bring them all to utter destruction." So adorned in divine splendour, he made a great blaze of light over the whole place, and approaching them, remained poised in the air towards the south, as he asked, "Has there been a god here?" "There has," was the reply. "What did he tell you?" They answered, "Thus and thus, my lord." He then said, "This god does not wish you to live here, and in anger speaks. Go not elsewhither, but stay even here."

When he was gone, the foolish carpenter lifted up his voice, and paying no heed to the saying of the righteous deity, he cried, "Let your honours listen to me!" and addressed all the carpenters: "That god, who from the southern quarter clear Cries out, All safe! from him the truth we hear; Fear or fear not, the northern knows no whit: Why grieve, then? take your pleasure—never fear!"

On hearing him, the five hundred carpenters who were greedy of good things inclined to the counsel of the foolish carpenter. But then the wise carpenter refused to hearken to his saying, and addressing the carpenters said: "While these two goblins gainst each other cry, One calling fear, and one security, Come hear my rede, lest soon and out of hand Ye all together perish utterly. Let us join all to build a mighty bark, A vessel stout, and place within this ark All fittings: if this southern spoke the truth, And the other said but folly, off the mark This vessel for us good at need shall be; Nor will we leave this isle incontinent; But if the northern god spake truthfully, The southern did but foolishness present — Then in the ship we all embark together, And where our safety lies, all hie us thither. Take not for best or worst what first you hear; But whoso lets all pass within the ear, And then deliberating takes the mean, That man to safest harbourage will steer."

After this, he again said: "Come now, let us follow the words of both the deities. Let us build a ship, and then if the words of the first be true, into that ship we will climb and depart; but if the words of the other be true, we will put the ship out of the way, and dwell here." When he had thus spoken, said the foolish carpenter: "Go to! you see a crocodile in a teacup! you are too-too slow! The first god spake in anger against us, the second in affection. If we leave this choicest of isles, whither shall we go? But if you needs must go, take your tail with you, and make your ship: we want no ship, we!"

The wise man with those that followed him, built a ship, and put all the fittings aboard, and he and the whole company stood in the ship. Then on the day of the full moon, at the time of moon-rising, up front the ocean a wave arose, and knee-deep it swept over the whole island. The wise man, when he observed the rising of the wave, cast loose the ship. Those of the foolish carpenters party, five hundred families they were, sat still, saying to one another, "A wave has arisen, to sweep over the island, but it will be no deeper." Then the ocean-wave rose waist-deep, man-deep, deep as a palm-tree, as seven palm-trees, and over the whole island it rolled. The wise man, fertile in resource, not snared by greed of good things, departed in safety; but the foolish carpenter, greedy of good things, not regarding the fear of the future, with five hundred families was destroyed.
moral: Accomplish then the deed before the need, Let not lack hurt me of the needful thing. Who timely do the necessary deed Come time, come never into suffering.

title: Tenali Raman: The King's Lingering Illness
alt_titles: Tenali Raman and the Ghostly Cure
narrative_text:
King Krishnadevarayar of Vijayanagaram was a just ruler, but even the wisest can err. In a fit of rage, he had once sentenced Tenali Raman to death, believing him to be a Brahmin who had committed a grave offense. Though Raman cleverly escaped, the king was led to believe that Raman had been executed. Soon after, a deep melancholy settled upon the king. He grew withdrawn, his appetite waned, and he suffered from restless nights, plagued by vivid dreams of Raman's ghost. The royal physicians were baffled, unable to diagnose or cure his mysterious ailment. The court priests, ever eager to assert their influence, declared that the king was suffering from the curse of a Brahmin's ghost, a grave sin that could only be appeased through elaborate rituals and a hefty reward for anyone who could exorcise the spirit. 

The news of the king's 'illness' and the reward spread throughout the kingdom. Tenali Raman, who had been living in hiding, heard of the king's plight. Realizing the king's suffering was a result of his own guilt and the priests' manipulation, Raman devised a plan. He disguised himself as a powerful ascetic, with long matted hair, a flowing beard, and saffron robes. He approached the palace, announcing his ability to communicate with spirits and cure the king's affliction. 

The king, desperate for relief, welcomed the sage. The court priests, initially suspicious, were convinced by Raman's convincing demeanor. The disguised Raman declared that the Brahmin's ghost was indeed haunting the king, but it was not a malevolent spirit; rather, it was a soul seeking justice and an acknowledgment of its innocence. He proposed a ritual to appease the spirit, which involved the king publicly confessing his regret and promising to never again act in haste or allow himself to be swayed by false accusations. 

During the ritual, as the king poured out his heart, the 'sage' dramatically revealed himself to be Tenali Raman. The king was stunned, then overjoyed. The realization that Raman was alive and well instantly lifted the heavy burden from his heart. His 'illness' vanished as quickly as it had appeared. The king, humbled and relieved, embraced Raman, publicly apologizing for his rash judgment. He rewarded Raman handsomely and swore an oath to always seek wisdom and truth before making decisions, and never to sentence Raman to death again. The court priests, exposed for their deceit, were reprimanded, and the kingdom rejoiced at the king's recovery and Raman's return.

The moral of the story is that guilt and fear can manifest as physical or mental ailments, and true wisdom lies in seeking truth and justice, rather than succumbing to superstition or hasty judgment.
moral: Guilt and fear can manifest as severe ailments, and true wisdom involves seeking truth, acknowledging mistakes, and acting with justice rather than succumbing to superstition or hasty judgment.

title: Vikram-Betal: The Generous Prince and the Monstrous Bird
narrative_text:
In a forest-laden kingdom, there lived a benevolent Prince named G. Mutvahan. One day, he noticed his men attempting to uproot a small plant. The Prince immediately intervened, ordering them to cease and declaring that he would personally nurture the plant. With his diligent care, regular irrigation, and proper fertilization, the plant soon blossomed into a magnificent tree.

One day, as the Prince was watering the tree, a voice resonated, "I am the Kalp Tree, and I am deeply pleased with your kindness. I can grant your wishes; tell me what you desire." The Prince, humble and content, replied that he was a prince and lacked nothing for himself. The Kalp Tree then suggested, "If not for yourself, you can ask for others." The Prince, ever thoughtful of his people, responded that all the inhabitants of his land were his subjects, and he wished to do good for them all. Instantly, the Kalp Tree showered countless coins, which the Prince generously distributed among his people.

During his distribution, the Prince encountered a distraught lady whose son was being prepared as an offering to a monstrous bird. Overwhelmed with grief, she pleaded for his help. The Prince, without hesitation, promised his assistance, vowing to confront the bird and save her son's life. He swiftly made his way to the designated spot where the monstrous bird was expected to arrive.

Meanwhile, Shankhchoor, the young man destined to be the bird's meal, had gone to the temple to offer prayers. Upon learning that the Prince intended to sacrifice himself in his stead, Shankhchoor adamantly refused, insisting that it was his fate and the Prince should not risk his life. As the monstrous bird descended, Shankhchoor bravely stepped forward, ready to fulfill his role. However, the Prince declared his intention to fight and slay the bird.

The bird, intrigued, questioned the Prince's motive for protecting the young man and endangering himself. The Prince, with unwavering conviction, proclaimed that as the king of the land, it was his moral duty to protect his subjects. Impressed by the Prince's courage and dedication, the monstrous bird expressed its admiration. The Prince then seized the opportunity, asking the bird to spare the innocent people and cease its harm. The bird inquired about its sustenance, to which the Prince promised, "From this day forward, you shall be my honored guest, and I will personally arrange your food." The monstrous bird agreed.

Whenever the bird required food, the Prince would approach the Kalp Tree, explaining that his guest needed nourishment. The Kalp Tree would then instantly shower an abundance of fruits for the bird.

After narrating this tale, the Vetala posed a question to King Vikram: "Between the Prince and Shankhchoor, whose sacrifice is superior? Answer truthfully, or your head will shatter." Vikram pondered and replied, "Shankhchoor's sacrifice is superior. He was not driven by self-interest. When the Prince offered to die, Shankhchoor tried to stop him, willing to sacrifice his own life." As King Vikram had spoken, the Vetala, seizing the opportunity, flew back to his tree.
moral: True generosity and selflessness are exemplified when one is willing to sacrifice for others without expectation of reward, even when faced with their own impending doom. The story also highlights the importance of a ruler's duty to protect their subjects, and the power of compassion and wisdom to resolve seemingly insurmountable conflicts.

title: Ghatotkacha: The Son of Bhima and His Ultimate Sacrifice
alt_titles: The Story of Ghatotkacha in Mahabharata
Ghatotkacha's Sacrifice in the Kurukshetra War
narrative_text:
The story of Ghatotkacha begins in the deep, mystical forests where the Pandavas, exiled from their kingdom, sought refuge. One day, the mighty Bhima, second of the Pandava brothers, encountered a formidable Rakshasi named Hidimbi. After a fierce battle with her brother, Hidimba, Bhima took Hidimbi as his wife. From their union, a powerful son was born, named Ghatotkacha, so called because his head was bald and shaped like a pot. Ghatotkacha was no ordinary child; he was born with the immense strength of his father, Bhima, and the magical abilities of his Rakshasa mother. He grew to adulthood almost instantaneously, a testament to his extraordinary heritage. After his birth, Ghatotkacha promised his father that he would always come to their aid whenever needed, a vow he would honor with unwavering loyalty. He then departed with his mother, living in the forests, honing his unique powers.

Years later, during the epic Kurukshetra War, a conflict of unparalleled scale between the Pandavas and their Kaurava cousins, Ghatotkacha was summoned by his father, Bhima. His arrival brought a significant boost to the Pandava forces. With his formidable strength and mastery over illusions, Ghatotkacha wreaked havoc upon the Kaurava army, particularly under the cover of darkness when his magical powers were at their peak. He fought valiantly, engaging powerful warriors like Alambusha and Alayudha, and inspiring fear among the Kaurava ranks. His ability to change size, disappear, and create terrifying illusions made him a formidable opponent, often turning the tide of battle in favor of the Pandavas.

On the fourteenth night of the war, as the battle raged fiercely past sunset, Ghatotkacha became an unstoppable force. His relentless attacks threatened to annihilate the entire Kaurava army. Desperate, Duryodhana, the leader of the Kauravas, implored Karna, his most skilled warrior, to intervene. Karna possessed a divine weapon, the Vasavi Shakti, bestowed upon him by Lord Indra, which could only be used once. He had been saving this potent weapon to defeat his arch-rival, Arjuna. However, faced with the imminent destruction of his army, Karna, bound by his loyalty to Duryodhana, had no choice but to unleash the Vasavi Shakti upon Ghatotkacha. The divine missile struck Ghatotkacha, mortally wounding him. Yet, even in his death, Ghatotkacha performed one last, heroic act. As he fell, he used his Rakshasa powers to enlarge his body to an immense size, crushing a vast portion of the Kaurava army beneath him. His sacrifice saved Arjuna from Karna's deadly weapon and significantly weakened the Kaurava forces, proving to be a pivotal moment that ultimately led to the Pandavas' victory. Lord Krishna, understanding the profound significance of Ghatotkacha's sacrifice, smiled, knowing that the war was now truly won for the Pandavas.

The moral of Ghatotkacha's story lies in the profound impact of **selfless sacrifice** and **unwavering loyalty**. Despite his unique heritage and immense power, Ghatotkacha remained humble and dedicated to his family. His ultimate act of enlarging his body to crush the enemy, even in death, exemplifies the highest form of sacrifice for a greater cause. It teaches us that true strength lies not just in power, but in the willingness to use that power for the well-being of others, even at the cost of one's own life. His story also highlights the importance of fulfilling one's promises and standing by loved ones in their time of need, demonstrating that even those from unconventional backgrounds can play crucial roles in shaping destiny.
moral: The moral of Ghatotkacha's story lies in the profound impact of selfless sacrifice and unwavering loyalty. It teaches that true strength is found in using one's power for the well-being of others, even at great personal cost, and in fulfilling promises to loved ones.

title: The Tortoise and the Geese
narrative_text:
Master. There was once a tortoise who lived by the side of a beautiful lake. He spent his days shaded by the long grass, and nibbling on the juicy weeds. One year the rains failed to fall in season and the sun baked the land into hard clay. The water in the lake dried first into puddles, and then disappeared altogether The animals that came there in search of a drink were reduced to skin and bones, and tottered away on weak legs.

Now, this tortoise was on friendly terms with some of the geese of the lake. One day, a friendly pair of these birds came to wish him farewell, for their flock was planning to migrate to the Himalayas in search of water and fresh grass.

“And what will become of me?” Asked the tortoise. “I am 100 years old, my shell is heavy, and my legs are short. If I plodded all day I would reach no further than that boulder that you see over there. I must stay here until I am all dried up.”

The two geese took pity on the old tortoise and they agreed to help him. This was the plan: They would hold a stick between them as they flew. The tortoise must hold on to the stick with his mouth – and in this unusual way they would transport him to cooler and wetter climes.

The tortoise found himself flying through the skies. It was a most unusual feeling and he felt quite air sick . The other geese in the flock thought that the sight was hilarious.

“Hey look,” said one, “I’ve seen everything now. A tortoise has grown wings”.

“No he’s hasn’t,” said another, “He’s chewing on a stick because he’s hungry.”

“I’m sure he would fly better if he left his heavy shell behind,” remarked a third.

The comments continued, because the geese had nothing better to talk about than the tortoise and his unusual mode of transport. Eventually the tortoise could stand it no more. “See here,” he said, “Do you think I’m enjoying this?”

And that, of course, was when he opened his mouth, let go of the stick, and started to fall. He fell, and he fell and fell, until he hit the ground, my lord, here in the garden of your palace.

And now, O mighty master, mark it well. See thou speak wisely, see thou speak in season. To death the Tortoise fell. He talked too much: That was the reason.
moral: See thou speak wisely, see thou speak in season. To death the Tortoise fell. He talked too much: That was the reason.

title: The Elephant and the Jackal: A Story About Using Your Brain
alt_titles: The Jackal's Clever Trick: Outsmarting the Strong
narrative_text:
In the heart of a dense, green forest, a group of Jackals roamed together. These Jackals were cunning and resourceful, but they were small in size compared to the other creatures in the forest. Among them was a particularly clever Jackal named Kshudrabuddhi. He was known for his sharp mind and ability to come up with plans that others wouldn't even think of.

For a long time, the Jackals had eyed a giant Elephant named Karphooratilaka, who lived near the center of the forest. The Elephant was massive, with thick gray skin, enormous tusks, and a trunk that could move with incredible strength. Karphooratilaka was the king of his territory, and his size and strength made him nearly invincible to the smaller animals in the forest.

Despite their admiration for the Elephant's power, the Jackals were hungry. They wanted to find a way to feast on Karphooratilaka, but every time they tried to approach him, they were easily thwarted by his size and strength. The Elephant could easily defend himself, and the Jackals realized that they were no match for him in a direct confrontation. But Kshudrabuddhi, with his clever mind, had an idea.

Rather than using strength to defeat the Elephant, Kshudrabuddhi decided to outwit him. He knew that the Elephant was powerful, but even the strongest creatures could be defeated if they were caught in the right situation. So, Kshudrabuddhi devised a plan to lure the Elephant into a trap, one that would take advantage of the Elephant's size and strength in a way that he couldn't escape.

Kshudrabuddhi set out to find the Elephant and put his plan into action. He approached Karphooratilaka and, with a friendly tone, said, "Oh mighty Elephant, I have heard of your greatness. You are the most powerful creature in this forest, and I know that no one could ever challenge you. But there is a place nearby—a swamp, where the mud is thick and the ground soft. Many animals fear it, but I believe you are strong enough to walk through it without any trouble."

The Elephant, flattered by the praise and intrigued by the challenge, agreed to follow Kshudrabuddhi. He thought, "If this small creature believes I am strong enough to conquer this swamp, then I shall show him my power." So, he followed the Jackal through the forest, eager to prove his strength.

As they reached the swamp, the ground began to soften beneath the Elephant's heavy feet. The mud was thick and sticky, and soon, Karphooratilaka found himself sinking deeper and deeper with every step. The more he tried to pull himself out, the more the mud seemed to cling to his massive legs. The Elephant struggled, but his enormous weight made it impossible for him to move freely. Soon, he was completely stuck, unable to free himself.

Kshudrabuddhi, standing on the edge of the swamp, watched with a smile. He had successfully tricked the mighty Elephant into the swamp, where his size and strength became a disadvantage. The Elephant's powerful legs were now trapped in the mud, and no matter how hard he tried, he could not pull himself free.

The Jackal laughed, a triumphant sound that echoed through the trees. He had used his intelligence to outsmart the mighty Elephant. The Jackals, who had been waiting nearby, quickly gathered around the swamp, eager to take advantage of the Elephant's helplessness. With Karphooratilaka stuck in the mud and unable to defend himself, the Jackals now had the opportunity to feast.

However, as the Jackals moved closer, ready to claim their prize, they realized that the true lesson of the day wasn't in their ability to overpower the Elephant but in the cleverness of Kshudrabuddhi's plan. Even though the Jackals were small and weak compared to the mighty Elephant, they had been able to outwit him and turn the tables in their favor.
moral: The moral of the story is that physical power isn't always the key to success. Sometimes, being smart and thinking strategically can make all the difference. This lesson encourages us to value intelligence and resourcefulness, showing that there are many ways to solve problems beyond brute strength.

title: Matakabhatta-jātaka: The Story of the Foolish Goat
narrative_text:
In ancient times, when Brahmadatta reigned in Benares, a renowned Brahmin, skilled in the Three Vedas, decided to perform a 'Feast for the Dead' for his departed relatives. He instructed his pupils to fetch a goat, bathe it in the river, adorn it with a garland, feed it grain, groom it, and bring it back for the sacrifice. The pupils followed his instructions, taking the goat to the river. As they prepared it, the goat suddenly burst into loud laughter, like a pot shattering, and then began to weep profusely. The young Brahmins, bewildered, asked the goat the reason for its unusual behavior. The goat, however, insisted on explaining itself only in the presence of their master.

Upon returning to the Brahmin, the pupils recounted the strange incident. The Brahmin, equally perplexed, questioned the goat. The goat, possessing the ability to recall its past lives, revealed its story: 'In a previous existence, Brahmin, I was just like you – a Brahmin versed in the Vedas. I, too, offered a Feast for the Dead and sacrificed a goat. As a consequence of that single act, I have had my head severed five hundred times, lacking only one more instance to complete my suffering. This is my five hundredth and final birth. I laughed with immense joy at the thought that today, I would finally be freed from this cycle of misery. However, my tears flowed out of compassion for you, Brahmin. For by taking my life, you will incur the same fate as I did, and be doomed to lose your head five hundred times.'

The Brahmin, deeply moved by the goat's words, declared, 'Fear not, goat, I will not kill you!' But the goat replied, 'Brahmin, whether you kill me or not, I cannot escape death today.' The Brahmin, determined to protect it, offered to guard the goat. The goat, however, wisely stated, 'Your protection is weak, Brahmin, compared to the powerful force of my past evil deeds.'

Releasing the goat, the Brahmin instructed his disciples, 'Let no one harm this goat!' They followed the animal closely. The moment the goat was set free, it stretched its neck to nibble leaves from a bush growing near the top of a rock. At that very instant, a thunderbolt struck the rock, dislodging a large piece that fell upon the goat's outstretched neck, instantly severing its head. A crowd gathered, witnessing the tragic event.

At that time, the Bodhisatta had been reborn as a Tree-Fairy in that very spot. Using his supernatural powers, he appeared cross-legged in mid-air before the astonished crowd. He thought, 'If these beings truly understood the consequences of evil deeds, perhaps they would cease taking life.' With a sweet voice, he preached the Truth in a stanza: 'If folk but knew the penalty would be / Birth unto sorrow, living things would cease / From taking life. Stern is the slayer's doom.'

The Great Being thus instilled in his hearers a profound fear of hell, and the people, terrified by his words, abandoned the practice of taking life. The Bodhisatta, having established the multitude in the Commandments through his teachings, passed away according to his merits. The people, too, remained steadfast in his teachings, dedicating their lives to charity and good works, and ultimately ascended to the City of the Devas.
moral: The story teaches about the severe karmic consequences of taking a life, even for religious rituals. It emphasizes the importance of compassion and the understanding that actions have repercussions across lifetimes. It also highlights the futility of trying to escape one's destiny when past karma is strong.

title: Akbar-Birbal: The story of the magical donkey
narrative_text:
Once, Emperor Akbar gifted his queen a beautiful and precious pearl necklace. The queen was overjoyed and kept it safely in her chamber. However, a few days later, the necklace went missing. The queen was very upset and informed the Emperor. Akbar was concerned, as several other valuable items had also been stolen from the palace recently. He suspected that one of the servants was the thief. He summoned his wisest courtier, Birbal, and asked for his help in catching the culprit.

Birbal thought for a moment and then said, "Your Majesty, I have a plan. I will bring my magical donkey to the palace. It will help us identify the thief." The next day, Birbal arrived with a donkey and a tent. He placed the donkey inside the tent and announced to all the servants, "Each of you must go inside the tent alone and pull the donkey's tail. This is a magical donkey, and when the thief pulls its tail, it will kick him."

One by one, the servants went inside the tent. Each came out looking nervous, but none had been kicked. After all the servants had their turn, Birbal asked them to show him their hands. He then pointed to one of the servants and declared, "Your Majesty, this is the thief!"

Akbar was astonished. "But how do you know, Birbal? The donkey didn't kick him."

Birbal explained, "I had coated the donkey's tail with a special black paint. Out of fear of being kicked, the real thief never touched the donkey's tail. All the other servants have black paint on their hands, but his hands are clean."

The servant, caught in his lie, confessed to the theft and returned the queen's necklace and all the other stolen items. Akbar was once again impressed by Birbal's wit and wisdom.
moral: The guilty always live in fear, and the truth will always be revealed.

title: Tenali Raman: The Story of the Fake Ghost
narrative_text:
In the glorious Vijayanagaram Empire, a belief persisted that killing a Brahmin was a grave sin, demanding significant repentance. Tenali Raman, a Brahmin himself, was believed to have been killed by the King Krishnadevarayar, an act that queens feared would bring negative consequences upon the entire kingdom. To appease the supposed departed soul of Raman, the King, advised by his priests, decided to perform obsequies.

During a moonless night, the head priest and 108 assistant priests embarked from the palace towards a nearby temple, intending to bury Raman's soul under a banyan tree. However, Tenali Raman, who was very much alive and aware of this secret plan, decided to play a trick. He painted his body with black oil and positioned himself on the banyan tree, appearing like a ghost.

As the priests began chanting mantras to invoke the spirit, Raman suddenly jumped down amongst them. Bewildered and terrified, the priests cried out, believing they had encountered a real ghost. They fled in panic, seeking refuge back at the palace.

At dawn, the priests recounted their terrifying experience to the King, and the news of the Brahmin's ghost quickly spread throughout the empire, instilling fear among the populace. The King, worried by the widespread panic, sought a solution. He proclaimed a reward of 1000 gold sovereigns for anyone who could exorcise the ghost.

Raman, having returned home, heard the proclamation and was pleased that his plan was unfolding as intended. A few days later, with no one coming forward to claim the reward, the King sat gloomy in the durbar. Tathachary, the court priest, was also present. A soldier announced the arrival of a sage. The sage, with long hair, beard, and saffron robes, impressed the King. The sage offered to evict the Brahmin's soul, asking if the King was ready to reward him as he demanded, provided it didn't affect the nation's reputation or well-being. The King agreed, but also inquired if it was possible to absolve himself from the sin of killing Tenali Raman.

The sage, who was none other than Tenali Raman in disguise, replied that the obsequies would proceed as if the Brahmin was not killed at all. Tathachary, recognizing Raman's threat to his own position, advised the King against reviving Raman, stating that he would become a nuisance. However, Raman, still in disguise, countered Tathachary's arguments, questioning how a king's order to kill a law-breaker could be a sin. He then revealed his true identity by removing his disguise, shocking the King and terrifying Tathachary. Raman then demanded a thousand gold sovereigns and release from the death sentence, which the King readily granted.

Thus, Tenali Raman, through his cleverness and wit, exposed the superstition and greed of the priests, saved himself from a false accusation, and once again proved his invaluable intelligence to the King and the court.
moral: Intelligence and wit can overcome superstition, fear, and deceit. One should not be easily swayed by false claims or appearances.

title: Vikram-Betal: The Story of the Three Suitors
narrative_text: A beautiful daughter, Mandaravati, was born to a famous priest. When she came of age, three worthy bachelors asked for her hand in marriage, each threatening to take his own life if she were to marry another. The priest, in a dilemma, decided not to marry her to any of them. Suddenly, Mandaravati fell ill with a raging fever and died. The three suitors, heartbroken, cremated her. One of them, unable to part from her, slept on her ashes. The second collected her bones and took them to the holy river Ganges. The third became a wandering ascetic. During his travels, the ascetic discovered a book of spells that could bring the dead back to life. He stole the book and returned to the cremation ground where Mandaravati was laid to rest. With the help of the other two suitors, he recited the spell, and Mandaravati was reborn. A dispute then arose among the three as to who should be her husband, each claiming that her revival was due to his efforts. Betaal then asks King Vikram, "Who among the three suitors should be the husband of Mandaravati?" Vikram, after much thought, replies, "The one who brought her back to life is her creator, like a father. The one who took her bones to the Ganges is like a son. But the one who slept on her ashes out of pure love, he should be her husband."
moral: True love is demonstrated through selfless devotion and commitment.

title: A King and His Minister
narrative_text:
Once upon a time, there was a king in India. He had a minister who had great faith in God and would always say that whatever happens, happens for the good. The king never believed this.

Once the king cut his finger and as usual, the minister said that all happens for good. Hearing this, the king got quite angry and put the minister into prison. Even then the minister said that everything happens for the good!

A few days later, the king went hunting in the jungle, by himself, since his minister was in prison. As the king was hunting, some tribesmen trapped him and took him to their chieftain. But when the chieftain saw the cut on the king’s finger, he ordered the king to be released, saying that a person with a cut finger would not make a good offering to the tribe’s deity.

Happy, due to the narrow escape from death, the king returned to his kingdom remembering his wise minister’s words that even the finger getting cut was for the good. Upon his return, he immediately ordered the minister’s release and welcomed him back in his position as the royal minister.

The king said to the minister, "I now believe that everything happens for our good, as my life was saved because of the cut finger. But, what about you? How can you explain that it was good for you?"

The minister replied, "As you had put me in prison, I was not able to accompany you on your hunting trip. If I had been there with you, the tribesmen would have taken me along with you and would have definitely sacrificed me to their deity, as I do not have a cut finger!" The king was pleased with the minister’s reply, and from then on, always consulted the wise minister while making any decisions for his subjects.
moral: Great faith like the minister’s is needed to understand the truth that everything happens as per God’s will. Regular spiritual practice such as repeating God’s Name is a sure way to develop such faith.

title: The Boatman and the Scholar
alt_titles: The Learned Man and the Boatman
narrative_text:
Once upon a time, a scholar, known for his vast knowledge of holy books and various subjects, decided to cross a river. He hired a humble boatman to ferry him across in his canoe. As they journeyed across the calm waters, the scholar, brimming with pride in his learning, engaged the boatman in conversation. "My good man," the scholar began, "have you ever delved into the sacred texts? Have you read any of the great holy books?" The boatman, with a simple shake of his head, replied, "No, respected sir, I have not had the opportunity to read such esteemed works." The scholar, with a sigh of what he perceived as intellectual superiority, remarked, "Alas, then you have wasted half of your life, for true wisdom lies within the pages of these books!"

Their conversation continued, with the scholar expounding on various topics, often questioning the boatman about his knowledge, only to be met with humble admissions of ignorance. The boatman, however, remained serene and focused on his task. Suddenly, a crisis struck. The old canoe, perhaps weakened by time or a hidden snag, sprang a leak. Water began to rush into the boat with alarming speed, threatening to capsize them both. "O learned man!" the boatman cried out, his voice urgent but steady, "Can you swim? We must abandon the boat if we are to survive!"

The scholar, his face now pale with fear, stammered, "I... I have read countless books on the art of swimming. I know all the theories, the strokes, the techniques... but I have never actually entered the water to swim myself!" The boatman, already preparing to leap into the river, looked at the terrified scholar with a knowing gaze. "Then, respected sir," he said, "it seems your entire life has been wasted. For all your bookish knowledge, it is practical skill that will save us now. The boat is about to sink!" With that, the boatman plunged into the water and swam to safety, leaving the scholar to face the consequences of his unpracticed knowledge.

The moral of this story is that theoretical knowledge, however vast, is of little use if it is not accompanied by practical experience and application. True wisdom lies not just in knowing, but in doing.
moral: Theoretical knowledge, however vast, is of little use if it is not accompanied by practical experience and application. True wisdom lies not just in knowing, but in doing.

title: The Story of Sant Jnaneshwar: A Beacon of Devotion and Wisdom
alt_titles: Mauli: The Story of Sant Dnyaneshwar
narrative_text:
In the sacred land of Maharashtra, during the 13th century, a luminous soul named Dnyaneshwar was born on the auspicious day of Krishna Janmashtami in Apegaon, a village nestled by the Godavari river. He was the second of four children born to Vitthal Pant and Rakhumabai, a devout Brahmin couple. His elder brother was Nivruttinath, and his younger siblings were Sopan and Muktabai. Their lives, though destined for spiritual greatness, began amidst profound challenges.

Vitthal Pant, Dnyaneshwar's father, was a man deeply yearning for spiritual enlightenment. In his youth, he had renounced worldly life and become a sannyasin in Kashi. However, destiny had a different plan. His guru, Ramashrama Swami, upon discovering that Vitthal Pant had left his wife without her consent, commanded him to return to his family and fulfill his duties as a householder. Obediently, Vitthal Pant returned to Alandi, where he reunited with Rakhumabai, and together they raised their four extraordinary children.

Yet, their return to householder life was met with severe condemnation from the orthodox Brahmins of the time. They viewed a sannyasin's return to family life as a grave heresy. The Kulkarni family was excommunicated, denied social interaction, and even the children were refused the sacred thread ceremony, a vital rite of passage for Brahmins. This social ostracization caused immense suffering for the family.

Heartbroken by the plight of their children, and hoping to free them from the social stigma, Vitthal Pant and Rakhumabai made the ultimate sacrifice. They ended their lives by jumping into the Indrayani river, believing their penance would pave the way for their children to lead lives free from persecution. Though orphaned at a young age, the four siblings, guided by the spiritual wisdom inherited from their parents and the Nath yogi tradition, embarked on their own paths of spiritual realization. Nivruttinath, the eldest, became the guru to his younger siblings, initiating Dnyaneshwar into the Nath tradition.

Dnyaneshwar's life was adorned with numerous miracles, testaments to his divine powers and profound spiritual insight. One such incident occurred in Paithan, where the young Dnyaneshwar, along with his siblings, sought validation from the orthodox priests. When ridiculed for his concern for a buffalo being cruelly beaten, Dnyaneshwar famously demonstrated that divinity resides in all beings. To the astonishment of the priests, he placed his hand on the buffalo's head, and the animal began to recite Vedic verses in a clear, resonant voice, proving that spiritual truth transcends caste and creed.

Another celebrated miracle involved the arrogant yogi Changdev, who, proud of his mystical powers, rode a tiger to meet Dnyaneshwar. To humble Changdev's pride, Dnyaneshwar, with his siblings, rode a moving wall to greet him. This miraculous display of spiritual power led Changdev to recognize Dnyaneshwar's true greatness, and he became a disciple of Dnyaneshwar's sister, Muktabai.

Despite his short life of just 21 years, Dnyaneshwar's contributions to Marathi literature and philosophy were immense. He authored the 'Dnyaneshwari', a profound commentary on the Bhagavad Gita, and 'Amrutanubhav', a treatise on spiritual experience. These works, written in Marathi, made complex philosophical concepts accessible to the common people, laying the foundation for the Varkari Bhakti movement. He also embarked on a pilgrimage across India with his close friend, the saint-poet Namdev, spreading the message of devotion and love for Vithoba.

At the tender age of 21, Dnyaneshwar, having fulfilled his divine mission, voluntarily entered into Sanjeevan Samadhi in Alandi, consciously departing from his mortal body in a deep meditative state. His legacy continues to inspire millions, making him an eternal beacon of devotion, wisdom, and compassion in the spiritual landscape of India.
moral: The story of Sant Jnaneshwar teaches us that true spiritual greatness lies not in birth or social status, but in unwavering devotion, profound wisdom, and boundless compassion. It highlights the importance of inner purity over external rituals and the power of love and understanding to overcome adversity and social prejudice.

title: The Story of Princess Veeravati and Karva Chauth
narrative_text:
A long, long time ago, there lived a beautiful princess named Veeravati. When she reached a marriageable age, she was wed to a king. On the occasion of her first Karva Chauth after marriage, she returned to her parents’ home.

After sunrise, Veeravati observed a strict fast. However, being delicate, she found it difficult to endure the rigors of fasting. By evening, she grew weak and fainted. Her seven loving brothers, unable to bear their sister's suffering, decided to trick her into breaking her fast. They lit a fire on a nearby hill and convinced her that it was the moonrise, assuring her she could now break her fast.

The moment the gullible queen ate her dinner, she received the devastating news that her husband, the king, had died. Heartbroken, she rushed to her husband’s palace. On her journey, she encountered Lord Shiva and his consort, Goddess Parvati. Parvati revealed that the king's death was a consequence of Veeravati breaking her fast by mistaking a false moon for the real one. However, when Veeravati pleaded for forgiveness, the goddess, moved by her devotion, granted her a boon: the king would be revived but would remain ill.

Upon reaching the palace, Veeravati found the king unconscious, his body riddled with hundreds of needles. Each day, she diligently removed one needle. The following year, on Karva Chauth, only one needle remained embedded in the king's body.

That day, the queen observed a strict fast. While she went to the market to buy a 'karva' for the puja, her maid, out of curiosity or malice, removed the last needle from the king’s body. The king immediately regained consciousness, but, disoriented, he mistook the maid for his queen. When the true queen returned, she was relegated to the status of a maid.

Despite her reduced status, Veeravati remained steadfast in her faith and religiously observed the Karva Chauth vrat. One day, as the king prepared to travel to another kingdom, he asked the former queen (now a maid) if she desired anything. She requested a pair of identical dolls. The king obliged. Veeravati then began to sing a poignant song: "Roli ki Goli ho gayi... Goli ki Roli ho gayi" (the queen has turned into a maid and the maid has turned into a queen).

Intrigued, the king asked her the meaning of her song. Veeravati then narrated the entire story of her ordeal. Filled with remorse, the king repented and restored Veeravati to her rightful royal status. It was her unwavering devotion and faith that ultimately won back her husband’s affection and the blessings of Goddess Parvati.
moral: True devotion, faith, and perseverance are ultimately rewarded, and they can overcome even the most challenging adversities.

Upon reaching the palace, Veeravati found the king unconscious, his body riddled with hundreds of needles.
The king immediately regained consciousness, but, disoriented, he mistook the maid for his queen.
Filled with remorse, the king repented and restored Veeravati to her rightful royal status.

title: Mahabharata: The Story of the Kurukshetra War
narrative_text:
The ancient Indian epic, the Mahabharata, chronicles the lives and deeds of the Kuru clan across several generations. At its heart lies a dynastic struggle between two branches of this family: the five Pandava brothers and their hundred cousins, the Kauravas, vying for control of the throne of Hastinapura. The decisive battle unfolds at Kurukshetra, a place revered as the 'field of the Kurus' or 'field of dharma,' believed to absolve sins committed there.

The war narrative constitutes nearly one-fifth of the Mahabharata, primarily spanning its sixth to tenth books. Lord Krishna assumes a crucial role as both mediator and Arjuna's charioteer. On the eve of battle, as Arjuna grapples with the moral dilemma of fighting his own kin and revered teachers, Krishna imparts profound teachings on duty and existence, which are encapsulated in the Bhagavad Gita.

Before the war, in a final effort to avert bloodshed, Krishna journeys to Hastinapura to appeal to the Kauravas' reason and advocate for a peaceful resolution. However, Duryodhana, the eldest Kaurava, spurns Krishna's overtures and plots to arrest and humiliate him. During a formal presentation of peace proposals, Krishna requests that Duryodhana return Indraprastha to the Pandavas or, at the very least, grant them five villages. Duryodhana defiantly refuses, and Krishna, revealing his divine form, curses Duryodhana, foretelling his downfall at the hands of Bhima. With his peace mission thwarted, Krishna returns to the Pandava camp, affirming that war is the only path left to uphold righteousness. On his return, Krishna encounters Karna, Kunti's firstborn, and urges him to join his brothers in the fight for dharma. However, Karna, bound by his loyalty to Duryodhana, declines.

Both Duryodhana and Arjuna seek Krishna's aid. Duryodhana arrives first and finds Krishna asleep, choosing to wait at his head. Arjuna, arriving later, waits at Krishna's feet. Upon waking, Krishna grants Arjuna the first choice: either his Narayani Sena (a powerful army) or Krishna himself as a non-combatant. Arjuna chooses Krishna, requesting him to be his charioteer. Both return satisfied.

The Pandavas establish their camp at Upaplavya, within Virata's territory, and assemble their forces, comprising seven akshauhinis (divisions). Dhrishtadyumna is appointed as the Major General of the Pandava Army.

The Kaurava Army, consisting of eleven akshauhinis, includes Krishna's Narayani Sena. Duryodhana appoints Bhishma as the Major General, who agrees on the condition that he will not harm the five Pandava brothers. Karna, unwilling to fight under Bhishma, joins the war later after Bhishma is incapacitated.

Neutral parties in the conflict include the Kingdom of Vidarbha, Vidura, and Balarama. Rukmi's offer to join the war is rejected by Arjuna due to his past boasting, and by Duryodhana due to Arjuna's rejection. Vidura, opposed to the bloodshed, remains neutral.

The Mahabharata records that three solar eclipses occurred within a thirty-day period in the year of the war, considered ill omens in Hindu astrology.

On the first day, the Kaurava Army faced west, and the Pandava Army faced east, with the Pandavas adopting a diamond (vajra) formation. The Kauravas arranged ten akshauhinis in a phalanx, with the eleventh under Bhishma's direct command, as his safety was paramount to Duryodhana's strategy.

As the armies stand poised for battle, Arjuna is overcome with despair at the prospect of fighting his beloved granduncle Bhishma and revered teacher Drona. He seeks guidance from Krishna, his charioteer. Krishna reminds Arjuna of his duty as a warrior and the righteousness of their cause, emphasizing that the war is a struggle between dharma and adharma. Krishna reveals his divine form, explaining his periodic incarnations to restore balance when evil prevails. Before the battle commences, Yudhishthira seeks blessings from Bhishma, who grants them.

The Pandavas suffer significant losses on the first day, with Virata's sons, Uttara and Sweta, slain by Shalya and Bhishma. Krishna reassures Yudhishthira of their eventual victory.

Arjuna, determined to reverse the Pandava's fortunes, attempts to engage Bhishma in a duel, but Bhishma is protected by Kaurava soldiers. Drona and Dhrishtadyumna engage in a fierce duel, with Drona prevailing until Bhima intervenes. Duryodhana dispatches the Kalinga troops to attack Bhima, resulting in the death of the Kalinga King and many of his forces. Bhishma comes to their aid. Satyaki wounds Bhishma's charioteer, causing Bhishma's chariot to retreat. The day ends with no decisive victory.

The Kauravas focus their assault on Arjuna, whose chariot is enveloped in arrows. Abhimanyu and Satyaki counter Shakuni's Gandhara forces. Bhima and Ghatotkacha attack Duryodhana from the rear, injuring him and causing his forces to scatter. Bhishma restores order, and Duryodhana, angered by Bhishma's perceived leniency towards the Pandavas, confronts him. Bhishma, stung by the accusation, returns to battle. Arjuna and Bhishma engage in another duel.

Bhishma orders an offensive. The Kauravas form a chakravyuha, which Abhimanyu penetrates but is surrounded. Arjuna comes to his aid, and Bhima attacks the Kauravas, killing eight of Duryodhana's brothers before being wounded by Dushasana. At the end of the day, Duryodhana questions Bhishma about the Pandavas' success despite their numerical disadvantage. Bhishma attributes it to their righteousness and advises Duryodhana to seek peace.

The Pandava Army endures heavy attacks from Bhishma. Satyaki is overwhelmed by Drona but is rescued by Bhima. Arjuna slays thousands of Duryodhana's soldiers. Bhima and Bhishma engage in an indecisive duel. Drupada and Shikhandi attempt to assist Bhima but are wounded by Vikarna.

Drona inflicts heavy casualties on the Pandava forces, disrupting their formations. Bhima breaches the Kaurava formation and attacks Duryodhana, who is defeated but escapes. The Upapandavas (Draupadi's sons) battle Ashwatthama, destroying his chariot.

Drona continues his relentless assault on the Pandava forces. Dhrishtadyumna, the commander of the Pandava army, engages Drona in a fierce battle. Arjuna, with Krishna as his charioteer, fights valiantly, causing significant damage to the Kaurava army. The day ends with heavy casualties on both sides, but no decisive outcome.

Bhima continues his rampage, killing seventeen of Dhritarashtra's sons. Iravan, Arjuna's son, slays five of Shakuni's brothers. Duryodhana unleashes the Rakshasa warrior Alambusha, who kills Iravan. The battle remains intense and brutal.

Bhishma devastates the Pandava armies. Arjuna, hesitant to fight his revered granduncle, engages him half-heartedly. Krishna, angered by Arjuna's reluctance, almost intervenes directly. Ghatotkacha kills the Rakshasa Alambusha. Krishna suggests a strategy to defeat Bhishma, involving Shikhandi.

Following Krishna's advice, the Pandavas place Shikhandi, who was a woman in a previous life, in front of Bhishma. Bhishma, bound by a vow not to attack a woman, allows Shikhandi's arrows to strike him. Arjuna, positioned behind Shikhandi, targets Bhishma's weak points, ultimately defeating him. Bhishma falls on a bed of arrows, but, due to a boon, he can choose the time of his death. He requests Arjuna to place three arrows under his head for support. Bhishma later imparts wisdom to the Pandavas before choosing to die on the first day of Uttarayana.

With Bhishma incapacitated, Karna enters the battlefield. Duryodhana, on Karna's advice, appoints Drona as the Major General of the Kaurava forces. Duryodhana aims to capture Yudhishthira alive, believing it would be strategically advantageous. Drona attempts to capture Yudhishthira, but Arjuna thwarts his efforts.

Drona informs Duryodhana that capturing Yudhishthira is difficult as long as Arjuna is present. The Samsaptakas, led by Susharma, challenge Arjuna to keep him occupied in a remote part of the battlefield. Arjuna defeats them and then confronts Bhagadatta, who had been wreaking havoc among the Pandava troops. Arjuna kills Bhagadatta. Drona continues his attempts to capture Yudhishthira, but Prativindhya repels his attacks. The Pandavas inflict heavy losses on the Kaurava army.

Drona's objective remains to capture Yudhishthira. To prevent Arjuna and Krishna from interfering, the Samsaptakas again engage Arjuna. Abhimanyu, despite knowing how to enter the chakra formation but not how to exit, bravely enters it. Jayadratha, using a boon from Shiva, prevents the other Pandavas from following. Inside the formation, Abhimanyu fights valiantly, killing many warriors, including Duryodhana's son Lakshmana. However, he is eventually surrounded by six Kaurava warriors and, after a fierce mace-fight, is killed by Dushasana's son. Upon learning of his son's death, Arjuna vows to kill Jayadratha.

Arjuna, in his quest to avenge Abhimanyu, slays seven akshauhinis of Kaurava soldiers. Duryodhana hides Jayadratha, but Arjuna, using a divine weapon, decapitates Jayadratha, fulfilling his vow. Drona and Karna attempt to protect Jayadratha but fail. Arjuna warns that all who supported unrighteousness would perish. Bhima kills Vikarna, one of Duryodhana's brothers, who had defended Draupadi during her humiliation. Drona kills Vrihatkshatra and Dhrishtakethu. Prativindya, Yudhishthira's son, slays Dushasana's son Drumsena. Ghatotkacha, using his magical abilities, kills Alambusha and Alayudha. Karna, using the divine weapon Vasava Shakti, kills Ghatotkacha, who, as he falls, crushes an akshauhini of the Kaurava army.

After Drupada and Virata are killed by Drona, Bhima and Dhrishtadyumna confront Drona. Krishna advises Yudhishthira to lie to Drona, telling him that his son Ashwatthama is dead, knowing that Drona would lay down his arms. Yudhishthira reluctantly does so, and Drona, disheartened, ceases fighting and is then killed by Dhrishtadyumna. Kunti secretly meets Karna and asks him to spare the Pandavas, revealing their brotherhood. Karna promises to spare all but Arjuna, and also vows not to use the same weapon against Arjuna twice.

Karna is appointed the Major General of the Kaurava Army. He faces a concentrated attack from Pandava generals but remains undefeated, inflicting heavy damage on the Pandava forces.

Bhima shatters Dushasana's chariot, then seizes and kills him, fulfilling his vow to drink Dushasana's blood and smear it on Draupadi's hair. Arjuna kills Susharma, the Trigartas, and the Samsaptakas. Karna defeats Satyaki, Shikhandi, and the Pandava brothers Nakula, Sahadeva, Yudhishthira, and Bhima, but spares their lives. Karna continues to decimate the Pandava army and kills the Panchalas. During his duel with Arjuna, Karna's chariot wheel gets stuck in the mud. Krishna reminds Arjuna of Karna's past ruthlessness towards Abhimanyu, and Arjuna, seizing the opportunity, decapitates Karna.

Shalya assumes command of the remaining Kaurava forces. Yudhishthira kills Shalya in spear combat, and Sahadeva kills Shakuni. Nakula kills Shakuni's son Uluka. Duryodhana, realizing his defeat, flees to a lake, where the Pandavas find him. A battle ensues between Bhima and Duryodhana, supervised by Balarama. Bhima, following Krishna's instructions, strikes Duryodhana below the waist, mortally wounding him. Ashwatthama, Kripa, and Kritavarma vow to avenge Duryodhana. They attack the Pandava camp that night, killing the remaining Pandava army and their children.

Only eleven major warriors survive the war: the five Pandavas, Krishna, Satyaki, Ashwatthama, Kripa, Yuyutsu, and Kritavarma. Yudhishthira is crowned King of Hastinapura. After ruling for 36 years, he abdicates the throne to Arjuna's grandson Parikshit. Draupadi and four of the Pandavas—Bhima, Arjuna, Nakula, and Sahadeva—die during their journey. Yudhishthira, the sole survivor, is invited by Yama to enter the heavens as a mortal.
moral: The story of the Kurukshetra War teaches that righteousness (dharma) will ultimately triumph over unrighteousness (adharma), even at great cost. It emphasizes the importance of duty, sacrifice, and the consequences of greed and injustice.

performance_notes.opening_line: In the ancient land of Bharata, a great war was fought, a war that would change the course of history forever...
pause_points: ...a war between cousins, the Pandavas and the Kauravas.
...Arjuna, faced with the prospect of fighting his own kin, was filled with despair.
...and so, the great war of Kurukshetra began.

safety_notes: The story contains descriptions of violence and warfare, which may be disturbing to some listeners. It is important to present the story in a way that emphasizes the moral and philosophical aspects, rather than glorifying the violence.

title: The Sage Agastya and the Vindhya Mountains
narrative_text:
In ancient times, the mighty Vindhya mountain range, stretching across central India, grew immensely proud and began to compete with the majestic Himalayas. Day by day, the Vindhyas grew taller, obstructing the path of the sun god, Lord Surya, as he traversed the sky. This caused great distress to all beings, as the southern lands were plunged into perpetual darkness, disrupting the natural order of day and night.

The gods, concerned by this unprecedented arrogance, sought a solution. They approached the revered sage Agastya, known for his immense spiritual power, wisdom, and humility. Agastya, understanding the gravity of the situation, agreed to intervene.

Accompanied by his wife, Lopamudra, Agastya Muni began his journey southward. As he approached the Vindhya mountains, the proud peaks, recognizing the great sage, bowed down in reverence, as was customary when a revered elder passed. Agastya, seizing this opportune moment, addressed the mountains with a calm and authoritative voice.

"O King of Mountains, Vindhya! I am on my way to the southern lands and request you to remain bowed until my return. It is improper for a sage to pass over a mountain that stands tall in his path." 

The Vindhyas, out of respect and awe for Agastya, promised to remain bowed until his return. Agastya, with his wife, then crossed over the humbled mountains and continued his journey to the south. He settled there, establishing ashrams and spreading knowledge, never returning to the north.

Thus, the Vindhya mountains remained bowed forever, unable to rise again, perpetually awaiting the sage's return. Agastya's wisdom and humble approach resolved a crisis that even the gods found challenging, restoring balance to the world and ensuring the sun's unobstructed passage.
moral: Humility triumphs over arrogance, and true wisdom lies in knowing when and how to apply one's power for the greater good. Even the mightiest can be humbled by wisdom and respect.

title: The Bear and The Two Friends
narrative_text:
Once two friends were walking through the forest. They knew that anything dangerous can happen to them at any time in the forest. So they promised each other that they would remain united in any case of danger.

Suddenly, they saw a large bear approaching them. One of the friends at once climbed a nearby tree. But the other one did not know how to climb. So being led by his common sense, he lay down on the ground breathless, pretending to be a dead man.

The bear came near the man lying on the ground. It smelt in his ears, and slowly left the place. Because the bears do not touch the dead creatures.Now the friend on the tree came down and asked his friend on the ground, "Friend, what did the bear tell you into your ears?" The other friend replied, "The bear advised me not to believe a false friend.

Moral: True Friend is the one who always supports and stands by you in any situation.
