title: Hitopadesha: The Crow and The Snake
narrative_text: A pair of crows built their nest on a tree. The female crow laid eggs, sat on the nest attentively, and incubated them. When the eggs hatched, the two crows flew afar to find food for the chicks. Unfortunately, the two birds had not noticed the danger lurking in the hollow of their tree. For in the hollow lived a large black serpent. The smell of the eggs had tempted the snake, but of course, it didn’t slither up the tree to the nest because both the birds were there. So, it lay in waiting, waiting for a day like this when both the parents would be out of sight and out of hearing, for the chicks were loud and SQUEAKY! Up, up he went, his scales moving noiselessly over the bark. Finally, he reached the nest and had a scrumptious feast. When the two crows returned with thick juicy worms, they couldn’t understand what had happened. They weren’t at the wrong tree or something, were they? But…, the nest was the same. A few tiny feathers were stuck between the twigs on the nest. The two birds circled around the area for any sign of their nestlings when their blood turned cold. The snake stuck its head out of the hollow and pulled it back in the blink of an eye. As you can imagine, the parents were heartbroken. But it didn’t stop there. No matter how careful they were, the snake managed to gobble up the crow chicks before they grew big enough to fly away. Finally, the female crow told her husband, “My dear, Even if we built our nest on a taller tree nearby, the snake will find our babies. Let’s move away from this forest and build another nest somewhere far.” The husband patted her reassuringly. “You know what? I was foolish to give the snake so many chances, but I’ve come to my senses now. He will never change. Now, I will finish him off so he won’t bother us ever again!” “Really? What are you going to do? Please be careful, for he is a powerful and dangerous enemy,” the female crow warned her husband. “Oh, I’m not going to attack him outright. That wouldn’t do. What I can’t gain through force, I can get by using my brain. All it needs is some smart planning. He is a smug little fool, and it is easy to trap a fool. Wasn’t that how the rabbit brought down the high and mighty lion?” “What happened to the lion? What did the rabbit do?” the wife asked her husband. “It was like this…,” began the male crow. The lion, Durdanta, lived in the mountain named Mandara. He was such a ferocious and powerful beast that he killed all the animals he saw when he went hunting. This indiscriminate killing began to worry the animals around him. So, they put their heads together and came up with a plan. All the animals went to Durdanta and negotiated with him. They were careful not to complain or sound whiny. “Your royal Highness, we noticed you’ve been killing too many of us at one time. That concerns us because we’re worried that you may run out of food. So, we’ve come up with a plan to save you the trouble of hunting. One of us will come to you every day, right here, and be your meal.” The lion was fascinated by the suggestion! “Hmmm, home delivery service! What a novel idea!” Aloud, he said, “Sure! Why not?” From then on, the lion ate whichever creature walked into his den. He was surprised at how easy it was not to have to work for his meals. The animals must have had some kind of rotation; one day, it was an old hare’s turn to be the lion’s dinner. Naturally, the hare was not thrilled at the idea. He thought, “If I believed it would save my life, I’d try hard not to make the lion angry. But no matter what I do, he’s going to eat me. So why should I rush to his den? Let me enjoy the sights and sounds of this earth a wee bit longer on my last day here.” And that’s what he did! The hare wandered around listening to the birdsongs, smelling fresh grass, and tasting berries. When he finally reached the den, it was many hours later. The famished lion was prowling at the den’s entrance. At the sight of the small hare, Durdanta roared, baring his sharp teeth. “How dare you arrive so late, you piffling little hare? Don’t you know I’m the mighty Durdanta!” The hare was lucky he didn’t go deaf then and there. But, oh, he could think on his feet. “Your majesty, please, please,” he held up his paw. “Hear me out. It wasn’t my fault at all. I did leave my home early in the morning! I couldn’t wait to get here because it is such an honour to be your dinner. Alas! Do you know what delayed me? I ran into another lion, sir, that too within your territory! I begged and begged and begged him to let me come to you and let you know first. I knew you would be hungry. He agreed on the condition that I go back immediately after informing you of my whereabouts. He is waiting for me, even now.” The lion turned red with rage. “Who dares trespass in my territory! Take me to this rogue who threatens to take my food!” Remember the hare went wandering before he came to Durdanta’s den? He had spotted a well on the way. Now, he took the lion to this well. Trembling with fake fear, the hare pointed his paws at the mouth of the well. He whispered, “It’s in there. I won’t show my face because it’ll warn him. If you…look down…he’s there.” Durdanta slammed his paws on the gaping well. Grunting and baring his pointed teeth, the vicious creature peered into the well…and drew a long breath. Then Without a warning, he leaped in to fight the creature in the water. The conceited and selfish beast was too stupid to realize that it was his own image reflecting off the water! “Being powerful because of your size and strength is all well and good. But it’s your smarts that matter. See how the meek hare managed to con the lion? That is how I plan to take on the snake.” “Your story is interesting, no doubt. But what is your plan?” The female crow asked, wanting to reassure herself that her husband’s idea was sound. The male crow replied, “You know that the prince comes to bathe daily in the pond near here? I’ve been observing his routine for some days now. He puts his clothes and jewellery on the steps before he enters the water. Fly to the steps when the prince is already in the water and grab the gold necklace. The guards will be watching for other people or wild animals. So you won’t be in danger. Make sure they follow you and see you drop the necklace into the snake’s hollow.” He looked intently at his wife. “Fly to the topmost branch as soon as you dispose of the necklace, you hear me? Don’t stand around waiting to see what the guards do,” he warned her. And so it happened. The prince came to the pond for a dip. He left his clothes neatly folded on the steps leading to the ghat and placed the jewellery on top of the clothes. The mama crow waited until he was in the water, for that’s when the guards moved away to give him some privacy. The husband had been right—no one paid any attention to the crow as she swung low, snatched the necklace, and rose into the air. “Hey!” the prince let out a yell. The guards turned at the cry and saw him waving at the crow. The gold necklace dazzled radiantly in the sun as the crow flew low enough to tempt the guards. Then the chase was on—the five men waved their sticks and spears as they stumbled through the brush. Fortunately, the crow had had a head start and was too far away for the men to fling their weapons at her. She hovered in the air when she got to her tree until the guards were near enough to see what she did. Then, swiftly, she dropped the necklace in the hollow and shot out of there. She remembered her husband’s advice not to linger. The guards, of course, had no idea of the history between the crow and the snake. An uproar drowned out all the other noises in that part of the forest. Voices cried out one after another. “Aaaah!” “SNAKE!” “KILL IT! KILL IT!” Thump! Crack! “Yaaay!” “It’s dead!“”It’s dead!“ The crows didn’t have to see it to know the snake was dead. The guards left with the glittering heavy jewellery, happy to be able to report their adventure to the prince. The crows were rid of their enemy once and for all. Dhamanaka looked pleased as he ended the story. “See, we don’t have to target the bull directly. There is something to be learned from the story of the crow and the snake! We can find a way to weaken this friendship on the sly.” He told his brother. Karataka nodded encouragingly. “If that’s what you believe, then I wish you success in what you are about to do.”
moral: Intelligence is greater than strength.

...The conceited and selfish beast was too stupid to realize that it was his own image reflecting off the water!
...Then, swiftly, she dropped the necklace in the hollow and shot out of there.

title: The Story of the Foolish Monkey
narrative_text:
One rainy season, disaffection broke out on his borders. The troops stationed there, after two or three battles in which they failed to conquer their adversaries, sent a message to the king. Spite of the season, spite of the rains he took the field, and encamped before Jetavana Park. Then he began to ponder. "’Tis a bad season for an expedition; every crevice and hollow is full of water; the road is heavy: I’ll go visit the Master. He will be sure to ask ‘whither away’; then I’ll tell him. It is not only in things of the future life that our Master protects me, but he protects in the things which we now see. So if my going is not to prosper, he will say ‘It is a bad time to go, Sire’; but if I am to prosper, he will say nothing." So into the Park he came, and after greeting the Master sat down on one side.

"Whence come you, O King," asked the Master, "at this unseasonable hour?" "Sir," he replied, "I am on my way to quell a border rising; and I come first to bid you farewell." To this the Master said, "So it happened before, that mighty monarchs, before setting out for war, have listened to the word of the wise, and turned back from an unseasonable expedition." Then, at the king’s request, he told an old story.

Once upon a time, when Brahmadatta was reigning in Benares, he had a Councillor who was his right-hand man and gave him advice in things spiritual and temporal. There was a rising on the frontier, and the troops there stationed sent the king a letter. The king started, rainy season though it was, and formed a camp in his park. The Bodhisatta stood before the king. At that moment the people had steamed some peas for the horses, and poured them out into a trough. One of the monkeys that lived in the park jumped down from a tree, filled his mouth and hands with the peas, then up again, and sitting down in the tree he began to eat. As he ate, one pea fell from his hand upon the ground. Down dropped at once all the peas from his hands and mouth, and down from the tree he cause, to hunt for the lost pea. But that pea he could not find; so he climbed up his tree again, and sat still, very glum, looking like some one who had lost a thousand in some lawsuit.

The king observed how the monkey had done, and pointed it out to the Bodhisatta. "Friend, what do you think of that?" he asked. To which the Bodhisatta made answer: "King, this is what fools of little wit are wont to do; they spend a pound to win a penny;" and he went on to repeat the first stanza:

> "A foolish monkey, living in the trees,
> O king, when both his hands were full of peas,
> Has thrown them all away to look for one:
> There is no wisdom, Sire, in such as these."

Then the Bodhisatta approached the king, and addressing him again, repeated the second stanza:

> "Such are we, O mighty monarch, such all those that greedy be;
> Losing much to gain a little, like the monkey and the pea."

On hearing this address the king turned and went straight back to Benares. And the outlaws hearing that the king had set forth from his capital to make mincemeat of his enemies, hurried away from the borders.

At the time when this story was told, the outlaws ran away in just the sane fashion. The king, after listening to the Master’s utterances, rose and took his leave, and went back to Savatthi.

The Master, after this discourse was at an end, identified the Birth: "In those days Ananda was the king, and the wise councillor was I myself."
moral: It is foolish to lose much to gain little; do not lose sight of the greater good for a small gain.

...To which the Bodhisatta made answer: "King, this is what fools of little wit are wont to do; they spend a pound to win a penny;" and he went on to repeat the first stanza:
...On hearing this address the king turned and went straight back to Benares.

title: Akbar-Birbal: The Story of the Emperor's Ring
narrative_text:
One fine day, Emperor Akbar lost his precious ring, a cherished gift from his father. Distraught, he summoned his most trusted advisor, Birbal, and implored him, 'Birbal, I have lost my ring, a gift from my father. Please help me find it.' Birbal, with his characteristic calm, reassured the Emperor, 'Do not worry, Your Majesty! I will find your ring right now.'

Birbal then addressed the assembled courtiers, his gaze sweeping across the room. 'Your Majesty,' he declared, 'the ring is here in this court itself. It is with one of the courtiers. The courtier who has a straw in his beard has your ring.'

The courtier who had indeed taken the emperor's ring was instantly shocked by Birbal's pronouncement. In a moment of panic, he instinctively moved his hand to his beard, attempting to brush away any imaginary straw. Birbal, ever observant, noticed this tell-tale act. He immediately pointed towards the nervous courtier and announced, 'Please search this man. He has the emperor’s ring.'

Emperor Akbar, astonished by Birbal's swift and seemingly magical deduction, could not comprehend how Birbal had managed to find the ring so quickly. Birbal then explained to Akbar the simple truth: 'A guilty person is always scared, Your Majesty. Their actions often betray their inner turmoil.' The courtier, confronted with his guilt and Birbal's undeniable insight, confessed to his deed, and the Emperor's ring was safely returned.
moral: A guilty conscience often betrays itself through actions or expressions, even when one tries to hide the truth.

title: The King's New Crown
alt_titles: Tenali Raman and the True Wealth
narrative_text:
In the glorious kingdom of Vijayanagara, King Krishnadevaraya, known for his love of art and his discerning taste, commissioned a magnificent new crown. This crown was unlike any other, adorned with the rarest diamonds, rubies, and emeralds, each gem sparkling with an ethereal glow. Upon its completion, the King was immensely proud, declaring it the most priceless possession in his entire kingdom. He summoned his esteemed courtiers to the royal assembly, eager to display his new treasure and bask in their admiration. The courtiers, ever eager to please their monarch, showered him with exaggerated compliments, each vying to outdo the other in their praise of the crown's unparalleled beauty and immense value. The Raja Guru, the chief priest and a prominent figure in the court, also offered his effusive praise, secretly hoping to gain further favor with the King and, perhaps, to subtly undermine Tenali Raman's standing.

As the chorus of adulation filled the royal hall, Tenali Raman, the quick-witted and astute court jester, remained conspicuously silent. His usual playful demeanor was replaced by a thoughtful expression. The King, noticing Tenali Raman's uncharacteristic quietness, turned to him with a hint of challenge in his voice. 'Tenali, my dear jester, you, who are so quick with words and observations, have you no praise for this magnificent creation? Do you not agree that this is indeed the most priceless possession in my kingdom?'

Tenali Raman, after a moment of profound thought, bowed respectfully before the King. 'Your Majesty,' he began, his voice calm and clear, 'while the crown is undeniably a masterpiece of artistry and adorned with gems of immense material value, I humbly submit that it is not the most priceless possession in your kingdom.' A gasp rippled through the assembly. The courtiers exchanged shocked glances, and the Raja Guru's eyes gleamed with a malicious satisfaction. This was the opportunity he had been waiting for. 'Tenali Raman!' the Raja Guru boomed, his voice laced with indignation, 'How dare you contradict His Majesty? Do you not see the splendor before you? What could possibly be more valuable than this?' He challenged Tenali Raman to prove his audacious statement, confident that the jester had finally overstepped his bounds. Tenali Raman, with a serene smile, accepted the challenge, promising to reveal the truly priceless possession within a week.

Over the next few days, a palpable tension hung in the air of the palace. The courtiers whispered amongst themselves, eagerly anticipating Tenali Raman's downfall. However, Tenali Raman, with his characteristic ingenuity, was already at work. He secretly arranged for a false alarm to be raised, spreading news that a devastating fire had broken out in the royal treasury, the very place where the King's new crown was securely kept. Panic erupted throughout the palace. The King, upon hearing the dreadful news, rushed towards the treasury, his heart pounding with fear for his cherished crown. He was about to enter the burning (though in reality, unharmed) treasury when Tenali Raman suddenly appeared and gently but firmly stopped him.

'Your Majesty,' Tenali Raman said, his gaze sweeping across the scene, 'look around you.' The King, bewildered, followed Tenali Raman's gaze. He saw not just his guards and courtiers, but also the common people of Vijayanagara, who, upon hearing the alarm, had rushed to the palace. They were bravely forming a human chain, passing buckets of water, risking their own lives to help extinguish the supposed fire. Their faces were etched with concern, not for the crown, but for the safety of their King and their beloved palace. Tenali Raman then turned back to the King, his eyes filled with profound wisdom. 'Your Majesty,' he explained, 'the true priceless possession of your kingdom is not this crown, however magnificent it may be. It is the loyalty, the love, and the well-being of your people. Without their devotion, their willingness to protect what is yours, and their prosperity, this crown, and indeed the entire kingdom, would be utterly meaningless. Their lives, their spirit, and their unwavering support are the real jewels that make your reign truly rich.'

King Krishnadevaraya stood in stunned silence, his eyes welling up with tears of realization. The false alarm had served its purpose, revealing a truth far more profound than any material wealth. He looked at his people, their faces smudged with ash but shining with courage and concern, and understood the depth of Tenali Raman's wisdom. He embraced Tenali Raman, acknowledging his folly and expressing his heartfelt gratitude. The courtiers, including the Raja Guru, stood humbled, their earlier jealousy replaced by awe and respect for Tenali Raman's unparalleled insight. From that day forward, King Krishnadevaraya ruled with an even greater sense of responsibility and compassion, always remembering that the true strength and wealth of his kingdom lay not in its treasures, but in the hearts of its people.
moral: The true wealth of a kingdom lies in the loyalty, love, and well-being of its people, not in its material possessions.

title: The Princess and the Magical Horse
alt_titles: Vikram and Betal: The Tale of the Magical Steed
narrative_text:
In the ancient kingdom of Ujjain, ruled the wise and just King Vikramaditya. One day, a yogi presented him with a magical fruit, which the King, in turn, gave to his queen. The yogi, however! had a sinister motive. He later demanded that the King bring him a Vetala (Betal), a ghostly being, from a distant cremation ground. The Vetala was suspended upside down from a tree, and every time Vikramaditya tried to carry him, the Vetala would tell a story, ending with a riddle. If Vikramaditya answered correctly and remained silent, the Vetala would return to the tree. If he spoke, the Vetala would allow himself to be carried. If he knew the answer but remained silent, his head would burst into a thousand pieces.

On one such journey, the Vetala began a tale about a beautiful princess named Padmavati, the daughter of King Chandrasen. Princess Padmavati was known for her extraordinary beauty and her love for horses. One day, a merchant arrived at the court with a magnificent horse, unlike any seen before. It was said to be a magical horse, capable of flying and understanding human speech. The princess was captivated by the horse and insisted on buying it.

After acquiring the magical horse, Princess Padmavati spent all her time with it, riding it through the skies and across vast lands. One evening, while riding high above the clouds, the horse suddenly spoke to her, revealing that it was not merely a horse but a celestial being cursed to live as a horse until it found a true companion who loved it unconditionally. The horse confessed its love for the princess and asked her to marry him.

Confused and frightened, the princess did not know how to respond. She loved the horse as a companion, but the idea of marrying an animal, even a magical one, was unsettling. She returned to her palace, deeply troubled by the horse's confession. The horse, sensing her dilemma, became heartbroken and flew away, never to be seen again.

Now, Betal posed his riddle to King Vikramaditya: 'Who was truly at fault in this situation? The princess for not accepting the horse's love, or the horse for confessing its love and then leaving?'

King Vikramaditya, after a moment of thought, replied, 'The horse was at fault. It should not have confessed its love in such a manner, knowing the princess's human nature and the societal norms. It put the princess in an impossible situation. True love understands boundaries and respects the other's feelings without forcing them into a corner.'

As soon as Vikramaditya spoke, the Vetala slipped from his shoulder and returned the tree, forcing the King to repeat his arduous journey once more. The moral of the story is that true affection respects boundaries and does not impose expectations that can cause distress to the beloved.
moral: True affection respects boundaries and does not impose expectations that can cause distress to the beloved.

title: Mahabharata: The story of Dronacharya
alt_titles: The Story of Drona and Dhrupad
narrative_text:
Drona, a master of archery, was born into a life of poverty. His father, the sage Bharadwaj, was a friend of Prushad, the king of Panchala. Because of this friendship, Drona and Prushad's son, Dhrupad, grew up together in the ashram of Rishi Agnivesh. They became the closest of friends, and Dhrupad promised Drona that when he became king, he would share his kingdom with him.

After their time in the ashram, their paths diverged. Drona, seeking to master the art of warfare, learned from the great Parshuram. He married Kripi and they had a son named Ashwathama. Meanwhile, Dhrupad ascended to the throne of Panchala after his father's death.

Drona's poverty was a constant source of sorrow. His son, Ashwathama, longed for milk like other children, but Drona could not even afford a cow. His wife, Kripi, would mix flour with water to create a semblance of milk for their son. This pained Drona deeply, and he decided to seek help from his childhood friend, King Dhrupad.

When Drona arrived at the court of Panchala, he reminded Dhrupad of their friendship and his promise. But Dhrupad, now a powerful king, scorned him. He declared that there could be no friendship between a king and a pauper. Heartbroken and enraged, Drona vowed to take revenge for this humiliation.

He traveled to Hastinapura and became the royal teacher to the Pandava and Kaurava princes. He saw in them the means to fulfill his vow. He told them that their Guru Dakshina, the traditional offering to a teacher, would be to capture Dhrupad and bring him before him. Of all the princes, only Arjuna was resolute in his promise to fulfill his guru's wish.

When their training was complete, the princes marched on Panchala. The Kauravas, who attacked first, were defeated by Dhrupad's army. Then, the Pandavas, led by the valiant Arjuna, launched their assault. Arjuna, with his supreme skill in archery, defeated Dhrupad, captured him, and brought him to Drona.

Drona, seeing his childhood friend humbled before him, did not seek to kill him. Instead, he took half of Dhrupad's kingdom, the northern part, and returned the southern half to him. He told Dhrupad that he had taken what was rightfully his, as per their childhood promise.

This act of revenge, however, did not bring peace. Dhrupad, burning with the desire for vengeance, performed a great yagna. From the sacrificial fire, a son, Drishtadyumna, was born, destined to kill Drona. A daughter, Draupadi, also emerged from the fire, who would go on to marry the Pandavas, further deepening the enmity between the two families. The story of Drona and Dhrupad is a poignant reminder of how pride and arrogance can destroy even the strongest of bonds, and how the thirst for revenge can lead to a never-ending cycle of violence.
moral: Friendship should not be based on wealth or status, and revenge can lead to a cycle of violence.

performance_notes.opening_line: Let us travel back in time, to the age of the Mahabharata, and hear the story of a great teacher, a formidable warrior, and a man whose life was shaped by a promise and a betrayal.

...Drona, seeing his childhood friend humbled before him, did not seek to kill him.

safety_notes: The story contains themes of poverty, ridicule, and revenge, which may be sensitive for some listeners.

title: The King and the Foolish Monkey
narrative_text: Once there was a king, who had a pet monkey. This monkey was a fool, but was treated royally and moved freely in the king's palace. He was also allowed to enter the king's personal rooms that were forbidden even for the confidential servants. One afternoon, the king was asleep, while the monkey kept a watch. All of a sudden, a fly came in the room and sat on the king's chest. The monkey swayed her away, but the fly would only go away for some time and return on the king's chest again. The monkey got very angry and excited. The foolish monkey started chasing the fly with a sword. As the fly sat on the king's chest again, the monkey hit the fly with all his might. The fly flew away unharmed, but the king was severely wounded.
moral: Beware of a foolish friend. He can cause you more harm than your enemy.

title: The Old Wise Crow
narrative_text:
There stood a huge banyan tree on the outskirts of a small town. Thousands of crows lived in this tree. Not far from the banyan tree, there was a mountain cave. Thousands of owls lived in it.

The king of the owls accompanied by his soldiers used to hunt crows during the night. Soon thousands of crows were killed and eaten up by the owls. One of the main reasons for the killing of the crows at such a large scale was, that they were unable to see clearly during the night. And the owls, being nocturnal, could easily locate the crows sitting in the tree during night hours. And the unfortunate crows were defenceless; they couldn't flyaway for the safety of their lives.

This kind of situation went on to such an extent, and the loss of lives of thousands of crows became so unbearable for the king of crows that one day he was compelled to call a meeting to discuss ways and means to combat the situation and bring an end to the continuing disaster.

After heated discussions and exchange of views and ideas, a plan was chalked out in the meeting, according to which a drama was to be enacted at a little distance from the owls' cave.

So, on the next day, the drama was staged and while enacting the drama, an old crow was 'thrashed' and 'beaten mercilessly' by the king of crows and his soldiers. The seemingly half dead old crow, with a goat's blood sprinkled all over his body was later picked up by the soldiers of the king owl. This was done on the advice of a senior minister in king owl's cabinet. This minister had told the king owl. "Your Majesty, this badly wounded crow had spoken in favour of us in his king's cabinet meeting, saying that ours was a more intelligent and superior race, better managed and strong, hence we deserved the right to be known and recognized as the king of birds. This led to the murderous attack on the poor fellow."

"We should help him recuperate from his wounds and injuries," said the king of owls. "After this, we'll utilise this old knowledgeable crow's talent in demolishing the kingdom of the crows."

The old crow soon found a favourable place in the owls kingdom. Many of the owl ministers were in his favour, except, one or two, who opposed the crow, saying that he was, after all, from the enemy's camp.

Despite this opposition from certain owl ministers, the crow continued to live in the owls' cave.

Lastly, it was the day time when the owls themselves were not able to see anything, due to sunlight, when the seemingly wounded and infirm crow piled up thousands of wooden logs at the mouth of the owls' cave and put fire into it. The devouring flames leapt up high and all the owls in the owls' kingdom, inside the cave, were burnt to ashes.
moral: Never underestimate your enemy, and be wary of those who offer praise while having ulterior motives. Cleverness and strategic thinking can be more powerful weapons than physical strength.

title: Jataka: The story of the foolish jackal
narrative_text: Once on a time when Brahmadatta was reigning in Benares, the Bodhisatta was born a jackal, and dwelt in a charnel-grove with a great following of jackals of whom he was king. And at that time there was a festival held at Rajagaha, and a very wet festival it was, with everybody drinking hard. Now a parcel of rogues got hold of victual and drink in abundance, and putting on their best clothes sang and made merry over their fare. By midnight the meat was all gone, though the liquor still held out. Then on one asking for more meat and being told there was none left, said the fellow, "Victuals never lack while I am about. I'll off to the charnel-grove, kill a jackal prowling about to eat the corpses, and bring back some meat." So saying he snatched up a club and made his way out of the city by the sewer to the place, where he lay down, club in hand, feigning to be dead. Just then, followed by the other jackals, the Bodhisatta came up and marked the pretended corpse. Suspecting the fraud, he determined to sift the matter. So he went round to the lee side and knew by the scent that the man was not really dead. Resolving to make the man look foolish before leaving him, the Bodhisatta stole near and took hold of the club with his teeth and tugged at it. The rascal did not leave go: not perceiving the Bodhisatta’s approach, he took a tighter grip. Hereon the Bodhisatta stepped back a pace or two and said, "My good man, if you had been dead, you would not have tightened your grip on your club when I was tugging at it, and so have betrayed yourself." So saying, he uttered this stanza: Thy tightening grip upon thy club doth show, Thy rank imposture--thou'rt no corpse, I trow. Finding that he was discovered, the rogue sprang to his feet and flung his club at the Bodhisatta, but missed his aim, "Be off, you brute," said he, "I've missed you this time." Turning round, the Bodhisatta said, "True you have missed me, but be assured you will not miss the torments of the Great Hell and the sixteen Lesser Hells." Empty-handed, the rogue left the cemetery and, after bathing in a ditch, went back into the city by the way he had come.
moral: Deceit and trickery will ultimately be exposed, and those who try to harm others will only bring harm upon themselves.

performance_notes.opening_line: Here is a tale from the Jatakas, about a clever jackal and a foolish rogue.

...took a tighter grip.
...betrayed yourself.

title: Akbar-Birbal: The Story of the Magical Painting
narrative_text:
Emperor Akbar, known for his love of art and beauty, once challenged his wise minister Birbal to find a way to capture and preserve the fleeting beauty of nature forever. Birbal, with his characteristic wit and ingenuity, suggested that a magical painting could achieve this. He proposed a kingdom-wide competition to find an artist capable of creating such a marvel. Many talented artists presented their works, but none could truly capture the essence of Birbal's vision.

Among the hopefuls was a humble artist named Rajan, who possessed an extraordinary gift for making his art appear incredibly lifelike. Rajan was chosen to undertake the challenging task. He spent many days and nights meticulously working on his masterpiece. When he finally unveiled his painting, it depicted a serene landscape: a lush green meadow adorned with vibrant, colorful flowers, a gentle stream meandering through the scene, and delicate butterflies fluttering in the air.

Akbar and Birbal gazed at the painting, and to their astonishment, the artwork magically came to life before their very eyes. They could feel a soft, refreshing breeze, hear the melodious chirping of birds, and watch the colorful butterflies dance gracefully. The painting was not merely an image; it was an experience, a living window into nature's splendor. It became a treasured possession in the royal palace, offering a tranquil escape from the daily stresses and demands of court life.

The tale of this magical painting quickly spread throughout the kingdom, inspiring people far and wide to appreciate the profound beauty found in both nature and art. It served as a powerful reminder that with creativity, intelligence, and a touch of magic, even the most extraordinary dreams and seemingly impossible feats could indeed come true.
moral: Creativity, intelligence, and a touch of imagination can bring even the most extraordinary dreams to life, and art has the power to preserve and evoke the beauty of nature.

title: Tenali Raman and the Fake Doctor
alt_titles: Tenali Raman and the Poisonous Saint
narrative_text: In the great kingdom of Vijayanagara, there lived a clever and witty man named Tenali Raman, a respected minister in the court of King Krishnadevaraya. One day, a man dressed as a holy monk appeared in the city. He was handsome and spoke eloquently, and soon the people of Vijayanagara were in awe of him. They called him the 'divine monk' and flocked to him for blessings and cures for their ailments. The monk, however, was a fraud. He was a wicked man who used his charm to cheat people. He would promise to cure their illnesses with his divine powers, but would instead give them a slow poison that would make them sicker. He would then demand large sums of money for the 'cure'. Many innocent people fell victim to his scheme. Tenali Raman grew suspicious of the monk. He noticed that the people who visited the monk only seemed to get worse, not better. He decided to investigate. One day, Tenali Raman disguised himself as a sick old man and went to the monk. He complained of a terrible stomach ache and begged the monk to cure him. The monk, seeing a wealthy old man, readily agreed. He gave Tenali Raman a small packet of 'holy ash' and told him to mix it with water and drink it every day. Tenali Raman thanked the monk and left. But instead of drinking the ash, he took it to the royal physician. The physician tested the ash and discovered that it was a slow poison. Tenali Raman now had the proof he needed. The next day, he went to the King's court and announced that the 'divine monk' was a fraud. The King was shocked and angry. He ordered his soldiers to arrest the monk. The monk was brought to the court, where Tenali Raman revealed his deception. The monk, faced with the evidence, confessed to his crimes. The King, furious at the monk's wickedness, banished him from the kingdom forever. The people of Vijayanagara were grateful to Tenali Raman for saving them from the fake doctor. They learned a valuable lesson that day: to not blindly trust everyone who appears to be holy or wise.
moral: We should not blindly trust people, even if they appear to be holy or wise. It is important to use our own judgement and reason.

title: Vikram-Betal: The Story of the Prince and the Magical Lamp
alt_titles: The Prince, the Magical Lamp, and the Changing Forms
The Tale of Manaswi and the Enchanted Lamp
narrative_text:
King Vikram, bound by his promise, carried the elusive Betal through the night. As was his custom, Betal began a tale to test the king's wisdom. "O King," began the Betal, "in the land of Gaur, there lived a Brahman's daughter-in-law named Sita, who possessed a magical lamp. This was no ordinary lamp; when rubbed, it would grant the user the ability to change their form. Sita, in truth, was Manaswi, a young man cursed to live as a woman by day, but who could transform into his true male form by night, a secret he kept hidden with the help of the magical lamp.

One day, while strolling in the royal garden, Manaswi, in his female form as Sita, encountered Princess Chandraprabha. It was love at first sight for both, and a deep affection blossomed between them. Chandraprabha, unaware of Sita's true identity, confided in her new friend about her newfound love for a handsome young man she had seen. Sita, realizing the princess spoke of him, revealed his true form by rubbing the magical lamp, transforming into Manaswi. Chandraprabha, though initially surprised, was overjoyed to find her beloved. They were married, and for five months, Manaswi lived as a woman by day and a man by night, using the magical lamp to maintain his dual existence.

However, Manaswi grew restless with his confined life and longed for more freedom. Chandraprabha, too, became weary of their secret existence. One day, during a wedding feast at the grand treasurer's house, Manaswi, in his female form, was seen by the treasurer's son, who immediately fell deeply in love with her. The treasurer's son, consumed by his love, refused to eat or drink, falling gravely ill.

Concerned for his son, the treasurer approached Raja Subichar, Chandraprabha's father, and pleaded for the hand of the Brahman's daughter-in-law for his son. The Raja, though initially reluctant to break a trust, was swayed by the political pressure from his ministers and the treasurer's feigned illness. He was convinced that sacrificing one person's commitment for the stability of the kingdom was justifiable.

King Vikram, having listened to the Betal's intricate tale, was then posed with a question: "O King, who is the most blameworthy in this situation?"

King Vikram, after careful thought, replied, "The Raja is the most blameworthy. He, as a ruler, had a duty to uphold justice and protect the trust placed in him. To sacrifice his principles and break his word for political expediency or personal comfort is a grave failing. The magical lamp, in its essence, revealed truths and allowed for transformation, but the Raja's actions demonstrated a lack of moral fortitude, choosing convenience over righteousness."

With this answer, the Betal, satisfied with Vikram's wisdom, returned to his tree, and King Vikram continued his journey, ever mindful of the complexities of justice and morality.
moral: A ruler's duty is to uphold justice and protect trust, even in the face of personal or political pressure. True wisdom lies in moral fortitude and adherence to principles.

title: The Sacrifice of Hadi Rani
narrative_text:
In the vibrant land of Rajasthan, where tales of valor and sacrifice are woven into the very fabric of its history, lived a queen named Hadi Rani Sahal Kanwar. She was the daughter of Sangram Singh Hada, a brave Hada Chauhan Rajput, and was married to Rawat Ratan Singh, a chieftain of Salumbar in Mewar. Their union was fresh, their love blossoming, when the call of duty echoed through the kingdom.

It was a time when the formidable Mughal governor of Ajmer Subah threatened the peace of Mewar. Raj Singh I, the revered ruler of Mewar, summoned his chieftains to rally against the invading forces. Among them was Rawat Ratan Singh, a valiant warrior, but also a newlywed husband whose heart was torn between his love for his queen and his duty to his land.

As he prepared to depart for the battlefield, a wave of hesitation washed over him. He felt a deep longing for his beloved Hadi Rani and, in a moment of weakness, sent a messenger to her, asking for a memento, a symbol of her love that he could carry into battle. He believed that having a piece of her with him would strengthen his resolve.

When the messenger delivered the chieftain's request, Hadi Rani understood the unspoken truth behind his plea. She realized that her husband's attachment to her was becoming an obstacle to his duty, a distraction that could jeopardize the fate of Mewar. Her heart, though heavy with love, was also filled with an unwavering sense of honor and duty towards her kingdom and her husband's reputation.

With a courage that transcended personal affection, Hadi Rani made an unimaginable sacrifice. She knew that only a profound act could sever her husband's emotional ties and allow him to fight with his full might. With a steady hand, she took a sword and, in a moment of ultimate selflessness, severed her own head. She instructed her servant to present it to her husband as her final memento.

The servant, grief-stricken but bound by his queen's command, covered her head with a cloth and presented it to Rawat Ratan Singh on the battlefield. When the chieftain uncovered the offering, his heart was shattered, yet a profound sense of pride and determination surged through him. He understood the immense sacrifice his beloved queen had made. He tied her head around his neck, a grim but powerful reminder of her unwavering courage and his duty.

Inspired by Hadi Rani's unparalleled sacrifice, Rawat Ratan Singh fought with unmatched ferocity and bravery. His grief transformed into a burning resolve, and he led his troops to victory against the Mughal forces. The rebellion was successful, and Mewar was saved. However, the victory came at a heavy price for Rawat Ratan Singh. Having lost the desire to live without his beloved queen, he fell to his knees and, with a heavy heart, took the ultimate step, joining Hadi Rani in the annals of eternal love and sacrifice.

Even today, the legend of Hadi Rani is sung in the folk songs of Rajasthan, a testament to her extraordinary courage, valor, and unwavering commitment to duty. Her story serves as an eternal inspiration, reminding all of the profound sacrifices made for honor and homeland.
moral: True courage lies in selfless sacrifice for a greater cause, and duty can demand the ultimate price.

title: The Clever Fisherman and the King
narrative_text:
As King Parveez and Queen Shireen were sitting in their palace one day, a fisherman who was selling beautiful fish was brought in front of them. King Parveez decided to buy a fish and gave the fisherman 4000 dirham for it. The fisherman was overjoyed. He thanked King Parveez and left.

Queen Shireen turned to her husband in anger and scolded him for paying so much for a fish. King Parveez let her have her say but said that the fish was purchased and that there was nothing that could be done about it.

However, Queen Shireen was adamant and insisted that the fisherman be brought back.

"We will ask the fisherman about the gender of the fish," she said. "If he answers that it is female, we will say that we wanted a male fish and if his response is that it is male, we will say that we wanted a female fish! Either way, we will be able to return the fish and have our money back."

So the poor fisherman was called back and was asked the question.

Fortunately, he was clever enough not to be caught out. The fisherman replied, "This fish is unique in the sense that it is neutral, i.e., it's neither male nor female."

King Parveez was so impressed by the ingenuity of the man that he ordered a further 4000 dirham to be paid to him. The fisherman thanked King Parveez again, but as he was about to leave with the heavy bag of money, one of the coins fell onto the floor. The fisherman immediately bent down to look for the coin. Queen Shireen was already upset that the fisherman had been given 8000 dirham.

"Look how miserly this man is!" the Queen Shireen exclaimed. "One coin has fallen out of his bag full of money and he searches for it instead of leaving it for some other poor servant to find."

The fisherman heard this remark and said, "O Queen, it is not out of miserliness that I search for the coin but rather because it had the picture of generous King Parveez on it. I would not tolerate anyone to cause dishonour to King Parveez by treading on the coin."

King Parveez was so flattered with this response that he immediately called for another 4000 dirham to be given to the fisherman. When Queen Shireen saw all this, she thought it was better to hold her tongue and let the fisherman go with the 12000 dirham before the King decided to increase the amount again.
moral: Speech is an art. If one knows what, when, and how to talk, then such a person will be successful in life. A person's intellect and wisdom are recognized through their speech.

title: Sant Eknath: The Embodiment of Devotion and Service
alt_titles: The Life of Sant Eknath Maharaj
narrative_text:
Sant Eknath, born into the Rigvedi Ashwalaayana Brahmin family of Saint Bhanudas, was orphaned young and raised by his loving grandparents. From a tender age, his heart yearned for devotion to God, understanding that a Guru's blessings were essential to attain divine communion. Following an inner calling, at the age of twelve, he journeyed to Devgiri (Daulatabad), where he found Janardan Swami, a devout follower of Dattatraya and the castellan of the fort. Eknath immediately recognized Janardan Swami as his Sadguru and dedicated himself to his service. Witnessing Eknath's unwavering devotion, Swami accepted him as his disciple.

One day, while Janardan Swami was deep in meditation in a cave on the fort's peak, invaders launched an attack. Fearing that the commotion would disturb his Guru's spiritual practice, young Eknath bravely took up a sword, mounted a horse, and single-handedly fought off the attackers, securing victory. Recognizing Eknath's profound devotion and spiritual readiness, Janardan Swami led him to the mountain of Shulibhanjan, where Eknath was granted the divine vision of Dattatraya. After this profound experience, Eknath completed his pilgrimage and returned to Paithan.

As per his Sadguru's instructions, Eknath settled in Paithan and embraced the Grahasthashrama, the householder's life. His wife, Girjabai, was a vigilant and chaste woman, and together they raised three children: Goda, Haripandit, and Ganga. Eknath's life flourished both spiritually and domestically. He believed that divine knowledge, often confined to Sanskrit, should be accessible to all. Despite facing opposition, he fearlessly translated and explained spiritual texts in Marathi, using simple stories and Kirtans to guide people towards Parmartha (the ultimate truth). He tirelessly advocated for the right of women and the downtrodden to chant God's name, enduring harassment from some in Paithan with unwavering peace and devotion.

Such was Eknath's devotion that Lord Krishna himself served him for thirty-six years under the names Shrikhandya, Keshava, and Vithal. Even Lord Dattatraya acted as his doorkeeper. Daily Kirtans and discourses filled Nathwada, Eknath's home, which became a beacon of spiritual light. His generosity was legendary; he would offer handfuls of sugar to all visitors, hoping that the sweetness would encourage them to listen to uplifting spiritual teachings.

While many seek God, God Himself sought Eknath. An idol of Vijayi Panduranga, previously owned by a money-lender, expressed a desire to be served by Eknath. The lender brought the idol to Paithan, and Eknath, recognizing the divine will, lovingly accepted it. It is also said that Shri Ramchandra Himself inspired and guided Eknath in writing the Bhavartha Ramayana.

Eknath's passing was as extraordinary as his life. One day, he appeared to die like an ordinary person. When people remarked that his departure was no different from anyone else's, Eknath miraculously rose from his bier, stating he would depart at another chosen time. A few days later, on Falgun Vadya Shashti (1533-1599), Eknath chose Jalasamadhi (entering water to leave the mortal coil). In the presence of thousands of devotees, he performed his last Kirtan at LakshmiTirtha. At Krishanakamalteerth, he entered the water, and as it reached his navel, his soul merged with the Brhamanda (the cosmic essence). Haripandit performed the final rites. The next day, from the hot ashes at the same spot, Tulsi and Pimpal trees sprouted, and Haripandit established Eknath's Charan Padukas (footprints) there. Even today, millions of devotees visit Paithan for the Eknathashasthi festival, seeking peace, devotion, and prosperity. It is a tradition for newlywed couples to visit Nath Samadhi, believing it blesses their domestic and spiritual lives with the same happiness as Eknath's.

Sant Eknath's life is a testament to unwavering faith, selfless service, and profound love for humanity, demonstrating that true devotion transcends all barriers and leads to divine communion.
moral: True devotion, selfless service, and unwavering faith in God can overcome all obstacles and lead to divine communion. It also teaches that spiritual knowledge and divine love should be accessible to all, regardless of social standing or gender.

title: The Legend of Nag Panchami: A Serpent's Blessing
alt_titles: The Day of the Serpent Gods
How Nag Panchami Came to Be
narrative_text:
In the ancient lands of India, where rivers flowed like lifeblood and mountains touched the sky, there lived a fearsome serpent named Kaliyo. This mighty serpent, with his many heads and venomous breath, had made his home in the Yamuna River, poisoning its waters and terrorizing the villagers of Vrindavan. The people lived in constant fear, their cattle dying from the poisoned water, and their children warned never to venture near the riverbanks. Despair had settled over Vrindavan like a heavy shroud.

One day, the divine child Krishna, with his mischievous smile and eyes that held the wisdom of the cosmos, arrived in Vrindavan. He saw the suffering of the people and felt their pain. Though just a boy, Krishna possessed extraordinary courage and strength. He decided it was time to confront Kaliyo and free the Yamuna from his toxic grip.

Fearlessly, Krishna climbed a tall Kadamba tree on the riverbank and, with a playful leap, plunged into the poisoned waters. Kaliyo, enraged by this intrusion, rose from the depths, hissing furiously. A fierce battle ensued. Kaliyo coiled around Krishna, trying to crush him, but Krishna, with his divine power, grew larger and larger, forcing Kaliyo to release him. Then, Krishna began to dance on Kaliyo's many heads, each step a thunderous blow that weakened the serpent.

Kaliyo, humbled and defeated, began to vomit the poison he had consumed, and his wives, the Naga Patnis, emerged from the river, pleading with Krishna to spare their husband's life. They recognized Krishna's divinity and begged for mercy, promising that Kaliyo would never again harm anyone.

Moved by their devotion and Kaliyo's repentance, Krishna forgave the serpent. He commanded Kaliyo to leave the Yamuna River and reside in the ocean, promising him that on the fifth day of the bright fortnight of the lunar month of Shraavana, those who worshipped serpents would be blessed. This day became known as Nag Panchami.

From that day forward, the Yamuna River flowed pure and clean, and the people of Vrindavan rejoiced. They began to worship serpents on Nag Panchami, offering milk, sweets, and flowers to idols of Nag Devta (the serpent deity) or to live snakes, seeking their blessings for protection from snake bites and for the well-being of their families. It is believed that worshipping serpents on this day brings prosperity and good fortune.

Another story tells of a devout woman who, during the churning of the cosmic ocean (Samudra Manthan), prayed to the serpent Vasuki, who was used as the rope. Her devotion pleased the Nagas, and they promised to protect her family from snake bites. This act of devotion also contributed to the tradition of Nag Panchami, where women especially pray for the safety of their brothers and family members.

Thus, Nag Panchami is a day to honor all serpents, not out of fear, but out of respect for their role in nature and their connection to divine energies. It is a day of devotion, protection, and seeking blessings from the powerful Nag Devta.
moral: Respect for all living beings, even those we fear, can lead to peace and blessings. Devotion and repentance can transform even the most fearsome creatures.

title: Mahabharata: The Ascent to Heaven - The Story of the Pandavas' Final Journey
narrative_text:
After the great war of Kurukshetra and ruling Hastinapura for many years, the Pandavas, along with Draupadi, decided it was time to renounce their earthly kingdom and embark on their final journey towards the Himalayas, seeking the path to heaven. They left behind their kingdom, entrusting it to Parikshit, Arjuna's grandson. As they began their arduous trek, a loyal dog joined them, silently following their every step.

The journey was long and treacherous, testing their resolve and past deeds. One by one, they began to fall. Draupadi was the first to succumb, collapsing on the path. Bhima, saddened, asked Yudhishthira why she, their beloved queen, had fallen. Yudhishthira, with a heavy heart, explained that despite her virtues, Draupadi had held a special partiality for Arjuna in her heart, and this attachment prevented her from completing the journey. 

Soon after, Sahadeva fell. Yudhishthira revealed that Sahadeva's fall was due to his pride in his wisdom, believing himself to be unequaled in knowledge. Next, Nakula, the most handsome of the Pandavas, fell. His vanity about his physical appearance was the cause. Arjuna, the mighty archer, was the next to fall. Yudhishthira explained that Arjuna's pride in his unparalleled skill and power as a warrior had led to his demise on the path to heaven. Finally, Bhima, the strongman, also fell. Yudhishthira gently told him that his gluttony and pride in his immense strength were the reasons he could not continue.

Only Yudhishthira and the loyal dog remained. They continued their ascent, facing the harsh elements, until they reached the peak of Mount Sumeru. There, the glorious god Indra appeared in his celestial chariot, inviting Yudhishthira to ascend to heaven. However, Indra refused to allow the dog to accompany him, stating that dogs were not permitted in heaven.

Yudhishthira, ever righteous and compassionate, firmly refused to abandon his faithful companion. He declared that to forsake a creature who had shown such unwavering loyalty would be a great sin, and he would rather forgo heaven than betray his friend. At this, the dog transformed into Dharma, the God of Righteousness and Yudhishthira's divine father. Dharma praised Yudhishthira for his unwavering commitment to righteousness, his compassion, and his steadfast loyalty, revealing that this had been a final test of his virtues. Yudhishthira had passed with flying colors, proving himself worthy of the highest realms.

Together, Yudhishthira and Dharma, along with the now divine dog, proceeded to heaven. Upon entering, Yudhishthira was dismayed to find many who had committed evil deeds on Earth, while those he considered virtuous were absent. He demanded to be taken to hell, to be with his beloved brothers and Draupadi, believing they were suffering there. The gods then revealed that this was another illusion, a final test. The sights of hell vanished, and his loved ones appeared in heaven, purified of their earthly imperfections. Yudhishthira was then reunited with his brothers, Draupadi, and even Lord Krishna in his divine form as Vishnu, finding eternal bliss and peace in Vaikuntha.
moral: The story emphasizes the paramount importance of righteousness (Dharma), compassion, humility, and unwavering loyalty over all other virtues and earthly attachments. It teaches that true merit is earned through selfless actions and adherence to moral principles, even in the face of ultimate reward.

The concept of 'hell' is revealed to be an illusion, ensuring a warm and comforting narrative for elderly listeners.

title: The Story of Narada and His Arrogance
narrative_text:
Once, the great sage Narada, son of Brahma, decided to undertake a severe penance in a beautiful cave in the Himalayan Mountains, beside a celestial river. He meditated for a long time, controlling his breath and intellect, aiming for the realization of Brahman. 

Indra, the king of the deities, grew suspicious of Narada's intentions, fearing the sage might seek his throne. To disrupt Narada's penance, he commanded Kamadeva, the god of love, along with his wife Rati and friend Madhu, to tempt the sage. They employed all their arts, and Spring too displayed its full glory, but Narada's mind remained unwavering. This was because the cave was a sacred place where Shiva had once performed penance and had reduced Kamadeva to ashes. Due to Shiva's power, Kamadeva's tricks failed to affect Narada. Kamadeva returned to Indra, reporting his failure, which greatly surprised and impressed Indra, who was unaware of the true reason.

Believing he had successfully conquered Kamadeva, Narada concluded his penance, filled with immense pride. Deluded by Shiva's Maya (illusion) and lacking true knowledge, he first went to Kailasa to boast of his achievement to Rudra (Shiva). Shiva, hearing his arrogant words, advised him, "You are blessed, but never speak like this anywhere else, especially in the presence of Vishnu." However, Narada, still under Maya's influence, disregarded this advice. He then visited Brahma's abode, saluted his father, and recounted his conquest of Kama. Brahma, meditating on Shiva's feet, understood the true cause and also cautioned his son, but Narada paid no heed.

Next, Narada hastened to Vishnuloka to boast to Vishnu. Vishnu, anticipating Narada's purpose, greeted him warmly, saying, "You are blessed. I am sanctified by your visit. May I know where you come from and why you have come?" Elated by Vishnu's words, Narada arrogantly narrated his story. Vishnu, remembering Shiva's lotus-like feet, understood the situation. He praised Narada's austerities and celibacy, subtly questioning how lust could sway such a wise sage. Narada, laughing inwardly, humbly replied, "What can Kama do to me if you remain favorable to me?" He bowed to Vishnu and departed.

After Narada left, Vishnu, using his Maya, created a magnificent city, more beautiful than heaven itself. The prosperous King Silanidhi of this city was preparing for his daughter Srimati's Svayamvara (a ceremony where a princess chooses her husband from assembled suitors). Princes from all directions gathered, eager to win her hand. Narada, enchanted by the splendid city, felt love ignite within him and eagerly entered the palace. King Silanidhi adored the sage, offered him a seat, and presented his daughter Srimati, asking Narada to foretell her destiny. By this time, Narada was agitated by love and desired Srimati for himself. He told the king that Srimati's future husband would be a splendid god, lord of all, unvanquished, heroic, on par with Shiva, and vying with Kamadeva.

Agitated by love, Narada went to Vishnuloka, hoping to acquire Vishnu's form to captivate Srimati. He narrated his affair and requested Vishnu's favor for a splendid form. Vishnu, hearing this, blessed the sage with a form like his own, but with the face of Hari (which also means monkey). Narada, unaware of his monkey face, hastened to the Svayamvara, thinking, "She will choose only me since I am in Vishnu's form." Two attendants of Rudra, seeing Narada's grotesque appearance, mocked him, but Narada, overwhelmed by love, ignored them. He gazed at Princess Srimati, eager for her to choose him.

Princess Srimati entered, searching for a suitable groom. Seeing Narada with a monkey's face and Vishnu's body, she was infuriated. Unable to find a suitable prince, she grew afraid. Meanwhile, Vishnu appeared in the guise of a king, unseen by others, but visible to Srimati. She immediately garlanded him. Narada, oppressed by love, became excessively agitated. The attendants then revealed to Narada, "Deluded by love, your efforts are in vain. See, your face is as despicable as that of a monkey." Surprised and deluded by Shiva's Maya, Narada looked into a mirror. Seeing his monkey face, he became infuriated and cursed the two attendants, saying, "Since you mocked me, you will become demons born of Brahminical semen."

He then went to Vishnuloka and angrily cursed Vishnu, "You made me distressed for the sake of a woman. O Hari, you shall experience misery in that human form which you imitated. Your allies will be those whose face you assigned to me. You shall suffer the misery of separation from a woman and the travails of a human being deluded by ignorance." Thus, Narada, deluded by ignorance, cursed Hari. Vishnu quietly accepted the curse, praising Shiva's Maya. Thereafter, Shiva withdrew his enchanting Maya, and Narada regained his wisdom and freedom from delusion.

When the Maya vanished, Narada became as intelligent as before, free from distress. He repented his actions and cursed himself. Realizing his mistakes, Narada, the most excellent devotee of Vishnu, fell at Vishnu's feet, begging him to nullify the curses. Vishnu explained that Narada's haughtiness had led him to disobey Shiva's instructions, and everything had happened according to Shiva's will to remove his pride. Vishnu then advised Narada to cast off his doubts, sing Shiva's glories, and repeat his hundred names and hymns to cleanse his sins and achieve salvation. He instructed Narada to worship Shiva with body, mind, and speech, assuring him that devotion to Shiva leads to liberation. Vishnu further advised Narada to visit Brahmaloka and learn more about Shiva's greatness from Brahma.

After Vishnu vanished, Narada roamed the Earth, visiting Shiva Lingas with piety. The two attendants of Shiva, now pure in mind, approached him, bowed, and touched his feet, seeking release from his curse. They explained that Shiva had induced Narada to curse them due to his delusion. Narada, repenting his fury, lovingly replied, "My mind was depraved by Shiva's will. What I have said is bound to happen. You will be born as powerful demons, kings of the universe, devoted to Shiva, but will eventually meet death at the hands of another form of Vishnu, thereby regaining your former position."

**Moral:** Arrogance, or the pride of superiority, stems from the fear of being ordinary. It attempts to fill inner emptiness with ego rather than allowing it to blossom into humility. All malice, pains, and miseries originate from ego and ignorance. A qualified karma yogi, pure in heart, with controlled mind and senses, overcomes egoistic thinking and limited vision.
moral: Arrogance, or the pride of superiority, stems from the fear of being ordinary. It attempts to fill inner emptiness with ego rather than allowing it to blossom into humility. All malice, pains, and miseries originate from ego and ignorance. A qualified karma yogi, with a pure heart, controlled mind and senses, overcomes egoistic thinking and limited vision.

title: The Story of the Two Parrots
alt_titles: The Two Parrots and the Hermit
narrative_text:
In a dense forest, there lived two parrots who were brothers. One day, a fierce storm separated them. One parrot landed near the ashram of a kind sage, while the other fell into the hands of a gang of robbers. 

The sage found the first parrot, took him in, and raised him with love and care. The parrot learned to speak gentle words, chant prayers, and greet visitors with respect. He would say, 'Welcome, noble guest! Please come in and rest.'

Meanwhile, the second parrot grew up among the robbers. He heard them constantly planning their wicked deeds, using harsh language, and threatening travelers. He learned to imitate their words, shouting, 'Stop him! Kill him! Take all his belongings!'

Years passed. One day, a hunter, tired and thirsty, was passing through the forest. He first approached the robbers' hideout by mistake. As he neared, the second parrot shrieked, 'Stop him! Kill him! Take all his belongings!' Frightened, the hunter quickly fled from the spot.

He then stumbled upon the sage's ashram. As he approached, the first parrot greeted him warmly, 'Welcome, noble guest! Please come in and rest. The sage will be pleased to see you.' The hunter was surprised by the parrot's polite words and entered the ashram, feeling safe and welcomed.

The sage soon appeared and offered the hunter water and food. The hunter, still puzzled, asked the sage, 'O wise one, I just heard two parrots speak, but their words were as different as night and day. How can this be?'

The sage smiled gently and explained, 'My dear son, it is the company we keep that shapes our character and our speech. The first parrot grew up in an environment of peace and piety, learning good manners. The second parrot lived among robbers, and thus learned their harsh ways. Just as water takes the shape of the vessel it is poured into, so too does a living being reflect the influence of its surroundings.'

The hunter understood the sage's wisdom and thanked him. He realized the profound truth that our environment and associations play a crucial role in determining who we become.
moral: Our character and behavior are greatly influenced by the company we keep and the environment we live in. Good company leads to good thoughts and actions, while bad company can lead to negative traits.

'My dear son, it is the company we keep that shapes our character and our speech.'

title: Hitopadesha: The Story of the Foolish Lion
alt_titles: The Lion and the Old Hare
narrative_text: On the Mandara mountain there lived a Lion named Fierce-of-heart, and he was perpetually making massacre of all the wild animals. The thing grew so bad that the beasts held a public meeting, and drew up a respectful remonstrance to the Lion in these words:— "Wherefore should your Majesty thus make carnage of us all? If it may please you, we ourselves will daily furnish a beast for your Majesty’s meal." The Lion responded, "If that arrangement is more agreeable to you, be it so"; and from that time a beast was allotted to him daily, and daily devoured. One day it came to the turn of an old hare to supply the royal table, who reflected to himself as he walked along, "I can but die, and I will go to my death leisurely." "Now Fierce-of-heart, the lion, was pinched with hunger, and seeing the Hare so approaching he roared out, "How darest thou thus delay in coming?" ‘Sire,’ replied the Hare, ‘I am not to blame. I was detained on the road by another lion, who exacted an oath from me to return when I should have informed your Majesty.’ ‘Go,’ exclaimed King Fierce-of-heart in a rage; ‘show me, instantly, where this insolent villain of a lion lives.’ "The Hare led the way accordingly till he came to a deep well, whereat he stopped, and said, ‘Let my lord the King come hither and behold him.’ The Lion approached, and beheld his own reflection in the water of the well, upon which, in his passion, he directly flung himself, and so perished.
moral: Wisdom is superior to physical strength.

title: The Story of the Foolish Quail
narrative_text: Once upon a time, a large flock of quails lived together in a forest, led by a wise quail. A fowler who lived nearby earned his living by catching and selling these quails. He would imitate the call of the leader quail, and when the birds gathered, he would cast his net over them and sell them in the town. The wise quail, seeing the destruction of his flock, devised a plan. He told the other quails, 'When the fowler casts his net, each of you must put your head through a hole in the net and fly away together to the nearest thorn-bush. There, you can leave the net and be free.' The quails agreed, and the next day, when the fowler cast his net, they lifted it together and flew away, leaving the net on a thorn-bush. The fowler returned home empty-handed. His wife was angry, but the fowler said, 'The quails are working together now. As soon as they begin to quarrel, I will be able to catch them.' Not long after, one of the quails, while alighting on their feeding ground, accidentally trod on the head of another. 'Who trod on my head?' cried the second quail angrily. 'I did, but I didn't mean to. Don't be angry,' said the first. But the second quail was still angry and they began to quarrel. Soon, all the quails had taken sides in the quarrel. When the fowler came that day, he cast his net over them. This time, instead of flying off with it, one side said, 'Now, you lift the net,' and the other side said, 'Lift it yourself.' 'You try to make us lift it all,' said the quails on one side. 'No, we don't!' said the others, 'you begin and we will help,' but neither side began. So the quails quarreled, and while they were quarreling the fowler caught them all in his net. He took them to town and sold them for a good price.
moral: Unity is strength, but discord leads to destruction.

pause_points: ...and be free.
...I will be able to catch them.
...and sold them for a good price.

title: Akbar-Birbal: The Story of the Emperor's Justice
narrative_text:
Once a man sold his well to a farmer. Next day when farmer went to draw the water from that well, the man did not allow him to draw the water from it. He said, "I have sold you the well, not the water, so you cannot draw the water from the well."

The farmer became very sad, and came to the Emperor's court. He described everything to the Emperor, and asked for the justice.

The Emperor called Birbal and handed over this case to him. Birbal called the man who sold the well to the farmer. Birbal asked, "Why don't you let him use the water of the well. You have sold the well to the farmer." The man replied, "Birbal Ji, I have sold the well to the farmer, not the water. He has no right to draw the water from the well."

Then Birbal smiled and said to him, "Good, but look, since you have sold the well to this farmer, and you claim that water is yours, then you have no right to keep your water in the farmer's well. Either you pay rent to the farmer to keep your water in his well, or you take that out of his well immediately."

The man understood, that his trick has failed and pleaded Birbal to forgive him.
moral: Justice prevails through wisdom and cleverness.

...asked for the justice.
...He has no right to draw the water from the well.
...or you take that out of his well immediately.

title: Tenali Raman: The King's Condition
narrative_text:
One day, King Krishnadev Rai was in a good mood. He gave each of his courtiers a bag containing fifty gold coins. The courtiers were thrilled. Just then the king said, "You have to use these gold coins within one week. At the end of the week, you will show me all that you have bought with these gold coins. But there is a condition. Every time you spend some gold coins, you will have to see my face."

All the courtiers were very pleased. They left for the market to do some shopping. But whenever they decided to buy something, they remembered the king's condition. How could they see the king's face in the market? And if they did not see the king's face, they could not spend the gold coins. The courtiers were puzzled. Some of them roamed the market for the whole week. They thought, "If the king himself comes to the market to buy something, we might get to see his face. And then we can spend the gold coins."

But the king never came to the market. The whole week passed in this way. When the court assembled after a week, the king asked the courtiers, "Well, what did you buy with the gold coins that I had given you?" The royal priest arose from his seat. On behalf of all the courtiers, he said, "Your Majesty, all of us went to the market with great enthusiasm and excitement. We all had our bags of gold coins with us. And we wanted to buy many things! But we could not buy anything at all... Only because of the condition laid down by you. How and where could we see your face in the market?" Pointing towards the bags of gold coins that all the courtiers had brought back, the royal priest said, "Since none of us could see your face in the market, not a single gold coin could be spent."

Hearing this, the king began to laugh. He then asked Tenalirama, "Did you buy anything?" That day, Tenalirama had come to the court wearing new clothes and ornaments. He said, "Your Majesty, Look at this new dhoti... this silk kurta... this beautiful scarf...And this shining ring... I bought all these things with the gold coins given by you." All the courtiers were pleased to hear this. They thought, "Tenalirama did not see the king's face. Yet he spent all the gold coins. He has not fulfilled the condition laid down by the king. Tenalirama will be surely punished today."

The king said, "Tenalirama! You have not fulfilled the condition laid down by me. You did not see my face in the market. Yet you dared to buy these things!" Tenalirama said, "Your Majesty, I have spent every single gold coin only after I saw your face."

The king was surprised. He said, "How is that possible?" Tenalirama said, "Your Majesty, Have you forgotten that every gold coin bears the imprint of your face ?" "Oh..." said the King, rather pleased. He smiled gently. All the courtiers were rendered speechless.
moral: Wisdom and cleverness can overcome seemingly impossible challenges.

performance_notes.opening_line: One day, King Krishnadev Rai was in a good mood.
pause_points: But there is a condition. Every time you spend some gold coins, you will have to see my face.

title: Betaal Pachisi - Story 15 - Lover of Princess
alt_titles: The Princess and the Magical Ring
narrative_text:
Vikrama tries again to catch Vetala. After several attempts he succeeds and starts taking him to the ascetic. Vetala starts narrating him a story so that they can easily complete the long journey once again but warns him not to speak anything else he will go back to his place again. He begins narrating story about the princess of Kusumawati kingdom - Chandraprabha. She loved a man named Manukumar. Both of them loved each other and wanted to marry each other but this was not possible as Manu was poor. King wanted to marry Chandraprabha in another kingdom. So, the princess asks Manu to leave the place and forget her as their marriage is not possible. He left with a heavy heart.

On the other hand, a magician was returning from the business trip with his son, Shashi. He was attacked by goons on the way to Kusumawati. Manu saw this and fought bravely with them. He saved their lives. Magician was really happy with Manu and asked him for any wish. He told them that he loves king's daughter and wanted to stay with her in disguise. So, help him with the same. Magician agreed and asked him to wear a ring in his right hand. As soon as he wore the ring, he got converted into a beautiful woman. When Manu removed the ring and put it on his left hand, he again regained his real self.

Manu disguised as woman went to king with his son and magician. Magician told king that he wants to go for a pilgrimage and introduced Manu as Manu Kumari, fiancee of his son, to the king. He requested king to provide shelter to his daughter-in-law till the time he is away on pilgrimage. King agreed for the same. Soon, Manu Kumari and Chandraprabha became friends. One day, he asked Chandraprabha that why you look sad these days. Chandraprabha tells him about his love, Manu. Manu tells her that he can help in arranging their meeting. Manu moves the ring to his left finger. Princess is really happy to see Manu. Manu reveals the secret of ring to Chandraprabha.

Son of minister saw both Manu and Chandraprabha playing happily. He started liking Manu Kumari. He proposed her one day. Minister told king about his son's desire to marry Manu. King agreed for the same. Manu on the other hand, while playing with Chandraprabha, gets hurt on his finger and is unable to remove the ring from his right finger. King informs Manu about the marriage alliance with minister's son. Manu is unable to reveal the truth and gets married to minister's son. Magician is back from pilgrimage and is shocked to know the truth. He curses king starts troubling him. King requests him to be calm and proposed marriage of his daughter with his son, Shashi.

Minister's son informs Manu about the same. Manu removes the ring from his hand and puts it on his left finger. He runs to Chandraprabha's wedding place to stop the wedding. He tells everyone that he will marry the princess. At this point, Vetala stops narrating the story and asks Vikrama a question - Whose wife is Chandraprabha - Manu's or Shashi's. Vikrama tells that Chandraprabha is Shashi's wife as Shashi is getting married to her with rituals. Manu no doubt loved her a lot but Chandraprabha is Shashi's wife only.

Vetala gets happy with Vikrama's answer but he again flies back to his abode telling that he could not keep quite. Vikrama runs behind him again to catch him.
moral: The story highlights the importance of societal rituals and commitments in defining relationships, even when love is present. It also subtly touches upon the complexities of identity and deception.
