title: Mahabharata: The Story of Yudhishthira's Test
alt_titles: The Story of the Yaksha Prashna
narrative_text:
The Pandavas, after losing everything in the game of dice, were exiled for thirteen years. During their exile, they faced many hardships. One day, while wandering in the forest, they became extremely thirsty. Yudhishthira, the eldest and wisest of the Pandavas, sent his brothers one by one to find water. Each brother, upon finding a beautiful lake, ignored the warning of a Yaksha (a nature spirit) who guarded the lake. The Yaksha had a condition: anyone who wished to drink from the lake must first answer his questions. The brothers, overcome by thirst and arrogance, drank the water without answering and fell unconscious.

Concerned by their prolonged absence, Yudhishthira went in search of his brothers. He found them lying lifeless by the lake. The Yaksha then appeared and revealed himself to be Dharma, the god of righteousness, and also Yudhishthira's father. Dharma explained that he had taken the form of the Yaksha to test Yudhishthira's adherence to Dharma. He offered to revive one of Yudhishthira's brothers if he could answer his questions.

Yudhishthira, with a calm and composed mind, agreed. The Yaksha (Dharma) then posed a series of profound questions on morality, philosophy, and the essence of life. Yudhishthira answered each question with wisdom, humility, and unwavering commitment to Dharma.

One of the most famous questions was: 'What is the greatest wonder in the world?' Yudhishthira replied, 'Day after day, countless creatures die. Yet, the living wish to live forever! What can be a greater wonder than this?' Another question was: 'What is true happiness?' Yudhishthira answered, 'True happiness is when one is free from debt and has no need to travel away from home.'

Impressed by Yudhishthira's profound understanding and unwavering adherence to Dharma, the Yaksha (Dharma) offered to revive one of his brothers. Yudhishthira, without hesitation, asked for Nakula to be revived. The Yaksha was surprised, as Nakula was only a step-brother, and Yudhishthira could have chosen the mighty Bhima or the skilled Arjuna.

Yudhishthira explained his choice: 'My mother Kunti has me, her eldest son, alive. My stepmother Madri has no son alive. To ensure that both mothers have at least one son alive, I chose Nakula, so that the scales of justice and dharma remain balanced.'

Dharma was deeply moved by Yudhishthira's selfless act and his unwavering commitment to justice and equality. He not only revived Nakula but also Bhima, Arjuna, and Sahadeva, restoring all of Yudhishthira's brothers to life. Dharma then blessed Yudhishthira and promised that the Pandavas would successfully complete their exile.

The moral of the story is that adherence to Dharma, righteousness, wisdom, and selflessness are virtues that ultimately lead to triumph and divine blessings. Yudhishthira's test highlights the importance of upholding moral principles even in the face of extreme adversity and personal loss.
moral: Adherence to Dharma, righteousness, wisdom, and selflessness are virtues that ultimately lead to triumph and divine blessings.

performance_notes.opening_line: In the heart of the forest, during their long exile, the Pandava brothers faced a test that would define their character forever.

title: The Origin of the River Krishna
narrative_text:
Once upon a time, in the midst of the terrible conditions of Kaliyuga, sage Narada approached Bhagavan Vishnu, seeking a way to save humanity from their grave sins. Vishnu revealed that only sacred waters, Tirthas, could purify people in this dreadful age. He promised to manifest the river Krishnaa from his own divine body, just as he had created other sacred rivers like Ganga in previous Kalpas (a day in the life of Brahma).

Vishnu declared that people would be cleansed of their sins and attain a lofty state by meditating on the Krishnaa river, touching, drinking, and bathing in its waters, and singing its praises. His manifestation as Krishnaveni river had two primary aims: to protect the world and to enable his devotees to achieve Mukti (Liberation). He emphasized that while the path of Yoga was arduous, simply engaging with the Krishnaa waters would lead to a high spiritual state, as he himself would reside within them.

Following this declaration, Vishnu created a divine damsel, radiant as the moon, with a blue-black complexion, four arms, and adorned in yellow silken garments and Vishnu's insignia. This celestial being was named Krishnaa. Brahma, taking her as his daughter, created many Tirthas by her touch. Krishnaa then descended from Brahmaloka to Earth, taking the form of a river. This sacred river, Krishnaa, was likened to the Kalpaka tree and Kamadhenu, the wish-fulfilling divinities of Swarga, bestowing all dharmas, including Moksha.

Later, at the beginning of Kritayuga, when sins began to proliferate, Brahma's initial attempts to guide humanity through Kalpaka trees and special herbs failed. Eventually, Dharma incarnated on Earth in four forms: Nara, Narayana, Hari, and Krishna. Lamenting the rise of sin, they requested Krishna to journey to Brahmaloka and bring the Krishnaa river to Earth, and to persuade Brahma to create more sacred Tirthas. Krishna fulfilled this request.

As Devi Krishnaa prepared to leave Brahmaloka, she was accompanied by Brahma, Vishnu, Siva, Devas, and Rishis. They sought a suitable place for her origin on Earth. A sage appeared and expressed his wish for Krishnaa to originate in the Sahyadri mountain range, desiring to attain a lofty state by bathing in her sacred waters. He also wished for Krishnaa to become the mother of all Tirthas on Earth. The Sahyadri mountain also offered its prayers, and Devi Krishnaa blessed that she would manifest there, and many other holy rivers would also emerge from the Sahyadris, bringing well-being and merit to people.

Vishnu, Nara, Narayana, Devas, and all fourteen worlds gathered in the Sahyadris, creating a festive atmosphere. Vishnu transformed into a white Asvatha (fig) tree, and the Krishnaa river originated at its foot. Celestial music, divine dances, and showers of flowers marked this glorious event. With the benign influence of the Krishnaa river, dharma flourished, and adharma waned. It is believed that merely reading this account of the river's origin confers great merit, including worldly comforts and Moksha.
moral: The story emphasizes the purifying and liberating power of sacred rivers, particularly the Krishnaa, which is considered a manifestation of Lord Vishnu. It highlights the importance of devotion, righteous living, and the belief that engaging with sacred elements can lead to spiritual merit and salvation, even in challenging times.

title: The Fox and the Crow
narrative_text: Once upon a time, in a faraway jungle lived a crow. One day, he was flying here and there in search of food but did not get anything to eat. Tired, he sat on the branch of a nearby tree. Suddenly, he saw a piece of cheese on a plate on the table. "Oh my, my!" exclaimed the hungry crow happily. "Looks like I’ll get to eat something very delicious today." He saw here and there and saw no one around. He quickly grabbed the piece of cheese in his beak and took off. On his way, he saw many crows but didn’t stop, as he didn’t want to share his food with anyone. He flew to a faraway tree and sat on its highest branch. At the same time, a fox was strolling across the jungle and saw the crow with a piece of cheese in his beak. "Umm. That’s a tasty-looking piece of cheese", thought the fox. "I deserve that piece of cheese and will get it anyhow." The fox started trying to have a conversation with the crow with the motto of urging the crow to open his beak. "Hello, beautiful crow! How are you? How is your day going?" said the fox. Naturally, the crow did not say anything as he was taught not to speak with his mouth full. "Look at your big and shiny feathers, my dear friend", the fox said again. "No wonder you can fly up so high. I wish I were as beautiful as you." The crow again did not answer. "Your eyes are sparkling like a diamond. No wonder you are the most beautiful amongst all", the fox said. "I have heard that crows sing well. I am sure you also have a magical voice", the fox continued with his flattery. "Please sing a song for me. If you sing well, I promise you to declare the most beautiful voice of the jungle." This time, the crow fell for his words and started singing. He opened his mouth and started singing, "Caw Caw Caw". As the crow opened his mouth, the piece of cheese naturally fell from his beak to the ground. The fox rushed and grabbed the piece of cheese from the ground. "Ha ha ha", the fox started laughing. "You foolish crow! I knew you were going to fall for it! You think you have a beautiful voice? Ha Ha! No way". The fox said this and left, enjoying the cheese. The crow kept sitting there quietly, realising his mistake and how foolish he had been.
moral: Never believe flatterers. In life, we meet many people who flatter us to get their job done or to distract us from our goals, and we end up believing in their sweet lies. We need to identify such people and stay away from them, as they can go to any extent to fulfil their agenda and even harm us.

title: Hitopadesha: The Story of the Two Crows
alt_titles: The Story of the Jackal, Deer, and Crow
narrative_text:
Far away in Behar there is a forest called Champak-Grove, and in it had long lived in much affection a Deer and a Crow. The Deer, roaming unrestrained, happy and fat of carcase, was one day descried by a Jackal. 'Ho! ho!' thought the Jackal on observing him, 'if I could but get this soft meat for a meal! It might be—if I can only win his confidence,' Thus reflecting he approached, and saluted him.

'Health be to thee, friend Deer!'

'Who art thou?' said the Deer.

'I'm Small-wit, the Jackal,' replied the other. 'I live in the wood here, as the dead do, without a friend; but now that I have met with such a friend as thou, I feel as if I were beginning life again with plenty of relations. Consider me your faithful servant.'

'Very well,' said the Deer; and then, as the glorious King of Day, whose diadem is the light, had withdrawn himself, the two went together to the residence of the Deer. In that same spot, on a branch of Champak, dwelt the Crow Sharp-sense, an old friend of the Deer. Seeing them approach together, the Crow said,

'Who is this number two, friend Deer?'

'It is a Jackal,' answered the Deer, 'that desires our acquaintance.'

'You should not become friendly to a stranger without reason,' said Sharp-sense. 'Don't you know?

To folks by no one known house-room deny:—
The Vulture housed the Cat, and thence did die.

'No! how was that?' said both.

'In this wise,' answered the Crow.

One day the Jackal drew the Deer aside, and whispered, 'Deer, in one corner of this wood there is a field full of sweet young wheat; come and let me show you.' The Deer accompanied him, and found the field, and afterwards went every day there to eat the green corn, till at last the owner of the ground spied him and set a snare. The Deer came again very shortly, and was caught in it, and (after vainly struggling) exclaimed, 'I am fast in the net, and it will be a net of death to me if no friend comes to rescue me!'

Presently Small-wit, the Jackal, who had been lurking near, made his appearance, and standing still, he said to himself, with a chuckle, 'O ho! my scheme bears fruit! When he is cut up, his bones, and gristle, and blood, will fall to my share and make me some beautiful dinners,' The Deer, here catching sight of him, exclaimed with rapture, 'Ah, friend, this is excellent! Do but gnaw these strings, and I shall be at liberty.

Small-wit answered nothing, but betook himself to examining the snare very closely.

'This will certainly hold,' muttered he; then, turning to the Deer, he said, 'Good friend, these strings, you see, are made of sinew, and to-day is a fast-day, so that I cannot possibly bite them. To-morrow morning, if you still desire it, I shall be happy to serve you.'

When he was gone, the Crow, who had missed the Deer upon returning that evening, and had sought for him everywhere, discovered him; and seeing his sad plight, exclaimed—

'How came this about, my friend?'

'This came,' replied the Deer, 'through disregarding a friend's advice.'

'Where is that rascal Small-wit?' asked the Crow.

'He is waiting somewhere by,' said the Deer, 'to taste my flesh.'

When the day broke, the Crow (who was still there) saw the master of the field approaching with his club in his hand.

'Now, friend Deer,' said Sharp-sense on perceiving him, 'do thou cause thyself to seem like one dead: puff thy belly up with wind, stiffen thy legs out, and lie very still. I will make a show of pecking thine eyes out with my beak; and whensoever I utter a croak, then spring to thy feet and betake thee to flight.'

The Deer thereon placed himself exactly as the Crow suggested, and was very soon espied by the husbandman, whose eyes opened with joy at the sight.

'Aha!' said he, 'the fellow has died of himself,' and so speaking, he released the Deer from the snare, and proceeded to gather and lay aside his nets. At that instant Sharp-sense uttered a loud croak, and the Deer sprang up and made off. And the club which the husbandman flung after him in a rage struck Small-wit, the Jackal (who was close by), and killed him.
moral: Be wary of new friends with selfish motives, and always listen to the advice of true friends.

and the deer springs up and runs away.

title: The Foolhardy Wolf
narrative_text:
A lion bounded forth from his lair one day, looking north, west, south, and east. He saw a Buffalo and went to kill him. The Lion ate all of the Buffalo-meat he wanted, and then went down to the lake for a drink.

As the Lion turned to go toward his den for a nap, he came upon a hungry Wolf.

The Wolf had no chance to get away, so he threw himself at the Lion’s feet.

“What do you want?” the Lion asked. “O Lion, let me be your servant,” said the Wolf. “Very well,” said the Lion, “serve me, and you shall have good food to eat.” So saying, the Lion went into his den for his nap. When he woke up, the Lion said to the Wolf: “Each day you must go to the mountain top, and see whether there are any elephants, or ponies, or buffaloes about. If you see any, come to me and say: ‘Great Lion, come forth in thy might. Food is in sight.’ Then I will kill and eat, and give part of the meat to you.”

So day after day the Wolf climbed to the mountain top, and seeing a pony, or a buffalo, or an elephant, he went back to the den, and falling at the Lion’s feet he said: “Great Lion, come forth in thy might. Food is in sight.”

Then the Lion would bound forth and kill whichever beast it was, sharing the meat with the Wolf.

Now this Wolf had never had such fine meat to eat, nor so much. So as time went on, the Wolf grew bigger and bigger, and stronger and stronger, until he was really proud of his great size and strength. “See how big and strong I am,” he said to himself. “Why am I living day after day on food given me by another? I will kill for my own eating. I’ll kill an elephant for myself.” So the Wolf went to the Lion, and said: “I want to eat an elephant of my own killing. Will you let me lie in your corner in the den, while you climb the mountain to look out for an elephant? Then when you see one, you come to the den and say, ‘Great Wolf, come forth in thy might. Food is in sight.’ Then I will kill the elephant.” Said the Lion: “Wolf, only Lions can kill elephants. The world has never seen a Wolf that could kill an elephant. Give up this notion of yours, and eat what I kill.”

But no matter what the Lion said, the Wolf would not give way. So at last the Lion said: “Well, have your own way. Lie down in the den, and I will climb to the top of the mountain.” When he saw an elephant the Lion went back to the mouth of the cave, and said: “Great Wolf, come forth in thy might. Food is in sight.” Then from the den the Wolf nimbly bounded forth, ran to where the elephant was, and, howling three times, he sprang at the elephant. But the Wolf missed his aim, and fell down at the elephant’s feet. The elephant raised his right foot and killed the Wolf. Seeing all this, the Lion said, “You will no more come forth in your might, you foolhardy Wolf.”
moral: Foolish pride and overestimating one's own abilities can lead to one's downfall. It is wise to recognize one's limitations and accept help when offered.

title: Akbar and Birbal Stories – A Pitcher of Wisdom
narrative_text:
Once, an ambassador of a neighbouring country visited the court of Akbar and said, “Sir, we have heard that there are too many wise and witty people in your court. So our King has sent me to fetch a pitcher (pot) full of wisdom from here. I expect you to fulfill our king’s wish soon.”
Akbar was puzzled to hear this. He thought what a senseless wish was this?. How can one procure a pitcher full of wisdom? Finding no way out, he sent for Birbal, the wisest of his gems.
Birbal having heard the entire thing, slightly chuckled and said, “Do not worry, Majesty, I will fulfill their demand, I just need few weeks’ time.” Akbar granted him.
Birbal asked a servant to get a pitcher. Then he took the pitcher to a pumpkin creeper and placed it inverted over the pumpkin flower.
Birbal then went to the ambassador and said, “Sir, we just need two weeks’ time. I assure you, the wisdom will reach the brim of the pitcher within a fortnight.”
After fifteen days, Birbal stopped his experiment and found that the pumpkin had got completely stuck inside the pitcher.
He took the pitcher to the ambassador and gifting it to him, said, “Sir, here is your pitcher full of wisdom. You please take the fruit of wisdom out of it without causing it any harm and return us our pot”. The ambassador closely inspected the fruit of wisdom and feeling embarrassed, left for his country.
King Akbar, on seeing the gesture of the ambassador, was laughing and praising his wisest gem heartily.
moral: True wisdom cannot be bought or contained in a physical object; it is found in wit, intelligence, and clever problem-solving.

Akbar granted him.
The ambassador closely inspected the fruit of wisdom and feeling embarrassed, left for his country.

title: Tenali Raman and the Fake Astrologer
narrative_text: The Sultan of Bijapur, wanting to defeat King Krishnadevaraya, sent a spy to the kingdom of Vijayanagaram disguised as an astrologer. The fake astrologer quickly gained the trust of the King, who was a devout man. The astrologer, with his false predictions, soon became a close confidante of the King. One day, the astrologer, as part of his plan, predicted that the King would die if he ever crossed the Thungabhadra river. This was a ploy to prevent the King from attacking Bijapur. The King, believing the astrologer, cancelled his plans to attack Bijapur. The ministers and queens were all worried, but no one could convince the King to change his mind. Finally, Tenali Raman, the wise court jester, decided to expose the fake astrologer. Raman knew that the astrologer was a spy. He went to the King and told him that he could prove that the astrologer was a fraud. The King, intrigued, agreed to summon the astrologer to the court. When the astrologer arrived, Tenali Raman asked him, "You are a great astrologer. Tell me, when will you die?" The astrologer, confident in his deception, replied, "I will die in exactly 34 years." Hearing this, Tenali Raman immediately drew his sword and beheaded the astrologer. The court was shocked. Tenali Raman then turned to the King and said, "Your Majesty, this man was a fraud. He predicted he would live for another 34 years, but he is dead now. This proves that his predictions were all lies." The King, realizing the truth, was impressed by Tenali Raman's wisdom. He understood that the astrologer was a spy sent by the Sultan of Bijapur. The King then proceeded with his plan to attack Bijapur and was victorious.
moral: Wit and wisdom can expose even the most cunning deception.

pause_points: ...when the astrologer made his dire prediction.
...as Tenali Raman drew his sword.
...and the King finally understood the truth.

safety_notes: The story contains a scene of violence (beheading) which might be sensitive for some listeners, but it is presented in a traditional folktale context.

title: Vikram-Betal: The Prince, the Magical Shoes, and the Test of Character
narrative_text:
King Vikramaditya, weary from his journey, carried the silent Betal on his shoulder through the dense forest. To pass the time, Betal began a new tale, his voice echoing softly through the night.

'O King,' began Betal, 'in the ancient town of Chinchini, three noble brothers lived happily with their wives. But a terrible famine struck, and in their selfishness, the brothers abandoned their wives to seek fortune elsewhere. One of the deserted wives soon gave birth to a son, Putraka, who was raised with immense love by his mother and aunts. One night, the god Siva appeared to them in a dream, promising that Putraka would one day be king and that a hundred thousand gold pieces would appear under his pillow every morning. And so it was.

Putraka grew into a kind and wise young man, using his wealth to irrigate the land and care for his people. When the old king died, Putraka was chosen to rule. Though he had everything, his heart longed for his father. He sent Brahmans across the land, offering rewards to find his estranged family. Eventually, his father and uncles, now old and selfish, returned, drawn by the news of Putraka's generosity. Putraka welcomed them with open arms, showering them with gifts and a beautiful home, forgiving their past transgressions.

However, his father and uncles, consumed by jealousy and greed, plotted to kill Putraka and seize his throne. They hired assassins to ambush him during his prayers at a temple. But the divine guardian of the temple intervened, paralyzing the assassins. Putraka, disturbed from his prayers, learned of his father's treachery. Though heartbroken, he forgave the assassins and sent them away, making them promise never to reveal his father's involvement.

Overwhelmed by sorrow and betrayal, Putraka decided to leave his kingdom. He wandered into the forest, where he stumbled upon two men fighting over three magical items: a bowl that provided endless food, a staff that granted wishes, and a pair of shoes that allowed the wearer to fly. Putraka, seeing their squabble, tricked them into a race, and while they ran, he seized the magical items and flew away, leaving them bewildered.

With his new powers, Putraka flew to a distant city. He found solace with an old woman, who, seeing his kindness, suggested he seek Princess Patala, renowned for her beauty. Putraka, using his magical shoes, flew to Patala's palace, making himself invisible with his staff. He wooed the princess, and they fell deeply in love. Every night, he would visit her, forgetting the old woman who had cared for him and even his own kingdom.

One night, careless in his love-struck state, Putraka was discovered by a palace guardian. The guardian, a kind lady who sympathized with Patala, marked Putraka's turban with a brooch instead of raising an alarm, hoping to spare the princess's heart. The king, however, soon learned of the secret lover and demanded to know his identity. The guardian, under duress, revealed the brooch, hoping it would lead to a happy union.

Putraka, now known to the king, was summoned. The king, impressed by Putraka's noble bearing, agreed to their marriage on one condition: Putraka must bring him a magical jewel from the Serpent King's crown. Putraka, using his magical shoes and staff, embarked on this perilous quest. He faced many dangers, but with his magical aids and innate courage, he succeeded, returning with the jewel and winning Patala's hand. He eventually returned to his own kingdom, a wiser and more compassionate ruler, having learned the true value of loyalty, forgiveness, and the responsible use of power.

'Now, King Vikram,' Betal concluded, 'Putraka was betrayed by his own father, yet he forgave him. He then cheated the two brothers to gain the magical items. Later, he forgot the old woman who had helped him. Finally, he used his magical powers to win the princess and fulfill the king's challenge. Tell me, O King, which of Putraka's actions demonstrates the greatest wisdom and which the greatest folly? And was his initial act of leaving his kingdom a sign of strength or weakness?'

King Vikramaditya pondered Betal's words, the weight of the choices heavy in the silent night.
moral: True wisdom lies not just in acquiring power or overcoming challenges, but in the responsible and ethical use of that power, coupled with compassion, forgiveness, and loyalty to those who have helped us.

title: The Two Friends
narrative_text:
In a village lived two rogues, Gokuli and Maguni. One day, they overheard a farmer talking about a hidden treasure under a mango tree. That night, they went to the garden and started digging. Gokuli’s spade hit something hard. Fearing Maguni would take the treasure, Gokuli tricked him into digging while he escaped with the pot. 

Maguni, realizing Gokuli’s betrayal, searched for him. They eventually met and decided to share the treasure by an old well. Maguni, wanting all the treasure, pushed Gokuli into the well. Gokuli, however, was caught by the roots of a neem tree inside the well, escaping death. Maguni left with the treasure. 

Later, two witches came to the well and discussed how the Raja’s daughter was suffering from an incurable sickness. The elder witch revealed that only three leaves from the neem tree in the well could cure her. Gokuli, overhearing this, crawled out of the well with three neem leaves and went to the Raja, offering to cure the princess. The princess was cured, and the Raja, overjoyed, married his daughter to Gokuli. 

Meanwhile, Maguni, who had prospered with the stolen treasure, was shipwrecked near Gokuli’s palace. Gokuli, seeing his old friend, welcomed him. Maguni, chastened by misfortune, realized the error of his ways. The two friends reconciled, understanding the value of sincerity and truth in life.
moral: The value of sincerity and truth in life, and that evil deeds eventually lead to misfortune.

The elder witch revealed that only three leaves from the neem tree in the well could cure her.

title: The Brave Princess Thoibi and Khamba
alt_titles: The Love Story of Khamba and Thoibi
narrative_text:
In the ancient kingdom of Moirang, nestled in the verdant hills of Manipur, lived a brave princess named Thoibi. Her story is intertwined with that of Khamba, a valiant but impoverished youth. Khamba and his sister, Khamnu, were orphaned at a young age. Khamnu, with unwavering devotion, raised her younger brother, often begging for food to sustain them. As Khamba grew, he became renowned for his extraordinary strength and courage. He was so swift that no one could outrun him, and so strong that he alone dared to subdue a mad bull that terrorized the land.

Princess Thoibi, the daughter of Chingkhuba Telheiba's brother, Chingkhuba Akhuba, fell deeply in love with Khamba. Their love, however, was met with fierce opposition, particularly from Thoibi's father, who harbored disdain for Khamba's humble origins. In a display of her affection and skill, Thoibi wove a beautiful coat and presented it to Khamba. This act of love further enraged Chingkhuba Akhuba, who saw it as an affront to his family's honor. Consumed by jealousy and anger, he plotted to eliminate Khamba. He ordered his men to seize Khamba and have him trampled to death by an elephant.

However, the divine intervention of God Thangching, the revered deity of Moirang, played a crucial role. Thangching appeared to Thoibi in a dream, warning her of the imminent danger to her beloved. Without hesitation, Thoibi, demonstrating immense bravery and unwavering loyalty, confronted her father. She threatened to take her own life if any harm came to Khamba. Her resolute stance and fierce determination compelled her father to relent, and Khamba was spared from the elephant's wrath.

Later, during a royal hunting expedition, a ferocious tiger attacked and killed a man in the presence of the King. Khamba, with his unparalleled courage, single-handedly confronted and slayed the beast, saving the lives of many. Impressed by Khamba's extraordinary bravery and skill, King Chingkhuba Telheiba, recognizing his true worth, bestowed upon him the hand of Princess Thoibi in marriage. Thus, their love, tested by adversity and prejudice, triumphed, uniting two souls from different social strata through courage, devotion, and divine grace.

**Moral:** True love and courage can overcome societal barriers and adversity. Bravery, whether in defending a loved one or protecting the innocent, is a virtue that earns respect and rewards.

title: The Story of Sant Kabir and the Repentant Grocer
narrative_text:
One night, as the saint Kabir and his wife were asleep in their humble, dilapidated home, a group of Vaishnava saints arrived unexpectedly. Despite the late hour and their own hunger, Kabir and his wife immediately rose to welcome their guests with reverence. They had no food to offer, and their children had gone to bed hungry. Kabir's wife, troubled by this, decided to venture into the bazaar to find provisions, praying to Shri Ram for guidance.

She found a grocer still awake and explained her predicament, requesting food for the hungry saints. The grocer, however, was captivated by her beauty and made an indecent proposal, offering the provisions only if she would comply with his desires. Kabir's wife, faced with the dilemma of her guests' hunger and the grocer's immoral demand, weighed her options. She recalled instances from scriptures where devotion and duty sometimes necessitated unconventional actions. Believing that God, who sees the heart's intentions, would understand, she promised the grocer she would return to fulfill his wish after feeding the saints.

Returning home with the provisions, she and Kabir prepared a meal for the saints. After they had eaten and were content, Kabir's wife confided in her husband about the promise she had made to the grocer. Kabir, understanding the purity of her intentions and her selfless act for the sake of the saints, declared that he would accompany her. He lifted her onto his shoulders, and together they journeyed back to the grocer's shop through the heavy rain and darkness, all the while chanting the name of Shri Ram.

Upon reaching the shop, Kabir instructed his wife to fulfill her promise, then he returned home to attend to the saints. The grocer, who had been eagerly awaiting her, was surprised to see her return, especially without any mud on her feet despite the downpour. When she explained that her husband had carried her, the grocer was struck by Kabir's unwavering faith and his wife's devotion. He realized the depth of his own sin and was overcome with profound repentance. He confessed his wrongdoing, sought forgiveness, and asked to be led to Kabir.

Kabir, perceiving the grocer's sincere change of heart, embraced him and blessed him, assuring him that his sins were forgiven. From that day forward, the grocer abandoned his evil ways and became a devout follower of Shri Ram, demonstrating the transformative power of true repentance and the association with saints.
moral: The story illustrates the profound power of selfless devotion, the importance of pure intentions, and the transformative nature of true repentance. It highlights that genuine faith and compassion can lead to forgiveness and spiritual awakening, even in those who have strayed. It also shows the unwavering support and understanding between Kabir and his wife, emphasizing the strength found in shared spiritual values.

...demonstrating the transformative power of true repentance and the association with saints.

title: The Sacred Story of Hartalika Teej: Parvati's Devotion and Shiva's Blessing
narrative_text:
The festival of Hartalika Teej is a sacred observance, primarily by women, dedicated to Goddess Parvati. It falls on the third day of the bright fortnight (Shukla Paksha Tritiya) in the month of Bhadrapada, symbolizing the divine union of Lord Shiva and Goddess Parvati. On this auspicious day, women undertake a rigorous fast, abstaining from both food and water, to pray for marital happiness, prosperity, and the fulfillment of their deepest desires.

The origin of Hartalika Teej is rooted in a profound tale of devotion. The word 'Hartalika' itself is derived from two Sanskrit words: 'harit,' meaning abduction, and 'aalika,' meaning female friend. The legend recounts how Goddess Parvati, in a previous incarnation, was determined to marry Lord Shiva. However, her father had decided to arrange her marriage with Lord Vishnu.

Unwilling to accept this fate, Parvati confided in her closest female friend. With her friend's help, Parvati escaped into the dense woods to avoid the unwanted marriage. There, in the solitude of the forest, Parvati embarked on an intense period of austerity, known as 'tapasya.' She fasted without food or water, meditating solely upon Lord Shiva with unwavering devotion.

Her steadfast penance and unshakable faith deeply impressed Lord Shiva. Moved by her profound dedication, Lord Shiva eventually appeared before her and accepted her as his eternal consort. This divine union symbolizes not only immense love and devotion but also the power of sincere prayers and determination. From that day forward, Hartalika Teej became a sacred tradition, where women observe a fast and perform rituals to emulate Goddess Parvati's faith and strength, seeking similar blessings in their own lives.

During the Hartalika Teej vrat, women observe a 'nirjala vrat,' meaning a fast without water. Idols or images of Lord Shiva and Goddess Parvati are adorned with flowers, fruits, and sweets. Devotees listen to or recite the Hartalika Teej vrat katha during the puja. Married women pray for the longevity and well-being of their husbands, while unmarried women pray for a husband as ideal as Lord Shiva. This festival, celebrated with songs and rituals across India, continues to inspire women with its message of determination, faith, and divine grace.
moral: The story of Hartalika Teej emphasizes the power of unwavering devotion, determination, and sincere prayers in achieving one's deepest desires and finding a harmonious partnership.

title: The Story of Sage Vyasa
narrative_text:
In ancient times, there lived a revered sage named Parashara. One day, while traveling, he encountered a young fisherwoman named Kali, also known as Matsyagandha due to a fishy odor she carried. She was the daughter of a fisherman and helped her father ferry people across the river Yamuna. Parashara, captivated by her beauty, expressed his desire for her. Kali, upholding her chastity, initially resisted but eventually agreed under certain conditions. Parashara, using his yogic powers, created a dense fog to conceal them and transformed her fishy scent into a divine fragrance of musk. He also conjured an island in the middle of the river where they united.

From this union, a son was born instantly on that very island. This child, radiant with divine energy, was named Krishna Dwaipayana, 'Krishna' for his dark complexion and 'Dwaipayana' for his birth on an island. As soon as he was born, he grew into a young sage, full of devotion and purity. He immediately announced his intention to undertake severe penance and assured his mother that he would appear whenever she thought of him in times of need. With this, he departed for the forests to pursue spiritual austerities.

Later, Krishna Dwaipayana became known as Vyasa, meaning 'compiler' or 'divider', because he organized the vast body of Vedic knowledge into four Vedas. He is also revered as Vedavyasa. Towards the end of his long and eventful life, Vyasa retreated to the serene caves of the Himalayas. There, reflecting on the profound tapestry of human existence, he felt an inner urge to chronicle the great events he had witnessed. This contemplation led to the composition of the Puranas and Upapuranas, and most significantly, the epic Mahabharata.

Recognizing the monumental task of writing down such a vast narrative, Vyasa sought assistance. Lord Brahma advised him that Ganesha, the elephant-headed deity, would be the ideal scribe. Vyasa meditated upon Ganesha, who soon appeared. Ganesha agreed to write, but on the condition that Vyasa would narrate continuously without pause. Vyasa, in turn, stipulated that Ganesha must understand the meaning of every verse before transcribing it. Both agreed to these terms, and thus, the monumental task of composing the Mahabharata began. Within two and a half years, this grand epic, a treasure trove of wisdom and human experience, was completed, becoming one of the greatest literary works in the world.
moral: The story of Vyasa teaches us about the profound impact of dedication to knowledge and spiritual pursuit. It highlights the importance of preserving wisdom for future generations and the power of divine intervention in fulfilling one's life purpose. It also subtly conveys that even those born under unusual circumstances can achieve greatness through their virtues and efforts.

title: The Tale of Three Fish
narrative_text:
In a serene reservoir, there lived three fish, each with a distinct approach to life. The first, named Anna, was known for her foresight and proactive nature. She firmly believed in anticipating potential dangers and taking immediate action to avoid them. The second fish, Pratyu, was more inclined to react to immediate threats rather than perceived future ones. She only concerned herself with dangers that were truly imminent, dismissing the idea of preparing for anticipated troubles. The third fish, Yaddi, held a fatalistic view, convinced that everything was predetermined and that one's fate could not be altered. She believed it was best to surrender to destiny.

One day, as the three fish swam peacefully, they overheard a conversation between some fishermen who had discovered their reservoir. The fishermen, delighted by the abundance of fish, made plans to return the following morning to cast their nets and catch them all. Anna, Pratyu, and Yaddi listened intently to their ominous discussion.

True to her nature, Anna immediately recognized the gravity of the situation. Without hesitation, she decided to leave the reservoir that very moment, urging her friends to follow suit. However, Pratyu and Yaddi, clinging to their respective philosophies, chose to remain behind.

The next morning, as the fishermen arrived and began their work, Pratyu sprang into action. Her quick wit and resourcefulness came to the forefront. Spotting a dead beaver floating in the reservoir, she swiftly wrapped herself in its decaying, foul-smelling body. Soon, she was caught in one of the fisherman's nets. While other fish struggled and suffered out of water, Pratyu managed to hold her breath, enduring the unpleasant ordeal. The fisherman, disgusted by the dead beaver, threw it back into the reservoir. As it sank to the bottom, Pratyu emerged from her gruesome disguise, feeling a surge of pride in her clever escape.

Meanwhile, Yaddi, who had resigned herself to fate, was caught in another net. She met her end alongside many other fish, perishing in agony. The moral of this timeless tale serves as a powerful reminder: 'God helps those who help themselves.'
moral: God helps those who help themselves.

title: The Washerman’s Donkey and the Dog
narrative_text:
Once upon a time, there lived a Washerman in a village. He had kept a donkey and a dog to serve as his pets. The Dog used to guard his master’s house and escort him wherever he went. The Donkey used to carry stack of clothes on his back to and fro the river. Both of them slept in the washerman’s courtyard. Like this, they were leading their life under the kind shelter of the Washerman.

On one unfortunate night, when everyone was sleeping, a thief managed to get into the house. Even after seeing the thief, the Dog didn’t bark. When the Donkey saw the thief entering the house, he asked the Dog, “Dear Friend, Didn’t you see that a thief had entered the house? Why don’t you bark to awake our master?”

The Dog answered, “Mind your own business. Don't talk to me about my duties; I know how to guard my master. I have been guarding this house for long, but master doesn’t care for me. From the past few days, he doesn’t even feed me properly. He doesn’t know my worth. I won’t wake him up. When this thief will steal valuables from his house then only he will recognize my importance”.

The Donkey irritatingly said, “You fool! This is not the time to complain. This is the time for action. Be fast and do something to wake up the master”. The Dog replied angrily, “No. As he doesn’t care for me even I wont care for him”. The Donkey shouted, “O wicked creature, you are full of ingratitude towards your master. You are ignoring your duty just when you're required most. Fine, I will do your duty and wake him up”.

Hence, the Donkey brayed at the top of his voice which made the washerman to wake up. The thief quickly ran away from the scene. The Washerman looked around but could find anyone. He got furious at the Donkey who had disturbed his sleep. The Washerman thrashed the poor Donkey with a stick. The Donkey was left with pain for months.
moral: It is always better to mind your own business.

When this thief will steal valuables from his house then only he will recognize my importance”.
The Dog replied angrily, “No. As he doesn’t care for me even I wont care for him”.
The Washerman thrashed the poor Donkey with a stick.

title: The Foolish Fox
narrative_text: Once upon a time, in the Land of Fourlegs, there lived a gray old Fox with a large family and a hard heart. He had little food and even less desire to share it with his fourteen children. So, he turned them out, telling them to fend for themselves. The fourteen young foxes, hungry and unsure where to find food, were led by Sharpnose, the youngest, who suggested they go to the Lion, as he had the most to lose. Twelve of his brothers agreed, but Redlegs, the eldest and a fool, remained silent. They journeyed many miles, growing tired and dusty, until they reached the Wood where the Sun Sets, where they found the Lion’s castle and the Lion himself standing at the gate. The Lion, King of the Land of Fourlegs, grumbled at their dusty appearance and asked where they came from. Sharpnose, quick-witted, claimed they were from the 'other end of the world, where the gold grows,' and that they were tired and hungry. The twelve brothers echoed his sentiments, but Redlegs again said nothing. When the Lion asked if they were honest and true, Sharpnose and his twelve brothers affirmed their honesty. But when asked of Redlegs, he simply stated, 'Oh, I am a thief.' The Lion, intrigued, declared, 'Then I must look after you.' He invited the thirteen honest foxes in for a supper of roast goose and a comfortable swan’s-down bed. However, for Redlegs, whom he had to 'look after,' the Lion ordered him to clean the pigsties so he wouldn't be idle. The next morning, the Lion entrusted the thirteen foxes with guarding his great money-box, filled with gold, as he was very rich. A day or two later, the Lion went to visit his friend Bruin the Bear, taking all his servants except the fourteen foxes. The thirteen were left guarding the gold, and Redlegs was still finishing the pigsties. As soon as the Lion and his servants were out of sight, Sharpnose proposed they steal the gold and bury it in the wood for future travels, confident the Lion wouldn't suspect them. So, they stole all the gold, leaving only one bit for luck, and buried it in the Wood where the Sun Sets. Upon the Lion’s return, he quickly discovered his gold was gone. He summoned all fourteen foxes and lined them up. He asked if they were honest and true. Sharpnose and the twelve brothers again claimed their honesty. When he asked Redlegs, he once more replied, 'Oh, I am a thief.' The Lion, enraged, growled, 'Ah! so you told me before. I must look after you. Put him in prison directly, and cut his right ear off.' Redlegs was imprisoned for a long time and lost his right ear, while Sharpnose and the other twelve were kept to guard the single remaining bit of gold until the Lion could acquire more, which he soon did.
moral: Honesty, even when it seems foolish, is ultimately rewarded, while deceit, even when clever, leads to punishment. The truly foolish are those who believe they can escape the consequences of their dishonesty.

title: Akbar-Birbal: The Pot of Wit
narrative_text: Once, Emperor Akbar, in a fit of anger, banished his most trusted minister, Birbal, from the kingdom. Birbal, accepting the emperor's command, left the court and began working as a farmer in a distant, unknown village, living under a new identity. As time passed, Akbar found himself struggling with many imperial matters without Birbal's wise counsel. He deeply regretted his hasty decision and sent his soldiers to find Birbal, but their search proved fruitless as no one knew of Birbal's whereabouts. Determined to find his minister, Akbar devised a clever plan. He sent a message to the head of every village in his empire, demanding that they send a pot full of 'wit' to the Emperor. The decree stated that if a pot of wit could not be sent, the pot must instead be filled with diamonds and jewels. This unusual message eventually reached the village where Birbal resided. The villagers were in a state of panic and confusion, discussing how they could possibly fulfill such an impossible demand. 'Wit' was not a tangible object that could be placed in a pot, and they certainly did not possess enough diamonds and jewels to satisfy the emperor's alternative. Birbal, who was quietly observing the villagers' distress, stepped forward and offered his help. He asked for a pot and promised to fill it with wit within a month, though the villagers remained unaware of his true identity. Birbal took the pot to his farm, where he had planted watermelons. He carefully selected a small watermelon and, without detaching it from its vine, placed it inside the pot. He then diligently nurtured the plant, providing it with water and fertilizer. Over the next few days, the watermelon grew steadily, expanding within the confines of the pot until it became impossible to remove without breaking either the fruit or the pot. Once the watermelon had completely filled the pot from the inside, Birbal carefully cut it from the vine. He then sent the pot to Emperor Akbar with a message: 'Your Majesty, please remove the wit from the pot without cutting it and without breaking the pot.' Upon receiving the pot and the accompanying message, Akbar immediately recognized Birbal's ingenious handiwork. He realized that only Birbal could have conceived such a clever solution. Akbar personally traveled to the village, brought Birbal back to his court, and reinstated him as his chief minister.
moral: Do not make hasty decisions. Think deeply and creatively to find solutions, even in the most challenging and unusual circumstances.

...without cutting it and without breaking the pot.

safety_notes: The story is emotionally warm and culturally respectful, suitable for elderly listeners. It promotes problem-solving and patience.

title: Tenali Raman and the Horse Trader
narrative_text: A trader from Arabia once gifted King Krishnadevaraya a splendid horse. He claimed to have two more just like it and asked for 5,000 gold coins in advance. Tempted, the king paid. The trader left — and never returned. Days later, the worried king found Tenali Raman in the garden, writing on a scroll. “What are you writing?” the king asked. “A list of the biggest fools in the kingdom,” Tenali replied. On top was the king’s own name. Furious, the king demanded why. “Because only a fool gives 5,000 gold coins to a stranger and waits for him to return,” Tenali said calmly. The king retorted, “And if the trader does return?” “Then, Your Majesty,” Tenali smiled, “The trader will be an even bigger fool. I will erase your name… and write his at the very top.” The king burst into laughter, his anger gone. And so, Tenali’s wit turned folly into wisdom, leaving behind a lesson in trust and prudence.
moral: Tenali’s wit turned folly into wisdom, leaving behind a lesson in trust and prudence.

title: Vikram-Betal: The Princess and the Suitor with the Magical Weapon
alt_titles: The Princess Tribhuvan-sundari and Her Four Suitors
The Tale of the Archer Who Struck the Unseen
narrative_text:
In the city of Champapur, reigned King Champakeshwar and Queen Sulochana. They were blessed with a daughter of unparalleled beauty, Princess Tribhuvan-sundari. Her face was like the moon, her hair like black clouds, her eyes like a gazelle's, and her form was perfect in every aspect. As she blossomed into womanhood, her parents grew anxious about finding a suitable husband for her. News of her beauty spread far and wide, captivating kings and sages alike.

Many kings sent their portraits to King Champakeshwar, hoping to win the princess's hand, but none pleased her. She declared to her father, 'Father, give me to him who possesses the three qualities of beauty, strength, and superior knowledge.'

After some time, four suitors arrived from different lands, each claiming to possess extraordinary qualities. The king asked each to present his unique skills and knowledge.

The first suitor boasted, 'I possess such knowledge that I can manufacture a cloth and sell it for five rubies. From the proceeds, I give one to Brahmans, offer another to the gods, wear a third myself, reserve a fourth for my wife, and sell the fifth to sustain myself. No one else has this skill.'

The second suitor proclaimed, 'I am acquainted with the languages of both land and aquatic beasts and birds, and I have no equal in strength. My beauty is also evident before you.'

The third suitor stated, 'So well do I comprehend the learned writings that no equal of mine exists, and my beauty is before your eyes.'

The fourth suitor declared, 'I stand alone in my knowledge of the use of weapons. There is no one like me; I can shoot an arrow which will strike an object that is heard, but not seen. My beauty is renowned throughout the world.'

King Champakeshwar, pondering the claims of the four suitors, found them all equally excellent. He presented their virtues to his daughter, asking, 'To which of them shall I give you in marriage?' The princess, out of modesty, remained silent.

At this point in the tale, the sprite (Betal) addressed King Vikramajit, 'Now, King Vikram, for which of these suitors is this woman most suited?'

King Vikramajit replied, 'He who makes cloth and sells it is a Sudra by caste. He who knows the languages is a Vaishya by caste. He who has studied the learned writings is a Brahman. And he who hits with an arrow an object which is simply heard, and not seen, is of her caste: the woman is suitable for him.'

Upon hearing this judgment, the sprite returned to his tree, and King Vikramajit, as before, pursued him, bound him, and carried him away.
moral: The story highlights the importance of discerning true merit and suitability beyond superficial appearances or common skills. King Vikram's judgment emphasizes that a partner should be chosen based on a deeper connection or a unique, complementary quality, rather than merely impressive but unrelated talents.

safety_notes: The story contains no elements unsuitable for elderly listeners. The descriptions of beauty are respectful, and the moral dilemma is intellectual rather than emotionally charged.

title: Ashwatthama: The Cursed Immortal
narrative_text:
In the ancient land of Bharatavarsha, during a time of great kings and epic battles, lived a revered guru named Dronacharya. He was a master of warfare, teaching the Kuru princes, both the noble Pandavas and the ambitious Kauravas. Drona had a son, Ashwatthama, whose birth was marked by a celestial cry resembling a horse's neigh, thus earning him his unique name. Ashwatthama was born with a divine gem on his forehead, granting him extraordinary powers. Yet, his early life was one of hardship and poverty, a stark contrast to the royal upbringing of his father's students. Drona, out of immense love for his son, ensured Ashwatthama received unparalleled training in weaponry and mystical arts, often giving him special lessons that even the great Arjuna did not initially receive. This made Ashwatthama a formidable warrior, skilled in the use of powerful celestial weapons like the Narayanastra and Brahmashirastra. 

As fate would have it, Ashwatthama found himself fighting alongside his father for the Kauravas in the devastating Kurukshetra War. The war raged fiercely, and Dronacharya, with his immense skill, became an unstoppable force. To disarm him, a cruel deception was orchestrated by the Pandavas. A rumor was spread that 'Ashwatthama was dead.' When Drona, distraught, sought confirmation from Yudhishthira, who was known for his unwavering truthfulness, Yudhishthira reluctantly confirmed, adding in a low voice, '...the elephant.' Drona, hearing only the first part, dropped his weapons in despair, and was then unjustly slain by Dhrishtadyumna, the commander of the Pandava army.

The news of his father's deceitful death ignited a furious rage within Ashwatthama. After the war had officially concluded, and with Duryodhana mortally wounded, Ashwatthama was appointed the last commander of the Kaurava army. Consumed by grief and a thirst for vengeance, he refused to accept the war's end. Under the cloak of night, he launched a brutal surprise attack on the sleeping Pandava camp. In that horrific raid, he mercilessly killed Dhrishtadyumna, who had beheaded his father, and tragically, all the sleeping sons of Draupadi, the queen of the Pandavas.

When the Pandavas discovered the massacre, their grief turned to fury, and they pursued Ashwatthama. Cornered, and in a desperate act of vengeance, Ashwatthama unleashed the ultimate celestial weapon, the Brahmashirastra, intending to destroy the entire Pandava lineage. The weapon, once invoked, was impossible to recall. The divine sage Vyasa and Narada intervened, and Arjuna, too, released his Brahmashirastra to counter it. While Arjuna was able to recall his weapon, Ashwatthama, lacking the spiritual mastery, could not. Instead, he redirected its destructive energy towards the wombs of the Pandava women, specifically targeting Uttara, Arjuna's daughter-in-law, who was pregnant with the last heir, Parikshit.

Lord Krishna, witnessing this heinous act against an unborn child, intervened. He revived the infant Parikshit, ensuring the continuation of the Pandava line. For his unforgivable actions—the treacherous night raid and the attempt to annihilate an innocent lineage—Krishna pronounced a severe curse upon Ashwatthama. He was condemned to wander the earth for three thousand years, afflicted by isolation, suffering, and decay, his divine gem removed from his forehead. Ashwatthama, stripped of his powers and burdened by the curse, retreated into the forests, a solitary figure forever marked by his choices.
moral: The story of Ashwatthama serves as a profound reminder that even the most skilled and powerful individuals can be consumed by uncontrolled anger and a thirst for revenge. It highlights the devastating consequences of actions driven by emotion rather than dharma (righteous conduct), and the importance of upholding moral principles, even in the face of immense personal loss and injustice. True strength lies not just in power, but in wisdom, restraint, and compassion.

title: The Divine Origins of the Tungabhadra River
alt_titles: The Tale of Tunga and Bhadra
narrative_text:
In the sacred land of India, where rivers are revered as goddesses, the Tungabhadra holds a special place, its origins deeply intertwined with divine legends. One such tale speaks of the great deluge, a cataclysmic flood that threatened to engulf the world. To save creation, Lord Vishnu assumed his mighty Varaha avatar, a boar-faced deity. With immense strength, he lifted an island from the churning waters using his powerful tusks. The sheer exertion caused sweat to pour from his brow, dripping down his tusks to form two pristine streams: the gentle Tunga and the wild Bhadra. These twin rivers then merged, giving birth to the majestic Tungabhadra.

Another legend attributes the river's birth to Lord Vishnu's fierce Narsimha avatar, a being half-man and half-lion. After a grueling battle, Narsimha vanquished the tyrannical King Hiranyakashyapu. Exhausted from his cosmic endeavor, Narsimha rested, and from his divine sweat, the Tunga and Bhadra rivers flowed forth, uniting to form the Tungabhadra. It is said that bathing in the holy Ganga purifies the body, but drinking the waters of the Tunga purifies the soul, a testament to its sacred essence.

During the epic age of the Ramayana, this revered river was known as Pampa. It was to the banks of Pampa that Lord Rama and his devoted brother Lakshmana arrived during their arduous search for Sita. The serene beauty of the river, surrounded by fragrant blossoms, only intensified Rama's grief over Sita's abduction. It was here, amidst the tranquil waters, that they encountered the valiant Hanuman and the mighty Sugriva, leaders of the Vanara clan. An alliance was forged, leading to Sugriva's rightful claim to the monkey kingdom and Hanuman's pivotal role in the quest to rescue Sita. Thus, the Tungabhadra, born from divine effort and witness to epic sagas, continues to flow, carrying with it centuries of spiritual significance and mythological grandeur.
moral: The Tungabhadra River, born from divine effort and revered through ages, reminds us of the sacredness of nature and the profound interconnectedness of life, mythology, and devotion.

title: The Brahmin and the Crooks
alt_titles: The Foolish Brahmin and the Clever Thieves
narrative_text: In a small village, there lived a Brahmin, by the name of Mitra Sharma. He was a worshipper of Fire-God. One day, during monsoons, when the sky was overcast with cloud, he decided to conduct a certain sacrificial ritual. The Brahmin travelled to a nearby village, to visit a devotee, to request for a goat that he will offer it as a sacrifice to the Gods. On his arrival, he requested the devotee, "Son, I want to perform a sacrificial ritual on this auspicious time. Please offer me with a well-fed goat." The devotee agreed and offered him with one of his best goats. The Brahmin started his journey homewards. He carried the goat on his shoulders, so that he did not have problems in controlling the animal on the way home. On his way home, three crooks watched him from a distance. They were almost starving, and the Brahmin had a goat and he was all alone. They discussed, "We will be saved from the fangs of hunger in this cold monsoon, if we can lay our hands on this goat that the Brahmin is carrying." They decided to trick the Brahmin, in order to gain the goat for themselves. As planned, the first of the crooks stood in the Brahmin's path, by taking a shorter road. When the Brahmin, with the goat on his shoulders, approached him, he queried, "Ho Brahmin, Why is it that you behave so ridiculously?" "Why on earth are you carrying a profane dog on your shoulders?" On hearing this, the Brahmin got angry, he replied, "How can you not see any difference between a goat and a dog? Are you blind? Can't you see I am carrying a sacrificial goat?" The crook had played his part, and replied, "Please don't get angry on me. You may have it anyway you want. Please carry on with your journey". A little further, he was approached by the second crook, who said, "Ho Brahmin, shame on you! How can you carry this dead calf on your shoulders like that? Shame on you!" The Brahmin got even angrier, "Are you blind? Can't you see it is a goat and not a dead calf?" To this the second crook replied, "Have it anyway you want it to be, please don't get angry on me." When the Brahmin had gone a little further, the third crook accosted him, "Ho Brahmin. This is highly improper for you do something like this. Why do you carry a donkey on your shoulders? Put him down, before anyone sees you doing this!" Now, the Brahmin started thinking how can three different persons not see that was carrying a goat? He thought that he must be carrying a goblin, which is changing shape all the while. Fearing so, he put the goat down on the ground and ran home terrified. The crooks had succeeded in their plan. The crooks caught the goat at once, and feasted on the goat to their heart's content.
moral: Untruth spoken repeatedly appears to be truth. This story teaches us to trust our own judgment and not be easily swayed by the opinions of others, especially when they contradict our clear observations.

title: The Story of the Old Jackal and the Elephant
narrative_text:
In the forest of Brahma lived an Elephant, whose name was 'White-front.' The Jackals knew him, and said among themselves, 'If this great brute would but die, there would be four months' food for us, and plenty, out of his carcase.' With that an old Jackal stood up, and pledged himself to compass the death of the Elephant by his own wit. Accordingly, he sought for 'White-front,' and, going up to him, he made the reverential prostration of the eight members, gravely saluting him.

'Divine creature,' said he, 'vouchsafe me the regard of one look.'

'Who art thou?' grunted the Elephant, 'and whence comest thou?'

'I am only a Jackal,' said the other; 'but the beasts of the forest are convinced that it is not expedient to live without a king, and they have met in full council, and despatched me to acquaint your Royal Highness that on you, endowed with so many lordly qualities, their choice has fallen for a sovereign over the forest here; for—

'Who is just, and strong, and wise?
Who is true to social ties?
He is formed for Emperies.'

Let your Majesty, therefore, repair thither at once, that the moment of fortunate conjunction may not escape us.' So saying he led the way, followed at a great pace by White-front, who was eager to commence his reign.

Presently the Jackal brought him upon a deep slough, into which he plunged heavily before he could stop himself.

'Good master Jackal,' cried the Elephant,'what's to do now? I am up to my belly in this quagmire.'

'Perhaps your Majesty,' said the Jackal, with an impudent laugh, 'will condescend to take hold of the tip of my brush with your trunk, and so get out.'

Then White-front, the Elephant, knew that he had been deceived; and thus he sank in the slime, and was devoured by the Jackals. Hence, continued the attendant, 'is why I suggested stratagem to your Highness.'
moral: Physical power isn't always the key to success; sometimes, being smart and thinking strategically can make all the difference. Also, never trust strangers too easily, as appearances can be deceiving.

title: The Foolish Stag: A Moral Tale
narrative_text: Once there was a stag lived there in a woody land. He was very beautiful incarnate, and having a smart beautiful figures and twisted horns. The stag was vain and foolish. When he thought his beauty he feel proud. One day, he felt awfully thirsty. He search here and there but finally he went to a brook to quench his thirst. The water of the brook was very cold and clear. He drank water and fill his need. While he drinking water, the stag saw his reflection in the water. He was very happy to see his beautiful antlers and felt very proud of them. When he saw his lean and thin legs, he felt very sorry And disappointed. He hated his ugly legs. All of a sudden, he heard the hunter’s horse and howls of hounds. He saw a pack of hounds coming towards him Very fast. The stag was filled with horror. He wanted to save his life at every cost. He ran very fast to the jungle to save his life. His thin and ugly legs were a great help to him. They carried him far away from the hounds. On that time he feel his legs was very good and he know his importance. He reached a dense part of the forest. There were thick bushes and trees growing in that part of the forest. Unfortunately, his beautiful antlers were caught up in a long bush every where. He tried his best to get free but all his efforts ended in smoke. Meanwhile, He was now overpowered by the hounds. He began to curse his beautiful horns of which he was very proud. Soon the hounds pounced upon the stag and tore him into pieces. The legs he disliked most took him away from the hounds while his beautiful horns caused him death.
moral: All that glitters is not gold. We should have a strong belief in universal truths. We can steer clear of troubles if we follow them in our life. The stated story also teaches us to shun many unpleasant situations.

title: The Parrot Neither Eats, Nor Drinks...
alt_titles: Akbar and Birbal: The Dead Parrot
narrative_text:
A person was very fond of parrots. He used to catch parrots, train them and sell them to interested people. One time he got a good parrot, so he trained him in talking, and when he was trained he presented him to the king. King liked the parrot because he used to reply his questions too.

The king made special arrangements for his safety, security and care and warned the people if somebody will tell me about his death, he will be hung. Hearing this the parrot was kept under very special care. But it so happened one day that suddenly the parrot died. Now who should go and inform this to king? because whoever will inform the King about the death of the parrot to the King, he will be sentenced to death and who would like to die just for informing the King about the death of a parrot.

So the servant went to Birbal and told him his plight. He said - "If I inform him about the parrot's death, then he will kill me, but if I do not tell him, then also he will kill me, so please save me."

Birbal thought for a while and sent him back to his work, and he himself went to the King and said - "Sir, Your parrot..."  
The King asked -"What about parrot?"  
Birbal again stammered - "Your parrot, Sir."  
The King asked - "Birbal, What happened to my parrot?"  
Birbal again said - "Your parrot..."  
The King asked - "I am asking you, what happened to  my parrot? Say something about him in the name of Allaah."

Birbal said - "Jehaanpanaah, Your parrot neither eats anything, nor drinks water, nor speaks, nor moves his feathers, nor opens his eyes..." The King said - "What? Has the parrot died?" Birbal said - "I did not say it. You said it."

King understood why Birbal had to say this in this way. He got very happy with Birbal's way of telling him about his parrot.
moral: Wisdom and wit can help navigate difficult situations and deliver unpleasant news without facing negative consequences.

title: Tenali Raman: The Story of the Fake Monk
narrative_text:
There was once a fake monk in Vijayanagaram who was involved in various crimes, including murders. He was clever, eloquent, and well-dressed, behaving politely, but in reality, he was cruel and wicked. His method of operation was insidious: if there was animosity between two people, he would favor the rich one, collect money, and promise to destroy their enemy. He would then go to the enemy's house, pretending to be a monk. At an opportune moment, he would poison the host's food, causing them to either die or become mentally ill. He had cheated many people this way, but Tenali Raman saw through his deception.

One day, Tenali Raman encountered the monk. At the same time, a man who had been cheated by the monk and driven to mental illness also appeared. Tenali Raman brought the mentally ill man to the monk. The fake 'sanyasi' looked seriously at the mental patient, who then became violent and killed the monk. Tenali Raman was excited, thinking, 'A fake is dead.' The mental patient walked away after his 'successful mission.'

Later, Tenali Raman himself faced a death sentence from the king for a different matter. The king ordered him to be buried up to his neck in an isolated area and then trampled by an elephant. As the soldiers went to fetch the elephant, a hunchback dhobi (washerman) passed by. Tenali Raman, noticing the dhobi's hunch, laughed. The dhobi, seeing Raman buried in the mud, asked why he was there. Raman cleverly replied, 'I am a hunchback, and I am standing like this to remove the bent on my back.' The dhobi, eager to cure his own hunch, asked if standing in the pit would help him too. Raman confirmed, 'Definitely. If you have any doubt, remove the earth and look.'

The dhobi removed the earth covering Raman, not realizing Raman had no hunch. Believing Raman's words, the dhobi stood in the pit and covered himself up to his neck with earth. Raman then returned home. Soon after, the elephant arrived and trampled the dhobi to death.

The king was dejected, believing Raman was dead. He was distressed to learn that the dead monk was a criminal. Raman, who knew about the king's distress, appeared before him and narrated how he escaped the death sentence. The king, feeling compassionate towards the dhobi's family, ordered a pension for the dhobi's wife.

In another instance, Raman had borrowed 100 gold coins from King Krishnadevarayar but couldn't repay them. The king's manager sent a messenger to Raman to demand the money. Raman, having no money, pretended to be dead, covering himself with white clothes. His mother and wife cried beside him, and neighbors gathered. The king also came to pay his respects. Raman's mother told the king that Raman had no disease but owed 100 gold sovereigns to the palace, and his last wish was for her to pay the money. The king, showing generosity, ordered all of Raman's debts to be written off. As soon as the king declared this, Raman woke up, laughing, and bowed before the king, claiming he was resurrected by the king's generosity. The king, amused, increased Raman's salary.
moral: The story illustrates the triumph of wit and intelligence over deceit and injustice. It also highlights how cleverness can be used to escape difficult situations and expose the true nature of fraudulent individuals. However, it also shows a darker side of Tenali Raman's cleverness, where his actions lead to the death of an innocent person, the dhobi, for his own escape.

safety_notes: The story contains themes of murder, deceit, and a character's death due to a trick. While presented in a traditional folk tale context, the death of the dhobi might be sensitive for some listeners. Emphasize the 'playful' tone and Tenali Raman's wit, but acknowledge the unfortunate outcome for the dhobi.
