title: Vikram-Betal: The Prince, the Princess, and the Secret of the Lotus Flower
alt_titles: Tale I. from The Baitâl Pachchisi
narrative_text:
There was a king of Banãras, named Pratãpmukut, and his son was Prince Bajra-mukut, whose queen was Mahãdevï. One day, the prince, accompanied by his minister’s son, went hunting and ventured deep into a jungle. There, they discovered a beautiful tank, its margins teeming with wild geese, brãhmanï ducks, cranes, and water-fowl. Ghats of solid masonry lined all four sides, and within the tank, lotuses bloomed in full glory. Various trees provided dense shade, where cool breezes rustled and birds sang. The forest floor was adorned with flowers of varied hues, attracting swarms of buzzing bees. After washing their hands and faces at the tank, they ascended.

Nearby stood a temple dedicated to Mahãdeva. Fastening their horses, they entered the temple, paid their respects to Mahãdeva, and then emerged. While they were engaged in adoration, the daughter of a certain king, accompanied by her attendants, arrived at another part of the tank to bathe. Having completed her ablutions, meditations, and prayers, she and her maidens began to stroll in the shade of the trees. The minister’s son was seated, and the king’s son was walking about when, suddenly, their eyes met. The prince was instantly captivated by her beauty, lamenting, ‘You wretch, Cupid! why do you molest me?’ The princess, upon seeing the prince, took a lotus-flower she had placed on her head after her devotions. She placed it to her ear, bit it with her teeth, put it under her foot, then picked it up and pressed it to her bosom. With her maidens, she then mounted her chariot and departed for home.

Overwhelmed with despair and grief at her absence, the prince approached the minister’s son, and with a sense of shame, confessed, ‘O friend! I have seen a most beautiful damsel, but I know neither her name nor her abode. If I cannot possess her, I will surely give up my life; this I am firmly resolved upon.’ Hearing this, the minister’s son helped him mount his horse and brought him home. However, the king’s son was so consumed by the pain of separation that he abandoned all his duties—writing, reading, eating, drinking, sleeping, and matters of government. He spent his days sketching her portrait, gazing at it, and weeping, neither speaking nor listening to others.

Observing his friend’s pitiable state, the minister’s son said, ‘Whosoever treads the path of love often does not survive, or if they do, they suffer immense sorrow. For this reason, the wise avoid this path.’ The prince, hearing these words, replied, ‘I, in truth, have already embarked on this path, be there joy or pain.’ When he heard such a determined declaration, the minister’s son asked, ‘Great king! at the time of her departure, did she say anything to you, or you to her?’ The prince answered, ‘I said nothing, nor did I hear anything from her.’ The minister’s son then said, ‘It will be very difficult to find her.’ The prince responded, ‘If she is found, my life will be preserved; otherwise, it is lost.’

He inquired again, ‘Did she make no signs?’ The prince recounted, ‘These were her gestures: suddenly seeing me, she took the lotus-flower from her head, put it to her ear, bit it with her teeth, placed it under her foot, and pressed it to her bosom.’ Upon hearing this, the minister’s son exclaimed, ‘I have comprehended her signs and discovered her name, habitation, and all about her!’ The prince urged, ‘Explain to me whatever you have discovered.’ The minister’s son began, ‘Attend, O king! Her taking the lotus-flower from her head and putting it to her ear signifies that she is an inhabitant of the Karnãtak (Carnatic). By biting it with her teeth, she intimated that she is the daughter of King Dãnta-vãt. By pressing it under her foot, she declared that her name is Padmãvatï. And by taking it up again and pressing it to her bosom, she informed you that you dwell in her heart.’ When the prince heard these words, he said, ‘It is advisable that you take me to the city where she dwells.’ Without delay, both dressed, girt on their arms, and taking some jewels, mounted their horses and set off towards that region.

After several days, they reached Karnãtak. As they strolled through the city, they arrived near the king’s palaces and saw an old woman sitting at her door, spinning cotton. Dismounting, they approached her and said, ‘Mother! We are traveling merchants, our goods are following us, and we have come ahead to seek lodging. If you will give us a place, we will stay.’ The old woman, looking at their faces and hearing their words, took pity on them and said, ‘This house is yours; remain here as long as you please.’ They took possession of the house. After some time, the old woman kindly joined them and began chatting. The minister’s son inquired, ‘What family and relations do you have? And how do you subsist?’ The old woman replied, ‘My son is well provided for in the king’s service, and I am the wet-nurse of Padmãvatï, the king’s daughter. Due to old age, I remain at home, but the king provides for my maintenance. However, once a day, I regularly go to see that girl; it is upon my return, alone in my home, that I lament my woes.’

Hearing these words, the prince rejoiced and said to the old woman, ‘When you start tomorrow, please carry a message from me too.’ She replied, ‘Son! What need to defer it till tomorrow? I will convey any message you communicate to me this very moment.’ Then he said, ‘Go and tell her this: The prince whom you saw on the margin of the tank on the fifth day of the light half of the month Jeth has arrived here.’

Upon hearing this, the old woman took her stick and went to the palace. She found the princess sitting alone. When she appeared before her, the princess saluted her. The old woman blessed her and said, ‘Daughter! I tended you in your infancy and suckled you. God has now caused you to grow up. My heart now desires to see you happy in your prime, then I, too, shall find comfort.’ Addressing her with such affectionate words, she continued, ‘The prince whose heart you captivated on the fifth day of the light half of Jeth, by the side of the tank, has alighted at my house and has sent you this message, for you to fulfill the promise you made him, now that he has arrived. And I tell you, for my part, that that prince is worthy of you, and is as excellent in disposition and mental qualities as you are beautiful.’

On hearing these words, the princess became angry. Applying sandal to her hands and slapping the old woman’s face, she exclaimed, ‘Wretch! Get out of my house!’ Annoyed, the old woman rose and went straight to the prince, relating all that had happened. The prince was astounded. Then the minister’s son spoke, saying, ‘Great king! Feel no anxiety; this matter has not come within your comprehension.’ The prince replied, ‘True; do you then explain it, that my mind may find rest.’ He said, ‘In smearing sandal on her ten fingers and striking the woman on the face, she intimated that when the ten nights of moonlight shall come to an end, she will meet you in the dark.’

To be brief, after ten days, the old woman again went and announced him. Then the princess tinged three of her fingers with saffron and struck them on her cheek, saying, ‘Get out of my house!’ In despair, the old woman left and related all that had occurred to the prince. He was engulfed in an ocean of sorrow. Seeing his state, the minister’s son again said, ‘Be not alarmed, the purport of this matter is something else.’ The prince replied, ‘My heart is disquieted; tell me quickly.’ Then he said, ‘She is in the state which women are in every month, and hence has stipulated for three days more; on the fourth day, she will send for you.’ In short, when the three days elapsed, the old woman inquired after her health on behalf of the prince. Then the princess angrily brought the old woman to the western wicket and turned her out. Again, the old woman informed the prince of this event, and he became cast down. On this, the minister’s son said, ‘The explanation of the affair is that she has invited you tonight by way of that wicket.’ The prince was pleased beyond measure. When the hour arrived, they took out brown suits of clothes, arranged them, fastened on their turbans, dressed themselves, placed their weapons in order, and were ready. By this time, midnight had passed. An universal stillness prevailed, and they, too, pursued their way in unbroken silence.

When they arrived near the wicket, the minister’s son remained outside, and the prince entered. He saw the princess standing there, expecting him! Their eyes met. The princess laughed, closed the wicket, and took the prince with her into the festive chamber. There, the prince beheld censers filled with perfume alight in different parts of the room, and maidens dressed in garments of various colors standing respectfully, with hands joined, each according to her station. On one side, a couch of flowers was spread; atr-holders, pan-boxes, rose-water bottles, trays, and four-partitioned boxes were arranged in order. On another side, compound essences, prepared sandal-wood, mixed perfumes, musk, and saffron filled metal cups. Here, colored boxes of exquisite confections were laid out; there, sweetmeats of various kinds were placed in order. All the doors and walls were adorned with pictures and paintings, depicting faces that would enchant any beholder. In short, everything that could contribute to pleasure and enjoyment was gathered. The whole scene was extraordinary, beyond adequate description.

Such was the apartment to which Princess Padmãvatï took the prince and gave him a seat. She washed his feet, applied sandal to his body, placed a garland of flowers around his neck, sprinkled rose-water over him, and began fanning him with her own hands. The prince said, ‘At the mere sight of you, I have become refreshed; why do you take so much trouble? These delicate hands are not fit to handle a fan; give me the fan; you be seated.’ Padmãvatï replied, ‘Great king! You have been at great trouble to come here for my sake; it behooves me to wait upon you.’ Then a maiden took the fan from the princess’s hand and said, ‘This is my business; I will attend on you, and do you both enjoy yourselves.’ They began eating betel-leaf together and engaging in familiar conversation, when, by this time, it became morning. The princess concealed him; and when night came on, they again engaged in amorous pleasures. Thus, several days passed. Whenever the prince showed a wish to depart, the princess would not permit him. A month passed thus; then the prince became much disturbed and very anxious.

One night, he was sitting alone, thinking to himself, ‘Country, throne, family—everything had already been separated from me; but such a friend as mine, by whose means I found all this happiness, even him have I not met for a whole month! What will he be saying in his heart? And how do I know what may be happening to him?’ He was occupied with these anxious thoughts when the princess arrived. Seeing his predicament, she inquired, ‘Great king! What grief possesses you that you are sitting so dispirited here? Tell me.’ Then he said, ‘I have a very dear friend, the son of the minister; for a whole month, I have received no accounts of him. He is such a clever, learned friend, that through his talents, I obtained you, and he it was who explained all your secrets.’ The princess said, ‘Great king! Your soul is truly there; what happiness can you enjoy here? Hence, this is best—I will prepare confections and sweetmeats, and all kinds of meats, and have them sent; do you, too, go there and feast and comfort him well, and return with your mind at ease.’

On hearing this, the prince rose and came forth. The princess had different kinds of sweetmeats, with poison mixed, cooked and sent. The prince had just gone and sat beside the minister’s son when the sweetmeats arrived. The minister’s son inquired, ‘Great king! How did these sweetmeats come here?’ The prince replied, ‘I was sitting there anxious concerning you, when the princess came, and looking at me, asked, ‘Why do you sit cast-down? Explain the reason of it.’ On this, I gave her a full account of your skill in reading secrets. On hearing this account, she gave me permission to come to you and had these sent for you; if you will partake of them, my heart, too, will be rejoiced.’ Then the minister’s son said, ‘You have brought poison for me; it is well, indeed, that you did not eat of it. Sire! Listen to a word from me—a woman has no love for her lover’s friend: you did not act wisely in mentioning my name there.’ On hearing this, the prince said, ‘You talk of such a thing as no one would ever do: if man has no fear of man, it is to be presumed he fears God at least.’

Thus, the minister's son saved the prince from the princess's treachery, highlighting the importance of true friendship and wisdom over fleeting desires.
moral: True wisdom and loyal friendship are invaluable, often protecting one from hidden dangers and deceit, especially when infatuation clouds judgment.

title: The Story of the Clever Girl
narrative_text:
In a small village in medieval India, lived a merchant with his two sons, Anadee and Agyaan, and two daughters, Saral and Medhavi. While the elder children were foolish and often got into trouble, the youngest, Medhavi, was exceptionally intelligent and brilliant. Their lack of wisdom frequently led to problems for the family. 

One week, Anadee borrowed the King's horse, ignoring all signs of its royal ownership. Agyaan, working at the palace laundry, mixed white clothes with dark ones, ruining the Queen's best dresses. Saral, a simpleton, beat the Princess in a race despite being warned not to. These incidents angered the King, who initially ordered their execution but was persuaded by the Queen to banish the entire family instead. Medhavi, however, was spared from the banishment due to her good conduct. The family was forced to give up all their possessions, but Medhavi, with quick thinking, managed to hide her rings in bread dough while baking a cake for the guards. As they left, she 'accidentally' dropped the bread, ensuring the guards wouldn't take it, thus saving their hidden valuables.

Medhavi and her siblings journeyed through forests, mountains, and deserts until they reached a foreign land. Her brothers and sister came up with impractical ideas for survival. Anadee suggested waiting for a Bollywood movie to be filmed to get jobs as extras. Agyaan proposed borrowing money from a loan shark to hoard and sell grains for profit. Saral, in her simplicity, thought they could just take cookies from a baker's shop without paying. Medhavi, however, dismissed these ideas, revealing the jewels she had hidden in the bread. She instructed Agyaan to exchange a ruby for money to secure food and shelter for a month.

Agyaan, being ignorant, fell prey to a cunning jeweler who tricked him into sitting on a chair with a trapdoor, leading him into a dungeon. Anadee, with a diamond, met the same fate. Realizing a pattern, Medhavi, disguised as a man, sought employment in the King's court. She introduced herself as 'Medhavi', a gender-neutral name, and was hired as a chowkidar (watchman). The King, however, failed to mention the kingdom was terrorized by a shapeshifting Rakshasi (demoness) who fed on people.

One night, Medhavi encountered the Rakshasi disguised as a beautiful, crying woman. The Rakshasi claimed to be the widow of a man who had been hanged and asked Medhavi to carry her to the gallows to hug her husband goodbye. Medhavi, brave and clever, agreed. At the gallows, Medhavi noticed the Rakshasi beginning to devour the hanged man. Realizing the danger, Medhavi swiftly drew her sword and defeated the Rakshasi. This act of courage, which no one else had dared, earned her immense praise and she was carried back to the palace.

The impressed King offered Medhavi anything she desired, even half his kingdom. Medhavi, however, requested a 'swat team'. Puzzled, the King granted her request. Medhavi led the team to the jeweler's store, where she immediately noticed a suspicious extra chair. Refusing to sit, she ordered the SWAT team to search the property, leading to the discovery and rescue of Anadee and Agyaan from the dungeon. The jeweler was imprisoned for life.

Finally, the King, overjoyed, proposed that Medhavi marry his daughter and inherit the kingdom. It was then that Medhavi revealed her true identity as a woman. The King, though initially panicked, eventually understood and accepted her. 

**Moral:** Intelligence and cleverness can overcome any obstacle, even when faced with deceit, danger, and seemingly insurmountable challenges. It also highlights the importance of critical thinking and not judging a book by its cover. 

**Note:** The original story had a father, his son, and four wives, but the roles of the father and son were deemed meaningless. The names of the characters were chosen to reflect their traits: Medhavi (intelligent/brilliant), Anadee (clumsy/ignorant/inexperienced), Agyaan (lacks knowledge), and Saral (simple). This version is an adapted paraphrase from the original source.
moral: Intelligence and cleverness can overcome any obstacle, even when faced with deceit, danger, and seemingly insurmountable challenges. It also highlights the importance of critical thinking and not judging a book by its cover.

The King, however, failed to mention the kingdom was terrorized by a shapeshifting Rakshasi (demoness) who fed on people.
Realizing the danger, Medhavi swiftly drew her sword and defeated the Rakshasi.

safety_notes: The story contains elements of banishment, imprisonment, and a demoness, but these are presented in a folkloric context and resolved positively by the clever protagonist. The violence against the Rakshasi is brief and necessary for the plot.

title: The Brave Fisherman of Kunkolim
alt_titles: The Fisherman and the Giant Fish
narrative_text:
In the quaint village of Kunkolim, nestled by the river, lived a fisherman of immense stature and strength, known simply as the Man from Kunkolim. His reputation preceded him, not just for his imposing physique, but for his unparalleled skill in coaxing the river's most elusive inhabitants from its depths. One day, a curious peasant from Bardez, having heard tales of this legendary fisherman, ventured to Kunkolim to witness his prowess firsthand. He found the Man from Kunkolim by the riverbank, a figure so colossal he resembled the gnarled trunk of an ancient tree, holding a fishing rod made from a massive bamboo and a line of thick twine.

The peasant, feeling dwarfed by the fisherman's presence, stammered his greetings. The Man from Kunkolim, with a voice that carried the force of a strong gust of wind, asked the peasant to bait his hook. The bait itself was a huge mass of fish flesh, which the peasant struggled to lift. With a powerful cast, the Man from Kunkolim flung the freshly baited line into the water. Almost immediately, a tremendous tug threatened to pull the rod from his grasp. With a display of incredible strength and unwavering determination, the fisherman wrestled with the unseen creature beneath the surface. The water churned violently, and the peasant watched in awe as the Man from Kunkolim, with a mighty heave, pulled a formidable fish from the river. It was a whale of a fish, unlike anything the peasant had ever seen, even in his wildest dreams.

'Now, hold this rod, and come along with me,' the Man from Kunkolim commanded, hoisting the colossal fish onto his back as if it were a mere sardine. The peasant, clutching the bamboo rod with both hands, trudged behind him, feeling like a tiny rat in the shadow of a mountain. Upon reaching the fisherman's humble abode, the giant fish was handed over to his wife for smoking, a task that would surely feed the village for days. The Man from Kunkolim then settled down to sharpen his already formidable appetite with large draughts of liquor. The peasant, still reeling from the day's events, sipped a drink that felt like fire in his throat.

At mealtime, the fisherman devoured huge chunks of the fish and mountains of rice, while the peasant could manage only small morsels, marveling at the Man's insatiable hunger. After the feast, the Man from Kunkolim requested his snuff-box, a full-sized wooden trunk, and his 'nose-tickler,' a bundle of coconut fronds. He took a deep breath of snuff through one nostril, and then another through the other. The force of his second inhalation, combined with the peasant's unfortunate proximity, lifted the peasant off his feet and drew him in with the snuff. The fisherman then tickled his nose with the fronds, resulting in a deafening sneeze that echoed like monsoon thunder across the village. The sheer power of the sneeze hurtled the peasant through the air, across Ilhas into Bardez, landing him right under the eaves of his own house, covered in snuff. His wife, hearing the thud and groans, found him in this bewildered state, a testament to the extraordinary encounter with the brave and mighty fisherman of Kunkolim.

Moral: True strength and courage are not always found in grand battles, but often in the daily endeavors and remarkable feats of ordinary life, leaving an unforgettable impression on those who witness them.
moral: True strength and courage are not always found in grand battles, but often in the daily endeavors and remarkable feats of ordinary life, leaving an unforgettable impression on those who witness them.

safety_notes: The story contains elements of exaggerated strength and a humorous, albeit forceful, expulsion of a character. Ensure the tone remains lighthearted and clearly fantastical for elderly listeners.

title: Sant Tukaram and the Ant's Wisdom
alt_titles: The Ant and the Sugar Hill
narrative_text:
In the serene village of Dehu, lived the revered saint, Sant Tukaram. His heart was always brimming with devotion for Lord Vitthal, and his life was a testament to humility and profound spiritual wisdom. Though he was a simple shopkeeper, his teachings resonated deeply with all who heard them, from the common folk to the most learned scholars. He often used simple analogies from everyday life to convey the deepest truths of existence. 

One day, as Sant Tukaram sat in quiet contemplation, he observed a tiny ant diligently making its way towards a mound of sugar. The ant, with great effort, picked up a single grain of sugar, its small body almost dwarfed by its precious cargo. It then turned and began its journey homeward. On its way, the ant paused, and in that moment, Tukaram, with his divine insight, perceived the ant's thoughts. The little creature, proud of its find, was thinking, "This one grain has filled my stomach, but next time, I shall carry the entire sugar hill back to my home!"

Sant Tukaram smiled gently. He understood the ant's innocent ambition, yet he also recognized the profound lesson embedded in this small scene. He often reflected on how human beings, much like the ant, often perceive their understanding of the divine or the vastness of the universe as complete, even when they have only grasped a minuscule part of it. He would often say, "Men often think they have understood Brahman fully." 

He knew that the true nature of Brahman, the ultimate reality, was beyond the grasp of words and thought, immeasurable and infinite. Just as the ant could never truly carry the entire sugar hill, no human, however great their intellect or spiritual attainment, could ever fully comprehend the entirety of the divine. Even the greatest sages, he believed, were like ants carrying only a few grains of sugar from a vast hill. This parable served as a constant reminder of the boundless nature of the divine and the importance of humility in one's spiritual journey. Tukaram emphasized that true wisdom lay not in claiming to know everything, but in recognizing the infinite mystery of God and approaching it with an ever-humble heart.
moral: True humility lies in recognizing the vastness of the divine and the limitations of one's own understanding. Even the greatest knowledge is but a tiny fraction of the infinite truth.

title: The Story of Chhath Puja
narrative_text:
In the ancient lands of India, where rivers flow like veins of life and the sun shines with divine grace, there is a sacred festival known as Chhath Puja. It is a time when hearts are filled with devotion, and prayers rise to the heavens, honoring Surya Dev, the Sun God, and Chhathi Maiya, his benevolent sister. This festival, celebrated with immense purity and austerity, weaves together tales of devotion, sacrifice, and the enduring power of faith.

One of the most cherished legends traces back to the epic Ramayana. After Lord Rama's triumphant return to Ayodhya, following his victory over Ravana, the kingdom rejoiced. Yet, to atone for the sins of war, Lord Rama and Mata Sita performed a special puja. It was Mata Sita, with her unwavering devotion, who observed the rigorous fast and rituals of Surya Shashthi, which we now know as Chhath Puja. Through her sincere prayers to the Sun God and Chhathi Maiya, she was blessed with their divine grace, and soon, the beloved twins, Lava and Kusha, graced their lives. This act of devotion by Sita became a beacon of hope and tradition, inspiring generations to seek blessings for their children and family well-being.

Another profound story emerges from the Mahabharata, the grand epic of dharma and duty. After escaping the treacherous Lakshagriha, Queen Kunti, the mother of the Pandavas, found solace and strength in performing Chhath Puja. Her fervent prayers to Surya Dev, the father of her valiant son Karna, are believed to have bestowed upon her and her sons the resilience to face their arduous exile. It is also said that Karna, the great warrior, himself was a devout worshipper of the Sun God, standing in the waters and offering his prayers, drawing immense power and vitality from the divine Surya. Later, Draupadi, the wife of the Pandavas, also observed this sacred fast, seeking victory and prosperity for her husbands during the Kurukshetra War, demonstrating the festival's deep connection to strength, protection, and success.

Beyond these epic narratives, there is a poignant folk tale about King Priyavrata. The king and his beloved Queen Malini were heartbroken, for they had no heir to their throne. In their despair, the sage Kashyapa advised them to perform a yajna. Soon, a son was born to the queen, but alas, he was stillborn. Overwhelmed with grief, the royal couple was visited by Mata Shashthi, who appeared in the sky. She revealed herself as Chhathi Maiya, the protector of all children and the bestower of progeny. With her divine touch, the lifeless child was brought back to life. Overjoyed and filled with gratitude, King Priyavrata worshipped Mata Shashthi with utmost reverence. This miraculous event solidified the belief in Chhathi Maiya's power to grant children and protect them, making Chhath Puja a cherished festival for those seeking blessings for their offspring.

Thus, Chhath Puja is not merely a set of rituals; it is a tapestry woven with threads of ancient legends, deep faith, and profound devotion. It is a celebration of life, gratitude, and the enduring bond between humanity and the divine forces of nature. The moral of Chhath Puja lies in the unwavering faith and purity of heart with which devotees offer their prayers to the Sun God and Chhathi Maiya, seeking health, prosperity, and the well-being of their families. It teaches us the importance of self-discipline, gratitude, and the belief that sincere devotion can overcome any adversity and bring forth divine blessings.
moral: The moral of Chhath Puja lies in the unwavering faith and purity of heart with which devotees offer their prayers to the Sun God and Chhathi Maiya, seeking health, prosperity, and the well-being of their families. It teaches us the importance of self-discipline, gratitude, and the belief that sincere devotion can overcome any adversity and bring forth divine blessings.

title: Mahabharata: The Death of Duryodhana
narrative_text:
After the great warrior Karna had fallen in the Kurukshetra war, Duryodhana, the eldest son of Dhritarashtra, was plunged into deep grief and despair. He had lost all his friends and kinsmen, and with his forces decimated, he fled the battlefield, seeking refuge in the depths of a terrible lake, fearing his enemies.

On the afternoon of that fateful day, Bhimasena, one of the mighty Pandava brothers, discovered Duryodhana's hiding place. With the assistance of other powerful car-warriors, Bhima surrounded the lake and summoned Duryodhana to emerge. Though reluctant, Duryodhana was compelled to face his adversary.

A fierce mace duel ensued between Bhima and Duryodhana. Bhima, remembering his vow to break Duryodhana's thighs for the humiliation of Draupadi, fought with immense strength and determination. Despite the rules of mace warfare forbidding strikes below the navel, Bhima, urged by Krishna's subtle signal, struck Duryodhana on his thighs, fulfilling his oath. Duryodhana fell to the ground, mortally wounded, his thighs shattered.

After Duryodhana's fall, the remaining three unslain Kaurava warriors – Ashvatthama, Kripa, and Kritavarma – filled with rage and a thirst for revenge, launched a surprise night attack on the Pandava camp, slaughtering many Pancala troops in their sleep.

The next morning, Sanjaya, the charioteer and narrator, returned to Hastinapura, the Kuru capital, bearing the devastating news of Duryodhana's death. He found the blind King Dhritarashtra, surrounded by his daughters-in-law, Gandhari, and Vidura, already grieving the loss of Karna. Sanjaya, with a voice choked with tears, recounted the complete annihilation of the Kaurava forces and the death of Duryodhana, whose thighs were broken by Bhima.

Dhritarashtra, upon hearing the tragic news, collapsed, overwhelmed with sorrow. He lamented his past mistakes, his failure to heed the wise counsel of Bhishma, Krishna, and Vidura, and his blind affection for his son that led to this catastrophic end. He was comforted by Sanjaya and Vidura, who reminded him of destiny and the consequences of his choices.
moral: The story of Duryodhana's death highlights the destructive consequences of unchecked pride, envy, and unrighteousness. It underscores the importance of heeding wise counsel and the inevitable repercussions of one's actions, emphasizing that even great power cannot overcome the force of destiny and dharma.

title: The Story of the Sage Vishwamitra
narrative_text:
The story of Vishwamitra begins not with a sage, but with a powerful and righteous king named Kaushika, born into the lineage of Kusha, a mind-born son of Lord Brahma. King Kaushika ruled his kingdom with great wisdom and ensured the prosperity of his people. His heart, however, yearned for something more profound than earthly dominion. This yearning set him on an extraordinary path of transformation. 

One day, while returning from a grand expedition with his vast army, King Kaushika chanced upon the serene hermitage of Sage Vasistha. Out of deep respect for the revered sage, Kaushika decided to visit the ashram. Sage Vasistha, known for his boundless hospitality, welcomed the king with traditional warmth, offering him water for his feet and hands, and a comfortable seat. 

As the time for meals approached, Vasistha insisted that Kaushika and his entire army partake in his hospitality. Kaushika, knowing the immense size of his retinue, humbly declined, believing it would be an impossible task for a hermit. But Vasistha, with a gentle smile, summoned his divine cow, Sabhala, also known as Kamadhenu, the celestial wish-fulfilling cow. To Kaushika's utter astonishment, Sabhala, with her divine powers, manifested a magnificent feast, providing delicious food and drink for every single soldier, attendant, and animal, satisfying their hunger completely. 

Witnessing this miracle, a powerful desire arose in Kaushika's heart. He felt that such a divine treasure, capable of sustaining an entire kingdom, rightfully belonged to a king. He approached Vasistha and offered immense wealth—golden chariots, elephants, horses, and countless cows—in exchange for Kamadhenu. However, Vasistha steadfastly refused, explaining that Sabhala was not a mere possession but the very essence of his hermitage, integral to his spiritual practices and his way of life. 

Kaushika's desire, when met with refusal, slowly transformed into anger and a sense of humiliation. This moment marked a pivotal turning point in his life. He realized that his kingly power, though vast, was insignificant compared to the spiritual might of a sage. He resolved to attain spiritual power greater than Vasistha's, to become a Brahmarishi. 

Thus began Kaushika's arduous journey of penance and self-transformation. He renounced his kingdom and worldly attachments, dedicating himself to severe austerities. His penance was so intense that it disturbed the celestial realms. Lord Indra, fearing that Kaushika's power might rival his own, sent the beautiful celestial nymph Menaka to distract him. Menaka, with her enchanting beauty and grace, succeeded in breaking Kaushika's meditation. They lived together for a time and had a daughter, Shakuntala. 

Upon realizing he had been swayed from his path, Kaushika was filled with remorse and anger, but he eventually overcame these emotions. He understood that true spiritual power lay in conquering one's own desires and weaknesses. He resumed his penance, even more determined than before. He endured further tests, including one where Indra, disguised as a poor Brahmin, sought to take his last meal. Kaushika, without hesitation, gave away his food, demonstrating his complete detachment. 

After countless years of unwavering dedication and profound austerities, Kaushika finally transcended his earthly desires and anger. His spiritual power reached its zenith. Lord Brahma, along with other deities, appeared before him and bestowed upon him the title of 'Brahmarishi Vishwamitra'—the 'Friend of All'—recognizing his boundless compassion and ultimate spiritual attainment. 

Vishwamitra then approached Sage Vasistha, no longer with rivalry, but with humility and respect. Vasistha, recognizing the profound transformation in Vishwamitra, embraced him, and their long-standing conflict dissolved into mutual admiration. Vishwamitra's journey from a powerful king to a revered Brahmarishi stands as a timeless testament to the power of perseverance, self-control, and the pursuit of spiritual truth. He is also credited with composing the sacred Gayatri Mantra, a hymn of immense spiritual significance, and played a crucial role in the life of Lord Rama, guiding him and imparting divine knowledge.
moral: The story of Vishwamitra teaches us the power of perseverance, self-control, and the pursuit of spiritual truth. It highlights that true strength lies not in worldly power, but in conquering one's own desires and weaknesses, and that humility and forgiveness lead to ultimate spiritual attainment.

title: The Tale of Two Snakes
narrative_text: There was a king called Devashakti who was very frustrated with his son, who was very lean and weak. He grew leaner and weaker every day. Well-known physicians from far off places could not cure him, because there was a snake inside his stomach. They tried all sorts of treatments, but in vain. The young prince was also frustrated to see his father sad for him, and he was fed-up with his life. One night, he crept outside the palace and went to another kingdom. He started living in a temple, and ate from whatever alms he collected from kind people. This new country was ruled by a king, who had two young daughters. They were raised with the best of learning. Every morning they would bow at their father's feet to seek his blessings. One of the daughters said, "O Father, all joys of the world is bestowed upon us, with your blessings", and the other daughter would say, "O King, one only gets the fruits that are destined for one's actions!". The comments of the second daughter made the king very angry and one day, he summoned his ministers, "Let her enjoy the fruits that are destined for her actions! Take her and marry her off to anybody you meet outside the palace." The ministers did so, and were unable to find anybody when they saw the young prince who was living in the temple. The two were married. The princess was a religious girl, and considered her husband as her God. She was very happy and content with the marriage. They decided to travel to a different part of the country, as it would be improper for them to make the temple as their home. On the way, the prince became tired and wanted to rest under the shade of a tree. He was getting weaker every day, and could not walk long distances. The princess decided to buy some food from a nearby market. When she returned, she saw her husband fast asleep and a snake emerging from an anthill nearby. As she was about to caution, she saw another snake emerging from her husband's mouth. She hid herself, to keep a watch. The snake from the anthill said to the other snake, "Why do you torture this handsome prince? You risk your own life, too. If the prince drinks soup of cumin seeds and mustard, you would die for sure!" The snake from the prince's mouth said, "Why do you guard two pots of gold which you do not have any need of? You risk your life, too. If someone pours hot water and oil on the anthill, you would die for sure!" After a heated argument, they went inside their respective places, but the princess had already known their secrets. She acted accordingly and fed her husband with a soup of cumin seeds and mustard. Within hours, the young prince began to recover and regained much strength. After that, they poured hot water and oil on the anthill, and dug out the two pots of gold that the snake was guarding. Now, that the young prince had started recovering, and they had two pot full of gold, they lived happily ever after.
moral: When your enemies quarrel, you are the winner.

title: The Story of the Camel, the Lion, and His Court
narrative_text:
A camel, separated from its caravan, wandered into a dense forest. This forest was ruled by a powerful lion named Fierce-fangs, who had three cunning courtiers: a Tiger, a Jackal, and a Crow. They discovered the lost camel and presented him before their king. The lion, after hearing the camel's tale, welcomed him into his service and named him 'Crop-ear.'

During a particularly harsh rainy season, the lion fell ill, and food became scarce for the entire court. The courtiers, driven by their own hunger and a desire to rid themselves of the camel, began to plot. 'What use is this thistle-eater to us?' they mused, resolving to persuade the lion to kill him.

The Tiger questioned whether the Rajah would go back on his word of protection. But the Crow, ever the schemer, confidently replied, 'Hunger knows no bounds; it makes one forget all promises. A starving snake will even devour its own eggs.'

They then approached the ailing Lion. 'Have you brought me food, my friends?' growled the King. 'None, your Majesty,' replied the Crow, 'unless you consider the food that is already before you.' When the Lion inquired further, the Crow whispered, 'Crop-ear, the Camel!'

The Lion, however, was hesitant. He touched the ground and his ears, a gesture of solemnity, stating, 'I have pledged his safety; how can I slay him?' The Crow, quick-witted, responded, 'Nay, Sire! I did not suggest you slay him. But what if he were to offer himself as food? Would your Majesty object to that?' The Lion, weakened by hunger, muttered, 'I am terribly hungry.'

With this subtle manipulation, the Crow gathered the Camel and the other courtiers before the King under a false pretense. He then addressed the Lion, lamenting their inability to find food and the King's declining health. He dramatically offered himself as a meal for the King. The Lion, feigning reluctance, refused. The Jackal then offered himself, followed by the Tiger, both of whom were also refused by the Lion.

Seeing his companions make what appeared to be safe offers, the foolish Camel, not wanting to seem ungenerous, also offered his own body as sustenance for the King. Before he could even finish his sentence, the Tiger pounced, tearing open the Camel's flank, and all the courtiers, along with the Lion, immediately devoured him.

This story illustrates how easily the naive can be deceived and sacrificed by the cunning and powerful, especially when driven by self-interest and hunger.
moral: Beware of false friends and cunning advisors, especially when they appeal to your generosity or self-sacrifice. The naive can easily be exploited by the powerful and self-serving.

title: The Jackal and the Crow (Jambu-Khadaka-Jataka, No. 294)
narrative_text:
This story the Master told at the Bamboo-grove, about Devadatta and Kokālika. At the time when Devadatta began to lose his gettings and his repute, Kokālika went from house to house, saying, "Elder Devadatta is born of the line of the First Great King, of the royal stock of Okkāka, by an uninterrupted noble descent, versed in all the scriptures, full of ecstatic sanctity, sweet of speech, a preacher of the law. Give to the Elder, help him!" In these words he praised up Devadatta. On the other hand, Devadatta praised up Kokālika, in such words as these: "Kokālika comes from a northern brahmin family; he follows the religious life; he is learned in doctrine, a preacher of the law. Give to Kokālika, help him!" So they went about, praising each other, and getting fed in different houses. One day the brothers began to talk about it in the Hall of Truth. "Friend, Devadatta and Kokālika go about praising each other for virtues which they haven't got, and so getting food." The Master came in, and asked what they were talking about as they sat there. They told him. Said he, "Brethren, this is not the first time that these men have got food by praising each other. Long ago they did the same," and he told them an old-world tale.

Once upon a time, when Brahmadatta was king of Benares, the Bodhisatta became a tree-sprite in a certain rose-apple grove. A Crow perched upon a branch of his tree, and began to eat the fruit. Then came a Jackal, and looked up and spied the Crow. Thought he, "If I flatter this creature, perhaps I shall get some of the fruit to eat!" So in flattery he repeated the first stanza:

"Who is it sits in a rose-apple tree--
Sweet singer! whose voice trickles gently to me?
Like a young peacock she coos with soft grace,
And ever sits still in her place."

The Crow, in his praise, responded with the second:

"He that is noble in breeding and birth
Can praise others' breeding, knows what they are worth.
Like a young tiger thou seemest to be:
Come, eat, Sir, what I give to thee!"

With these words she shook the branch and made some fruit drop.

Then the spirit of the tree, beholding these two eating, after flattering each other, repeated the third stanza:

"Liars foregather, I very well know.
Here, for example, a carrion Crow,
And corpse-eating Jackal, with puerile clatter
Proceed one another to flatter!"

After repeating this stanza, the tree-sprite, assuming a fearful shape, scared them both away.

When the Master had ended this discourse, he summed up the Birth-tale; "At that time the Jackal was Devadatta, the Crow was Kokālika, but the Spirit of the Tree was I myself."
moral: Flattery, especially when undeserved, often leads to foolish actions and can be a tool for manipulation. True wisdom lies in recognizing genuine worth and not being swayed by insincere praise.

title: The Loyal Gardener
narrative_text: One day, Emperor Akbar was walking through his royal garden when he tripped over a rock. Already in a bad mood, this incident infuriated him, and he immediately ordered the arrest and execution of the gardener responsible for the garden's upkeep. The next day, as the gardener awaited his fate, he was asked for his last wish. He requested a final audience with the Emperor. When brought before Akbar, the gardener loudly cleared his throat and, to everyone's shock, spat at the Emperor's feet. Akbar was naturally outraged and demanded an explanation for such an audacious act. At this moment, Birbal stepped forward to defend the gardener. "Your Majesty," Birbal explained, "there is no one more loyal to you than this man. He feared that people would criticize you for ordering his execution over a minor incident like stumbling on a rock. To spare you such a reputation, he deliberately gave you a genuine and justifiable reason for his punishment." Realizing the profound wisdom in Birbal's words and the injustice he was about to commit, Emperor Akbar rescinded his order and set the loyal gardener free.
moral: Sometimes, a seemingly offensive act can be a display of profound loyalty and wisdom, designed to prevent a greater injustice.

title: Tenali Raman and the King's New Law: The Human Sacrifice for the Bridge
alt_titles: The Bridge and the Human Sacrifice
Tenali Raman's Solution to the King's Dilemma
narrative_text:
Human sacrifice was a practice common in ancient India, though it was banned in the Vijayanagaram empire during the rule of King Krishnadevaraya. However, a great dilemma arose when a bridge, being constructed across the Tungabhadra river, repeatedly collapsed before its completion. The superstitious public believed that the destruction was caused by the anger of a ghost, and to appease it, a human sacrifice was deemed necessary. A cruel monk further fueled these fears, stating that without a sacrifice, the bridge would never stand.

King Krishnadevaraya was deeply troubled. He desired a strong and durable bridge for his kingdom's progress but was unwilling to sacrifice innocent lives. He consulted his wise court jester, Tenali Raman, who, to the king's initial dismay, also stated that a human sacrifice was indeed necessary for a durable bridge. This response doubled the king's grief, as he had hoped for a humane solution.

However, Tenali Raman, with his characteristic wit, then presented his 'other ideas.' He explained that while the bridge was vital for the nation's progress and the ghost needed appeasing, there was a just way to perform the sacrifice. He proposed that there were many criminals in the kingdom who had already been sentenced to death. Their lives, he suggested, could be sacrificed for public welfare. He advised the king to issue a new law: henceforth, all death sentences should be executed at the site of the bridges under construction.

The king was greatly relieved and excited by this ingenious solution. It satisfied the public's demand for a sacrifice while upholding the king's moral stance against harming innocent people. He rewarded Tenali Raman with a thousand gold sovereigns for his wisdom. Tenali Raman, always keen to acquire wealth, returned home and, in his usual manner, hung a board in one of his houses to signify his new acquisition.
moral: This story illustrates Tenali Raman's exceptional wisdom and ability to find practical, humane, and just solutions to complex problems, even when faced with deeply ingrained superstitions. It teaches that true intelligence lies in navigating difficult situations with wit and integrity, satisfying all parties without compromising on ethical principles.

title: Vikram-Betal: The story of the princess and the magical fruit
narrative_text: A hermit, through his penance, received a magical fruit that granted eternal youth and vitality to whoever ate it. Having renounced all desires, the hermit presented the fruit to the virtuous King Bartruhari, the elder brother of King Vikramaditya, for the good of his people. The king, however, was deeply in love with his third queen and gave the fruit to her. The queen, secretly in love with a courtier, gave the fruit to him. The courtier, in turn, was infatuated with a courtesan and gave the fruit to her. The courtesan, however, was a great admirer of the king and, unaware of the fruit's journey, presented it to him. The king was astonished to see the fruit again and, after a quiet investigation, discovered the chain of desire and betrayal. Instead of punishing them, the king went into deep introspection about the nature of desire and renunciation. He retired to the forest and composed the Vairagya Shatakam, a collection of poems on renunciation. The fate of the magical fruit remains unknown.
moral: The story explores the illusory nature of love and the destructive power of desire. It highlights the importance of introspection and renunciation in achieving true wisdom.

title: Mahabharata: The Story of Gandhari's Curse
narrative_text:
The devastating war of Kurukshetra had ended, leaving behind a trail of unimaginable sorrow and loss. On the battlefield, amidst the fallen warriors, stood Gandhari, the queen of Hastinapura, her eyes, though blindfolded by choice for her husband's sake, perceived the profound tragedy through her inner vision. She had lost all her hundred sons, the Kauravas, in the war. Her heart, heavy with grief and despair, sought answers and accountability for such immense destruction.

Lord Krishna, the divine charioteer and guide to the Pandavas, approached Gandhari to offer his condolences. He found her consumed by an anguish so deep it transcended ordinary sorrow. Gandhari, known for her unwavering devotion and spiritual strength, had always maintained a sense of justice and righteousness. Yet, in that moment of profound loss, her grief turned into a searing anger, directed at the one she believed could have prevented the war: Lord Krishna himself.

With eyes that had never seen the world but now saw the truth of her suffering, Gandhari accused Krishna. She believed that as the Supreme Being, he possessed the power to avert the catastrophe, to stop the bloodshed, and to save her sons. Her voice, trembling with pain and accusation, declared, "Krishna, you stood by and watched as my entire lineage was annihilated. You, who are capable of anything, did nothing to prevent this war. Therefore, I curse you! Just as I have witnessed the destruction of my family, you too shall witness the destruction of your own Yadava clan. Thirty-six years from now, your kinsmen will perish, fighting amongst themselves, and you shall meet a similar end, alone and helpless."

Krishna, with his characteristic calm and serene demeanor, accepted Gandhari's curse without a word of protest. He understood the depth of her pain and the righteousness of her anger, even if it was misdirected. He knew that the curse was not merely an outburst of a grieving mother, but a divine decree, a part of the cosmic plan for the dissolution of the Yadava dynasty, which had grown arrogant and powerful. Krishna's acceptance of the curse demonstrated his profound understanding of dharma and karma, and his willingness to bear the consequences of events, even those beyond his direct control.

True to Gandhari's words, thirty-six years after the Kurukshetra war, the Yadavas, under the influence of a curse from some sages and their own internal strife, engaged in a fratricidal war. They fought fiercely amongst themselves, intoxicated and blinded by rage, until almost all of them perished. Krishna, witnessing the tragic end of his clan, retired to the forest. There, while meditating under a tree, he was mistakenly shot by a hunter named Jara, who mistook his foot for a deer. Thus, Lord Krishna departed from the earthly realm, fulfilling Gandhari's powerful curse.

The story of Gandhari's curse serves as a poignant reminder of the profound consequences of war and the immense suffering it inflicts. It highlights the spiritual strength and unwavering devotion of Gandhari, even in her darkest hour. More importantly, it underscores the universal principle that even divine beings are subject to the laws of karma and the unfolding of destiny. Krishna's acceptance of the curse teaches us about humility, the inevitability of certain events, and the importance of understanding and accepting the pain of others, even when we are wrongly accused. It is a tale that speaks to the cyclical nature of existence, the interconnectedness of actions and consequences, and the ultimate triumph of dharma, even through sorrow and loss.
moral: Even divine beings are subject to the laws of karma and destiny. It teaches humility, the inevitability of certain events, and the importance of understanding and accepting the pain of others, even when wrongly accused.

Therefore, I curse you!
Krishna, with his characteristic calm and serene demeanor, accepted Gandhari's curse without a word of protest.
Thus, Lord Krishna departed from the earthly realm, fulfilling Gandhari's powerful curse.

safety_notes: The story involves themes of war, death, and a curse, but is presented in a culturally respectful and elder-friendly manner, focusing on moral lessons and divine acceptance.

title: The Origin of the River Godavari: Sage Gautama and the Divine Ganga
alt_titles: The Story of Sage Gautama and the Godavari
narrative_text:
In the ancient land of Bharatavarsha, amidst a prolonged and severe drought that parched the earth and brought immense suffering to all living beings, lived the revered sage Gautama. He resided with his devoted wife, Ahalya, and his disciples on the sacred Brahmagiri mountain, a majestic peak in the Sahyadri ranges of Nashik. The drought, lasting a hundred years, tested the resilience of all, yet Sage Gautama, through his unwavering penance and devotion, managed to sustain his hermitage and its inhabitants.

One day, driven by compassion for the suffering world, Sage Gautama undertook rigorous austerities to appease Lord Varuna, the deity of oceans and rain. Varuna, pleased by Gautama's devotion, appeared before him. However, due to the divine will that decreed the drought, Varuna could not bring rain. Instead, he offered Gautama a boon: an inexhaustible source of sacred water. Varuna instructed the sage to dig a ditch, which he then filled with divine, ever-flowing water, proclaiming that this water would forever be known by Gautama's name. With this miraculous water, Gautama and his disciples were able to cultivate crops and alleviate the suffering of many.

Yet, prosperity often invites envy. The wives of other sages, observing Gautama's abundance, grew jealous. They conspired to create discord, preventing Gautama's disciples from accessing the life-giving water. When Ahalya, Gautama's wife, was the first to draw water, it further fueled their resentment. Seeking to undermine Gautama, these envious sages prayed to Lord Ganesha, requesting his intervention to bring misfortune upon the sage.

Lord Ganesha, ever-wise, initially advised against harming a virtuous soul like Gautama. However, succumbing to their persistent pleas, Ganesha agreed to play a part in their scheme. He assumed the form of a frail, emaciated cow and entered Gautama's fields, feigning to graze upon his crops. Gautama, wishing to gently shoo away the cow without causing it harm, inadvertently touched it with a blade of grass. To his horror, the cow immediately collapsed and died. Unbeknownst to Gautama, this was Ganesha's divine illusion, designed to make it appear as though Gautama had committed the grave sin of *Gohatya*, cow-slaughter.

Overwhelmed with sorrow and believing himself guilty of such a heinous act, Sage Gautama resolved to atone for his perceived sin. He left his hermitage, circumambulated the holy Brahmagiri mountain, and confessed his transgression to everyone he met. He then began to worship Lord Shiva, installing numerous Lingams and performing intense penance, seeking absolution.

Lord Shiva, the compassionate one, eventually appeared before Gautama. He revealed the truth of the illusion and reassured the sage that he was innocent, having committed no sin. Shiva further declared that Gautama's mere presence was enough to purify others. Despite this divine reassurance, Gautama, ever selfless, requested that Ganga, the celestial river, descend to earth to cleanse not only himself but also all others who suffered from sins and the lingering effects of the drought. Shiva, pleased with Gautama's profound compassion, granted his wish. He bestowed upon Gautama "the essence of the earth and heaven," his inheritance from his father Brahma, to summon Ganga. Ganga, the divine river, agreed to descend to the mortal realm on one condition: that Lord Shiva also remain on earth. Thus, Lord Shiva, in his infinite grace, manifested as Tryambakeshwara, a revered Jyotirlinga, at the source of the river.

With Ganga's presence, the sacred waters began to flow, forming the river that would forever be known as Gautami, or Godavari. Sage Gautama and his disciples, now absolved of all perceived sins, bathed in its purifying waters. Even the envious sages who had conspired against Gautama, witnessing the divine miracle and feeling the weight of their own transgressions, sought purification. Though initially hesitant, Ganga, moved by Gautama's gentle persuasion, agreed to cleanse them. Gautama, with his divine power, dug a special ditch for Ganga to emerge, allowing the sages to bathe and be purified. This sacred spot became known as the Kushavartha Thirtha, a revered pilgrimage site at the Tryambakeshwara temple.

And so, the great river Godavari, born from divine intervention and the unwavering devotion of Sage Gautama, flowed across the land, bringing life, purity, and spiritual solace to all who encountered her. Her waters, imbued with the blessings of Shiva and Ganga, continue to cleanse sins and nourish the earth, a testament to the power of faith, forgiveness, and selfless compassion. The moral of this tale reminds us that true purity comes not from external rituals alone, but from the purity of heart, forgiveness, and unwavering devotion to dharma. Even in the face of adversity and false accusations, righteousness prevails, and divine grace descends upon those who remain steadfast in their virtue.
moral: The moral of this tale reminds us that true purity comes not from external rituals alone, but from the purity of heart, forgiveness, and unwavering devotion to dharma. Even in the face of adversity and false accusations, righteousness prevails, and divine grace descends upon those who remain steadfast in their virtue.

title: The Foolish Sage and the Swindler
narrative_text:
In a tranquil village, on the outskirts of a bustling town, lived a revered sage named Deva Sharma. He resided in a humble temple, known far and wide for his wisdom and blessings. Devotees from all walks of life sought his guidance, offering him gifts, food, and garments. The sage, being a man of simple needs, would sell the surplus, accumulating a considerable sum of wealth. Yet, despite his outward piety, Deva Sharma harbored a deep distrust of others, a trait that led him to guard his money bag with unwavering vigilance, never letting it out of his sight.

One day, a cunning swindler, observing the sage's possessiveness, deduced that the bag must contain a fortune. He plotted to steal it, realizing that a direct approach was futile. 'I cannot breach the temple walls or scale its gates,' he mused, 'but I can charm him with sweet words and feign devotion, becoming his disciple.' His plan was to gain the sage's trust, await an opportune moment, and then abscond with the treasure.

With this wicked scheme in mind, the swindler approached Deva Sharma with feigned reverence, prostrating himself and uttering, 'Om Namah Shivaya! O Guruji, I am weary of this worldly life and seek the path to peace. Please guide me.' The sage, touched by what he perceived as genuine spiritual yearning, replied kindly, 'My son, you are blessed to seek peace at such a tender age. I shall surely guide you.'

The swindler, seizing the moment, pleaded, 'O Guruji, please accept me as your disciple. I shall obey your every command.' Deva Sharma, though accepting, laid down a condition: 'A holy man like myself requires solitude for meditation at night. You shall not enter the temple after dusk but will sleep in a hut at the gate.' The swindler readily agreed, eager to serve the sage's wishes.

That evening, the sage formally initiated the swindler as his disciple. The swindler proved to be exceptionally diligent, massaging the sage's feet, assisting with rituals, and meticulously cleaning the temple. Despite his exemplary service, the sage's deep-seated mistrust prevented him from ever entrusting the money bag to his disciple. The swindler grew increasingly frustrated, contemplating drastic measures like poisoning or stabbing the sage to gain access to the bag.

As these dark thoughts consumed him, a young boy, the son of one of the sage's followers, arrived to invite Deva Sharma to a sacred thread ceremony at his home. 'O Guruji,' the boy implored, 'your presence would sanctify our ceremony.' The sage accepted the invitation and, accompanied by the swindler, set off for the town.

En route, they reached a river where the sage decided to relieve himself. He carefully wrapped his money bag within his robe and handed it to the swindler, instructing, 'My child, guard this robe until I return.' This was the moment the swindler had patiently awaited. As soon as the sage disappeared behind the bushes, the swindler snatched the money bag and fled.

Upon his return, Deva Sharma found his robe discarded and his money bag gone. He instantly understood the betrayal and cried out, 'Oh, you rascal! You have robbed me!' He pursued the swindler's footprints towards the town, but the cunning thief had vanished. The sage spent a night in town, returning to his temple the next morning, empty-handed and heartbroken.

The wise often say: **Do not be taken in by the sweet words of a swindler.**
moral: Do not be taken in by the sweet words of a swindler.

title: The Wise Old Bird
narrative_text: This is one of the great Panchatantra stories for children. Once upon a time, there lived a flock of geese in a huge tamarind tree in the jungle. The geese had made the tree their home because of its constant supply of tamarind. One day, a wise old goose noticed a tiny, little creeper growing at the foot of the tamarind tree. “Let us get rid of that creeper,” he told the other geese. “Someday, it may be too big for us to destroy and we will regret it.” The other geese paid no attention to the wise old goose. They went about their usual business without a worry or care. The creeper grew little by little. Soon, the creeper had grown large enough and strong enough for a man to climb. The old goose warned them once again, “We must get rid of this creeper! If we do not, we will be sorry.” The other geese still did not listen to him. Then, one day, when they were out flying, a hunter came to the forest. He saw the tamarind tree and decided that it would be a good place to put his net. He went away deciding to come back the next day. And so, the hunter came the very next day and climbed up the tree with the help of the creeper. He set up his net on the tree and went away. When the birds came back to the tree, they flew right into it without noticing the net. Soon, they were trapped under it. The birds began to squawk loudly. They flapped their wings about in panic. Only the wise old goose sat still and very quiet. “How can you be so calm?” asked one of the other geese. “I tried to tell you that a day would come when we would regret not destroying the creeper. But no one listened,” said the wise old bird. He wanted to help the other geese to escape. So he said, “When the hunter comes, lie down perfectly still. He will throw you down one by one thinking that you are dead. After all of us had been thrown down, and before the hunter climbs down, we shall take flight together.” When the hunter came, the birds lay perfectly still pretending to be dead. The hunter climbed the tree and threw them one by one. As soon as the last bird had been thrown to the ground, all of them took flight together. The hunter stood there, shocked at how he had been fooled by the geese. The geese were very grateful to the wise old goose. They had realized that they ought to solve their problems before the problem grows too big to be solved.
moral: We ought to solve our problems before the problem grows too big to be solved. Listen to wise advice.

title: The Foolish Weaver
narrative_text: Once upon a time in India, there lived a poor weaver. Now, this weaver did not have many helpers, so he had to do all the work by himself. One day, as he was weaving, his weaving frame broke. The poor man became sad and went to the woods nearby. There he looked at the trees and decided on one of them, to cut up. Just as he was preparing to chop the tree, he heard a voice, “Halt!” The weaver turned around and found a genie swirling in the air. “Don’t cut this tree,” the genie said. Now, the poor weaver had to cut it because if we did not chop that tree, there would be no weaving frame. And if there was no weaving frame, the weaver and his family would starve. So, the weaver told the genie about his reasons for chopping the genie. But the genie then told the weaver that the tree he was going to chop was his home. And if the weaver did so, then the genie would become homeless. After much discussion, the genie then suggested, “Would you stop if I grant you a wish?” Now, the weaver had no idea what to ask for. So, he asked the genie if he could take the advice of his family or friends. The genie agreed. The weaver hurried back. “My wife would probably have a better idea. And we may be able to use this chance to make our lives better,” the weaver thought. But little did he know that his wife was even dumber. When the weaver narrated the entire incident, the wife instantly told him to ask for more pairs of hands and more pairs of legs. Her logic was that these new pairs would help the weaver do his work faster. So, the foolish weaver hurried back to the genie and he repeated what his wife had advised him. The genie granted the weaver his wish. As a result, the weaver had many hands and legs. He hurried back to his village. But when the people saw the weaver, they thought that he was some monster. They beat him up with axes and sticks. And very soon, the weaver lay dead.
moral: LACK OF GOOD JUDGEMENT RUINS A GOOD OPPORTUNITY.

title: Akbar-Birbal: The Magical Sticks
narrative_text:
A rich merchant had many servants in his house. One day, his wife's beloved necklace disappeared from her vault. Soon after, other precious things in the house also began to disappear. The merchant was greatly troubled by the presence of a thief among his servants but found it difficult to identify the culprit. In his distress, he sought the help of his wise friend, Birbal.

Upon hearing the merchant's predicament, Birbal agreed to assist him. He went to the merchant's house and summoned all the servants. Birbal interrogated each one of them, but every servant denied any involvement in the thefts.

An idea then struck Birbal. He handed a stick of equal length to each servant. He told them that these were magical sticks, and the stick of the thief would grow by two inches by the next day. He ordered all the servants to present their sticks to him the following day.

The next day, as instructed, the servants gathered at the merchant's house with their sticks. Birbal carefully examined each stick. He noticed that one of the servants was holding a stick that was two inches shorter than the others.

'This is your thief, merchant,' Birbal declared, pointing to the servant with the shorter stick.

When the merchant asked Birbal to explain his accusation, Birbal revealed his clever reasoning: 'Honest men would have had no fear about their stick growing. However, the thief, fearing that his stick would indeed grow by two inches by morning, cut his stick shorter by two inches in anticipation.' The thief, caught in his own deception, confessed to his crimes.
moral: Guilt often leads to self-incrimination, and a clear conscience has nothing to fear.

title: Tenali Raman: The Story of the Fake Painter
narrative_text:
In the illustrious court of King Krishnadevaraya, a foreign painter arrived, boasting of his unparalleled skill. He claimed his paintings were so lifelike that it was impossible to distinguish them from reality. The king and his courtiers were captivated by the artist's works, marveling at their astonishing realism. However, Tenali Raman, known for his sharp wit and keen observation, harbored doubts about the painter's claims, sensing a deception beneath the surface.

To expose the painter's trickery, Tenali Raman devised a clever plan. He brought a caged monkey and placed it near one of the painter's masterpieces – a painting depicting a vibrant array of fruits. The monkey, fooled by the incredibly realistic depiction, eagerly reached out to grab the painted fruits. To its disappointment, its hand met only a flat, unyielding surface. The monkey, realizing the deception, retreated, chattering in frustration.

Tenali Raman then explained to the king and the court that while the painter possessed remarkable technical skill, his art lacked "life and soul." He argued that true art captures the essence and spirit of a subject, not merely its superficial appearance. The painter's works, though visually stunning, were nothing more than clever illusions designed to trick the eye. The king, understanding Tenali Raman's profound insight, agreed that the painter's skill had been used for mere trickery rather than genuine artistic expression.

Humbled by this simple yet effective demonstration, the foreign painter's pride was shattered. The incident served as a powerful lesson to all present: that substance and honesty hold far greater value than deceptive outward appearances. True artistry lies not just in imitation, but in the ability to imbue a creation with genuine feeling and meaning.
moral: True art captures the essence and soul of a subject, not just its superficial appearance. Substance and honesty are more valuable than deceptive outward appearances.

title: Vikram-Betal: The Story of the Prince and the Magical Tree
narrative_text: In the ancient land of India, there reigned a great king named Vikramaditya, renowned for his wisdom, courage, and unwavering sense of justice. His capital, Ujjain, was a prosperous city, and his people lived in peace and happiness. One day, a mendicant Jogi, a magician of great power, came to his court and presented the king with a rare fruit. The king, in his generosity, offered it to his treasurer. This continued for many days, until one day, the king, curious about the fruit, accidentally dropped it, and it broke open to reveal a magnificent ruby. The king, impressed by the Jogi's silent generosity, was determined to reward him. The Jogi, in return, requested the king to bring him a Betal, a vampire-like spirit, that hung from a magical tree in a dense, dark forest. The king, ever true to his word, promised to do so. King Vikramaditya, armed with his sword, set out for the forest. After a long and arduous journey, he found the magical tree, and hanging from one of its branches was the Betal. The king, with great effort, managed to capture the Betal and started his journey back to the Jogi. However, the Betal was a clever spirit and laid down a condition for the journey: the king must not utter a single word. If he did, the Betal would fly back to its tree. The Betal then began to narrate a story to the king, and at the end of each story, it would pose a question that the king was compelled to answer, for not to do so would be a dereliction of his kingly duty. And so, every time the king spoke, the Betal would escape and return to the tree, and the king would have the arduous task of going back and fetching it again. This cycle of capture and escape, story and question, continued, testing the king's perseverance and wisdom.
moral: One must always keep their promises, no matter how difficult the task. Perseverance and determination are key to overcoming any obstacle.

title: The Story of Keshavati and the Rakshasas
narrative_text: Once upon a time, in a land ruled by a king, there lived a beautiful princess named Keshavati. Her kingdom, however, was overrun by seven hundred fearsome Rakshasas, who had devoured her family and all the inhabitants, sparing only Keshavati due to an old Rakshasi's strange affection for her. The Rakshasas would leave every morning to hunt and return by dusk. The old Rakshasi would kill Keshavati with a silver stick each morning and revive her with a gold stick in the evening. One day, a young man named Champa Dal, who had been banished from his home, stumbled upon Keshavati's palace. Keshavati, seeing him, warned him of the danger, but together they devised a plan to escape. Keshavati, with Champa Dal's help, learned the secret of the Rakshasas' immortality from the old Rakshasi. Their lives were bound to two bees hidden in a crystal pillar in the middle of a tank. If these bees were destroyed without a single drop of their blood touching the ground, the Rakshasas would die. Champa Dal, armed with this knowledge, dove into the tank, retrieved the bees, and destroyed them over a pile of ashes, ensuring no blood touched the ground. With the bees destroyed, all seven hundred Rakshasas perished instantly. Keshavati and Champa Dal, now free, married and lived happily. However, Keshavati's beauty was such that a lock of her hair, carried by the river, caught the attention of Sahasra Dal, a prince from another kingdom. Sahasra Dal's mother, a disguised Rakshasi, abducted Keshavati using a magical boat. Champa Dal, heartbroken, searched for his wife and eventually found her in Sahasra Dal's kingdom. Keshavati, having made a vow not to see any man for six months, used this to delay her forced marriage to Sahasra Dal. Champa Dal, disguised as a learned Brahman, offered to recite the story of Keshavati's vow. During the recitation, he revealed the entire story of Keshavati's past, exposing the Rakshasi maid-servant and Sahasra Dal's deception. Sahasra Dal, realizing the truth, punished the maid-servant and reunited Keshavati with Champa Dal. They lived happily ever after.
moral: Cleverness, courage, and loyalty can overcome even the most formidable challenges. True love and perseverance will always prevail.

performance_notes.opening_line: In a land once vibrant, now shadowed by fear, lived a princess named Keshavati, whose spirit shone clear.
pause_points: The Rakshasas, fearsome and vast, had cast their dark shadow, a terror that lasted.
But Keshavati, with courage in her heart, sought a way to play a different part.
The secret revealed, a fragile thread, held the lives of the monsters, living and dead.
With cunning and bravery, the bees were undone, and freedom for Keshavati, finally won.
Yet fate, ever weaving, had more in store, as a lock of her hair reached a distant shore.
Separated once more, by deceit and by might, they yearned for reunion, through the darkest of night.
Through clever disguise and a tale bravely told, their love was rekindled, a story of old.

safety_notes: The story contains elements of danger (Rakshasas, abduction) but resolves happily with the triumph of good over evil. The description of the Rakshasas' demise is not overly graphic. Suitable for elderly listeners.

title: The Legend of Kannappa Nayanar
narrative_text:
In the dense forests of Andhra Pradesh lived a hunter named Thinnan, a man of simple origins but profound spirit. He was not learned in the scriptures or the elaborate rituals of worship, yet his heart was pure and filled with an unyielding devotion to Lord Shiva. One day, while hunting, Thinnan stumbled upon a Shiva Lingam, an aniconic representation of the deity, nestled deep within the woods. He was immediately captivated by its presence and felt an overwhelming urge to worship it. 

Unaware of the traditional methods of worship, Thinnan offered to the Lingam what he knew best: fresh meat from his hunt, water carried in his mouth, and flowers he had gathered. He would clean the Lingam with his mouth and adorn it with his simple offerings, believing this to be the most sincere form of devotion. The temple priests, who later discovered the Lingam, were horrified by what they perceived as sacrilege. They meticulously cleaned the Lingam and performed their rituals, only to find it 'defiled' again the next day by Thinnan's unconventional offerings. 

Determined to uncover the perpetrator, one priest hid near the Lingam. He witnessed Thinnan's heartfelt, albeit unorthodox, worship. As Thinnan was about to offer his usual fare, the priest saw blood flowing from the eyes of the Shiva Lingam. Distraught, Thinnan tried to stop the bleeding with herbs, but to no avail. In a moment of extreme devotion, remembering a belief that the eye of a deity could be healed by offering one's own eye, he plucked out one of his eyes and placed it on the bleeding eye of the Lingam. Miraculously, the bleeding stopped from that eye, but then the other eye of the Lingam began to bleed. 

Without a moment's hesitation, Thinnan prepared to pluck out his second eye. To ensure he would place it correctly on the Lingam's bleeding eye, he placed his foot on the Lingam to mark the spot. At this ultimate act of sacrifice and devotion, Lord Shiva appeared before him, deeply moved by Thinnan's unwavering faith. Shiva restored Thinnan's eyes and declared him Kannappa Nayanar, meaning 'the devotee with eyes,' and granted him salvation. Kannappa's story became a testament to the idea that true devotion transcends ritual and convention, residing instead in the purity and intensity of the heart.
moral: True devotion comes from the heart and is not bound by rituals or conventions. Sincere faith and selfless sacrifice are highly valued.

pause_points: ...he plucked out one of his eyes and placed it on the bleeding eye of the Lingam.
...the other eye of the Lingam began to bleed.
...Thinnan prepared to pluck out his second eye.

safety_notes: The story involves self-mutilation (plucking out an eye) as an act of extreme devotion. While presented as miraculous and leading to divine intervention, it should be narrated with sensitivity, emphasizing the symbolic nature of the sacrifice and the purity of intent rather than promoting literal interpretation of such acts.
