title: The Devotion of Sant Namdev and the Turning Temple
alt_titles: Sant Namdev: A great devotee of Bhagwan Vitthal
The Miracle of Aundha Nagnath Temple
narrative_text:
In the vibrant land of Maharashtra, there lived a young boy named Namdev, whose heart overflowed with love for everyone, especially for Bhagwan Vitthal, the revered deity of Pandharpur. From his earliest years, Namdev's unwavering faith in Bhagwan Vitthal was evident, and his melodious voice filled the air with beautiful bhajans, songs of praise that resonated with his belief in God's omnipresence.

Namdev shared a profound connection with Bhagwan Vitthal, spending countless hours in the temple. He would offer flowers, sing hymns, and converse with the idol as if it were his dearest friend. One day, while singing a heartfelt abhang, a form of devotional poetry, he perceived a joyful sparkle in the idol's eyes, confirming his belief that his songs reached the very heart of Bhagwan Vitthal.

However, not all children shared Namdev's conviction. They challenged him, questioning, "If your God truly listens, why doesn't He partake of the food you offer?" Determined to demonstrate his faith, Namdev prepared a plate of delectable food and presented it to the idol of Vitthal, declaring, "I will not eat until You have eaten first." Though others mocked him, Namdev remained steadfast, his smile reflecting his unwavering belief that Bhagwan was listening.

As dusk settled and shadows lengthened, an extraordinary miracle unfolded. To the astonishment of all present, the idol stirred to life, extended its hand, and began to consume the offered food! Namdev's heart swelled with joy and profound faith. He imparted to everyone that true devotion lies in trusting God and believing in miracles.

News of this wondrous event spread far and wide, drawing people from various places to seek Namdev's presence. As he matured, he journeyed to Pandharpur, the sacred city of Bhagwan Vitthal, spreading a message of love and humility. His devotion was so deep that he composed numerous soulful abhangs, devotional poems that continue to inspire to this day. One of his renowned mantras, "Sri Vitthala Giridhari Parabrahmane Namaha," signifies, "I bow down to the divine Bhagwan Vitthal, who uplifts our spirits and is the supreme being." Namdev's life exemplified kindness, as he perceived Bhagwan in every individual, regardless of their social standing.

On one occasion, Sant Namdev visited the Aundha Nagnath Temple to sing bhajans. Yet, some priests barred him from entering, deeming him unworthy to sing within the sacred precincts. Heartbroken but undeterred, Namdev sat outside the temple and began to sing with all his heart. Such was the power of his devotion that the entire temple miraculously turned around to face him! This awe-inspiring event made everyone realize that God heeds only a pure and loving heart, not one's status or position. Namdev taught that true worship transcends mere rituals and prayers; it is about living with love and compassion towards all. He became a beloved saint, inspiring countless individuals to embrace Bhagwan Vitthal and dedicate themselves to serving humanity.
moral: True devotion and a pure heart are more important than rituals or social standing. Faith can lead to miracles, and God listens to those who love with sincerity and compassion.

title: The Return to Kailash: The Story of Ganesh Visarjan
alt_titles: Ganesh Visarjan: A Story of Impermanence and Return
narrative_text:
In the vibrant land of Bharat, where devotion blooms like the lotus, there is a beloved festival that fills hearts with joy and reverence: Ganesh Chaturthi. For ten glorious days, homes and communities welcome Lord Ganesha, the remover of obstacles and the harbinger of good fortune. His idols, crafted with love and devotion, become the center of joyous celebrations, prayers, and sweet offerings.

But as all beautiful journeys must have a gentle conclusion, so too does Lord Ganesha's visit. The final day of this grand festival is known as Ganesh Visarjan, a day of both tender farewells and profound spiritual understanding. It is believed that after gracing our earthly abodes, Lord Ganesha prepares to return to his celestial home on Mount Kailash, to reunite with his divine parents, Lord Shiva and Goddess Parvati.

Imagine the scene: families gather, their hearts a mix of happiness for the time spent with their beloved Ganesha, and a gentle sadness at his departure. With hymns and chants, they carry the beautifully adorned idols in grand processions. The air resonates with the joyous cries of "Ganpati Bappa Morya, Pudhchya Varshi Lavkar Ya!" – “O Father Ganesha, come again soon next year!”

The procession moves towards a body of water – a river, a lake, or the vast ocean. Here, with utmost respect and devotion, the idols are gently immersed. This act of immersion, or Visarjan, is not an end but a beautiful symbol of life's cyclical nature. It reminds us that everything in this world is temporary, from the grandest celebrations to our very own forms. Just as the clay idol dissolves back into the earth from which it came, so too do our physical bodies return to nature, while the spirit, like Lord Ganesha, continues its eternal journey.

It is a lesson in detachment, teaching us to cherish moments and relationships without clinging to them, understanding that true joy lies in the spiritual connection, not just the physical presence. The immersion signifies Lord Ganesha taking away with him all the troubles, sorrows, and negativity of his devotees, leaving behind a renewed sense of peace, prosperity, and spiritual growth.

As the waters embrace the idol, a profound sense of peace settles over the devotees. They know that though the physical form of Ganesha departs, his blessings, wisdom, and divine presence remain in their hearts, guiding them until his joyous return the following year.
moral: The story of Ganesh Visarjan teaches us the profound truth of impermanence and the beauty of detachment. It reminds us that true devotion lies not in holding onto the physical, but in embracing the cyclical nature of life, trusting in divine blessings, and finding peace in the continuous journey of the spirit.

title: Mahabharata: The Death of Krishna
narrative_text:
After the Great War of Kurukshetra, which devastated humanity, Gandhari, the mother of the Kauravas, cursed Krishna. She blamed him for not preventing the war and prophesied that 36 years hence, Krishna and his Yadava clan would perish by killing each other, and Dwaraka would be submerged. Krishna accepted the curse with a gentle smile, acknowledging its inevitability due to the changing sands of time and her devotion.

Years passed, and the Yadavas in Dwaraka, blessed with prosperity, grew arrogant and lost their sense of discipline. One day, sages like Vishwamitra and Durvasa visited Dwaraka. A group of mischievous Yadava boys, including Samba, Krishna's son, decided to play a prank. They dressed Samba as a pregnant woman and asked the sages to predict the gender of the child. The enraged sages, seeing through the deception, cursed them, declaring that Samba would give birth to an iron mace that would destroy the entire Yadava race.

Samba soon delivered an iron mace. Fearing the curse, the Yadavas, on Akroora's command, ground the mace into a fine powder and cast it into the sea. However, a small, sharp triangular piece remained and was also thrown into the ocean. This piece was later swallowed by a fish, which was caught by a hunter named Jara. Jara used the iron piece to craft a poisonous arrow. The fine powder, washed ashore, grew into lush, sharp grass on the coast of Dwaraka.

Thirty-six years after the Mahabharata war, omens appeared in Dwaraka. The Sudarshana Chakra, Panchajanya Shankh, Krishna's chariot, and Balarama's plough weapon disappeared. The Yadavas, gathered on the beach, consumed liquor and lost their senses. They began to taunt each other, recalling past grievances. A brawl erupted between Satyaki and Krithavarma, leading to Satyaki beheading Krithavarma. This ignited a mass slaughter among the Yadavas. Pradyumna, Krishna's son, was also killed in the chaos.

Without weapons, the Yadavas used the sharp grass blades, which were the remnants of the iron mace, to kill each other. The curse of the sages and Gandhari had come to fruition. Soon, all Yadavas lay dead, except for Krishna and his charioteer Daruka, and Babhru. Krishna sent Daruka to Hastinapura to inform Arjuna of the tragedy and seek his help.

Krishna then found Balarama meditating under a tree. As Krishna watched, a great serpent emerged from Balarama's mouth, signifying the end of his mortal incarnation. Realizing his own time had come, Krishna went into Yoga Samadhi under a tree. Rishi Durvasa had once told Krishna that his death would be in his foot, as he had not applied Payasam to his feet when applying it to the rest of his body. 

At that moment, Jara, the hunter who had crafted the poisonous arrow, mistook Krishna's moving foot for a deer and shot his arrow, piercing Krishna's foot. Realizing his grave mistake, Jara pleaded for forgiveness. Krishna consoled him, explaining that his death was inevitable and part of the divine plan. Thus, Lord Krishna left his mortal coil, marking the beginning of the Kali Yuga.
moral: The story of Krishna's death highlights the inescapable nature of karma and the fulfillment of divine prophecy. Even divine beings are subject to the cycles of creation and destruction, and the consequences of actions, whether individual or collective, will eventually manifest. It also emphasizes the importance of righteousness and discipline, as the Yadavas' arrogance and lack of virtue led to their downfall.

safety_notes: The story contains themes of war, death, and a curse leading to self-destruction within a clan. While presented in a mythological context, care should be taken to ensure elderly listeners are comfortable with these themes. The narrative focuses on the spiritual and karmic aspects rather than graphic details.

title: The Story of Sage Jamadagni
narrative_text:
The lineage of Sage Jamadagni begins with an intriguing tale. King Kuśāṃba had a son named Gādhi, whose daughter was Satyavatī. The hermit Ṛcīka, desiring Satyavatī's hand, offered a dowry of a thousand horses, each with a black ear, and married her. One day, Satyavatī and her mother both expressed a wish for children. Ṛcīka performed a sacrifice to Agni and prepared two portions of sacred rice, imbued with mantras. One portion was charged with the radiance of Brahmā, and the other with the radiance of Kṣātra (warrior spirit). He instructed Satyavatī to consume the Brahmā-infused rice and give the Kṣātra-infused rice to her mother. However, the women secretly exchanged the portions. Consequently, Satyavatī conceived a child embodying Kṣātra tejas, and her mother conceived a child with Brāhma tejas. When Ṛcīka discovered the switch, he foretold their children's destinies. Thus, Satyavatī gave birth to Jamadagni, a sage with a warrior's spirit, and her mother gave birth to Viśvāmitra, a Brahmin who later became a great warrior-sage. This explains why some Purāṇas describe Viśvāmitra as Jamadagni's uncle, while others consider them brothers.

As Jamadagni matured, he embarked on a pilgrimage, visiting various holy sites. His journey led him to the palace of King Prasenajit of the Ikṣvāku dynasty, where he beheld the exquisite Princess Reṇukā. Enamored by her beauty, he sought her hand in marriage, and King Prasenajit, without objection, consented. Jamadagni and Reṇukā established a hermitage on the banks of the Narmadā river, dedicating themselves to 'tapas' (penance). They were blessed with four sons: Ṛumaṇvān, Suhotra, Vasu, and Viśvāvasu.

The Earth, suffering under the tyranny of wicked Kṣatriya kings, appealed to Brahmā, who, in turn, sought the aid of Mahāviṣṇu. Mahāviṣṇu promised to incarnate as Jamadagni's son to eradicate these oppressive rulers. In due course, Reṇukā gave birth to Paraśurāma, an incarnation of Mahāviṣṇu, destined to fulfill this divine purpose.

One day, while Jamadagni was away, Reṇukā went to the Narmadā to fetch water. At the river, she witnessed Kārtavīryārjuna and his wives sporting in the water. Later, she saw Citraratha, the King of Sālva, with his wife, and was captivated by their beauty. This momentary distraction caused her to be late returning to the hermitage. Jamadagni, having returned and grown impatient, was enraged by her delay. He commanded his sons, one by one, to kill their mother. The elder four sons refused, deeming matricide a grave sin. Enraged by their disobedience, Jamadagni cursed them to become foresters. However, Paraśurāma, unwavering in his devotion to his father, obeyed the command and beheaded Reṇukā with a cutting arrow. Overwhelmed with grief, Paraśurāma fainted. When Jamadagni's anger subsided, he asked Paraśurāma to choose a boon. Paraśurāma requested his mother's resurrection, and Jamadagni, pleased by his son's obedience, brought Reṇukā back to life.

Later, Kārtavīryārjuna, while hunting near the Narmadā, stumbled upon Jamadagni's hermitage. The sage, with his divine cow Suśīlā, miraculously provided a lavish feast for the king and his retinue. Coveting the cow's divine powers, Kārtavīryārjuna sent his minister, Candragupta, to demand Suśīlā. When Jamadagni refused, Candragupta forcibly took the cow, striking the sage dead in the process. Reṇukā, discovering her husband's lifeless body, cried out in anguish, beating her breast twenty-one times. Paraśurāma, arriving with disciples, vowed to avenge his mother's sorrow by annihilating the Kṣatriya kings twenty-one times. The sage Śukra, using the Mṛtasañjīvanī mantra, resurrected Jamadagni, and Suśīlā also returned.

Paraśurāma, accompanied by Akṛtavraṇa, challenged Kārtavīryārjuna at Māhiṣmatī. In the ensuing battle, Kārtavīryārjuna and his sons were slain. Jamadagni then instructed Paraśurāma to perform penance on Mahendragiri to atone for the bloodshed. While Paraśurāma was away, Śūrasena, a surviving son of Kārtavīryārjuna, attacked the hermitage, beheaded Jamadagni, and fled with his head. Paraśurāma and the disciples cremated Jamadagni's body, and Reṇukā, in an act of devotion, committed Sati by jumping into the pyre. This final tragedy fueled Paraśurāma's resolve, leading him to embark on twenty-one campaigns to eradicate the Kṣatriya kings, fulfilling his oath to his mother.
moral: The story of Jamadagni highlights the complexities of dharma, obedience, and justice. It underscores the profound consequences of actions driven by anger and desire, as seen in Jamadagni's rash command and Kartaviryarjuna's greed. Ultimately, it emphasizes the cyclical nature of karma and the unwavering devotion of a son to his parents, even in the face of extreme circumstances.

It is important to frame these events within the cultural and historical context of ancient Indian epics, focusing on the lessons of dharma and karma rather than literal interpretation.

title: Dharmabuddhi and Pâpabuddhi
alt_titles: The Story of the Two Friends and the Buried Treasure
The Just-Hearted and the Unjust-Hearted Friend
narrative_text:
In a certain place there lived two friends, Dharmabuddhi, which means "having a just heart" and Pâpabuddhi, which means "having an unjust heart."

One day Pâpabuddhi thought to himself, "I am a simpleton, plagued with poverty. I am going to travel abroad with Dharmabuddhi, and earn money with his help. Then I will cheat him out of it and thus gain a good situation for myself."

One day he said to Dharmabuddhi, "Listen, friend! When you are old, which of your deeds will you be able to remember? You have never seen a foreign country, so what will you be able to tell the young people? After all, don't they say: His birth has borne no fruit, who knows not foreign lands, many languages, customs, and the like. And also: One never properly grasps knowledge, wealth, and art, until joyfully one has wandered from one land to another."

Pâpabuddhi, as soon as he had heard these words, took leave from his parents with a joyful heart, and one happy day set forth for foreign lands. Through their diligence and skill, Dharmabuddhi and Pâpabuddhi acquired great wealth on their travels. Happy, but also filled with longing, they turned homeward with their great treasure. For it is also said: For those who gain wisdom, art, and wealth in foreign lands, the absence of one hour has the length of hundreds.

As they approached their city, Pâpabuddhi said to Dharmabuddhi, "Friend, it is not prudent for us to return home with our entire treasure, for our families and relatives will want part of it. Therefore let us bury it somewhere here in the thick of the forest and take only a small part home with us. When the need arises, we can come back and get as much as we need from here. For they also say: A smart man does not show off his money, not even in small amounts, for the sight of gold will agitate even a good heart. And also: Like meat is devoured in the water by fish, on land by wild animals, and in the air by birds, he who owns money is everywhere at risk."

Upon hearing this, Dharmabuddhi said, "Yes, my friend, that is what we will do!"

After having thus buried their treasure, they both returned home and lived happily together.

However, one day at midnight Pâpabuddhi went back into the forest, took the entire treasure, refilled the hole, and returned home.

Then he went to Dharmabuddhi and said to him, "Friend, each of us has a large family, and we are suffering because we have no money. Therefore, let us go to that place and get some money."

Dharmabuddhi answered, "Yes, my friend, let us do it!"

They went there and dug up the container, but it was empty.

Then Pâpabuddhi struck himself on the head and cried out, "Aha! Dharmabuddhi! You and only you have taken the money, for the hole has been filled in again. Give me my half of what you have hidden, or I will bring action against you at the king's court."

Dharmabuddhi said, "Do not speak like that, you evildoer. I am in truth Dharmabuddhi, the one with a just heart! I would not commit such an act of thievery. After all, it is said: The person with a just heart treats another man's wife like his own mother, another man's property like a clod of earth, and all beings like himself."

Quarreling thus, they proceeded to the court where they told their stories and brought action against one another.

The top judges decreed that they submit to an Ordeal of God, but Pâpabuddhi said, "No! Such an ordeal is not just. After all, it is written: In a legal action one should seek documents. If there are no documents, then one should seek witnesses. If there are no witnesses, then wise men should prescribe an Ordeal of God. In this matter the goddess of the tree will serve as my witness. She will declare which one of us is a thief and which one an honest man."

To this they all replied, "What you say is right, for it is also written: An Ordeal of God is inappropriate where there is a witness, be he even a man of the lowest caste, to say nothing of the case where he is a god. We too are very curious about this case. Tomorrow morning we shall go with you to that place in the forest."

In the meanwhile, Pâpabuddhi returned home and said to his father, "Father! I have stolen this money from Dharmabuddhi, and one word from you will secure it for us. Without your word, we shall lose it, and I shall lose my life as well."

The father said, "Child, just tell what I have to say in order to secure it!"

Pâpabuddhi said, "Father, in thus and such a place there is a large mimosa tree. It has a hollow trunk. Go hide yourself in it. When I swear an oath there tomorrow morning, then you must reply that Dharmabuddhi is the thief."

Having made these arrangements, the next morning Pâpabuddhi bathed himself, put on a clean shirt, and went to the mimosa tree with Dharmabuddhi and the judges.

Once there, he spoke with a piercing voice, "Sun and moon, wind and fire, heaven and earth, heart and mind, day and night, sunrise and sunset, all of these, like dharma, know a man's deeds. Sublime goddess of the forest, reveal which of us is the thief!"

Then Pâpabuddhi's father, who was standing in the hollow trunk of the mimosa tree, said, "Listen! Listen! The money was taken away by Dharmabuddhi!"

Having heard this, the king's servants, their eyes opened wide with amazement, searched in their law books for an appropriate punishment for Dharmabuddhi's theft of the money.

While they were thus engaged, Dharmabuddhi himself surrounded the tree's opening with flammable material, and set it on fire. When it was well ablaze, Pâpabuddhi's father emerged from the hollow tree. His eyes streaming, he cried out bitterly.

"What is this?" they asked him.

He confessed everything, and then died. The king's servants forthwith hanged Pâpabuddhi from a branch of the mimosa tree, but they had only words of praise for Dharmabuddhi.
moral: Dishonesty and deceit ultimately lead to ruin, while truth and justice prevail. One should not attempt to gain wealth through wrongful means, as such actions will inevitably be exposed and punished.

title: The Foolish Wolf and the Clever Lamb
narrative_text: There was a large flock of sheep grazing on a lawn. A wolf stealthily stole a lamb. The lamb tried hard to get away but it could not. As the wolf was about to kill the lamb, an idea struck in the lamb’s mind. The lamb pleaded, "Oh wolf! I know that I am going to die now. Will you be kind enough to fulfill my last wish?". The wolf asked, "What is your last wish?". The lamb replied, "I am fond of music. Before I die, I wish to hear you play the flute.". The wolf agreed and played the flute. When the wolf stopped the lamb said, "Oh! what beautiful music! Will you play a bit louder, please? You play far better than my shepherd". The wolf was pleased by the flattery and started to play louder. In the mean time, the shepherd and the dogs who were in search of the missing lamb heard the sound. They came running and caught hold of the wolf. The lamb happily trotted away to his flock.
moral: If there is a will there is a way.

title: Jataka: The story of the foolish elephant
narrative_text: Once upon a time, a king had an Elephant named Girly-face. The Elephant was called Girly-face because he was so gentle and good and looked so kind. But one day Girly-face began to be very bad. He would not do what his keeper told him to do. He struck the keepers with his trunk and tried to hurt them. The king was told that his favorite Elephant was no longer gentle and good, but was now so bad that he tried to kill his keepers. The king sent for his wisest man and asked him what was the matter with Girly-face. The wise man went to the stall where the Elephant was kept. He looked the Elephant over carefully and could find nothing that seemed to be the matter. He thought at last, "Girly-face must have heard some bad men talking. Have there been any bad men talking about here?" asked the wise man. "Yes," one of the keepers said, "a band of robbers were caught here a few weeks ago. They had met in the yard to talk over their plans. They were talking together near the stall where Girly-face sleeps." So the wise man went back to the king. Said he, "I think Girly-face has been listening to bad talk. If you will send some good men to talk where Girly-face can hear them I think he will be a good Elephant once more." So that night the king sent a company of the best men to be found to sit and talk near the stall where Girly-face lived. They said to one another, "It is wrong to hurt any one. It is wrong to kill. Every one should be gentle and good." "Now those men are teaching me," thought Girly-face. "I must be gentle and good. I must hurt no one. I must not kill any one." And from that time on Girly-face was tame and as good as ever an Elephant could be.
moral: The company you keep can influence your behavior. Choose your friends and influences wisely.

title: Akbar-Birbal: The Floating Palace
narrative_text: One night, the great Emperor Akbar had a vivid dream of a magnificent palace, unlike any he had ever seen. This palace was not built on solid ground, but floated gracefully in the air, adorned with countless shimmering gemstones. The dream captivated him, and he awoke with its image firmly etched in his mind. The next day, in his royal court, Akbar recounted his extraordinary dream to his assembled courtiers. He spoke of the floating palace with such wonder that it was clear he could not shake its ethereal beauty from his thoughts. A mischievous idea then sparked in the Emperor's mind. He decided to play a prank on his courtiers. With a grand pronouncement, he declared, "I shall bestow twenty sacks of gold coins upon anyone who can construct this floating palace for me!" A hush fell over the court. The courtiers exchanged bewildered glances, for they knew such a feat was impossible. Throughout the day, Akbar repeatedly pressed his courtiers, asking if they would undertake the challenge of building his dream palace. Yet, each time, they remained silent, unable to offer a solution to such an impossible task. The following morning, as the court gathered, Birbal, the Emperor's most trusted and witty advisor, burst into the hall, feigning great distress. "Your Majesty!" he cried, his voice filled with mock despair, "I had a terrible dream last night! I dreamt I possessed immense wealth, countless riches, but alas, someone stole it all! I am utterly ruined!" Emperor Akbar, seeing through Birbal's act, chuckled and replied, "Birbal, my dear friend, it was merely a dream! There is no need to be so distraught." Birbal, seizing the opportunity, responded with a knowing smile, "Ah, Your Majesty, if your floating palace, which exists only in a dream, is to be considered real, then surely my dreamt-of riches and their theft must also be real! Therefore, the thief who stole my dream money should be apprehended and punished!" Akbar, understanding the clever parallel Birbal had drawn, burst into hearty laughter. He recognized Birbal's wisdom in subtly pointing out the absurdity of his own demand. Amused and impressed by Birbal's wit, the Emperor generously rewarded him with gold coins, not for building a palace, but for his invaluable wisdom and quick thinking.
moral: Dreams are not reality, and one should not demand the impossible based on mere imagination. Wisdom and wit can elegantly highlight the truth.

title: Tenali Raman: The Story of the King's New Poet
alt_titles: Tenali Raman and the Challenge of Verse
narrative_text:
In the glorious court of Emperor Krishnadevaraya, known for his love of arts and wisdom, there arrived a new poet named Dhurandhar. Poet Dhurandhar was a man of imposing stature and even more imposing words. His poetry was filled with complex metaphors, obscure allusions, and grandiloquent phrases that left most courtiers bewildered, yet impressed by their sheer complexity. The King, initially captivated by Dhurandhar's elaborate verses, began to shower him with praise and gifts, much to the dismay of the other court poets, and even Tenali Raman, who observed the new favorite with a knowing smile.

Dhurandhar, puffed up with pride, soon grew arrogant. He openly mocked Tenali Raman's simpler, more accessible poetry, calling it 'pedestrian' and 'lacking in true depth.' One day, during a court assembly, Dhurandhar, emboldened by the King's favor, challenged Tenali Raman to a public poetry contest. 'Let us see whose verses truly hold wisdom, mine or this jester's simple rhymes!' he declared with a sneer.

King Krishnadevaraya, intrigued by the challenge, agreed to host the contest the following day. That evening, Tenali Raman did not spend his time poring over ancient texts or crafting intricate verses. Instead, he visited the royal stables and spent a considerable amount of time observing the horses.

The next day, the court was abuzz with excitement. Poet Dhurandhar, dressed in his finest attire, recited a long, convoluted poem filled with flowery language and obscure philosophical concepts. The courtiers clapped politely, though many secretly confessed they understood little of it. When it was Tenali Raman's turn, he stepped forward with a calm demeanor and recited a short, simple verse:

'The horse, though grand, with noble mane,
Eats but grass, and feels no pain.
Its strength it lends, without a fuss,
A loyal friend, to all of us.'

The court was silent, some confused by the apparent simplicity, others amused. Dhurandhar burst into laughter. 'Is this your great wisdom, Tenali? A poem about a horse eating grass? How utterly mundane!'

King Krishnadevaraya, however, looked at Tenali Raman with a thoughtful expression. 'Tenali,' he said, 'explain the profound meaning of your verse.'

'Your Majesty,' Tenali Raman began, 'Poet Dhurandhar's verses are like a grand feast with many exotic dishes, but none that truly nourish the soul. They are complex for the sake of complexity, designed to impress rather than to enlighten. He uses words that few understand, and even he, I suspect, struggles to grasp their true essence. My poem, on the other hand, speaks of a simple truth. Just as a horse, despite its strength and beauty, finds sustenance in humble grass, true wisdom often lies in simplicity and clarity. It is not the number of difficult words, but the depth of understanding and the ability to convey truth that makes poetry profound. Can Poet Dhurandhar explain the true meaning of his own complex verses in simple terms that all can understand?'

Dhurandhar stammered, unable to articulate the 'profound' meanings he had claimed for his poetry. He had merely strung together impressive words without true comprehension. The King, seeing the truth in Tenali Raman's words, nodded sagely. He realized that true wisdom is not about ostentation but about genuine insight and the ability to communicate it clearly. He thanked Tenali Raman for his valuable lesson and once again recognized his unparalleled wit and wisdom, while Poet Dhurandhar, humbled, learned that true artistry lies not in complexity, but in clarity and genuine understanding.

Moral: True wisdom and artistry are found in clarity and genuine understanding, not in pretentious complexity or obscure language.

title: Betaal Pachisi - Story 22 - The Love Story of Four Princes
alt_titles: The Princess and the Magical Mirror
The Love Story of Four Princes
narrative_text:
Vikram again controlled Vetala and took him on his shoulder. Vetala started narrating him a story so that they could easily complete the long journey. He warns the king not to speak anything, else he would go back to his place again.

In a certain kingdom, there was a beautiful princess. The princes of the nearby region who knew her, wanted to marry her. They approached the king in the pursuit. Seeing the four of them the king asked them to bring the rarest gift for the princess in a months time, if they want her hand. Whose gift she would like, she will garland him and accept him as her husband. The princes took their horses and left in four different directions.

One prince comes across a yogi who was engaged in his meditation. When he opened his eyes he was able to read in the eyes of the prince that he was in love with the princess and wanted his help in the matter. He said that he will be able to help him only after the completion of his meditation. This will give him supernatural powers to help him. He stayed with him and saw to it none tries to obstruct the meditation of the yogi. After sometime when the yogi succeeded in his mission, he was pleased with the prince and offered him a magic mirror in which he could see his beloved by remembering her.

The second prince served a Blind Sanyansini. Built a cottage for her to protect her from the rain. She was much pleased and wanted to help him. She said that her Mantras will not be effective unless she has sight. To get sight a small tomb was to be dug. It was a difficult task, because swords were hanging on the top of it and within a fixed period the job had to be done. Otherwise there was risk of the swords falling. There was pitcher also on the top of it and it was to burst soon after the digging started. However the prince succeeded and was able to dig the star that brought light to her sight. The Sanyansini was much pleased. She through her supernatural powers produced a small one wired instrument and gave it him She said that through the instrument he could hear his beloved voice easily.

The third prince Amar was a lame and he served a Vaidya who was making a miraculous medicine which could cure lepresy even. But they all needed help to carry on their mission unhindered. For this he had to make certain austerities and so he needed the services of an unmarried man. The prince offered himself for the purpose. The mission was successfully completed and the Vaidya was able to make an elixir drinking which he became young. With his supernatural powers he gave a flower to the prince. He said that the flower has the capacity to cure. It will cure any disease of any one. But it can be used only once. The prince Amar took the flower and rushed to meet the princess.

The fourth prince sees a man going on a flying mat. He calls him and approaches him to get the mat. Initially he cheats him but a moment comes when during their fight a snake was about to bite him when the prince saves his life by picking and throwing away the snake. The man felt obliged to the prince for saving his life. So he willingly offered the mat to the prince.

Now after having obtained the precious gifts the princes met at one place. The prince who had the mirror remembered the princess and saw that she was lying on the bed unconscious. The man who had the stringed instrument said that he will find it out what is the matter. With his instrument he heard the voices there in the room and told them that the princess was sick and lying unconscious. The prince who had the flying mat offered the services of his mat and they all rushed to the spot where the princess was bed ridden. When Amar saw that the princess was unconscious and that the King was worried over her condition, the Vaidyas were also showing helplessness, he said that he will cure the princess. He took out the flower and after smelling it the princess became alright. Amar did his duty but he did not stay there. He left the place.

After narrating the story, Vetala asked king Vikrama to do justice in the matter that with whom she should marry because all the four had been instrumental in curing her. They all played a different part. To this Vikram replied that the sacrifice of Amar is greater that that of others. He himself was lame. If he wanted he could have cured himself first. But he kept the flower for the princess and cured her. He has sacrificed most. The princess came running with a garland in hand to wed Amar. He said that she was pitying him. She replied that his sacrifice has turned pity into love. Amar accepted the garland.

But as the King Vikram had spoken, The Vetala as his commitment slipped away from his hands and Vikram again chased him.
moral: The story teaches that true love and sacrifice are more valuable than magical powers or material possessions. King Vikramaditya's judgment highlights the importance of selfless acts and prioritizing the well-being of others.

title: Ramayana: The Story of Shabari
narrative_text:
In the heart of a dense forest, there lived a woman named Shabari. She was a devotee of the sage Matanga, and her heart was filled with an unwavering love for the divine. Before the sage left his earthly body, he told Shabari that her devotion would be rewarded, for one day, the great Lord Rama himself would visit her humble ashram.

From that day on, Shabari's life had a single purpose: to wait for her beloved Lord. Every morning, she would wake before the sun, sweep the paths of the ashram, and decorate them with flowers. She would then go deep into the forest to collect sweet berries for Rama. To ensure that only the sweetest berries would be offered to her Lord, she would taste each one before placing it in her basket. She did this for years, her heart filled with hope and her mind constantly chanting the name of Rama.

One day, her long wait came to an end. Lord Rama, along with his brother Lakshmana, arrived at her ashram. Shabari's heart overflowed with joy. With tears in her eyes, she washed their feet and offered them the berries she had so lovingly collected. Seeing the half-eaten berries, Lakshmana was hesitant, but Rama, seeing the pure love and devotion in Shabari's heart, ate them with great delight. He told her that these berries, offered with such pure love, were the sweetest food he had ever tasted.

Shabari, her life's purpose fulfilled, sought Rama's permission to leave her mortal body. With his blessings, she entered the sacred fire and attained moksha, her soul merging with the divine.
moral: The story of Shabari teaches us that true devotion is not about wealth, knowledge, or social standing. It is about the purity of love and the sincerity of faith. Even the simplest offering, when given with a pure heart, is accepted by the divine.

title: Vidura: The Embodiment of Dharma
narrative_text:
In the ancient kingdom of Hastinapura, amidst the unfolding saga of the Kuru dynasty, lived Vidura, a figure revered for his unwavering adherence to Dharma, righteousness. His birth was unique, born of the great sage Vyasa and Parishrami, a humble handmaiden to the queens Ambika and Ambalika. Though not of royal blood, Vidura was destined for greatness, embodying wisdom and justice from his very inception. He was the half-brother to Dhritarashtra and Pandu, the future kings, and thus uncle to both the Kauravas and the Pandavas.

From a young age, Vidura displayed extraordinary intellect and a profound understanding of moral principles. He was educated alongside his royal brothers and quickly surpassed them in wisdom and statesmanship. His counsel was sought by all, and he served as the prime minister to King Dhritarashtra, a position he held with utmost integrity. Vidura’s advice, often referred to as 'Vidura Niti,' was a beacon of ethical governance, guiding the blind king through complex decisions and forewarning him of impending dangers.

Vidura’s life was marked by his constant efforts to uphold justice and prevent the catastrophic war between his nephews. He tirelessly advised Dhritarashtra to treat the Pandavas fairly, recognizing their rightful claim to the throne. He foresaw the ruin that Duryodhana’s greed and malice would bring upon the Kuru lineage and repeatedly cautioned against his nefarious schemes. When Duryodhana plotted to burn the Pandavas alive in the house of wax, it was Vidura who subtly warned them, enabling their escape.

One of the most poignant moments demonstrating Vidura’s moral courage occurred during the infamous dice game. As Draupadi, the wife of the Pandavas, was publicly humiliated and disrobed in the Kaurava court, Vidura was the sole voice of protest among the elders. He condemned the heinous act, risking Duryodhana’s wrath and Dhritarashtra’s displeasure. His impassioned plea for justice, though unheeded, solidified his reputation as a true upholder of Dharma.

Despite his deep loyalty to Hastinapura, Vidura’s allegiance was first and foremost to righteousness. When the Kurukshetra War became inevitable, and Krishna visited Hastinapura as a peace emissary, he chose to stay at Vidura’s humble abode rather than Duryodhana’s opulent palace. Krishna recognized Vidura’s pure heart and unwavering commitment to Dharma, valuing his genuine affection over Duryodhana’s manipulative hospitality. Vidura, in protest against the impending war and the injustice he witnessed, resigned from his ministerial post, choosing to side with the Pandavas and the path of righteousness.

Vidura’s life was a testament to the power of wisdom, integrity, and moral courage. He remained a steadfast guide and a moral compass throughout the tumultuous events of the Mahabharata, his wisdom shining brightly even in the darkest of times. After the war, he continued to serve as a revered elder, offering solace and guidance to the grieving Dhritarashtra and Gandhari, and eventually retired to the forest to live a life of asceticism, ultimately merging with his divine father, Dharma.

His story reminds us that true strength lies not in power or position, but in the unwavering pursuit of truth and righteousness.
moral: True strength and lasting respect are earned through unwavering adherence to Dharma, integrity, and moral courage, even in the face of adversity and personal cost.

title: The Birth of the River Brahmaputra
narrative_text:
In ancient times, nestled amidst the majestic peaks of Kailash, Gandhamadana, Jarudhi, and Sambwartakka, lay a sacred region known as Yugandhara. Here, a revered sage named Shantanu resided with his virtuous wife, Amogha. Though they lived a life of devotion and tranquility, a deep longing for a child filled their hearts. Through their fervent prayers and unwavering penance, they sought the blessings of Lord Brahma, the creator of the universe.

Moved by their devotion, Lord Brahma granted their wish, bestowing upon Amogha a divine son, not in human form, but as a magnificent body of water. This celestial child, destined to become a mighty river, was named Brahmaputra, meaning 'son of Brahma'. He was placed in a vast and serene lake, known as Brahmakunda, cradled by the four sacred mountains. The Brahmaputra grew, his waters swelling with divine energy, becoming a vast and tranquil lake, a source of immense spiritual power.

Centuries passed, and the Brahmakunda remained a sacred, yet contained, entity. It was then that the powerful Sage Parashurama, a fierce warrior-sage and an avatar of Vishnu, arrived in the region. After completing his arduous penance and seeking purification, he perceived the immense potential and divine essence held within the Brahmakunda. Recognizing that the Brahmaputra was meant to flow freely and bestow its life-giving waters upon the lands and people, Parashurama took up his mighty axe.

With a single, powerful stroke, Sage Parashurama broke the banks of the Brahmakunda. The waters of the Brahmaputra, thus released from their mountain cradle, surged forth with immense force, carving a majestic path through the valleys and plains. This act of liberation transformed the divine lake into the mighty Brahmaputra River, which flowed eastward, bringing fertility, sustenance, and spiritual purity to the lands it traversed. Thus, the Brahmaputra, born of divine blessing and liberated by a sage's powerful act, became a lifeline for millions, a testament to the interplay of divine will and heroic action.
moral: The story of the Brahmaputra teaches us that divine blessings often manifest in unexpected forms, and that great potential, though initially contained, is meant to be unleashed for the greater good of all. It also highlights the transformative power of purposeful action.

title: The Crafty Crane and the Craftier Crab
alt_titles: Panchatantra: The story of the clever crab and the foolish crane
narrative_text: Once, by a large lake teeming with fish and other aquatic life, lived an old heron. With age, his ability to catch fish had diminished, leaving him weak and hungry. To overcome his plight, he devised a cunning plan. He sat by the lake's edge, openly weeping. A compassionate crab, noticing his distress, approached him and inquired, "Uncle, why are you crying instead of catching fish?" The heron, feigning sorrow, replied, "My dear child, I have renounced all worldly desires and vowed to fast until death. I will no longer touch any fish." The crab, puzzled, asked, "If you have renounced worldly matters, why then do you weep?" The heron explained, "I have lived in this lake since birth, and now that I am old, I have heard a dire prophecy: there will be no rain for the next twelve years. The lake's water level is already low, and it will soon dry up completely." Alarmed by this news, the crab rushed to inform the other water creatures. Panic spread throughout the lake. They trusted the heron, as he had stopped fishing, and sought his counsel. "Please guide us to safety from this impending disaster," they pleaded. The heron, seizing the opportunity, said, "Not far from here lies another lake, abundant with water and adorned with lotus flowers. It holds enough water to last twenty-four years without rain. I can transport you there, one by one, on my back." Having gained their complete trust, the creatures eagerly requested him to carry them to the new lake. The wicked heron's scheme was now in full swing. Each day, he would carry a creature on his back, pretending to take them to the other lake. However, after flying a short distance, he would smash them against a rock and devour them. He would then return to the lake, fabricating stories of how happy the relocated creatures were. This continued for many days until the crab approached the heron, saying, "Uncle, you have taken everyone else to the new lake, but I, your first friend, remain. Please take me to save my life." The heron was delighted, thinking, "Eating fish daily has become monotonous. A crab today will be a welcome change." With this thought, the heron took the crab towards the same rock where he had killed the others. From above, the crab saw the gruesome pile of bones and skeletons. Instantly, he understood the heron's treachery. Remaining calm, he said to the heron, "Uncle, the lake seems far, and I am quite heavy. You must be tired; let us rest for a moment." The heron, confident that the crab could not escape in mid-air, revealed his true intentions: "There is no other lake. This journey is for my meal. Just as I do every day, I will smash you against this rock and feast upon you." As the heron confessed, the clever crab swiftly gripped the heron's neck with his powerful claws and strangled him to death. The crab chuckled, realizing he had saved himself and the other water creatures from the heron's deceit. He dragged the heron's body back to the lake. The other creatures were astonished to see him return and eagerly questioned him. The crab laughed and declared, "We were all being fooled! The heron was a fraud, and his tale of the lake drying up was a lie. He was taking one of us each day to that rock for his meal." He proudly concluded, "I understood his wicked plan and have killed the trickster. There is no need to worry, for we are safe in this lake, and it will not dry up." Indeed, the wise say: **When things go wrong, use your wit to overcome the situation.**
moral: When things go wrong, use your wit to overcome the situation.

title: The Golden Swan
narrative_text:
Once upon a time, in the ancient city of Benares, there lived a Brahmin who, after a life of virtue, was reborn as a magnificent golden mallard, a swan with feathers like hammered gold. This golden swan, the Bodhisatta, retained the memories of his past life. He remembered his wife and three daughters, Nandā, Nanda-vatī, and Sundari-nandā, who were now living in poverty, dependent on the kindness of neighbors and friends.

Filled with compassion, the golden swan resolved to help his family. He flew to their humble dwelling and alighted on the central beam of their roof. His former wife and daughters, astonished by the sight of such a splendid bird, asked him who he was. The swan revealed his true identity, explaining that he was their father, reborn, and had come to alleviate their suffering.

'You shall have my feathers,' he announced, 'one by one. Each feather is pure gold and will sell for enough to ensure your comfort and ease.' With these words, he shed one of his golden feathers for them. The family, overjoyed and grateful, sold the feather and used the proceeds to improve their lives. From time to time, the golden swan would return, generously offering another feather, and with each visit, the Brahmin women grew more prosperous and well-to-do.

However, as their wealth increased, so did the mother's greed. One day, she spoke to her daughters, 'My children, animals cannot be trusted. What if your father decides one day not to return? Let us seize the opportunity next time he comes and pluck all his feathers at once, so we can secure all his riches.' The daughters, sensing the cruelty in her plan, refused to partake in such an act, knowing it would cause their father pain.

But the mother's greed consumed her. The next time the golden mallard visited, she called him to her. Without hesitation, she seized him with both hands and began to pluck his feathers. The Bodhisatta's golden feathers possessed a unique property: if plucked against his will, they would lose their golden luster and become ordinary, white feathers, like those of a common crane. And so it happened. The magnificent golden feathers turned white in her grasping hands.

The poor swan, now stripped of his golden plumage, tried to fly but could not. The greedy mother, seeing her plan backfire and the feathers turn worthless, flung the now ordinary bird into a barrel, providing him with food but no freedom. As days turned into weeks, his feathers slowly grew back, but they were plain white, devoid of their former brilliance. Once his feathers were fully restored, the golden swan, now a white swan, flew away from the house of greed, never to return to his ungrateful family.

Thus, the family, once prosperous through the swan's generosity, lost everything due to the mother's insatiable greed. They were left with nothing but the bitter taste of their own avarice.
moral: Excessive greed leads to loss and sorrow. Be content with what you have, for avarice can turn blessings into burdens.

title: Akbar and Birbal and the Green Horse
narrative_text: One evening, Emperor Akbar and Birbal went out for a walk in the magnificent garden. There was greenery all around, the fragrance of flowers was pleasing, and the weather was very nice. Many types of birds were flying, especially the colorful birds attracting their attention with their voices. The king, who always tested Birbal, said, "Birbal! We wish to roam around in this lush green garden sitting on a green horse. I want you to arrange a green horse for me within seven days. If you fail, do not come back to the palace until you fulfill this order." The king knew that a green horse did not exist, but he wanted Birbal to admit defeat. Birbal, being very clever, understood the king's intention. He spent seven days wandering here and there, pretending to search for the horse. On the eighth day, Birbal came before the king in the court and said, "Jahanpanah! As per your request, I have arranged for a green horse for you, but its owner has two conditions." The king curiously asked about both conditions. Birbal replied, "One condition is that you have to go yourself to bring the green horse." The king agreed to this condition. For the second condition, Birbal said, "The second condition of the owner of the horse is that you will have to choose a day other than the seven days of the week to go to get the horse." Hearing this, the king was very surprised. Birbal cleverly replied, "Jahanpanah! The owner of the green horse believes that to get a special kind of horse, one must accept these special conditions." King Akbar was happy to hear this clever response from Birbal and agreed that it would be very difficult to defeat Birbal.
moral: The story highlights Birbal's presence of mind and intelligence in handling seemingly impossible demands with wit and cleverness.

title: Tenali Raman and the Fake Godman
alt_titles: Tenali Raman Exposes the Fake Monk
narrative_text: In the prosperous kingdom of Vijayanagara, ruled by the wise King Krishnadevaraya, lived a court jester named Tenali Raman, renowned for his sharp intellect and wit. One day, a godman arrived in the kingdom, claiming to have divine powers and the ability to perform miracles. He quickly gained a large following among the people, who were mesmerized by his seemingly magical feats. The king, too, was impressed and began to shower the godman with gifts and attention. However, Tenali Raman was skeptical. He observed the godman closely and noticed that his miracles were nothing more than clever tricks. He decided to expose the charlatan and save the king and his people from being deceived. One day, in the royal court, Tenali Raman challenged the godman to a test of his powers. He asked the godman to prove his divinity by making a nearby stone statue speak. The godman, confident in his ability to fool the people, accepted the challenge. He closed his eyes, chanted some mantras, and then commanded the statue to speak. To everyone's astonishment, the statue remained silent. The godman, flustered, tried again, but with the same result. Just as the crowd began to murmur in doubt, Tenali Raman stepped forward with a mischievous glint in his eye. He whispered something in the statue's ear, and to everyone's amazement, the statue spoke! It declared that the godman was a fraud and that his miracles were mere illusions. The crowd was stunned, and the godman, exposed and humiliated, fell at the king's feet, begging for forgiveness. King Krishnadevaraya, realizing he had been duped, was furious. He ordered the godman to be banished from the kingdom and thanked Tenali Raman for his wisdom and courage. From that day on, the people of Vijayanagara were more cautious and less easily swayed by false prophets.
moral: True wisdom lies in questioning what seems too good to be true and not blindly following anyone, no matter how charismatic they may seem.

performance_notes.opening_line: In the grand court of Vijayanagara, where wit was as valued as wealth, a new sensation had arrived – a godman who promised miracles.
pause_points: ...and then commanded the statue to speak.
...and to everyone's amazement, the statue spoke!

safety_notes: The story involves exposing a religious fraud, which should be handled with care to avoid offending any religious sentiments. The focus should be on the theme of critical thinking and not on discrediting any particular faith.

title: Vikram-Betal: The Prince, the Magical Flute, and the Test of True Love
alt_titles: Prince Dhruv and the Celestial Music
narrative_text:
King Vikramaditya, burdened by the task of carrying the elusive Betal, once again found himself traversing the dense forest under the cloak of night. Betal, hanging upside down from Vikram's shoulder, began his customary tale, a condition for his continued silence. "O King, listen to this story, and if you speak, your vow shall be broken."

Betal began: "In a kingdom far away, nestled amidst verdant hills, lived a young and virtuous Prince named Dhruv. He was known not for his prowess in battle, but for his gentle heart and his profound love for music. One day, while wandering through a secluded part of the royal gardens, he stumbled upon an ancient, gnarled tree. From its branches hung a flute, unlike any he had ever seen. It shimmered with an ethereal glow, and as his fingers brushed against it, a soft, melodious hum resonated through the air.

Curiosity piqued, Prince Dhruv took the flute and brought it to his lips. The moment he played, the forest around him burst into life. Flowers bloomed in vibrant hues, birds sang in perfect harmony, and a gentle breeze carried the enchanting melody across the land. This was no ordinary flute; it was a magical instrument, capable of stirring the deepest emotions and revealing hidden truths. News of the magical flute and the prince's captivating music spread far and wide, reaching the ears of Princess Mrinalini of a neighboring kingdom, renowned for her wisdom and discerning spirit.

Princess Mrinalini, intrigued by the tales, decided to visit Prince Dhruv's kingdom, disguised as a common traveler. She listened to his music, and her heart was deeply moved, not just by the beauty of the melody, but by the purity of the prince's soul that shone through every note. She fell in love with the man, not the prince or the magic. However, a powerful and jealous Sage, Vishwamitra, who coveted the magical flute for his own nefarious purposes, learned of their budding affection. He cast a spell, causing the flute to play only discordant, harsh notes whenever Prince Dhruv tried to play it for Princess Mrinalini, hoping to drive her away.

Distraught, Prince Dhruv tried everything to restore the flute's melody, but to no avail. Princess Mrinalini, seeing his despair, did not waver. She knew the magic was not in the flute itself, but in the heart of the one who played it. She approached the prince and, instead of asking him to play, she simply spoke of her love for his true self, his kindness, and his unwavering spirit, regardless of the flute's current state. Her words broke the spell, for true love and understanding were more powerful than any magic. The flute once again sang its celestial tunes, even more beautifully than before, for it was now imbued with the power of unconditional love. Prince Dhruv and Princess Mrinalini were married, and their kingdom flourished under their compassionate rule, their love a testament to the enduring power of genuine affection.

Now, King Vikram," Betal concluded, "tell me, who was truly responsible for breaking the spell and restoring the flute's magic: the Prince, for his pure heart, or the Princess, for her unwavering love and understanding?"

King Vikramaditya, after a moment of thoughtful silence, replied, "Betal, the spell was broken by the Princess's unwavering love and understanding. While the Prince's pure heart was the vessel for the flute's magic, it was the Princess's ability to see beyond the superficial and embrace his true self, even in his moment of despair, that dissolved the Sage's enchantment. Her love was the catalyst that allowed the flute's true magic, which stemmed from the Prince's pure heart, to manifest once more. Without her insight and acceptance, the Prince might have remained trapped in his sorrow, unable to overcome the spell."

As Vikram finished his answer, Betal chuckled, acknowledging the King's wisdom, and once again, he slipped away, only to reappear hanging from another tree, ready for the next tale.
moral: True love and understanding can overcome any enchantment or adversity, as they allow the inherent goodness within a person to shine through, regardless of external circumstances.

performance_notes.opening_line: King Vikramaditya, burdened by the task of carrying the elusive Betal, once again found himself traversing the dense forest under the cloak of night.
pause_points: ...a soft, melodious hum resonated through the air.
...revealing hidden truths.
...hoping to drive her away.
...more powerful than any magic.
...ready for the next tale.

safety_notes: The story contains elements of magic and a jealous sage, but these are presented in a mild, folkloric context, suitable for elderly listeners. The themes of love and understanding are positive.

title: The Brave Queen Abbakka of Ullal
alt_titles: Abbakka Mahadevi: The Unsung Warrior Queen
Rani Abbakka: The Queen Who Defied the Portuguese
narrative_text:
The Arabian Sea, as it gently laps the shores of Ullal in Karnataka, whispers tales of a remarkable queen, Abbakka Mahadevi. She was not just a ruler of a small princely state; she was a beacon of indomitable courage and patriotism, a true folk legend in the region. Long before Rani Jhansi became a symbol of bravery, Abbakka, nearly three centuries earlier, fiercely resisted the Portuguese invaders, though her valiant struggles are not as widely celebrated in historical records.

Ullal, Abbakka's capital, was a strategically vital port town. From a young age, Abbakka displayed extraordinary vision and prowess in military science, particularly in archery and sword fighting. Her father nurtured these talents, preparing her for the challenges ahead. However, her marriage to a neighboring king of Bangher was short-lived. She returned his jewels, severing ties, which fueled his resentment and later led him to conspire with the Portuguese against her.

The Portuguese, eager to control Ullal, launched numerous attacks, but Abbakka, with her sheer courage and ingenuity, repelled each one. Her story is kept alive through vibrant folk songs and Yakshagana, the traditional folk theatre. In local rituals like Bootaradhana, where individuals in a trance narrate her great deeds, Abbakka is revered. She was known for her simple attire, resembling a common village woman, yet she rode her horse with regal grace, dispensing justice deep into the night. Folklore even credits her as the last known person to wield the Agnivana, a fire-arrow, in her battles.

Abbakka, a Jain by faith, fostered an inclusive administration and army, comprising Hindus, Muslims, and the Moggaveeras, a fisherfolk community. When the Portuguese first attacked the South Kanara coast in 1525, destroying the Mangalore port, Abbakka was alerted and began preparing her kingdom for defense. In 1555, she bravely confronted Admiral Don Alvaro da Silvereira, who demanded tribute, and successfully drove him back.

Years later, in 1567, the Portuguese launched another devastating assault on Ullal. General Joao Peixoto, sent by Viceroy Antony Norohna, managed to capture Ullal and enter the royal court. But Abbakka, ever resourceful, escaped to a mosque. That very night, she led a counter-attack with 200 soldiers, killing General Peixoto and 70 Portuguese soldiers, forcing the invaders to retreat in disgrace. Another incident saw 500 Muslim supporters of Abbakka, aided by six thousand Muslim soldiers, attack the Portuguese in 1568, killing Admiral Mascarenhas and forcing them to abandon the Mangalore fort.

However, the tide turned in 1569 when the Portuguese regained Mangalore Fort and captured Kundapur. Recognizing Abbakka as a formidable threat, they exploited her estranged husband's animosity. He betrayed her strategies, leading to her capture and imprisonment. Yet, even in captivity, the warrior queen revolted, fighting until her last breath, dying as a true soldier.

Though her memory was long confined to local folklore, recent efforts by the Karnataka Government have honored her, including erecting a statue in Bangalore. The IGNCA is also creating a documentary, ensuring that the legend of Queen Abbakka, the brave queen of Ullal, continues to inspire generations.
moral: True courage lies not only in fighting battles but also in unwavering dedication to one's people and homeland, even in the face of betrayal and overwhelming odds. A leader's strength is amplified by unity and inclusivity.

title: The Clever Daughter-in-law
narrative_text:
A mother-in-law was a terrible tyrant. She gave her daughter-in-law no freedom. She saw to it that the young woman did all the housework, cleaned the cow shed, and carried water from the well. By this time it would already be evening. Mother and son would then eat by themselves, and give the daughter-in-law leftovers and stale rice. If the young woman so much as breathed a complaint, the mother-in-law would pick up the broomstick and rain blows on her head. If she wept, the old woman would let loose a barrage of abuse: ‘You slut, you hussy, you want to wash away our house in your tears and bring bad luck, you daughter of a whore,’ and so on. The son was meek and kept his mouth shut.

In their backyard, a snake gourd plant grew and thrived; long gourds swung from it. Everyone’s mouth watered when they saw the gourds. In season, the mother-in-law would make a big potful of delicious snake gourd talada. She and her son would eat most of it and give the daughter-in-law some leavings. Once, after several days of this semistarvation, the young woman was seized with a craving to eat a full meal of the delicious snake gourd talada.

One day, the daughter-in-law came home with her garbage basket and called her mother-in-law in a hurry.

‘Why are you howling like a vixen? What’s the matter with you?’ shrieked the mother-in-law.

‘I ran into Big Auntie’s husband. He says Auntie isn’t well and wants to see you. She is seriously ill and holding on to her life only to see you, he said. He wanted me to tell you that.’

‘Ayyo, my sister is dying. O Sister, what happened to you?’ cried the mother-in-law, beating her breast. ‘Come in now, I’m sautéeing the talada. You take care of it. I’ll go and see my sister,’ she said, and left the house.

The daughter-in-law was quite excited. She made some more talada. She cooked all sorts of other dishes and served her husband a big meal. Then she poured all the talada into a big vessel, carried it on her waist, and went out as if she were going to get water for the house. She went straight to Goddess Kali’s temple, entered it, and closed the door behind her. There was no one there. She sat there and ate an entire potful of talada. The Goddess, who was looking at all this, was astonished by the speed of her eating and the quantity she consumed. In her amazement, she put her right hand on her mouth. The daughter-in-law didn’t notice any of this but continued to eat to her heart’s content. When she was done, she belched a big belch of utter satisfaction, picked up the empty vessel, and went to the pond to wash it.

When she came home with a pot of water, the main door of her house was closed. Her mother-in-law had returned. She tapped on the door, which was soon opened by her angry mother-in-law. Sparks were flying from her eyes. She had a stick in her hand, and blows fell on the young woman’s back and waist till she fell to the ground crying pitifully. ‘You daughter of a whore, how long have you waited to cheat me like this? You’ve gobbled up a pot full of talada like a buffalo, you dirty slut!’ screamed the woman and rained some more blows. When the husband came home, he too joined in the punishment.

Meanwhile, the whole town was buzzing with the news that the Kali image in the temple now had its hand on its mouth. People from other towns also came to see this miracle, and everyone had their own interpretation. Everyone was scared that this was a bad sign. Something terrible was going to happen to the village, they thought, and shuddered. Worship and rituals were performed all over the village.

‘Someone has polluted the Goddess. That’s why she has shut her mouth with her hand. She is angry. There won’t be any rain. No children will be born in this village anymore,’ they said and were terror-stricken. They arranged festivals and sacrificed goats. But nothing seemed to please the Goddess. So the village elders sent the town crier through the area to announce a big reward to anyone who would make the Goddess remove her hand from her mouth. No one came forward.

The daughter-in-law watched all this and came to her mother-in-law one day and said, ‘Mother-in-law, tell the elders we’ll get the Goddess to remove her hand from her mouth. I know how to do it.’

The mother-in-law was furious at first. ‘Look at your mug! She wants me to lose face in the village. She wants to act big as if she is a holy woman. What no one could do, she says she’ll do. Fat chance!’ she sneered. But the daughter-in-law persisted and finally convinced her that she knew something no one else knew.

On the appointed day, she took with her the broomstick and the garbage basket full of rubbish and went to the temple. She shut everyone out and closed the door behind her. She put down the basket in front of the Black Goddess and, brandishing her broomstick, challenged Kali: ‘You jealous female! What’s it to you if I ate my snake gourd talada? Why do your eyes burn? If you’d only asked me, I’d have given you some. May your face burn, may your cheeks swell and explode, may your eyes sink and go blind! Will you take your hand off your mouth now or shall I beat you with my tamarind broomstick! Now!’

There was no answer. The daughter-in-law was now furious and looked like Kali the Black Goddess herself. She went up to the image and gave Kali’s face several whacks with her broomstick. Kali whimpered and cried, ‘Ayyo!’ She removed her hand from her mouth and the image now looked as it had always looked. ‘That’s better!’ muttered the daughter-in-law, picked up her basket and broom, and came home with the news that she had managed to get Kali to remove her hand from her mouth.

The whole village was agog with the news. Everyone ran to the temple to see for themselves and they couldn’t believe their own eyes. They praised the daughter-in-law as the greatest of chaste wives (pativrata) whose virtue had given her miraculous powers. They gave her a big reward and many gifts.

Now the mother-in-law was terrified by this incident. She felt that her daughter-in-law had strange powers and would take revenge against her for all the terrible things she had done all these years. The young woman knew some kind of magic, and who knows what she will do?

On a dark New Moon day, in the dead of night, mother and son whispered to each other. She said to him, ‘Son, this one frightened even Kali the Mother Goddess and made her take her hand off her mouth. She won’t let us go unharmed. We have beaten her, starved her, given her every kind of trouble. She’ll take revenge. She’ll finish us off. What shall we do?’

‘I can’t think of anything. You tell me,’ said the cowardly son.

She said, ‘She’s now asleep. We’ll gather her up in her mat, take her to the fields, and burn her in the pit there. I’ll get you a beautiful new bride.’

‘All right, let’s do it right now,’ said he, and they both gagged her quickly and rolled her in her bedclothes and mat. She knew that they were going to do something awful to her but she mustered courage and lay still. They carried her to the pit in the field outside the village and hid her behind the bush while they went looking for twigs and firewood. As soon as they left, she rolled around and loosened the mat around her. She slid out of it, pulled at the string around her hands and tore it, took the gag from her mouth, and found a log nearby, which she wrapped in her bedclothes and mat. Then she walked a little distance, climbed a banyan tree, and hid herself in its branches.

Mother and son came hurrying back, spread twigs and branches all around the bundle in the mat, put logs over it, and lighted it. They covered it with dry straw. It burned with leaping flames as they watched it burn and burn. When the knots in the fuel crackled and burst, they said, ‘The bones, the bones are splitting.’ When the log inside caught fire, its knot cracked in the flames and went off like a gunshot. They were satisfied that the skull had now exploded as it does in a cremation. It was dawn and they went home.

The daughter-in-law crouched in the branches. That night, four robbers came there to sit under the tree, to divide up the loot among themselves. They had just broken into a rich man’s mansion and plundered jewelry, gold, and cash. As they sat down, they saw a fire burning at some distance. So one of them climbed the tree to see if anyone was near the fire. He came right up to the branch where the daughter-in-law was perched. When he saw someone sitting there, he said softly, ‘Who’s there?’ She boldly put out her hand, gently shut his mouth, and whispered, ‘Ssh, not so loud. I’m a celestial. I’m looking for a good handsome man. I’ll marry you and make you rich beyond your dreams. Just be quiet!’

The robber couldn’t believe what was happening to him. He thought he had reached heaven and seen the Great White Elephant descend from the sky. He held her hand and said, ‘Are you for real?’ She said, ‘Hmm.’ Then she slowly pulled out her little satchel of betel leaf and betel nut, gave him some, and put some into her own mouth. He came closer to kiss her. She turned away, saying, ‘Look, we’re not married yet. But you can put your betel leaf into my mouth with your tongue. When I’ve eaten from your mouth, I’ll be as good as your wedded wife. All right?’

He was beside himself with joy. He put out his tongue with the chewed betel leaf on it and brought it close to her mouth. She at once closed her teeth on his tongue powerfully and bit it off. Screaming with unbearable pain, he lost his grip and fell down. The robbers below ran helter-skelter in panic. The man who had fallen had lost his tongue and could only babble and blabber and spit blood, making noises like ‘Da da dadadada…’ as he too ran after his companions. His noises scared them even more and they fled faster, with him squealing behind them.

When dawn came, the daughter-in-law cautiously climbed down the tree and saw to her amazement lots of gold, jewelry, and money! She quickly bundled them all up and went straight home. When she tapped on the door, calling out, ‘Mother-in-law, Mother-in-law, please open the door!’ the mother-in-law opened the door hesitantly, her face blanched with fear. There, in front of her, was her smiling daughter-in-law. The mother-in-law fainted at the sight. The daughter-in-law carried her into the hall, sprinkled cool water on her face, and revived her. Her son just stood there, not knowing what to think.

When she came to and opened her eyes, the mother-in-law asked her, ‘How did you…? How is it you are …?’

The daughter-in-law briskly replied, ‘After you cremated me, messengers from Yama, the god of death, took me to Him. His eyes were shooting flames like our Kali, our village goddess. As soon as He saw me, He said, ‘Send this one back. Her mother-in-law is a sinner. Bring her here and put the Iron Crow to work on her, to tear her to pieces with its beak. Dip her in cauldrons of boiling oil.’ He ranted on like that about you. I fell at His feet and begged Him, ‘Don’t do this to my mother-in-law. She is really a very fine woman. Give me whatever punishment you wish. Please spare my mother-in-law.’ He was pleased, even smiled and said, ‘You can go now. We’ll do as you say. But if your mother-in-law ever gives you any trouble, we’ll drag her here. My messengers will always be watching.’ Then He gave me all this gold, and jewelry, and money, and sent me home. People say bad things about the god of death. But He was so good to me.’

The mother-in-law embraced her daughter-in-law with fear and trembling in her heart.

‘Ayyo, you’re really the angel of this house. You’ve saved me from the jaws of death’s messengers. From now on, I’ll do as you say. Just forgive everything I’ve done to you. Will you, my darling daughter-in-law?’ she said, touching the daughter-in-law’s chin tenderly.

The daughter-in-law was now the boss in the house. Her mother-in-law and her husband followed her wishes and everyone was happy.

The moral of the story is that cleverness and courage can overcome tyranny and bring about a positive change in one’s life and circumstances.
moral: Cleverness and courage can overcome tyranny and bring about a positive change in one’s life and circumstances.
