title: Mahabharata: The Story of the Great War
alt_titles: The Story of the Great War
narrative_text:
The story of the Mahabharata begins with the Kuru race, descended from King Bharata and King Kuru. King Shantanu had a son, Bhishma, born of Ganga, the river goddess. Later, Shantanu had another son, Vichitravirya, with Satyavati. Vichitravirya's sons were Dhritarashtra, who was blind, and Pandu. Pandu became king, and his five sons were Yudhisthira, Bhima, Arjuna, Nakula, and Sahadeva, collectively known as the Pandavas. Dhritarashtra had one hundred sons, the Kauravas, with Duryodhana as the eldest.

The Pandavas and Kauravas grew up together in Dhritarashtra's palace, learning the arts of war from the same teachers. However, Duryodhana harbored deep jealousy towards the Pandavas, constantly seeking to harm them. He attempted to poison Bhima, who was saved by the Nagas, and tried to burn the Pandavas in a house of lac, from which they escaped through a secret passage.

After numerous adventures, the Pandavas married Draupadi, the beautiful and virtuous daughter of King Drupada. Upon their return to Hastinapura, Dhritarashtra divided the kingdom, granting Indraprastha to the Pandavas and Hastinapura to the Kauravas.

The Pandavas ruled Indraprastha with justice and wisdom, earning the love and prosperity of their people. Yet, Duryodhana's jealousy persisted, and he plotted to seize their kingdom. He invited Yudhisthira to a game of dice, where he cheated, causing Yudhisthira to lose all his possessions, including his kingdom, his brothers, and even Draupadi. Draupadi was then publicly insulted in the assembly hall, prompting Bhima to vow vengeance.

The Pandavas were exiled for thirteen years: twelve years in the forest and one year in disguise. During their forest exile, they faced immense hardships, subsisting on roots and fruits, and confronting various demons and challenges. They constantly moved to evade Duryodhana's spies, yet maintained their dharma and faith in Lord Krishna. In their thirteenth year, they lived in disguise in the kingdom of Virata. Yudhisthira became a courtesan, Bhima a cook, Arjuna a eunuch dance teacher, Nakula a horse trainer, and Sahadeva a cattle herder. Draupadi served as a maidservant to Queen Sudeshna. Despite many close calls, they successfully completed their year in disguise without being discovered.

After the thirteen years of exile, the Pandavas sent Krishna as their messenger to Hastinapura to reclaim their rightful share of the kingdom. Krishna earnestly sought peace, but Duryodhana stubbornly refused to concede even five villages, declaring he would not part with even a needle's point of land without a war. This rejection made war inevitable. Both sides gathered their armies, preparing for the great battle. The Pandavas, though fewer in number, were bolstered by the righteousness of their cause and the divine support of Lord Krishna. The Kauravas, with their vast army, were driven by Duryodhana's greed and hatred.

The Kurukshetra War commenced, a devastating conflict pitting cousins against cousins, teachers against students, and friends against friends. It was a war marked by heroic deeds, tragic losses, and profound moral dilemmas.

One of the most significant events was the fall of Bhishma, the grand-uncle of both sides and commander of the Kaurava army. Invincible while armed, he was defeated by Arjuna with the aid of Shikhandi, who was born a woman and later became a man. Bhishma fell onto a bed of arrows, awaiting the auspicious time of the sun's northward turn.

Drona, the revered teacher of both the Pandavas and Kauravas, assumed command after Bhishma. He was a formidable warrior, proving difficult for the Pandavas to overcome. Krishna devised a plan: Bhima killed an elephant named Ashwatthama, and Yudhisthira was made to proclaim, "Ashwatthama is dead," adding in a low voice, "the elephant." Drona, believing his son Ashwatthama was dead, laid down his weapons in grief and was subsequently killed by Dhrishtadyumna. This act, though strategically necessary, represented a moral compromise for Yudhisthira, known for his unwavering truthfulness.

Karna, the greatest warrior on the Kaurava side after Bhishma and Drona, was the son of Kunti (mother of the Pandavas) and the Sun God, Surya. He was a half-brother to the Pandavas, a secret known only to a few. Bound by loyalty to Duryodhana, who had befriended him when he was rejected by society, Karna was eventually killed by Arjuna in a fierce duel after his chariot wheel became stuck in the mud, leaving him momentarily unarmed.

Shalya, the maternal uncle of Nakula and Sahadeva, was tricked into fighting for the Kauravas. He served as Karna's charioteer and was later killed by Yudhisthira.

The war culminated in the death of Duryodhana at the hands of Bhima, who crushed his thighs, fulfilling his vow made when Draupadi was insulted. The Kaurava army was annihilated, and the Pandavas emerged victorious, but at a terrible cost. Almost all the great warriors on both sides were killed, leaving behind a trail of grief and despair.

Saupiika Parva describes the night attack by Ashwatthama, Kritavarma, and Kripacharya on the Pandava camp, where they massacred the remaining Pandava forces, including Draupadi's sons. This act of revenge was a dark stain on the already bloody war.

Strt Parva describes the lamentations of the women after the war, especially Gandhari, the mother of the Kauravas, who cursed Krishna for not preventing the war.

Shdnti Parva and Anushasana Parva contain the instructions of Bhishma to Yudhisthira on dharma, statecraft, and the duties of a king, as he lay dying on the bed of arrows. These sections are rich in philosophical and ethical teachings.

After the war, Yudhisthira was crowned king of Hastinapura. The Pandavas ruled with justice and wisdom for many years, performing the Ashwamedha Yajna (horse sacrifice) to establish their sovereignty.

In their old age, Dhritarashtra, Gandhari, and Kunti retired to the forest (Ashramavdsika Parva) to perform asceticism and prepare for death, eventually perishing in a forest fire.

The Pandavas, too, decided to renounce the world and embark on their final journey, the Mahaprasthanika Parva (the Great Departure). They climbed the Himalayas, accompanied by a dog, and one by one, they fell along the way due to their past sins. Only Yudhisthira and the dog reached the summit.

In the Svargarohanika Parva (the Ascent to Heaven), Yudhisthira was tested one last time. He refused to enter heaven without his brothers and Draupadi, and even the faithful dog. After witnessing the torments of hell and remaining steadfast in his compassion, he was finally reunited with his brothers, Draupadi, and other heroes in heaven. The Mahabharata thus concludes with the Pandavas attaining heaven, highlighting the ultimate triumph of dharma and righteousness, despite the suffering and losses endured.
moral: The Mahabharata teaches us that adherence to dharma (righteousness) is paramount, even in the face of immense adversity. It illustrates the devastating consequences of greed, envy, and pride, and the ultimate triumph of truth and justice. The epic emphasizes the importance of duty, sacrifice, and unwavering faith in the divine. It also highlights the complexities of moral choices and the nuanced nature of right and wrong, reminding us that even the most righteous can falter, and redemption is always possible. The story underscores the cyclical nature of time and the eternal struggle between good and evil, ultimately affirming the victory of dharma.

performance_notes.opening_line: In the ancient lands of Bharatavarsha, a tale of dharma and destiny unfolded, known as the Mahabharata, the story of the great war.

title: The Story of Sage Bhrigu Kicking Vishnu
narrative_text:
Long ago, on the sacred banks of the river Saraswati, a great assembly of revered sages, known as Maharshis, gathered. A profound debate arose among them: who among the divine Trinity – Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva – was the most supreme? Some argued for Brahma, the creator; others championed Vishnu, the preserver; while a third group upheld Shiva, the destroyer, as the greatest. Unable to reach a consensus, they collectively decided to send the wise and powerful Sage Bhrigu to ascertain the truth.

Bhrigu first journeyed to Brahmaloka, the abode of Lord Brahma. There, in the midst of many sages, Brahma sat in his majestic assembly. Bhrigu, without offering the customary respects, simply took a seat on a stool. This blatant act of disrespect deeply offended Brahma, who was filled with anger. Without a word, Bhrigu departed from Brahma's realm.

Next, he proceeded to Kailasa, the dwelling of Lord Shiva. As Bhrigu approached, Shiva, ever gracious, rose from his seat and moved to embrace him in welcome. However, Bhrigu recoiled, declaring, "Do not touch me!" Enraged by this insult, Shiva prepared to strike Bhrigu with his formidable trident. It was only through the timely intervention of Goddess Parvati that Shiva's wrath was appeased, and Bhrigu was spared.

Finally, Bhrigu made his way to Vaikuntha, the celestial abode of Lord Mahavishnu. There, he found Mahavishnu in a deep slumber. Witnessing the preserver of the universe seemingly oblivious in sleep, Bhrigu, in his audacious quest for truth, delivered a forceful kick to Vishnu's chest.

Startled awake, Vishnu immediately rose and, seeing Bhrigu before him, humbly begged the sage's pardon. He expressed deep regret for his perceived negligence and declared that he would forever bear Bhrigu's footprint on his chest as a mark of his repentance and respect for the sage. This sacred mark, known as 'Srivatsa', remains on Vishnu's chest to this day.

Through this extraordinary test, the Maharshis concluded that Lord Mahavishnu, with his boundless patience, humility, and forgiveness, was indeed the noblest and most supreme among the Trimurti. This story teaches us the profound value of humility and forgiveness, even in the face of extreme provocation, and highlights Vishnu's compassionate nature as the ultimate preserver of dharma.
moral: The story teaches the profound value of humility, patience, and forgiveness, demonstrating that true greatness lies in compassion and the ability to remain calm and forgiving even when provoked.

title: The Monkey and The Crocodile
alt_titles: The Foolish Crocodile and the Clever Monkey
narrative_text: A long time ago, there was a monkey who lived in a rose-apple tree by the side of a river. He lived alone, but was very happy. One day, a crocodile came out of the river. He swam up to the tree and told the monkey that he had travelled a long distance and was in search of food as he was very hungry. The kind monkey offered him a few rose apples. The crocodile thanked him and asked him if he could visit the monkey again. "You are always welcome," said the monkey. After that, the crocodile started visiting the monkey every day. They would share rose-apples and talk about everything they knew. The crocodile told the monkey that he had a wife and that they lived on the other side of the river. So the generous monkey plucked many rose-apples and gave them to the crocodile for his wife. The crocodile’s wife loved the rose-apples, but she grew jealous of her husband spending so much time away from her, in the company of his new friend. She pretended as if she did not believe that her husband, a crocodile, could be friends with a monkey. The crocodile convinced her of his friendship with the monkey. The crocodile’s wife thought to herself, "If the monkey eats only these sweet rose-apples, his flesh must be sweet too. He would be a delicious dinner." So she asked her husband to invite his friend home so that she could meet him. But the crocodile was not happy about inviting his friend home. So the wife thought of a plan. She pretended to be very ill and told the crocodile that the doctor had said that she could get well only if she ate a monkey’s heart. If her husband wanted to save her life, he must bring her his friend’s heart. The crocodile was stupid enough to believe his wife. But the thought of killing his friend made him very unhappy. He did not want to harm his friend. At the same time, he did not want his wife to die. So the crocodile went to the rose apple tree and invited the monkey home to meet his wife. The monkey was very happy and readily agreed. The crocodile told the monkey that he could ride across the river to the other bank on his back. On reaching the middle of the river, the crocodile began to sink. The frightened monkey asked him why he was doing so. "I want to kill you," said the crocodile. "My wife is ill and she can recover only if she eats a monkey’s heart." The monkey was shocked and started thinking fast. He told the crocodile that he would gladly give up his heart to save the life of the crocodile’s wife, but he had left his heart behind in his tree. He requested the crocodile to turn back quickly so he could fetch his heart. The stupid crocodile became happy when he heard that the monkey was kind enough to offer his heart without any resistance. He couldn’t believe that the monkey was willing to offer his heart without any resistance. The thought of not killing his friend while proving his love towards his wife made him happy. The crocodile turned back and swam as fast as he could to the tree. When they reached, the monkey climbed up the tree to safety. He looked down at the crocodile and said, "Now you can go back to your wicked wife and tell her that her husband is the biggest fool in this world. Your foolishness has no parallel. You were ready to take my life because of an unjust demand from your wife. Then you were stupid enough to believe me and brought me back to the tree."
moral: A wise enemy is better than a foolish friend.

title: The Wise Old Owl and the Overweening Crow
narrative_text: In a dense forest, there lived a boastful Crow who often mocked the quiet, nocturnal Owl. The Crow, proud of its daytime vision and loud caw, would frequently challenge the Owl, proclaiming its superiority. The Owl, wise and patient, never responded to the Crow's taunts, choosing instead to observe and plan. One day, the Crow, in its arrogance, ventured too close to the Owl's roost at dusk, believing its quickness would protect it. However, as night fell, the Owl, with its keen night vision and silent flight, swiftly apprehended the overweening Crow, teaching it a harsh lesson about humility and the power of choosing the right time and place for action. The Crow, humbled and defeated, finally understood that true strength lies not in boastfulness, but in wisdom and strategic thinking.
moral: Wisdom and patience can overcome arrogance and brute force. It is prudent to understand one's own strengths and weaknesses, and those of an adversary, before engaging in conflict.

title: The Foolish, Timid Rabbit
alt_titles: Jataka: The story of the foolish tiger and the clever rabbit
narrative_text: Once upon a time, a Rabbit was asleep under a palm-tree. All at once he woke up, and thought: "What if the world should break up! What then would become of me?" At that moment a ripe coconut fell down, making a great noise. The Rabbit jumped up and ran as fast as he could, without even looking to see what made the noise. Another Rabbit saw him running, and called after him, "What are you running so for, Brother Rabbit?" "The world is breaking up!" he cried. And on he ran. The second Rabbit ran after him. Soon they were joined by a third, then a fourth, and so on, and on, until a hundred thousand Rabbits were running as fast as they could. A Lion saw the Rabbits running, and called out, "Why are you running so fast?" "The world is breaking up!" they all cried, and never stopped. The Lion ran after them, and as he ran he called out, "Stop! Stop! Let me see what you mean." But the Rabbits could not stop. At last the Lion ran on ahead and roared three times. This stopped the Rabbits. "What is this you say?" asked the Lion. "The world is breaking up!" they cried. "Where did you see it breaking up?" asked the Lion. "I did not see it, but the other Rabbits told me," said one. "And I heard it from the other Rabbits," said another. At last the Lion came to the first Rabbit. "Where did you see the world breaking up?" he asked. "I was asleep under a palm-tree," said the Rabbit, "and I was awakened by a great noise. I thought the world was breaking up, and I ran away." "Let us go to the place and see," said the Lion. So the Lion and the Rabbits went to the palm-tree. There they saw the coconut that had fallen. The Lion turned to the Rabbits and said: "You see, the world is not breaking up. You heard the noise of a falling coconut, and you ran away without stopping to see what it was. Go back to your homes, and do not be so foolish again."
moral: Do not believe everything you hear without investigating it yourself.

title: Akbar-Birbal: The Story of the Emperor's New Law
narrative_text: King Akbar stormed into Birbal’s office and towered angrily over his chief advisor sitting at his writing table. “Birbal, why have you advised all servants in my palace to wash with soap each time they leave the latrine? Do you think they are careless enough to return to work with poop on their hands? My Chancellor of the Palace would throw them in the dungeon for life!” That fussy chief servant had scurried into Birbal’s chamber closely behind the king, and now nodded his double chins in agreement. Birbal hastily put down the parathas and pickles he had been eating and replied: “Your wisdom is as deep as the oceans, my lord. I was, of course in error. If there is nothing to see, then there can be no contamination. I will cancel my order at once.” But as Birbal forward leaned to write the new order, he bumped against his plate. A drop of pickle oil splattered just far enough to land on the king’s hand. “Oh, pardon me Jahanpanah [my lord]. I am most sorry.” King Akbar turned to his Chancellor, “Well, clean it off – and quickly!” The Chancellor moved with surprising speed for one so round, grabbing a nearby pitcher and pouring water over the King’s hand. Birbal, clearly relieved at the Chancellor’s quick work, continued: “As we were saying, hands that appear clean can never be contaminated. As his Highness suggests, I will require soap only when hands look dirty.” Although Birbal was speaking, the king had stopped listening. Instead, his highness sniffed the back of his hands and wrinkled his nose. Apparently, some of the smell from the pickle oil had remained, even after rinsing with water. King Akbar now held up his hand, and Birbal paused. “Wait,” the king said, “I have had a Royal Idea. The correct rule is: Require soap only when hands appear dirty or are smelly.” “I can no more count your wisdom, my lord, than the grains of sand on the beach. Of course I was wrong to insist on soap only when the contamination can be seen. As your royal nose points out, contamination can remain even when it cannot be seen. Fortunately, when the eyes cannot see it and the nose cannot smell it, we know there is no contamination. None can doubt your highness’s wisdom on this point. “Quite right,” the Chancellor of the Palace puffed. “In fact, if something were present but neither seen nor smelt, it must truly be the work of the devil! “My colleague’s keen mind moves much faster than my own,” Birbal replied. “In fact, possession of devil’s work is usually a crime punishable by death. “I see your point,” King Akbar added. “Yes, I shall make that law.” “Your understanding, my lord, is as vast as the sky,” added Birbal. He picked up his quill pen to write the new law, but he paused with his pen just above the page. “If we announced this law in the open court, criminals might flee with their devilish secrets out of the kingdom. The border police must be alerted before we announce the new law.” “Hmm, a good point,” King Akbar replied. “But how can we be sure criminals do not learn of it before the border police? Summon the Panther. He may have ideas.” The Chancellor of the Palace scurried off, and returned trailing the Panther, the well-deserved name of the king’s chief general. At two meters tall, the Panther towered over the Chancellor. The Panther’s solid muscle also contrasted with the rounded curves of the plump chief servant. The Panther was the only advisor allowed to carry a weapon in the presence of King Akbar– in part because nobody else could lift the great sword he kept by his side. The Panther bowed his muscular frame to the king. His voice rumbled deeply, sounding as one might imagine the speech of a mountain: “How may I serve you, my Lord?” Birbal explained, “We must write a new law, but we fear criminals will escape our borders when they hear of it. Thus, we want to send it first to all corners of the kingdom without the criminals learning about it. The Panther settled his massive head in his even larger hands. “That is a puzzle,” he declared as he began to think deeply. Seeing him lost in thought was a bit like watching a volcano waiting to explode. Suddenly his eyes shot up, and even the brave King jumped back in surprise as the Panther clenched his fists in excitement. “I have it! We have recently begun sending letters to our ambassadors in foreign lands using a special ink.” “But might our enemies read the letters as they travel?” the king asked. “Ah, that’s what makes it special. I write with this ink…” With that, he reached deep into a pocket of his cloak. One imagined him withdrawing a scimitar or throwing knife, but he pulled out only a quill pen and a small ink bottle, looking like children’s toys cupped within his massive hand. “Observe, my Lord.” the Panther said as he dipped the quill in the ink and wrote rapidly on a piece of parchment from Birbal’s desk. The king peered over the Panther’s tall shoulder, watching the pen move but seeing no change to the parchment. “But there is nothing there!” Akbar grabbed the paper to peer at it closely, but there was no sign (or even odor) to show the ink had passed over the page. “Ah, but as I said, that is what makes it special.” Now the general walked to the fireplace and waved the parchment just above the flames. The king watched in amazement as letters appeared. “What magic is this!?” He rushed over and grabbed the previously blank letter from his giant general’s hand. Where a moment before there had been nothing, now the letters clearly read: “This ink cannot be seen, heard, or smelled, but it still carries a message.” Birbal walked over and read the message. “Ah, it is a sad thing,” he muttered, shaking his head with a gloomy expression. “What is?” Akbar replied. “To have to execute such a fine general.” The muscle-bound giant’s eyebrows shot up in surprise. Nobody saw his massive hand even move, yet somehow the Panther had managed to draw his long sword and was now holding its sharp point at Birbal’s throat! Even the king, brave when hunting the most dangerous wild animals, shifted his body away from the Panther on the prowl. “Wait, wait, Birbal,” the king quickly said. “Your understanding of my newest law is mistaken. A new Royal Idea has struck me, and there shall be no executions today.” Watching the giant general’s body relax was like watching a mighty python uncoil. Birbal did not start to breathe until the Panther returned his great sword to its scabbard. The King continued hurriedly, “We all know, of course, that some substances can be present, even if one cannot see or smell them.” “Ah,” Birbal replied, “Your thoughts run more swiftly than any deer. I am so glad you corrected my error before I wrote such a foolish law.” “But I have thought more on contamination,” Akbar went on. “We have no need for any law requiring soap, as my servants use only their right hands to touch food. Thus, contamination cannot spread, even if it exists!” Birbal nodded in agreement, “My lord, you have more insights than there are stars in the sky.” The Panther was confused, so Birbal explained, “When one of the king’s servants leaves the latrine, they might have contamination on their left hand, but it cannot spread to food, so the king is safe.” “But can you be sure?” the Panther asked. At that moment the Mir Bakawal, the head of the royal kitchens, appeared, bearing a platter of snacks for the King and his advisors. Birbal took advantage of this opportunity and replied, “Let us demonstrate the unfailing wisdom of our noble king.” With that he took a pinch of the yellow saffron powder that always accompanied royal meals, and smeared a bit on the left palm of the Chancellor of the Palace and a pinch on the left palm of the Mir Bakawal. “Come, my friends, will you join the king for a bite?” Birbal arranged cushions on the beautiful carpet so the Chancellor and Mir Bakawal could each share a samosa with King Akbar. Both were honored to sit with their king. Each sat demurely, with their yellow-tinged left had resting carefully in their laps. “You see,” Birbal explained to the General-in-Chief, “As they eat only with their right hands, no contamination can spread.” “Hmm…,” the Panther replied, “I have great respect of your intelligence, Birbal, but perhaps you are not as clever as you think. The king had no idea what his giant general was referring to. Nevertheless, a few minutes after the Mir Bakawal returned to the royal kitchens, the King willingly followed the Panther’s lead. Birbal asked the Chancellor of the Palace to meet them in a few minutes in the throne room. The giant general led King Akbar and Birbal down passages his highness had never seen before, all the way to the royal kitchens. There they saw the Mir Bakawal preparing the king’s next meal. As he rolled and kneaded the white dough for the king’s naan, they saw small yellow dots mixing in. The king looked repulsed by what he had seen, as his cheeks turned a pale green. The Mir Bakawal noticed King Akbar watching, but misunderstood his anxious gaze. “Oh, don’t worry about a trace of saffron. It is too little for you to detect the smell or taste. In fact, it won’t even be visible once the naan is cooked.” The king’s complexion turned a deeper shade of green, and he looked about to be ill. “It is time to go,” King Akbar exclaimed as he rushed up the stairs to his throne room. He was relieved to find nobody there was cooking with stained hands. He sat in throne and caught his breath. A moment later he called, “My scepter, please.” The Chancellor rushed over, knelt on one knee, bowed his head low, and held out the scepter flat on his two hands: “My lord.” The king leaned forward to pick up his scepter – but it was no longer there! Almost faster than a human eye could see, the Panther leapt halfway across the room to grab the scepter from the Chancellor before the king could reach it. “What is the meaning of this!” the king demanded. “I am most sorry, my lord, but look what contaminates the scepter.” The king looked at the top of the scepter the Panther now held gingerly from the bottom. High on scepter, where the king usually held it, the king could see yellow powder! “My friend Birbal,” the King began, “I have had yet another Royal Idea. Contamination from the latrine can exist even if it is invisible and has no odor. In addition, contamination can spread from the left hand to food and to objects. Thus, the new law shall be: All servants must wash with soap each time they leave the latrine” Birbal bowed low and replied in a humble voice, “I am your servant, my lord. I shall write this law, and all shall see your wisdom.” THE END And they had clean hands happily and ever after
moral: The story highlights the importance of hygiene and the fact that contamination can exist and spread even when it is not visible or detectable by smell. It also showcases Birbal's cleverness in demonstrating this truth to King Akbar.

title: Tenali Raman: The story of the king's new advisor
alt_titles: Tenali Raman and the New Minister
narrative_text: In the grand court of King Krishnadevaraya, the ministers were singing praises of their king. The kingdom was prosperous, the treasury was full, and the people were happy. The King, swelling with pride, declared, 'It is because I am a good and honest person that my subjects are like me.' The courtiers all agreed, except for Tenali Raman, the King's special advisor, who remained silent. Noticing this, the King asked, 'Tenali Raman, you don't seem to agree?' Tenali Raman replied, 'Your Majesty, your virtues are great, but the reason for our prosperity is the excellent system of governance we have. Without it, things would be very different.' The King disagreed, and to prove his point, he declared that for one day, all locks and doors in the kingdom should be left open. He believed that his subjects were so honest that no one would steal. Tenali Raman knew this was a foolish idea and would only invite trouble. That night, a gang of thieves, hearing of the King's proclamation, decided to rob the royal treasury. As they approached the palace, they saw Tenali Raman disguised as a simpleton, carrying a heavy chest. The thieves, thinking it was full of gold, offered to help him. Tenali Raman, with a mischievous glint in his eye, agreed. He led them to a well and told them to hide the chest inside it, promising to share the loot with them later. The thieves, blinded by greed, readily agreed. Once the chest was in the well, Tenali Raman raised an alarm, and the palace guards rushed to the spot. The thieves were caught red-handed. The next day, the King, humbled by the incident, realized the wisdom in Tenali Raman's words. He understood that a good system of governance is essential for a prosperous kingdom and that even good people can be tempted by opportunity. From that day on, King Krishnadevaraya valued Tenali Raman's advice even more, and the kingdom of Vijayanagara continued to flourish under their wise rule.
moral: A good system of governance is more important than the individual goodness of the ruler or the subjects.

title: The Princess and the Magical Bird
alt_titles: The Generous Prince and the Monster Bird
narrative_text:
King Vikram, bound by his promise to a sage, carried the Vetala through the silent night. To lighten the arduous journey, the Vetala began a tale. Once, in a kingdom nestled amidst lush forests, lived a compassionate Prince Jimutvahan. He nurtured a small plant with great care, which blossomed into a magnificent Kalp Tree. One day, the tree, pleased with the prince's devotion, offered to grant his wishes. Prince Jimutvahan, ever selfless, wished for the well-being of his subjects. The Kalp Tree showered coins, which the prince generously distributed among his people.

Among those he encountered was a distressed Princess Indumati, whose younger brother, Shankhchoor, was destined to be sacrificed to a fearsome Monster Bird. The princess pleaded for help, and the prince, moved by her plight, vowed to protect Shankhchoor. He rushed to the designated spot, intending to face the Monster Bird himself. Shankhchoor, a noble soul, insisted on fulfilling his fate, arguing that the prince should not sacrifice himself. However, Prince Jimutvahan stood firm, declaring it his royal duty to protect his people.

The Monster Bird arrived, and impressed by the prince's unwavering courage and sense of duty, it questioned his motives. The prince explained that as the ruler, it was his responsibility to safeguard his subjects. The bird, deeply touched, agreed to spare the people. When asked about its sustenance, the prince offered to host the bird as a guest and provide for its needs. He then approached the Kalp Tree, requesting it to provide food for his new guest. The tree, in turn, showered an abundance of fruits for the Monster Bird.

After narrating this tale, the Vetala posed a question to King Vikram: 'Between Prince Jimutvahan and Shankhchoor, whose sacrifice was superior?' King Vikram, after careful thought, replied that Shankhchoor's sacrifice was superior. He was not selfish; even when the prince offered to die in his place, he insisted on sacrificing his own life. As soon as Vikram uttered these words, the Vetala, true to his nature, flew back to his tree, leaving King Vikram to continue his quest.
moral: True generosity lies not only in giving but also in the willingness to sacrifice for the well-being of others, even when it means facing personal danger. Selflessness and a strong sense of duty are virtues that inspire and protect.

safety_notes: The story contains themes of sacrifice and a 'monster bird,' which might be mildly unsettling for very sensitive listeners. However, the overall tone is positive, emphasizing courage and generosity.

title: The Brave Wrestler of Haryana
alt_titles: The Wrestlers: A Story of Heroes (Adapted)
The Mighty Wrestlers of the North
narrative_text:
Once upon a time, in a land far away, there lived a wrestler of immense strength and courage. He had heard tales of an equally mighty wrestler residing in the vibrant lands of India, specifically in the heart of Haryana. Driven by a desire to test his prowess, he packed ten thousand pounds of flour in his blanket, hoisted it onto his head, and set forth on his journey.

As evening approached, he discovered a small pond in the vast desert. After quenching his thirst, he mixed his flour into the remaining water, creating a thick gruel which he heartily consumed before falling into a deep slumber under a tree.

Meanwhile, an elephant, accustomed to drinking from this very pond, arrived to find it nearly empty, save for a muddy residue. Distraught, it spotted the sleeping wrestler and, believing him to be the cause of its predicament, furiously stamped on his head. The wrestler, barely stirring, sleepily remarked, 'What's the matter? If you wish to shampoo my head, do it properly! What's worth doing at all is worth doing well; put some weight into it, my friend!'

The astonished elephant ceased its stamping but, undeterred, wrapped its trunk around the wrestler's waist, intending to hurl him to the ground. 'Ho! ho! my little friend, is that your plan?' yawned the wrestler, and with a swift move, he caught hold of the elephant's tail, swung the massive creature over his shoulder, and continued his journey with a jaunty step.

Upon reaching his destination, the wrestler stood outside the Indian wrestler's home and called out, 'Ho! my friend! Come out and try a fall!' From within, the Indian wrestler's wife replied, 'My husband is not home today; he has gone to the wood to cut pea-sticks.'

'Very well,' said the foreign wrestler, 'when he returns, give him this with my compliments, and tell him his challenger has arrived from afar.' With that, he effortlessly tossed the elephant over the courtyard wall.

Suddenly, a child's voice cried from inside, 'Oh, mamma! mamma! That nasty man has thrown a mouse over the wall into my lap! What shall I do with it?' The mother calmly replied, 'Never mind, little daughter! Papa will teach him better manners. Take the grass broom and sweep the mouse away.' A sweeping sound followed, and moments later, the lifeless elephant came flying back over the wall.

'Ahem!' thought the foreign wrestler, 'if the little daughter possesses such strength, her father must indeed be a formidable opponent!' He then proceeded to the wood, where he soon encountered the Indian wrestler, who was pulling a hundred and sixty carts laden with brushwood.

'Now we shall see!' thought the stranger, and stealthily grabbing the last cart, he began to pull. The Indian wrestler, feeling an unusual resistance, thought, 'That's a deep rut!' and pulled harder. For an hour, the carts remained motionless. Finally, the Indian wrestler realized, 'I believe someone is hanging on behind!' He walked back to investigate, and the foreign wrestler stepped forward, saying, 'We seem well-matched; let us have a fall together.'

'With all my heart!' replied the Indian wrestler, 'but not here in the wilds; there's no joy in fighting without an audience.'

'But I haven't time to wait!' said the stranger, 'I must be off at once, so it must be here or nowhere.'

Just then, an old woman strode by with remarkable speed. 'Here's an audience!' cried the foreign wrestler, calling out, 'Mother! mother! Stop and see fair play!'

'I can't, my sons, I can't!' she replied, 'for my daughter is going to steal my camels, and I am off to stop her; but if you like, you can jump onto the palm of my hand and wrestle there as I go along.'

The wrestlers, amazed, jumped onto the old woman's palm and wrestled as she strode over hills and valleys.

When the old woman's daughter saw her mother approaching with the wrestlers on her hand, she thought, 'Here she comes, with the soldiers she spoke about! It's time for me to be off!' She then gathered the hundred and sixty camels, tied them in her blanket, and swinging it over her shoulder, ran off. One of the camels groaned from the blanket, so to quiet it, the girl tore down a tree or two and stuffed them into the bundle. The farmer to whom the trees belonged came running, shouting, 'Stop thief! Stop thief!'

'Thief, indeed!' retorted the girl angrily, and with that, she bundled the farmer, his fields, crops, oxen, house, and all into her blanket.

Soon, she arrived at a town, and feeling hungry, asked a pastry-cook for sweets. When he refused, she scooped up the entire town. She continued this, adding everything she encountered to her blanket until it was completely full.

Finally, she came across a large watermelon. Thirsty, she ate it, and feeling sleepy, decided to rest. But the camels in her bundle continued their noisy grumbling, disturbing her peace. So, she packed everything into the lower half of the watermelon rind, placed the upper half as a lid, rolled herself in her blanket, and used the melon as a pillow.

While she slept, a great flood arose and carried the watermelon downstream. It eventually lodged on a mud-bank. The lid fell off, and out hopped the camels, the trees, the farmer, the oxen, the house, the town, and all the other things, creating a new world on the mud-bank in the middle of the river.

This tale, though exaggerated, reminds us that true strength lies not just in physical prowess, but also in wit, determination, and the unexpected power found in ordinary beings. It also highlights the rich tapestry of life and how even the most extraordinary events can lead to new beginnings.
moral: True strength encompasses not only physical power but also wit, determination, and the unexpected capabilities of individuals. Extraordinary events can lead to new beginnings and the creation of new worlds.

title: The Clever Bandit
narrative_text:
In a dense forest, a mysterious sanyasi (ascetic) established an ashram, attracting many devotees and travelers. However, with the increase in visitors, the forest also became a haunt for robbers, leading to frequent complaints from the people. The sanyasi, known for his wisdom, assured everyone that he would ensure their safety. 

One night, a wealthy family traveling through the forest was accosted by a bandit chief. The chief, with a menacing voice, claimed to have a large gang hidden in a nearby pit and demanded all their valuables. Fearing for their lives, the family handed over their possessions. 

Later, the same bandit chief stopped a clever trader. The trader, understanding the situation, offered a small amount of money to the bandit. However, he then loudly announced to the supposed 'gang' in the pit that he had given their chief a much larger sum, hoping to create discord among the bandits. 

Unbeknownst to the bandit, the trader had soldiers accompanying him, who were hiding nearby. Upon hearing the trader's loud announcement, the soldiers emerged and captured the bandit. They then discovered that the 'gang' in the pit was nothing more than a collection of scarecrows. 

The soldiers took the captured bandit to the sanyasi's ashram, expecting to find the wise sanyasi. To their astonishment, the ashram was empty, save for a collection of stolen goods and a fake beard hanging on a peg. The bandit, now exposed, confessed his elaborate scheme: he was the sanyasi by day, luring wealthy travelers to the forest, and the bandit chief by night, robbing them with the illusion of a large gang. Everyone present was left dumbfounded by his audacious cleverness.
moral: Cleverness can be used for both good and ill, but deceit eventually unravels. True wisdom lies in honesty and integrity.

title: The Devotion of Sant Namdev and the Idol
alt_titles: Sant Namdev and the Idol Comes to Life
The Miracle of Sant Namdev's Offering
narrative_text:
In the ancient land of Maharashtra, there lived a young boy named Namdev, whose heart overflowed with love for Bhagwan Vitthal, the revered deity of Pandharpur. From his earliest days, Namdev's devotion was profound, and his sweet voice resonated with beautiful bhajans, songs of praise that he believed reached the divine. He saw God's presence in every corner of the world.

Namdev spent countless hours in the temple, offering flowers, singing hymns, and conversing with the idol of Vitthal as if it were his dearest friend. One day, as he sang a particularly moving abhang, he perceived a wondrous sparkle emanating from the idol, confirming in his innocent heart that his songs truly touched Bhagwan Vitthal.

However, not all children shared Namdev's unwavering faith. Some challenged him, questioning, "If your God truly listens, why does He not partake of the food you offer?" Determined to demonstrate his faith, Namdev prepared a plate of delicious food and reverently placed it before the idol. With deep conviction, he declared, "I will not eat until You have eaten first."

Though many around him scoffed and laughed, Namdev remained steadfast, his face alight with a serene smile, certain that Bhagwan was listening. As the sun dipped below the horizon and twilight deepened, an extraordinary miracle unfolded. To the astonishment of all present, the idol of Vitthal stirred, extended its hand, and began to consume the offered food. Namdev's heart swelled with boundless joy and an even deeper faith. He then shared with everyone the profound truth that genuine devotion lies in unwavering trust in God and the belief in miracles.

News of this miraculous event spread far and wide, drawing people from distant lands to witness Namdev's devotion. As he matured, Namdev journeyed to Pandharpur, the sacred city of Bhagwan Vitthal, tirelessly spreading messages of love and humility. His devotion was so intense that his soulful abhangs continue to inspire devotees to this day.

On another occasion, Sant Namdev visited the Aundha Nagnath Temple to sing bhajans. However, some priests, adhering to strict customs, forbade him from singing inside the sanctum. Undeterred but heartbroken, Namdev sat outside the temple and poured his heart into his devotional songs. Such was the purity and intensity of his love that, in response to his devotion, the entire temple miraculously turned to face him. This astonishing event revealed to everyone that God responds not to rigid rules or social standing, but to a pure and loving heart.

Namdev's life exemplified that true worship transcends mere rituals and prayers; it is found in living with love and compassion towards all beings. He became a revered saint, inspiring countless individuals to embrace devotion to Bhagwan Vitthal and to serve humanity with kindness.
moral: True devotion, characterized by unwavering faith and a pure heart, can manifest miracles and connect one directly with the Divine, irrespective of external circumstances or societal norms. God listens to the sincerity of the heart, not to one's position or place.

title: Makar Sankranti: The Sun's Journey and the Harvest's Embrace
narrative_text:
In the heart of winter, when the days are short and the air is crisp, comes a time of great joy and spiritual significance across India – Makar Sankranti. It is not just a single festival, but a tapestry of celebrations, each thread woven with devotion, gratitude, and the promise of new beginnings. At its core, Makar Sankranti marks a celestial journey, the moment when Surya Dev, the revered Sun God, begins his northward movement, entering the zodiac sign of Makara, or Capricorn. This transition, known as Uttarayan, is considered profoundly auspicious, a time when the divine energies are believed to be more accessible, and prayers are heard with greater clarity.

Across the vast lands of India, this day blossoms into various forms. In the northern plains, the skies come alive with vibrant kites, dancing in the crisp winter air, a symbol of freedom and aspiration. Families gather, sharing the warmth of bonfires, and indulging in delicacies made from sesame seeds and jaggery – 'tilgul' – a sweet reminder to speak kindly and spread sweetness in relationships. In the west, especially in Gujarat, the kite festival, 'Uttarayan', is a spectacle of color and camaraderie, where the spirit of competition blends with joyous celebration.

Down south, the festival transforms into Pongal, a four-day harvest celebration where freshly harvested rice is cooked with milk and jaggery, offered to the Sun God as a token of gratitude for a bountiful yield. Homes are adorned with intricate 'kolam' designs, and cattle, considered sacred, are honored for their contribution to agriculture. In the east, particularly Assam, it is Bihu, a time for feasting, traditional dances, and community gatherings, celebrating the hard work of farmers and the abundance of nature.

Beyond the agricultural and astronomical significance, Makar Sankranti also carries deep spiritual legends. One such tale speaks of Lord Vishnu's triumph over the asuras, the demonic forces. It is said that on this auspicious day, Lord Vishnu vanquished the asuras and buried their heads beneath the Mandar Parvat, signifying the victory of good over evil, and light over darkness. Another legend recounts the story of Sankranti, a powerful deity who defeated a demon named Sankarasur. The day following Makar Sankranti is sometimes referred to as Karidin or Kinkrant, commemorating the day Sankranti slew the demon.

These stories, passed down through generations, remind us that Makar Sankranti is a time for introspection, for shedding the old and embracing the new. It is a period to cleanse oneself, both physically and spiritually, often marked by taking holy dips in sacred rivers like the Ganga, believing it washes away sins and purifies the soul. It is a day to express gratitude to nature, to the Sun God who sustains life, and to the earth that provides sustenance. It is a time for families to unite, for communities to celebrate, and for hearts to be filled with warmth, generosity, and hope. The festival encourages us to look forward with optimism, to cultivate virtues, and to share our blessings with others, just as the sun begins its journey towards longer, brighter days.
moral: Makar Sankranti teaches us the importance of gratitude towards nature, the triumph of good over evil, and the joy of community and new beginnings. It reminds us to embrace warmth, generosity, and spiritual renewal as we move towards brighter times.

title: The Story of Ramayana: Hanuman Ji’s Devotion and His Union with Lord Ram
alt_titles: Hanuman's Unwavering Devotion to Lord Rama
narrative_text:
The timeless epic of Ramayana brings to mind images of devotion, love, and an ideal way of life. This living spiritual heritage of India is filled with divine teachings, profound lessons, and the inspiring lives of Lord Ram and his devotees. Lord Ram and his brother Lakshman were born as heirs to the kingdom of Ayodhya, raised with love and values by their parents, especially Queen Kaushalya. When circumstances led them to the forest along with Sita Ji, their journey became a true test of dharma and inner resolve. Their exile wasn’t just a personal sacrifice; it was a symbol of living one’s truth and standing by one’s word, setting an example for all humanity.

The abduction of Sita by Ravana was a moment of sorrow but also a turning point in divine play. With Sita gone, Lord Ram and Lakshman set out on a determined quest to find her, venturing deep into the forests and through many hardships. These moments highlight Lord Ram’s unwavering patience and deep inner strength.

Hanuman Ji, the son of the Wind God and a disciple of Surya Dev, the Sun God, was destined to serve. Surya’s teachings made Hanuman not just strong, but wise and full of spiritual insight. His life was centered on devotion and service to the divine, with a heart filled with unshakable love and humility for Lord Ram.

In disguise, Hanuman Ji approached Ram and Lakshman, seeking to understand who these noble figures were, and perhaps, to find the one his heart was always waiting for. Hanuman Ji introduced himself in refined Sanskrit, displaying knowledge and reverence. The moment Lord Ram met Hanuman, he knew this was his eternal devotee. With tears in his eyes, Ram embraced Hanuman, recognizing the soul connection they shared, praising Hanuman’s devotion and holding his hands in gratitude.

Hanuman Ji, despite his physical might, was a humble servant. He bore Lord Ram and Lakshman on his shoulders to higher ground, symbolizing both his strength and his dedication. His service was not out of obligation; it was an offering of love. Hanuman’s entire being was devoted to Ram, a divine reunion of the Supreme with His most loyal devotee.

Soon after, Ram formed an alliance with Sugreev, the exiled monkey king. Sugreev’s story reflects the importance of loyalty and true friendship. With Hanuman as the bridge between them, Ram and Sugreev joined forces to find Sita. Hanuman’s conversation with Sugreev was filled with trust and divine assurance, declaring that Lord Ram would rescue Sita and restore dharma. This reminds us how faith, when backed by service and action, becomes unstoppable.
moral: Through faith, service, and inner strength, one can overcome even the greatest challenges. Live with purpose, honor your promises, and serve selflessly. Let devotion guide your life.

title: Mahabharata: The Story of the Pandavas' Final Journey
alt_titles: The Great Journey of the Pandavas
narrative_text:
After the great war of Kurukshetra and ruling Hastinapur for many years, the Pandavas, along with Draupadi, decided to renounce their kingdom and embark on their final journey towards the Himalayas, seeking to ascend to heaven. Yudhishthira crowned Parikshit as his successor, and the five brothers and Draupadi began their pilgrimage. As they left, a loyal dog befriended them and accompanied them on their arduous trek.

Their journey began by heading east, reaching the 'red waters,' possibly the Brahmaputra. There, the god Agni appeared before them, commanding Arjuna to return his celestial bow, Gandiva, to Varuna, from whom he had borrowed it. Arjuna, urged by his brothers, cast the bow and his inexhaustible quivers into the waters. They then turned south, reaching the sea, and proceeded along the west coast of India until they arrived at Dvārakā, only to find it submerged beneath the waves, a sight that filled them with sorrow. Turning north, they stopped at Rishikesh before finally crossing into the Himalayas.

As they ascended the treacherous peaks, Draupadi was the first to fall and die. Bhima, saddened, asked Yudhishthira why she, who was equally devoted to all of them, had perished first. Yudhishthira explained that despite her devotion, Draupadi harbored a special partiality for Arjuna, and thus she reaped the consequences of her actions. The remaining Pandavas continued their ascent, but soon Sahadeva also fell. Yudhishthira revealed that Sahadeva, though virtuous, suffered from the vice of pride and vanity, believing himself unmatched in wisdom. Next, Nakula succumbed, and Yudhishthira explained that Nakula too was afflicted by pride, considering himself the most handsome man in the world. Arjuna, the mighty archer, was the next to fall. Yudhishthira sorrowfully told Bhima that Arjuna's pride in his unparalleled skill and strength led to his demise. Only Yudhishthira, Bhima, and the faithful dog remained.

Eventually, Bhima, exhausted, also collapsed. He questioned Yudhishthira why he, too, was unable to complete the journey. Yudhishthira explained Bhima's vice of gluttony, his habit of eating excessively without regard for others' hunger, coupled with his pride in his immense strength. Thus, Yudhishthira and the dog continued their journey alone.

As they neared the summit of Mount Meru, Indra, the king of gods, appeared in his chariot, offering Yudhishthira a ride to heaven. Yudhishthira, however, refused to enter heaven without his brothers and Draupadi. Indra assured him that they had all already entered heaven. But when Yudhishthira asked if the dog could accompany him, Indra refused, stating that animals were not permitted in heaven. Yudhishthira steadfastly refused to abandon his loyal companion, declaring that to betray a friend, even a dog, would be a great sin. Indra questioned his resolve, reminding him that he had abandoned his brothers and wife, yet clung to a dog. Yudhishthira replied that there was no friendship or enmity with the dead, and he had abandoned them only when they were beyond revival. However, he could not abandon a living being who had shown him such loyalty.

Witnessing Yudhishthira's unwavering commitment, the dog revealed his true form: Dharma, the god of righteousness. Dharma praised Yudhishthira for his virtues, recalling how Yudhishthira had previously chosen to revive Nakula over his other brothers to honor his stepmother, and now, by refusing to abandon his faithful dog, he had once again demonstrated unparalleled righteousness. Narada then appeared, proclaiming that Yudhishthira had surpassed even royal sages in his ascent to heaven in his mortal body. Yudhishthira, the righteous king, saluted the deities and finally entered heaven aboard Indra's chariot, having proven his unwavering adherence to Dharma above all else.

Moral: The story emphasizes the importance of Dharma (righteousness), loyalty, humility, and selflessness. It teaches that true virtue lies in upholding one's principles, even in the face of great temptation or loss, and that pride and attachment to worldly desires can hinder one's spiritual progress. Yudhishthira's unwavering loyalty to the dog, even when offered the direct path to heaven, highlights the supreme value of compassion and fidelity over personal gain.

Yudhishthira's unwavering loyalty to the dog, even when offered the direct path to heaven, highlights the supreme value of compassion and fidelity over personal gain.

title: The Birth of the Trimurtis as Sons of Atri
alt_titles: Atri and Anasuyā: The Trimurtis Incarnate
How Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva Became Sons of Atri
narrative_text:
In ancient times, there lived a virtuous woman named Śīlāvatī, whose devotion to her husband, Ugraśravas, was unparalleled. One day, to fulfill her husband's desire, she carried him on her shoulders towards a courtesan's house. On their journey, they encountered Māṇḍavya Muni, who, in a fit of anger, cursed Ugraśravas to die before sunrise. Distraught, Śīlāvatī, with the power of her chastity, pronounced a counter-curse that the sun should not rise the next day. Indeed, the sun failed to appear, plunging the world into darkness and chaos.

The Trimurtis—Brahmā, Viṣṇu, and Śiva—concerned by the cosmic imbalance, approached Śīlāvatī, accompanied by Anasuyā, the chaste wife of Sage Atri. Anasuyā gently persuaded Śīlāvatī to withdraw her curse, explaining the dire consequences of a world without sunlight. Convinced, Śīlāvatī relented, and the sun rose, bringing light and order back to the universe.

Overjoyed by the restoration of cosmic harmony, the Trimurtis offered Anasuyā a boon. With a humble heart, Anasuyā expressed her deepest wish: that the three supreme deities—Brahmā, Viṣṇu, and Śiva—should be born as her sons. The Trimurtis, impressed by her unwavering devotion and purity, granted her wish.

Thus, Mahāviṣṇu incarnated as Dattātreya, a sage of immense wisdom and spiritual power. Śiva, embodying his fierce yet benevolent aspect, was born as Durvāsas, known for his intense penance and occasional fiery temper. Brahmā, the creator, took birth as Chandra, the Moon god, whose gentle light illuminates the night. This miraculous event showcased the profound spiritual power of Anasuyā and Atri, and the divine grace that blessed their household, bringing the cosmic powers into their earthly abode.
moral: The story highlights the immense power of devotion, chastity, and penance. It teaches that true virtue can influence even the cosmic order and earn the blessings of the highest deities. It also emphasizes the importance of humility and selfless desire.

title: Panchatantra: The Story of the Foolish Jackal and the Wise Crow
narrative_text:
Once, a hungry jackal roamed the bush, his stomach rumbling. He spotted a crow flying overhead, a large piece of cheese clutched in its beak. The jackal's eyes lit up with a cunning idea. "That cheese looks delicious," he thought, "I must find a way to get it." He quickly devised a plan, wiping his mouth to hide his hunger from the crow.

The crow, weary from a long morning of flying, decided to rest on a tree branch. The jackal stealthily approached and sat directly beneath the crow's perch. With a wide, flattering smile, the jackal called out, "Hello, dear Crow! You look absolutely magnificent today. Your feathers are so glossy and healthy; you must take excellent care of yourself."

The crow, who secretly believed himself to be the most beautiful and strongest bird, was immensely flattered by the jackal's words. The jackal continued, "I've also heard that you possess the most enchanting singing voice among all the birds. Would you grace me with a song this morning?"

Swollen with pride, the crow stretched its wings, puffed out its chest, and prepared to sing. As it opened its beak to let out a loud, high-pitched sound, the piece of cheese tumbled from its grasp, landing right in front of the waiting jackal. The crow watched in dismay as the jackal swiftly snatched the cheese and disappeared into the bush. Left angry and hungry, the crow realized its foolishness.
moral: Do not be swayed by flattery, for it can often be a tool of deception used by the cunning.
